Lets run through our current system and make sure were happy with our cognitive equipment before moving on. After all, only a fool would try to climb a mountain with a ripped harness and broken clips!   By the end of this lesson, we would expect that you have met each of the outcomes listed below. Expand each item to read through the outcomes.  If there are any outcomes you have not yet reached, we recommend practising those before progressing. General competence:   Deep processing    You are gaining experience with finding similarities and differences between information, grouping them, and gaining a big-picture understanding.   You have familiarity with the discomfort and confusion involved in trying to find the right groups and express them with non-linear note-taking. Your tolerance for this discomfort is slowly starting to improve.    Self-regulation    You are developing an awareness of how different cognitive processes and methods of learning affect your memory, learning experience, and quality of knowledge.  You are beginning to understand how difficulties in learning can be desirable.  You have some experience with modifying your learning system to achieve different results and effects.    Retrieval    You are using SIR and developing competence with at least three interleaving methods  You are developing greater awareness for how different types of knowledge and different orders of learning require different types of thinking and practice.    Mindset    You have experience with experimenting with different learning methods and learning from mistakes.  You engage with Kolb's regularly.    Self-management    You are able to influence your behaviours and productivity using evidence-based strategies for affecting focus, attention, and motivation.  You regularly manage your tasks and time actively. Prestudy:   You do prestudy regularly before intensive learning events  Prestudy focuses on the big picture rather than small details  Main ideas and concepts are grouped into high-level categories (chunks)  Prestudy does not take too long (usually less than 30 minutes)  When possible, prestudy is done across multiple classes/lectures to cover the full topic in one go, which preserves the relationships within the topic Notes and Processing:   Notes always involve processing of information instead of passive, linear note-taking.  Processed information is made simpler and more concise.  You try to express information with non-linear notes representing relationships through arrows and spatial arrangement.  You avoid using full sentences and too many bullet points when writing notes.  You are used to not having to write everything and are able to tolerate the uncertainty and insecurity that can come from writing less (but thinking more).  You are developing competence with delaying your note-taking.  Questions of Why is this important?, How can I use this? and How can I apply it? are asked during the processing step for groups of information in order to help simplify it or connect ideas together more meaningfully.  You focus on developing a big-picture understanding of the topic before attempting to memorise details or understand concepts in great depth.  You are able to group concepts together based on shared similarities more comfortably. Revision/retrieval:   You have a dedicated spaced retrieval schedule that  generally  follows a spacing of one day, one week, one month.  You use full, uncued recall during retrieval sessions.  Retrieval involves multiple orders of learning (as required by your learning needs)  Retrieval methods are appropriate for the topic's knowledge type (declarative vs procedural) Learning system regulation:   Your learning methods are regularly evaluated for effectiveness to make sure you are using methods that are fit-for-purpose and logically aligned.  You use lower-order memorisation techniques for small details (if necessary)  You actively experiment with new methods and use Kolb's to turn them into marginal gains at least twice per week.  You use your skills at creating learning flows to assess your system. Focus management:   Distractions are removed from the environment before work sessions.  Distraction cheat sheets are used during work sessions to track unchecked distractions and remove them next time.  You take frequent breaks while working to keep your energy levels high  (as a rule of thumb, once you break out of flow and feel that it takes more effort to stay focused, it's break time).  Breaks are timed to prevent them from taking longer than expected.  You are gaining a higher level of self-awareness about what things improve or reduce your focus and distractibility.  You are developing experience with modifying your environment and observing how it impacts your motivation, productivity, and behaviours. Time and task management:   You regularly prioritise your tasks in a way that avoid urgency trapping  You schedule your time realistically using under scheduling  You preserve your sleep duration and quality  Sleep is never compromised within 48 hours of an exam or test By now, you're ready to attend a Live Clinic (if you haven't already).  Live clinics are live, interactive coaching workshops that help you build the skills for self-diagnosis and problem-solving. As you've probably realised, learning new skills always comes with uncertainty. Having the skills to find the root cause and make the right adjustments is a superpower for any learner. Learners who regularly attend Live Clinics tend to:   Develop skills faster  Make fewer mistakes and waste less time  Require less guidance as they can help themselves    Keep your eye out to enrol in the next Live Clinic.  Announcements are sent to your email, posted on our Discord, and appear on your dashboard! Sorry, your friends can't come!:  Live Clinics are exclusively for members. Your friends will have to join the program too! Our recommended tasks can't cover absolutely everything! Make sure you're adding custom tasks for your focus lists so you can keep translating new theory into practice. Remember, a skill is only useful if you can use it!  Spending time to practise and hone your mastery is always time well-spent. Recommended task 1Perform your first skills audit   As your learning system grows, so too does the importance of being able to modify and adjust it.   A skills audit is a core intermediate-level learning strategy that ensures your learning system is adaptable. To perform a skills audit:   Assess your effort and methods:  Am I putting in reasonable effort (at least 60-70% of my max)?  If  no, increase effort to around 70%. If  yes, answer the next question.   Are the methods I'm using giving me the types of results I want?  If  yes, there are no probably no major issues. If  no, your methods can be optimised. Move onto the next step.     Create a learning flow map as you learned in an  earlier   task.  Review each step of your learning flow to assess whether it follows the principles of efficient learning that you know so far.  Make adjustments and use Kolb's to gain familiarity with your adjusted system.   As you learn about more principles, your audit will become deeper and more fine-tuned.  To assist you in your first skills audit, you may wish to review the   Fundamentals 2 checkpoint lesson   and the   Gear checklist   items for an overview of the most important principles.   In the future, we recommend completing a skills audit every time you feel that your learning system is not achieving the results you want.   Briefing:  Proper planning prevents poor performance. We'll now start retraining your existing learning habits. Identify what makes people plateau and how to avoid them so you can keep your improvements steady.  **Key Dimensions:**  * Deep processing * Self-regulation * Self-management * Retrieval * Mindset  **Recommended Pace:**  * Theory: 4 hours spread over 2 weeks  **Recommended Practice:**  * 2 weeks of practice while learning theory  **Progression Tips:**  * Focus on the"Right vs. Wrong Way to Practice" lesson, which introduces Kolb's cycle. * Consider the remaining stage as a comprehensive checklist. * Prioritize time on the key dimensions if you have adequate competence in earlier stages. * The goal is to integrate multiple techniques into a cohesive system.

By now, your foundations are strong, and there shouldnt be any gaping weaknesses with your study methods. Now, lets elevate them to the next level!   The    Growth Phase    is broken down into 5 parts. 1. Basic skills and "fitness":  Well start off by addressing some essential foundational techniques to refine your system. Well also ensure you have the cognitive fitness to ascend smoothly in the later stages. 2. First climb:  Using your new skills may seem daunting and difficult at first, and it may seem like you are not progressing. We'll give you tasks and tools that help you to self-diagnose and adapt your learning effectively (even without us)! 3. Base camp:  Well start adding higher-level skills and mindsets that build on everything you've mastered so far. Most people choose to spend months at Base camp to let themselves acclimatise to their new systems. 4. Push to summit:  If the first 3 stages are done correctly, this is the easiest part of the program! On the other hand, people who rush through will find these techniques either impossibly difficult or completely useless (as they don't even realise they are doing it incorrectly)! 5. Summit:  Reach whatever learning goal you want. By this point, you've been equipped with the tools and trained your brain to think, learn and adapt to any learning challenge. Summit is the level of true confidence based on a track record of consistent results and refined processes. A special exam is unlocked upon completing Summit. This exam must be passed to apply for the iCanStudy coach training program (for those interested in pursuing a career in learning coaching). Recommended task 1Schedule your growth plan  As you did before in the High-Yield phase, schedule your theory and practice time using the time management principles we've already taught you.   During the Growth phase, we recommend the following:    Add a 30-minute block for critical reflection and planning at least twice per week (preferably daily if possible). We will upgrade your critical reflection technique shortly.  No more than 3 hours of theory per week. The amount of practice vs. theory in the Growth phase is generally higher than during the High-Yield phase. More than 3 hours of theory per week is likely to cause overload.  Continued spaced interleaved retrieval. During the Growth phase, you must continue to refine your mastery over SIR. As this can take a while, we will not be teaching you any more retrieval techniques for the next few stages. Don't forget to keep up with SIR, even if every lesson doesn't remind you!    Once you have created an appropriate schedule for at least the next 2 weeks, mark this task as complete. Repeat your scheduling and planning every fortnight.   Briefing:  Proper planning prevents poor performance. We'll now start retraining your existing learning habits. Identify what makes people plateau and how to avoid them so you can keep your improvements steady.  **Key Dimensions:**  * Deep processing * Self-regulation * Self-management * Retrieval * Mindset  **Recommended Pace:**  * Theory: 4 hours spread over 2 weeks  **Recommended Practice:**  * 2 weeks of practice while learning theory  **Progression Tips:**  * Focus on the"Right vs. Wrong Way to Practice" lesson, which introduces Kolb's cycle. * Consider the remaining stage as a comprehensive checklist. * Prioritize time on the key dimensions if you have adequate competence in earlier stages. * The goal is to integrate multiple techniques into a cohesive system.

By now, you should:    have had experience with the additional uncertainty and mental effort involved in assigning significance to relationships  be reasonably experienced with thinking relationally  have gained insights into how certain types of mental effort produce different effects on retention and depth of understanding  be familiar with exploring and learning more to gain clarity and more certainty while learning.   In this final week, we will combine all these experiences to start turning these processes into a more flexible and fluid habit of thinking.    This week's challenge will take the individual processes of deep processing you've experimented with so far and apply them in a way that is more natural and authentic of a high deep processor. Week 4 instructions -  Step 1: Every time you notice yourself entering into lower-order thinking, create networks by comparing ideas and looking for similarities, differences, trends, and relationships. Step 2: Try to limit the relationships you express in your notes to only those that feel 4 out of 5 importance or greater. Step 3: Any time it is not clear how what you're learning now relates to the big picture, try to create relationships between the existing networks you have and the wider topic. Aim to create a simple, overarching structure for the topic. Step 4: If you need to learn more to gain clarity, give yourself a3-minute time limit to explore. Step 5: At the end of each study session, reflect on how easy or difficult it was for you to form relationships, prioritise them, and explore further. You will have succeeded in this challenge if:   You are moderately comfortable with the uncertainty and mental effort involved in thinking of relationships and assigning significance (rather than avoiding it or only seeking simple relationships that are easier to think about)  You are able to relate new information to the big picture frequently  You can explore curiosities and gain clarity when needed to form networks    How has this challenge changed your habits of thinking and learning? Do you feel more aware of how different types of thinking and processing influence memory and understanding? Can you recognise how different types of mental effort can be beneficial? Beyond 4 weeks:  While this challenge is now officially completed, most people will likely take longer than four weeks to meet the success criteria above. If you still feel uncomfortable with this week's challenge and would like to deepen your newly forming habits, repeat this week's challenge as many times as you wish before commencing the core program (if you haven't already).

Reconstruction  goes a step beyond recall. It involves not only recalling the information but also applying it to construct new and valid relationships.  This forces you to evaluate each piece of information critically (to see if your new relationships are accurate) while comparing and examining multiple chunks. Key principle:  Reconstruction works at the Evaluate and Create levels while also testing the lower orders. In the next lesson, we will use a heavily reconstructive retrieval method.   Since reconstruction-based strategies tend to take longer, it is best to use a combination   of reconstruction and recall during your revision. Some students may find 10% recall and 90% reconstruction efficient, while others may find 50% recall and 50% reconstruction more manageable.   In the long term, most students find it efficient to do more reconstruction in the earlier phase of their learning. This is because it allows more rapid and comprehensive gap-finding. Recall can be used in later phases to develop more detailed retrieval performance and mastery.

metadata.docName: How long should I spend on each lesson or stage of the program?
metadata.originalText: General rules for progression: 1. Do not advance if you have more than 3 items in your task list that you can act on or practice.2. Do not advance if you are unable to do the current set of techniques to at least 80% consistency and accuracy (equivalent to moderate conscious competence). You are ready to move on to the next set of techniques if you:    Feel comfortable with your current techniques  Have practised them under varying challenges and subjects    Under no circumstances should you progress if you:    Have not practised the techniques  Have more than 3 new techniques that are not yet at moderate conscious competence   **Competence Levels and Associated Signs:**  **Unconscious Incompetence:** - Unaware of mistakes - Lack of practice or knowledge of techniques  **Low to Moderate Conscious Incompetence:** - Discovery of mistakes during practice - Difficulty overcoming mistakes  **High Conscious Incompetence:** - Multiple attempts to overcome mistakes - Targeted and marginal adjustments  **Low Conscious Competence:** - Ability to perform technique correctly - Poor consistency and accuracy - Occasional discovery of new mistakes  **Moderate Conscious Competence:** - Reasonable consistency and accuracy - Growing comfort with technique  **High Conscious Competence:** - High consistency and accuracy - Low effort required - High comfort level  **Unconscious Competence:** - Technique becomes a habit - Effort required for thinking and processing, but not for applying the technique correctly What if it takes too long to progress?:  Rate of improvement directly depends on:   Rate of experimentation  Rate of reflection   The faster you can identify mistakes, experiment on ways to overcome them, and reflect on your experiment, the faster you will be able to progress. The first thing to examine is:"is my rate of experimentation and reflection adequate?".   It is rare for someone to progress slowly when they have appropriate experimentation and reflection frequency.   Furthermore, consider the following:   There is no alternative. Learning too many techniques at once is guaranteed to cause overload and technique failure. This wastes more time.  It is impossible to gain new skills without going through the process of experimentation and reflection. Delaying this process only delays skill acquisition.  Your rate of development through our program is incomparably faster than for the general population. Many of the skills taught only in the High-yield phase are beyond what the majority of learners gain in their entire lifetimes. Dr Justin Sung took 10 years of constant experimentation to develop some of these systems.  Many of the changes (especially in the Growth phase) are about cognitive retraining. This involves changing habits that may have been present for several years. Unlearning old habits and reinforcing new ones takes time.    If it were easy, fast or effortless, everyone would already do it.   On the other hand, relatively few people are willing to go through this effort to achieve these results, meaning the transformation you achieve will remain uncommon and competitive. I feel like each technique is not too difficult. Can I learn them in batches of more than 3?:  It is not advisable to learn more than 3 techniques at a time (at most) because each technique in a system influences each other.  Even if each technique is simple to use by itself, when they are integrated into a cohesive system, the complexity of using them all correctly multiplies. More variables are introduced into the system.   For example, imagine a spinning top like the one shown below:    It is easy to predict how it will spin, slow down, and eventually fall.  However, if we were to introduce another spinning top next to it, it becomes almost impossible to predict when they will clash, how they will clash, and how they will bounce after clashing. Now imagine three spinning tops.  You can see how the complexity of the overall system becomes significantly higher, even though each piece is relatively predictable in isolation.   The most important part about building an efficient learning system is compounding your gains.   As long as you can continue to build on your system and enhance it gradually, you will win. One of the most difficult issues to try and solve is when a system becomes so complicated and entangled that when problems arise, it becomes impossible to identify where the root cause is. This makes problem-solving and improving significantly more challenging and often requires the extended attention of a specialist coach. So how long should I spend in terms of days or weeks?:  The actual time you should spend on each technique depends on how much you practice, experiment and reflect. It also depends on the quality of your experiment and reflection.   Therefore, you should first ensure that you have:    Appropriate experiments that allow compounding marginal gains (as per the conscious competence model in alignment with what we teach about skill development)  High-quality reflection (in alignment with our guidelines for proper Kolb's)  Sufficient frequency of practice and experiments to carry momentum    If you fulfill these criteria, you expect to spend:    5 to 10 hours of practice per technique in High-yield phase  20 to 30 hours of practice per technique in Growth phase   Stages with more techniques naturally require more time. As your system grows and becomes more personalised, the amount of practice can become longer as mistakes are less obvious and it requires greater challenges to identify weaknesses.   The table below outlines some general timelines based on our observation of thousands of previous successful learners.  **High-Yield Phase Completion Time:**  * 2-3 hours of theory online per week, 30 hours of practice per week: 4-6 weeks * 2-3 hours of theory online per week, 15 hours of practice per week: 6-12 weeks * 2-3 hours of theory online per week, 5 hours of practice per week: 8 weeks - 4 months  **Growth Phase Completion Time:**  * 2-3 hours of theory online per week, 30 hours of practice per week: 4-7 months * 2-3 hours of theory online per week, 15 hours of practice per week: 6-10 months * 2-3 hours of theory online per week, 5 hours of practice per week: 12-20 months  Note:  changes to theory time per week have minimal effect on completion and mastery time. More than 4 or 5 hours of theory per week generally has a detrimental effect on completion due to overload. What is an ideal theory vs. practice schedule for completing the program?:  An appropriate progression would be as follows.   Theory:  Learn 2 or 3 new techniques over the weekend    Practice:  Spend 15 to 30 hours during the following week or more to apply these techniques during self-study sessions.   Reflection:  Reflections on techniques and formulations of new experiments should occur daily, if possible, or at least 3 times per week. Reflection sessions can be boxed to a maximum of 30 or 45 minutes, depending on availability.   As the quality of reflection is limited by each prior stage of Kolb's, spending longer than 45 minutes is unlikely to have significant benefits. For example, if it takes 30 minutes just to complete the'reflection' stage, it indicates a lack of self-awareness and reflective ability. Therefore, the quality of the abstraction and experiment will be limited. Over time, self-awareness and reflection will improve, allowing this step to be completed to a high quality in just 10 minutes, leaving ample time.     Feedback:  Feedback on work should be sought at least weekly.  With this schedule, you should be able to reach moderate conscious competence for most techniques within 2 weeks, driving a steady rate of weekly improvement.   If at any point you feel your technique level is declining, especially as your system becomes more advanced, we recommend taking 2 to 4 weeks to consolidate before attempting to add new techniques.

Two major factors influence our ability to perform under pressure.   Stress and pressure can affect your cognitive performance and your ability to retrieve knowledge, especially at higher orders. Some people refer to getting"tunnel vision" when they are under pressure where they become blind to connections they could normally easily form while calm.  On the night before and the day of a high-pressure assessment, the main focus should be on keeping calm, relaxed, and confident. Last-minute revision and outside-the-room cramming are likely to detriment your performance more due to the negative impact on stress while providing a very low chance of covering material that makes a meaningful difference to your result. Tip:  Focus training, mindfulness meditation, and Wim Hof breathing cycles can all help to create alertness and calm before a high-stakes assessment. Application adjustment is about practising your ability to use knowledge in many different ways in preparation for assessments. The methods introduced in the  interleaving table  will protect you against most question styles to a high degree. However, in the later stages of learning, the objective may be to find those extra few marks that are much less predictable.   Finding gaps in the late stages of learning is difficult because:    There are generally very few gaps at this stage.  There are different types of gaps:  known  and  unknown. Known gaps are detected when we have difficulties in recall or knowledge application. Unknown gaps are things we never knew in the first place. With unknown gaps, we might feel confident in our ability to answer, based on what we know but have missed an entire perspective that we never considered.     Breaching questions  is a method that can be helpful for efficiently identifying both known and unknown knowledge gaps, even if they are rare. They are called  breaching  questions because they are designed to"breach" through plateaus in learning. Breaching questions is an advanced method because it requires a high level of cognitive load tolerance and the ability to do a significant level of non-linear mapping mentally. Instructions -  Step 1: Find the learning objectives (LO) of the subject and list them out. Step 2: For each LO, list all the keywords you can think of that are related to this. Step 3: As you list out each keyword, mentally map out how they are all related to each other. Step 4: When you do this, pay attention to any difficulties in mentally mapping. This may indicate a known knowledge gap. Step 5: After completing this for each LO of the topic, look through the keywords and see if there are any you have missed. A keyword you know belongs in the topic but has not been listed and mentally mapped could indicate a known or unknown gap. Step 6: Look for keywords that exist in the list/map for one LO but not another. Explore whether these keywords could apply to the other LOs. This forces us to try and find perspectives where this keyword is relevant, helping to reveal unknown gaps.

In the beginning, dont try to make perfect questions. It is normal and perfectly fine to struggle with asking questions, but you must keep asking them if you want to improve.   The objectives are to:    Think of questions that create curiosity and potential relationships  Train our brain to become more curious  Train our brain to learn based on our line of curiosity   Become familiar with how asking questions affects the way we process new information    The video below clarifies some of the nuances around performing TLS correctly. You may wish to come back to review this video after having your first attempt so it is more relevant. Example of TLS for maths:  It can be difficult to learn a topic like maths (or even chemistry) in a way you are not used to. Here is a worksheet created by one of our students and reviewed and approved by our team that you can use to solve some of the mistakes you might make while working through maths problems.  Although this worksheet is for maths, you can use the same approach for mistakes made in any subject!   This worksheet will be given to you again in Ascent 2 as a reminder.    Problem-Solving Worksheet  Maths (Download) Controversy and problems with inquiry-based learning (optional reading):   Researchers have recently contested the effectiveness and validity of inquiry-based learning. Their concerns are valid. Inquiry-based learning suffers from the following:   Lack of agreed definitions on what constitutes inquiry-based learning  No clear guidelines on how it can be implemented  Significant dependency on the facilitator  Significant variation in effectiveness observed in empirical research  Questionable underlying theory  Questionable underlying science   Furthermore, almost all of the research on inquiry-based learning is aimed at educators and curriculum makers rather than individual learners. It is currently not considered a method that individuals apply for themselves in self-regulated learning practice.  Learning researchers in the cognitivist school of thought (compared to traditionally constructivist perspectives on learning) argue that there is no validity to inquiry-based learning due to the above problems.   Inquiry-based learning in the current research space is indeed highly limited. In some cases, the way it is implemented is either useless or harmful. The direction of research in the mainstream research space is also not highly promising. Many researchers seem to be ignoring the criticisms and justifying the validity based on the same flawed arguments.  However, the underlying theory of inquiry-based learning has significant overlap with currently more empirically supported cognitivist theories within cognitive load theory. Our approach is to extract the useful aspects of inquiry-based learning and frame it from a self-regulated learner's perspective. Unlike any other approach to inquiry-based learning that we have seen, we teach it from a cognitivist perspective.  As a result, we have seen this approach being able to produce consistent, theoretically aligned effects in learners. The version of inquiry-based learning we teach is substantially modified and, therefore, technically not considered inquiry-based learning within mainstream research.   Constantinou, C. P., Tsivitanidou, O. E., & Rybska, E. (2018). What Is Inquiry-Based Science Teaching and Learning? In O. E. Tsivitanidou, P. Gray, E. Rybska, L. Louca, & C. P. Constantinou (Eds.),  Professional Development for Inquiry-Based Science Teaching and Learning  (pp. 123). Springer International Publishing.  https://doi.org/10.1007/978-3-319-91406-0_1   Edelson, D. C., Gordin, D. N., & Pea, R. D. (1999). Addressing the challenges of inquiry-based learning through technology and curriculum design.  Journal of the Learning Sciences,  8 (34), 391450.  Kirschner, P. A., Sweller, J., & Clark, R. E. (2006). Why minimal guidance during instruction does not work: An analysis of the failure of constructivist, discovery, problem-based, experiential, and inquiry-based teaching.  Educational Psychologist,  41 (2), 7586.  Khalaf, B. K. (2018). Traditional and Inquiry-Based Learning Pedagogy: A Systematic Critical Review.  International Journal of Instruction,  11 (4), 545564.  Pedaste, M., Mäeots, M., Leijen, Ä., & Sarapuu, S. (2012). Improving students inquiry skills through reflection and self-regulation scaffolds.  Technology, Instruction, Cognition and Learning,  9 (12), 8195.  Rönnebeck, S., Bernholt, S., & Ropohl, M. (2016). Searching for a common ground  A literature review of empirical research on scientific inquiry activities.  Studies in Science Education,  52 (2), 161197.  https://doi.org/10.1080/03057267.2016.1206351   Sweller, J. (2021).  Why Inquiry-based Approaches Harm Students Learning. The Centre For Independent Studies.  https://www.cis.org.au/publications/analysis-papers/why-inquiry-based-approaches-harm-students-learning/ The table below outlines how using this technique can feel and what these feelings mean at a cognitive level. Refer to this table to improve your metacognition and ensure you understand the purpose of the technique. Connecting your emotional experience with the cognitive processes of learning helps you make fine-tuned adjustments and solve any technique issues more easily. **Cognitive Outcomes of Non-Linear Note-Taking**  **Cognitive Outcomes:**  * Engages higher-order, integrative thinking, fostering network and relationship building. * Improves active learning and deep processing, leading to earlier knowledge mastery. * Forces prioritization and evaluation, promoting higher-level learning. * Indicates increased cognitive resource allocation, resulting in more effective learning. * Creates mental schemas and knowledge structures based on brain's relevance assessment.  **Emotional Experiences:**  * Chaotic: Alternating between questions and material. * Confusing: Attempting to answer questions. * Confusing/Overwhelming: Creating non-linear structures with relationships. * Curiosity/Active Effort: Enhanced cognitive engagement. * Insecurity/Anxiety: Skipping details and learning out of order. Using meta-checklists:  Meta-checklists are metacognitive observations that you can use to check that you are performing techniques correctly. We recommend using these checklists whenever you do  Kolb's  on this skill or conduct  skill audits  and  rapid learning system assessments. Recommended task 1Complete your first traffic light system  Following our instructions, complete your first learning session using the TLS. We recommend at least one hour for your first attempt to have sufficient experience with the types of desirable difficulty this technique induces.   Complete this task once you have completed your first attempt at using TLS.    Technique Training:  In this stage, we introduce variations of inquiry-based learning, which help you to create more meaningful relationships and networks.  **Learning Plan**  **Key Dimensions:**  * Deep processing * Self-regulation * Self-management  **Recommended Pace:**  * Theory: 10 hours over 4 weeks  **Recommended Practice:**  * Practice while learning theory: 2-4 weeks  **Progression Tips:**  * Prioritize the"Inquiry-based learning" lesson within the first week. * This lesson introduces an advanced encoding and note-taking technique. * Most learners require more practice with this technique compared to previous ones.Recommended task 2Complete Kolb's on your first TLS attempt  Complete a Kolb's cycle on your first TLS attempt. You can refer to the meta-checklist to help guide your metacognition, self-awareness, and reflection.   Complete this task once you have completed your Kolb's.   Technique Training:  In this stage, we introduce variations of inquiry-based learning, which help you to create more meaningful relationships and networks.  **Learning Plan**  **Key Dimensions:**  * Deep processing * Self-regulation * Self-management  **Recommended Pace:**  * Theory: 10 hours over 4 weeks  **Recommended Practice:**  * Practice while learning theory: 2-4 weeks  **Progression Tips:**  * Prioritize the"Inquiry-based learning" lesson within the first week. * This lesson introduces an advanced encoding and note-taking technique. * Most learners require more practice with this technique compared to previous ones.

Decisional delays are the time it takes to decide what to do next. Cutting this time out may potentially save you hours a day. Decisional delays also interrupt your  flow state. Decisional delays are harmful because...: 1. They are barriers to maintaining flow states. This has potentially game-changing impacts on overall productivity as each break in flow requires an average of 15 minutes to re-enter flow. This can easily contribute to over an hour of delays daily.2. They use limited cognitive resources on planning that can make you unnecessarily tired and unfocused.3. They provide minimal positive value. The extra processing time does not confer any meaningful advantage. Identifying decisional delays - Follow these instructions to start becoming aware of how decisional delays affect you. Step 1: Use a time-tracking app and track how you spend your time every day for around one week. Step 2: Every time you break out of a deep work/flow state, end the task time tracking and restart it once you are re-entering a focused session. Step 3: Compare the time you tracked to the schedule you set. Step 4: Identify the amount of time that was spent between tasks that your schedule did not plan for and the number of flow breaks that occurred. Each flow break can create 10 to 15 minutes of cognitive disruption and"lost time". Step 5: Using these numbers, you can estimate how much time and disruption is caused by decisional delays. Note that simply tracking your time and becoming aware of decisional delays is an important first step towards solving them. How to mitigate decisional delays -  Step 1: Experiment with your schedule and task flow to batch planning and prioritisation at the beginning or end of each day to avoid the need for mid-day planning. Step 2: Block distractions from focus zones so new decisions do not enter your radar while you are working. Step 3: Create very clear and specific instructions for yourself during planning sessions so there is no doubt or need for decision-making when you are in execution mode. Recommended task 1Identify decisional delays with time tracking  Follow the instructions in the decisional delays lesson to identify the magnitude and effect of decisional delays on your productivity.    Complete this task once you have tracked your time and identified decisional delays over at least four days.   Camp II:  Learn the most advanced interleaved retrieval methods in our program.  **Key Dimensions:**  * Retrieval * Self-Management  **Recommended Pace:**  * Theory: 3 to 4 hours across 2 weeks  **Recommended Practice:**  * Consistent practice for 3 to 4 weeks for optimal results  **Progression Tips:**  * Advanced retrieval techniques, including"WPW," are introduced in this stage. * Retrieval and self-management techniques require a spacing interval, making practice time-consuming. * Mastery of techniques can take months, but progress to the next stage is typically possible after 3 to 4 weeks of diligent practice.

Revision  involves looking at the information you have learned and then finding and filling any gaps. A robust revision strategy will catch any small details you missed when learning the information, meaning you dont need to worry too much about missing things!  Many students only revise by doing past papers or re-reading notes. Often, their strategies are passive and do not engage the appropriate levels of challenge that we introduced in the  interleaving table. Key principles for a safe revision strategy: 1. Interleave with different methods at different orders and types of knowledge.2. Use retrieval methods that are strategic for finding your gaps and filling them.3. Always utilise recall over recognition.4. Maximise the benefit from past papers using more active and elaborative methods, instead of passively solving and checking answers. Alert:  If you are unsure about which methods of retrieval are suitable for finding gaps or would help maximise the benefit from practice questions, we  strongly  recommend reviewing the previous recommended tasks around interleaved retrieval and the interleaving table.   At this point in the program, all of this should be a recap. A knowledge gap is any missing information or relationship that is important and relevant. You can identify gaps by assessing and testing yourself. There are different kinds of gaps, explained below. You struggle to identify the correct approach and strategy to solving a problem:  This indicates a declarative knowledge gap on how concepts can be related to each other and their purpose/influences. This kind of gap is common among engineering, coding, physics, and maths where there is insufficient attention given to the conceptual network and excessive focus is on procedural repetition and practice. You struggle to recall information:  This indicates a broad retrieval gap that should be filled with deeper relational encoding or a lower-order memorisation method, depending on the type of information and the layer it belongs to. If a high volume of information is forgotten, the problem is almost always due to a suboptimal chunk structure. Evaluate your maps for  waterfalling  and  spiderwebbing  and ensure the chunks are  intuitive. Beware of  simple question and answer, which can undermine the inquiry process. You struggle with consistently solving problems due to errors:  This indicates a procedural knowledge gap caused by a lack of practice and execution-style interleaving. In some cases, the errors are due to conceptual/declarative knowledge misunderstandings. You struggle to apply knowledge in different contexts or more nuanced settings:  This indicates a higher-order knowledge gap caused by missing relationships. Key principles for finding gaps: 1. Look for gaps - don't avoid your weaknesses2. Test at multiple orders3. Do not refer to answer sheets until you have created your own answer sheet through self-study first. Premature answer sheet checking often creates illusions of knowledge and is a common mistake.4. Remember that struggling to answer a question indicates a gap. Even if you get the question right, you might just have gotten lucky! Difficulty matters more than correctness.

Focus training/meditation and deep breathing exercises such as the Wim Hof method have been found to be effective for creating alertness, focus, and energy recovery if you have a short break between back-to-back exams. Note:  You can read more about Ajahn Brahm, whom I talked about in this lesson on  Wikipedia. He also created a growing mindfulness trend called Kindfulness. In this YouTube video, you can hear him talking about exams and his examination technique while he was a scholarship-awarded theoretical physics major at the University of Cambridge. Were not telling you to go and become a monk, but good advice can come from even the most unexpected sources!

To move on, you must have around 80% competence with the skills taught so far. Use the list below to check whether you're ready!    Note:  Lower levels of competence with even one of the items can be problematic during later stages where we build on these foundations. Technique training checkpoint -  Step 1: You use non-linear, processed notes. You are are becoming comfortable with non-linear mapping. Step 2: You have a consistent, regular and high-quality Kolbs cycle. Step 3: Your inquiry step helps you to explore and identify relationships. Step 4: You identify multiple relationships from a single concept, not just one or two. Step 5: You compare relationships to find similarities and differences. Step 6: You create groups based on these similarities and differences. Step 7: Your mindmaps have a clear backbone of major ideas. Step 8: You are seeing progress through marginal gains as a habit. Step 9: Your spacing schedule is more refined and consistent. Step 10: Your mastery of interleaving techniques is improving. Step 11: You do systematic prioritisation and task management as part of your routine. Step 12: You have reduced distractions and procrastination. **Level of Competency**  **Semi-Habitual**  * **Traffic Light System + Non-Linear Note-Taking:**     * Ask high-quality questions     * Chunk information and identify relationships     * Express chunks and relationships through processed notes  * **BEDS-M:**     * Improve focus and reduce distractions  **Consistent**  * **Scheduling Basics + Revision Basics:**     * Create flexible schedules and follow them     * Schedule regular revision  **Regular**  * **Prestudy:**     * Schedule in regular prestudy     * Use traffic light system and processed notes to create basic chunks and big relationships Unlocking the next stage:  Ascent 1 is unlocked 14 days after Briefing is completed. If you've been practising the new theory and progressing at the recommended pace, it should be unlocked already by now!   If the next stage is still not unlocked, please use this time to practise your current method and gain deeper mastery.    Warning:  In the Ascent stages, we have found that any weaknesses in fundamentals will be magnified. In some cases, it can take  months  to diagnose and repair problems caused by inaccurate fundamentals. Recommended task 1Complete a challenge week using all methods learned so far  For the next one week, use all of the methods you have learned so far around:   Prioritising  Time management  Pre-study  Note-taking and encoding with TLS  Interleaved retrieval  Focus and procrastination management    Complete at least three Kolb's cycles to increase your marginal competence with each technique and how the system works as a whole.   Technique Training:  In this stage, we introduce variations of inquiry-based learning, which help you to create more meaningful relationships and networks.  **Learning Plan**  **Key Dimensions:**  * Deep processing * Self-regulation * Self-management  **Recommended Pace:**  * Theory: 10 hours over 4 weeks  **Recommended Practice:**  * Practice while learning theory: 2-4 weeks  **Progression Tips:**  * Prioritize the"Inquiry-based learning" lesson within the first week. * This lesson introduces an advanced encoding and note-taking technique. * Most learners require more practice with this technique compared to previous ones.

When planning the process, you need to include metrics. When will you see development, and how much?   With metrics, youll know you are doing things correctly because the numbers will show if you are achieving the right processes, learning the right skills and keeping a productive mindset.   The  Feedback Analysis Loop (FAL)  compares your expectations with what happened in reality.    If your expectations  match  reality, you know theres no problem with your processes.   If your expectations  dont match  reality, you need to adjust your processes as they are unlikely to help you learn the skills and reach your goals.   The FAL is another framework we can apply to Kolbs experiential learning cycle. Using FALs gives us concrete, manageable information, making reflecting and finding abstractions easier. Note:  FALs can be modified for any need (e.g., studying, sports, personal development). You can add extra steps or criteria depending on what you want to measure.  The only components that need to stay are:   expectations,  reality, and   a comparison between the two.    If your FAL has these three elements, you can modify it however suits you!

metadata.docName: How do I know when to stop improving a skill? When is it "enough"?
metadata.originalText: As you progress through the program, you will learn many techniques. Some of these techniques build onto other techniques in later lessons.  As some techniques can theoretically be improved endlessly, we can identify which skills to work on and what is"good enough" using frequent reflection and marginal gains.   In any skill acquisition process, you should always work on improving your  rate limiters  as the next step. Rate limiters:   As you progress through the program, you will learn many techniques. Some of these techniques build onto other techniques in later lessons.  As some techniques can theoretically be improved endlessly, we can identify which skills to work on and what is"good enough" using frequent reflection and marginal gains.   In any skill acquisition process, you should always work on improving your  rate limiters  as the next step. It isn't always clear which part of a process is our rate limiter. However, even if we identify the wrong part of the process, we are much more likely to improve smoothly by at least attempting to identify and address rate limiters.   As our skills improve, our rate limiters also change and evolve. If we were first limited by a fixed mindset, we might become limited by our time management a month later. This may be because our time management worsened or our mindset improved. Therefore, our focus should also shift as the landscape of our skills also changes.   To identify a rate limiter, reflect regularly using the techniques of marginal gains and Kolb's taught in the program. Every one or two weeks, focus your reflection on exploring if there are parts of the process that seem to be holding you back. You might already be working on it. If so, continue. Sometimes, we discover rate limiters we have never considered before. In this situation, we can then change our experiments and efforts to improve our rate limiters instead of charging ahead with whatever technique we may have been working on before.   Making progress without addressing rate limiters can create significant wasted time and effort. Think of how much water we might waste trying to fill a bucket with a hole in the wall!    As we continue to target our rate limiters, our skills should gradually continue to improve. Whenever we hit a plateau, we can identify which rate limiter is holding us back and address it to break through more quickly. How to progress - Follow these general guidelines to navigate our program successfully. Step 1: Learn new methods and principles in the lessons Step 2: Practise them and apply them in real life Step 3: Reflect on the effectiveness using the frameworks taught (marginal gains + Kolb's) Step 4: Identify mistakes and barriers to success Step 5: Over time, prioritise barriers based on how rate limiting they are for you to improve Step 6: Work on the higher priority items by repeating these steps and making iterative adjustments We recommend reading  this article  on  How long you should spend on each lesson  to further your understanding of competence assessment. Clear competence goals and self-assessments help you to improve your skills with more focus and clarity.  You may also be interested in  this article  on  How to track marginal gains,  which explains how to use marginal gains to reflect on your progress accurately.

Progression note:   We recommend reading  this article  on  How long you should spend on each lesson  to further your understanding of competence assessment. Clear competence goals and self-assessments help you to improve your skills with more focus and clarity.  You may also be interested in  this article  on  How to track marginal gains,  which explains how to use marginal gains to reflect on your progress accurately. Click the link  here  to find a PDF with two examples of Kolb's which has been completed in accordance with our guidelines.

Expand the accordions below to see our checklist to ensure you are completing the PEER-Peer system effectively. Prep:   Identify cues that trigger unwanted behaviours such as procrastination. Aim to identify at least three or more.  Remove and lock away these cues however possible. Consider using site-blocking apps like SiteBlock. We want these bad cues to become INVISIBLE in our daily lives.  Identify cues that trigger desirable behaviours. Aim to identify at least three or more.   Plant these cues so that they are  unavoidable  in your daily life. We want these cues to almost intrude into our vision or path. Like a boulder rolling downhill to roll to the bottom inevitably, our environment should be crafted so that we  inevitably  roll towards our desired behaviour or action. Easy:   Write your final desired goal (for example, studying for 3 hours).   Write down all the steps required to get there. Make the first few steps as easy as possible to make it ridiculously easy to transition into your behaviour.   Conversely, we want to make our undesirable behaviour close to impossible.  Try to write down the steps required to start your undesirable behaviour to see how easy it might be!    For example, procrastinating on social media might be as simple as taking out your phone and opening the app or tapping the button if youre already on your phone. We can see how much more willpower it takes to do one type of behaviour (beneficial or productive) than another (harmful or time-wasting). This is partly because companies invest millions to make it as easy for us to get distracted as possible! They have crafted environments for us so we must take back control. Exit:   Remind yourself that you can quit after every MVG. The only decision you need to make is whether you do the next MVG or quit. Reward:   Identify two or three actions, behaviours, or activities you enjoy. Choose things that you can access and do readily. Consider how addictive your reward will be and if that affects your next scheduled task or event.   For example, if you have no other focus blocks planned after your reward break, it doesn't matter so much. Remember that a sleep and wind-down routine is also a scheduled task!     Make this reward inaccessible without doing the desired behaviour.   For example, blocking Netflix outside of specific hours of the day or using an accountability partner (e.g. a parent) to show that youve completed something before getting them to unlock apps or websites.       You can use incognito mode to stop getting personalised recommendations and subscriptions on YouTube that make it too easy for you to get side-tracked.   You can also block specific words from appearing in the URL instead of blocking entire websites. For example, if you need YouTube for studying, use incognito to prevent easy access to your recommended content and subscriptions.    This forces you to search for videos manually. When you search for something on Youtube, the search term appears in the website URL; therefore, if you block those search words in your site blocker, your site-blocking app will allow you to search for good content while blocking the site every time the distracting search term pops up in the URL!   You can also enable these in the parental control options in most router settings. Peer:   Find one or two accountability partners. Find a partner in the iCanStudy community if you need people on the same wavelength as you!  Establish and agree to consequences.   Ensure the consequences cause you to feel at least a moderate sense of dread and fear. If the consequence isnt significant, your laziness or procrastination will win.  Write down your agreed consequence to make it tangible and real. This symbolises that you are taking it seriously.    Find the balance. If the consequence is too extreme, you will likely lie to avoid the punishment. Find a consequence that is significant and undesirable yet not so extreme that you would avoid it at all costs. For example, giving someone $50 might be appropriate, while giving someone $500 is too extreme. Doing a dare in front of your class might be appropriate while doing a dare in front of your entire school during assembly might be too extreme.    Be responsible and do not act rashly. Recommended task 1Take control of your behaviours and habits using the PEER-Peer system  Using the checklist in our PEER-Peer system summary, create a plan to change your behaviours and develop new habits. Execute this plan for at least one week with at least three Kolb's cycles.   Complete this task after your week of experience using PEER-Peer.   Base Camp:  Resume upgrading your self-management skills and combine your advanced encoding methods to create nuanced systems of learning.  **Key Dimensions:**  * Self-management * Deep processing * Mindset  **Recommended Pace:**  * Theory: 5 hours over 4 weeks  **Recommended Practice:**  * 8 to 10 weeks of practice * Most theory covered within the first 4 weeks  **Progression Tips:**  * Base Camp covers a wide range of skills. * Practice time varies depending on individual challenges. * Most learners spend approximately 8 weeks in this stage. * Progress at a comfortable pace to consolidate the system. * The most technical lesson is"Hipshot".

It is not good to be dependent on a good teacher. By developing the skills to be your own best teacher, you will always be able to teach yourself!  This is where the Whole-Part-Whole (WPW) technique comes in. This technique is highly multi-order, covering all levels of retrieval in Bloom's revised taxonomy.   This multi-level coverage is what makes the technique so powerful. However, a significant amount of practice is required to perform this skill correctly. Even those who have a perfect level of competence with the methods in this program can take months to master WPW. Research on good teacher dependency:  The good teacher reliance phenomenon is partially responsible for the fade-out effect. Read this  summary article  on how it affects people who receive lots of tutoring. Research on the WPW technique:  Malcolm Knowles outlines the WPW model in his  book, The Adult Learner: The Definitive Classic in Adult Education and Human Resource Development. This is a recommended read for educators.  Barradell, Sarah, and Mary Kennedy-Jones. Threshold Concepts, Student Learning and Curriculum: Making Connections between Theory and Practice.  Innovations in Education & Teaching International, vol. 52, no. 5, Oct. 2015, pp. 536545.  EBSCOhost, doi:10.1080/14703297.2013.866592.  Mowling, Claire M., and Brent Heidorn. Every Shot Counts: Rallying Around Traditional Practices Strategies.  Strategies (08924562), vol. 26, no. 2, Mar. 2013, pp. 3036.  EBSCOhost, doi:10.1080/08924562.2013.764804. The WPW reteaching strategy is an example of  high volume retrieval practice (HVRP).  This is an excellent approach during the early to mid stages of revision as it allows you to catch mistakes and target weaknesses quickly. It covers the topic very comprehensively, including relationships and specific details. Another example of HVRP is brain dumps. However, we have found this method to be less effective at testing relationships and conceptual understanding.   We recommend using HVRP as part of your spaced retrieval. This will help prepare you to use your knowledge for exams, essays and problem-solving. Schedule HVRP for your  end-of-week  or  2-week  revision session. This way, your subsequent revision sessions will be more targeted. Below is an example of what your learning schedule might look like. **Pre-Class/Lecture Preparation:**  * Prestudy using inquiry-based learning through the BHS. * Create a basic mind map with at least 1-2 layers of learning (ideally 3).  **During Class/Lecture:**  * Consolidate layers 1, 2, and 3 of the mind map. * For subjects like Maths or Physics, practice applying techniques and solving problems.  **Same Day After Class:**  * Consolidate and add layer 4 to the mind map. * Skin the mind maps and create flashcards and test questions.  **Mid-Week (Optional):**  * High-Volume Rapid Preview (HVRP) using the Whole Page Writing (WPW) method.  **End of the Week:**  * HVRP using WPW for new content covered since mid-week. * Answer test questions created during the week or share them with a study group.  **3-4 Weeks Later and Before Test/Exam:**  * HVRP via brain dump (linear or mind map) and practice papers. You should consistently complete your flashcards daily using discrete pockets of available time via microlearning. Flashcards should not need their own scheduled session.

We've covered these principles before. This is an opportunity to check your system and ensure you're continuing to do your interleaved retrieval sessions.   The most basic principles of effective revision are to:     Schedule your revision: Have one weekly revision and one monthly revision. This is a very basic form of a learning strategy known as  spaced repetition.   Use full recall: Before you open your notes or textbook, try to recall the information first, using various methods such as writing, drawing, mindmaps, teaching someone else, or asking and answering practice questions. Feeling confident with revision and SIR?:  If you've been practising and feel confident, now is a good opportunity to experiment with other interleaving levels.   You may have skipped a lot of techniques in the Interleaving table because it was overwhelming at the time. No problem! Have a look at it again and see what you can experiment with.

From the lessons so far, we have learned steps to prevent neuroticism from overwhelming us.  These steps may initially feel unfamiliar or artificial, but they give you more freedom and time as they release you from the shackles of inefficiency and lack of control. It just takes a bit of effort to free yourself!   Managing    neuroticism is about training our brains to not fixate on some thoughts and emotions. This helps us become more focused, less distracted, and more emotionally stable.   We can achieve this using  focus training.   Emotional self-regulation and remaining focused is less about  forcing  our brain to focus and much more about  preventing  our brains from being hijacked by thoughts and emotions. Focus training helps improve your brain's ability to let go of thoughts and feelings and return to a stable baseline.    In this way, focus can be considered a cognitive skill.   Training focus is conceptually simple:   Put ourselves in a situation where our attention drifts (usually when there are no distractions - this is also why our mind wanders before we go to sleep).  Practice letting go and bringing our attention back.   We can use the steps below to train ourselves. Warning:  If you have past trauma, focus training (which is a form of  mindfulness meditation ) can resurface traumatic memories. If you are aware of having past trauma, we recommend engaging in this activity with the guidance of a therapist or psychologist. Most trained therapists will be experienced in guiding clients on this technique. Focus training instructions -  Step 1: Sit somewhere comfortable away from distractions. Close your eyes. Step 2: Take slow, deep breaths in and out. Step 3: Focus on the sensation of each breath in and out. Be attentive to the air flow, how your body moves, and any other sensation associated with breathing. Step 4: After a few seconds, you should start having other thoughts and your mind will wander. Each time your mind wanders, acknowledge the thought and let go of it without pursuing or fixating on it. Bring your attention gently back to your breathing. Step 5: Repeat this process for 15 to 20 minutes (it's hard!) You train your focus and self-regulation ability every time you let go and bring your attention back. It's normal for your mind to wander every three or four seconds. It can be frustrating at first, but remember that the point is to experience this and practice letting go and settling back into your breathing.  Over time, you will find this easier, and the time between wandering thoughts will grow longer. Even after years of practice, having no more than 10 seconds of"mental silence" is normal. However, this represents a tripling of focus ability! Tip:  Do not deliberately try to  stop  having distracting thoughts. Let those thoughts do what they will, like clouds floating in the sky or leaves floating down a stream. Observe them and let them pass without holding onto them, reaching out for them, or giving them your attention. The"dose" of focus training to make significant improvements is generally considered 20 minutes daily for 30 days. In our experience, effects are seen with just 10 minutes daily. Consistency is key.    We recommend anchoring this in your routine as a new habit to form. Research on focus training/meditation:  Read about   Acceptance and Commitment Therapy (ACT)   for  youth  and some of the  metaphors  it uses, including the yellow jeep. This is a form of cognitive behavioural therapy used by all clinical psychologists.  Read about   mindfulness meditation   and the related articles on this website to gain some basic knowledge about this highly effective practice.  There is so much research on mindfulness meditation and ACT that you may benefit from searching  Google Scholar  for those terms. There is a lot of very interesting research on many different types of people and problems. Helpful apps:  There are many apps to help with guided meditation. Different apps are suited for different people; however, you may wish to experiment with the following:   Calm  (usual top pick  start with 30-day beginners course)   Insight Timer  Buddhify  Headspace  Remente  Tide  Fabulous  Smiling Mind  Some of these (such as Calm) also have free YouTube channels. Recommended task 1Commit to focus training  Embrace the power of focus training. Perform one focus training session of at least 10 minutes. Schedule at least five 15-minute sessions during the next week.  If you are serious about improving your focus and emotional self-regulation ability, make this a habit for at least 30 days.   Complete this task once you have performed a session and scheduled your sessions.   Base Camp:  Resume upgrading your self-management skills and combine your advanced encoding methods to create nuanced systems of learning.  **Key Dimensions:**  * Self-management * Deep processing * Mindset  **Recommended Pace:**  * Theory: 5 hours over 4 weeks  **Recommended Practice:**  * 8 to 10 weeks of practice * Most theory covered within the first 4 weeks  **Progression Tips:**  * Base Camp covers a wide range of skills. * Practice time varies depending on individual challenges. * Most learners spend approximately 8 weeks in this stage. * Progress at a comfortable pace to consolidate the system. * The most technical lesson is"Hipshot."

The recommended tasks below are geared towards helping you remove as many barriers as possible from your growth journey. They are recommended for all learners going through this program. Recommended task 1Growth habit mapping  This quick activity helps us build awareness of growth-response habits that may hinder us from progressing.   In 10 minutes, write down in bullet points:    How you tend to respond to challenges and the fear of failure  How you tend to respond to learning methods that feel difficult or different  Are you normally aware of how you respond?   The purpose of this activity is to create  problem awareness. Because our responses are usually habit-driven, most people are unaware of how they tend to respond.   After completing this activity:    If you have found growth-resistant habits, but you  are not  normally aware of them at the time, try to catch yourself next time. Your crucial next step is to gain awareness of these habits when they affect you.  If you have found growth-resistant habits and you  are  aware of them at the time, try to use Minimum Viable Goals (MVGs) to expose yourself to challenges gradually. Your crucial next step is gaining exposure to gradually retrain these habits.  If you did not find any growth-resistant habits, it means you are truly growth-minded,  or  you are completely unaware of your habits! Your next step is to stay open-minded and observe how you respond to the challenges you will face throughout this program.    If you found multiple growth-resistant habits, we recommend repeating this activity every 2 to 3 months.      Rapid Start:  The fastest way to upgrade your learning is to clear your path of any obstacles. The Rapid Start lessons are designed to do just that!  **Learning Plan**  **Key Dimensions:**  * Self-management * Mindset  **Recommended Pace:**  * Theory: 2-3 hours spread over 1-2 weeks * Practice: 1-2 weeks  **Recommended Practice:**  * Focus on 2-3 tasks at a time. * Complete practice exercises for each task before moving on.  **Progression Notes:**  * Complete lessons to accumulate 2-3 focus tasks. * Practice until tasks are completed. * Move through remaining lessons after completing tasks. * Estimated progression time: 1-2 weeks   This stage will focus on improving your enabler skills. Working on these skills improves your ability to work through the program and accelerates your development speed.Recommended task 2Create a learning system flow diagram  How good is your learning system,  really? This activity will highlight the most common gaps in people's learning systems. Most of these gaps can go undetected for someone's entire life while constantly limiting their ability to learn!    On a blank piece of paper, write"new information" in the top centre of the page.  Under this, write down the first method you use to learn when new information enters your brain. Be specific. For example, instead of"listening to the lecturer", write down how you listen and what you think about. If you also write notes, write down how you make notes.  Draw an arrow from"new information" to your first method that you've just described.  Continue this process of building a learning flow diagram by writing down the next method you use to study that information, and the next, etc.   Because learning is complex, it is best to consider the methods you use for a  single new set of information  (e.g. a single lecture). Follow this set of information's learning journey and each method you use to learn it.  At the end of this, you should have a flow diagram that shows how one set of information is consumed and learned until there are no further methods it interacts with.   After you have completed this, take another colour pen or highlighter and mark methods where any of the following apply:   I am not sure exactly what method I use at this stage  This method is inconsistent for no deliberate reason  I am unsure what effect this method has on my learning quality and memory  I am unsure what alternatives there could be to this method  I use this method because of habit or because other people use it rather than because I understand what effect it has on my learning    More marks on your diagram indicate more gaps in your learning system. For many students, almost every method is marked at first.    Throughout this program, you will learn more about how learning works so that there are no marks on your system. Every method will become deliberate and designed to give you a specific, predictable effect.  Rapid Start:  The fastest way to upgrade your learning is to clear your path of any obstacles. The Rapid Start lessons are designed to do just that!  **Learning Plan**  **Key Dimensions:**  * Self-management * Mindset  **Recommended Pace:**  * Theory: 2-3 hours spread over 1-2 weeks * Practice: 1-2 weeks  **Recommended Practice:**  * Focus on 2-3 tasks at a time. * Complete practice exercises for each task before moving on.  **Progression Notes:**  * Complete lessons to accumulate 2-3 focus tasks. * Practice until tasks are completed. * Move through remaining lessons after completing tasks. * Estimated progression time: 1-2 weeks   This stage will focus on improving your enabler skills. Working on these skills improves your ability to work through the program and accelerates your development speed.Recommended task 3Schedule practice blocks  Finding your biggest mistakes as early as possible is the key to rapid skill development. Practice blocks are a dedicated time for finding as many mistakes as possible, as quickly as possible. We use this method throughout the entire program to accelerate skill development.    Schedule at least 30 minutes (up to 3 hours) every 3 to 5 days at a time that you can consistently adhere to. This is your  practice block.  During your practice block, apply a new technique on which you are gaining skill competence. It could be an enabler skill (like focus management), a learning skill or both in the same session!  Focus on applying each step of the technique as accurately as possible.  Note down on a separate document or paper whenever you face difficulties or deviate from the technique.   If you are unsure about whether you have used a technique correctly, ask for feedback on our safe and supportive community.    By the end of your practice block, your goal is to identify as many mistakes, habits, or unproductive tendencies as possible.   As you learn more technical skills in the following stages, practice blocks will become one of your most high-yield tools for rapid skill improvement.
