The Rapid Start lessons are designed to do just that!  **Learning Plan**  **Key Dimensions:**  * Self-management * Mindset  **Recommended Pace:**  * Theory: 2-3 hours spread over 1-2 weeks * Practice: 1-2 weeks  **Recommended Practice:**  * Focus on 2-3 tasks at a time. * Complete practice exercises for each task before moving on.  **Progression Notes:**  * Complete lessons to accumulate 2-3 focus tasks. * Practice until tasks are completed. * Move through remaining lessons after completing tasks. * Estimated progression time: 1-2 weeks   This stage will focus on improving your enabler skills. Working on these skills improves your ability to work through the program and accelerates your development speed.

Trigger avoidance can be an effective way to reduce overwhelming neuroticism. To prevent the triggers of neuroticism, there are three aspects we need to address: awareness, attention and analysis.    In our busy lives, we often ignore our thoughts and force through them by immersing ourselves in more work, responsibilities, and expectations. If these thoughts are not processed healthily, everyday life becomes stressful, with the outcomes of every action feeling like it could be potentially life-ending. This is a psychological phenomenon known as  catastrophising.   Cognitive distortions are mental biases and skewed thoughts that increase anxiety and make us feel bad about ourselves. They are common in highly neurotic individuals and may have acted as a cautionary mechanism for humans in our ancient past. Awareness involves identifying distortions and how they make us feel. Techniques like Kolb's naturally improves emotional self-awareness and root cause analysis.   Expand the accordions below to learn to assess and identify distorted thinking patterns. Assessing cognitive distortions:   From the list below, identify how many typical neurotic thoughts you resonate with:    Why am I so stupid? Why am I not understanding this?  I need to get this right; its so overwhelming. How am I going to get it perfect?  I must get top in class. If I dontI am a loser and Ill disappoint myself and my family.  I cant do it. This is not working. I need to just go back to what I was doing before. It doesnt matter if its not as effective, Im sure itll work out.  I know Ive done a lot of practice questions and past papers but I need to do more. The only way to get confident is to do more questions.  Sleep is for the weak! I need to keep working until I cant anymore so I secure success. Short term pain, long term gain.  Theres no point trying. Im just going to fail again anyway.  What if I fail? What if the questions I studied dont come up? What if I forget during the exam?  I have to get everything perfect and even if it means not sleeping as much or sacrificing my other priorities, I cant leave it imperfect.  I wonder what my friends got in the test?... I hope they did badly too.  But what if I try doing something new, and it doesnt work and then Ive wasted all this time and effort?    Identify your level of neuroticism based on how many thoughts you related to:  **Neuroticism Level and Growth Potential**  * **None or One:**     * Low neuroticism     * High growth potential  * **Two or Three:**     * Moderate neuroticism     * Moderate growth potential  * **Four or Five:**     * High neuroticism     * Significant limitations to growth potential  * **Six or More:**     * Very high neuroticism     * Highest priority for immediate action Attention involves diving deeper into those emotions and causes as we feel them. Analysis involves objectively dissecting the emotional process, including cues, behaviours, and patterns. This gives us more control over the emotions and their effects on us, rather than letting them affect us without restraint.  We often think that emotions are outside our control or influence. But by being attentive to the paths and life cycles of our emotions, we can think about them differently and even influence how they affect us. Example:  If we pay attention to where our anxiety comes from, we may be able to express gratitude for it since it means our brain is trying to protect us from danger. As a result, our anxiety can impact us less negatively, breaking the habit-forming cycles that make anxiety dangerous. Expand the accordion below to examine our neurotic tendencies and develop self-compassionate acceptance. Acceptance is critical as it gives us permission and emotional freedom to train ourselves out of our neurotic habits. Developing acceptance:   On a separate piece of paper, for each neurotic thought you have identified above, complete the following questions to help characterise them and their impact on you.    You may be unable to answer some of these questions immediately. If this is the case, you are advised to be attentive to this over the following few days or weeks and fill it in when you have a better self-understanding.    You may continue working through the rest of the program without completing all questions. However, we recommend that you come back to the task to complete it as you are able.    Questions     How often do I think this way?     When do I think this way?    What triggers these thoughts? Be as specific as you can.      Why do I think this way?    Be as descriptive as possible. Perhaps there are past experiences or parts of your upbringing.  Thinking about this can sometimes bring painful or traumatic memories. Accept your thoughts kindly and non-judgementally if this occurs, and take a break. There is no need to force the process.   You may need the help of a therapist or experienced professional to guide you through this process if it is very challenging for you, mentally or emotionally. Difficulty at this stage is widespread, especially among high-achievers with a history of overcoming adversity from a young age.      How does thinking this way affect my mood, confidence and productivity?    Be as descriptive as you can, giving examples. Avoidance behaviours:  While avoiding triggers can be effective, there are many situations where trigger avoidance is impossible. When we develop habits of avoiding discomfort and emotional triggers, we can enter into something called  avoidance behaviours, which can make anxiety worse.  With avoidance behaviours, we become consumed with avoiding triggers, leading to a restricted and hypervigilant lifestyle in constant fear of triggers. The right balance is to avoid triggers that lead to emotional overwhelm but learn to manage our exposure to unavoidable triggers for a free and healthy lifestyle. Recommended task 1Complete the first part of neuroticism prevention  Based on the guidelines in the Neuroticism: Prevention sublesson, complete a reflection composed of awareness, attention, analysis, and acceptance.   Complete this task once you have written out answers to the instructions given in the sublesson.   Base Camp:  Resume upgrading your self-management skills and combine your advanced encoding methods to create nuanced systems of learning.  **Key Dimensions:**  * Self-management * Deep processing * Mindset  **Recommended Pace:**  * Theory: 5 hours over 4 weeks  **Recommended Practice:**  * 8 to 10 weeks of practice * Most theory covered within the first 4 weeks  **Progression Tips:**  * Base Camp covers a wide range of skills. * Practice time varies depending on individual challenges. * Most learners spend approximately 8 weeks in this stage. * Progress at a comfortable pace to consolidate the system. * The most technical lesson is"Hipshot."

By now, you should:    Have some insights into some actions and modifications that help you to replicate your"perfect day" to varying degrees on a consistent basis.  Understand the level of energy, effort, and planning that is required to optimise your productivity and flow.  Be more self-aware regarding how different variables affect your flow throughout the day and how your responses, behaviours, and actions influence the impact these variables have on you.   In this week's challenge, we will explore the minimum possible intervention that achieves a worthwhile beneficial effect. While last week was about pushing our skills to the limit, this week is about creating the most sustainable set of tools that can be applied with the lowest level of effort while still helping us stay relatively optimum.  These insights are useful for when we are tired or sick and need to manage ourselves when energy is scarce. Week 4 instructions -  Step 1: Based on your insights from week 3, plan a moderately productive day but include what you feel is a simple and minimal set of actions, modifications, and techniques to stay in flow. Step 2: Execute on this day. At the end of the day, reflect on how successfully your day went compared to your plan. Step 3: If it was very successful, plan another day with an even simpler set of strictly high-yield adjustments and actions. If it was unsuccessful, bring your plan slightly closer to your"perfect day" plan until it is successful. Step 4: Continue step 3 until you identify the minimum threshold of actions and adjustments at which your day becomes undesirably unproductive. Things change:  Due to the interrelated nature of self-management skills, the impact of these variables on your productivity and flow will change over time. This 30-day challenge has been designed so that you can repeat it any time you feel that your self-management needs improvement.   As it is introspection-led, the type of insights you will gain from repeating this challenge will depend on your circumstances and growing level of self-management skills.

By now, you should have started to:    gain familiarity with how different methods can be used for different effects  understand which parts of your learning system are deliberate vs. residual habits  identify gaps in your understanding of learning and learning methods   In this week's challenge, we will start learning more about how to judge the effectiveness of methods and how to make potential adjustments based on these judgments. This is a lifelong skill with a variety of applications. Week 2 instructions -  Step 1: Based on the sheet you have created in week 1, identify methods that are either ineffective or not engaging. Methods that are not mentally engaging are often passive and inefficient and can be improved. Step 2: Using the suggestions below, try to modify or replace these methods to make them more effective and engaging. Step 3: Every few days, reflect on how these adjustments have changed the effectiveness and level of engagement. Follow the general advice below when modifying a technique to make it more effective or engaging. You will learn many other techniques and variations of these techniques through the core program. **Effective Learning Strategies**  **1. Practice How You Play:** - Consider how you will ultimately use the knowledge. - Adapt the learning method to resemble the real-world application.  **2. Move Between Levels:** - Connect detailed information to the broader context. - Seek opportunities to link the method to the wider topic and its significance.  **3. Find Gaps:** - Identify methods that test existing knowledge for consolidation. - Adjust the method to actively test for gaps in knowledge. - Avoid methods that rely on recognition rather than recall.  **4. Remove Unnecessary"Set-Up":** - Eliminate learning methods that do not contribute to understanding. - Experiment with removing or modifying these methods to promote effective processing. - Embrace discomfort as a sign of breaking away from ineffective habits. - Replace redundant processes with more effective alternatives. *Recognition vs. recall:   Recognition  is when we are familiar with information. Recognition-based learning methods might be looking at a diagram and asking yourself if it makes sense.   On the other hand,  recall  is when we can generate this knowledge from memory, like producing a diagram from memory. It is much easier to recognise something than to recall it.    For example, most people can recognise a coin. However, most people could not accurately draw a coin with all its details from memory. As we usually tested on recall, recognition-based revision methods should be avoided. As you can see, these adjustments are guidelines for experimentation. A crucial and unavoidable part of self-regulated learning is having a hypothesis for an adjustment that will make a method more effective and efficient and then testing it through application. There is often no"right answer" to how a technique should be adjusted. There are only  attempts  and  observations. The cycle of self-regulation:   Proficient self-regulated learners can engage in hypothesis, experimentation, observation, and refinement regularly and frequently to    eventually    arrive at an effective method. Those without much experience in self-regulating their learning can find this process daunting or overwhelming. However, improvement is impossible without  accepting  this feeling and continuing regardless. Over time, it will become second nature to you!   Difficulty and discomfort are signs that you are training a new habit!

Good note-taking will satisfy VPReFRE  Visual, Processed, Relational, Freehand, Reflective and Efficient. If your mind map is done correctly, it will satisfy this checklist.  We will look at a variety of examples and show you why the notes are good or very bad.  You might be shocked to see that common (even typically applauded) note-taking methods are very ineffective for learning.    We will also show you how traditional notes can be improved so you can retain, recall and apply more information.

Welcome back to marginal gains!  You should break down your final goal (i.e., your desired outcome) into smaller, performance-based goals. This will help you focus on the  process  rather than just the results. Remember that outcomes also depend on factors outside your control. Break down your goals - Use the following strategies to help reduce overwhelm Step 1: Use reverse goal-setting to find smaller, performance goals Step 2: Focus on the relationship between outcome, skills, attributes, and mindsets Step 3: Create plans on how you will address the limitations in skills/processes, attributes, or mindsets Step 4: Expect difficulty - it's much easier to persist through difficulty when we expect and accept it Reverse goal setting is any instance where the desired outcome is dissected into its determinants and then traced back to our current position. The example in this video is one way we can apply reverse goal setting. A slightly different method incorporating force-field analysis may also be appropriate for especially complex goals.  Learn about an alternative method with force-field analysis on the YouTube video  here. How to perform reverse goal setting -  Step 1: Identify the desired end outcome Step 2: Define the skills, attributes, and mindsets necessary to achieve this with maximal certainty Step 3: (Optional) Rate the level we need to be at each out of 10 Step 4: Rate our current level for each of these out of 10 Step 5: Create a plan to improve the skills and attributes necessary within the desired deadline (see additional notes below) We can create plans with a  top-down  vs.  bottom-up  approach. Expand the accordions below to learn about each approach. Top-down:  Top-down means we start at the end and work backwards. The example given in the video uses a top-down approach. This approach is best for when:   It is easier to deconstruct our goals from the end result (often the case with complex goals that involve several components with a variety of timings)  It is difficult to assess our current skill and attribute level, or we have not begun to develop them   We can assess the feasibility of the plan by seeing if our  true  current state is aligned with the  supposed  current state we arrive at by tracing back from the desired goal towards the present day. If (by working backwards), we find that we currently need to be in a better state than we are, the plan is not feasible and must be reconsidered. Bottom-up:  Bottom-up means we start at the current state and work forwards. The example in the YouTube video uses a bottom-up approach. This approach is best for when:   Our current skills and attribute level is clearer  It is unclear what is needed towards the later stages of achieving the goal  There is a more direct relationship between skills and attributes and the goal   We assess the feasibility of the plan by seeing if we can reach the goal within the deadline based on the step-wise progression of skill and attribute development. If (based on our anticipated pace of development) we are unable to reach the desired level of skill to reach our goals in the time available, the plan is not feasible and must be reconsidered.   Bottom-up approaches are still reverse goal setting if we identify the required skills and attributes based on analysing our desired goal. Use both:  In many cases, we may want to use both a top-down  and  bottom-up analysis to assess the viability of our plan and see if we have missed anything. Recommended task 1Create a reverse plan  Following the guidelines in our Neuroticism: Planning sublesson, perform a reverse goal setting analysis and construct a plan.   Complete this task once you have created your plan.   Base Camp:  Resume upgrading your self-management skills and combine your advanced encoding methods to create nuanced systems of learning.  **Key Dimensions:**  * Self-management * Deep processing * Mindset  **Recommended Pace:**  * Theory: 5 hours over 4 weeks  **Recommended Practice:**  * 8 to 10 weeks of practice * Most theory covered within the first 4 weeks  **Progression Tips:**  * Base Camp covers a wide range of skills. * Practice time varies depending on individual challenges. * Most learners spend approximately 8 weeks in this stage. * Progress at a comfortable pace to consolidate the system. * The most technical lesson is"Hipshot."

Viewing progress through marginal gains is about more than understanding the concept.  It needs to become a habit. Most people do not think about progress via marginal gains"naturally". We commonly see learners who use marginal gains perfectly when setting goals but forget about them when viewing their mistakes.   Their old thinking habits make them continue to think," I'm not improving even though I'm trying so hard". Despite knowing that they  should  think about marginal gains, they fail to catch themselves when they do not. Key principle:  Habits operate so fast and subconsciously that we can be aware of how we must think and act but still struggle to stop ourselves from doing the same thing we are used to.  Retraining this takes time, diligence, and self-awareness. Therefore, it's vital that we try to retrain our  habit  of thinking about progress through the lens of marginal gains. Complete the following recommended tasks to help start this retraining process. Recommended task 1Identify your marginal gains for learning efficiently  Follow the five steps outlined in our"Tracking marginal gains" guide  here  to identify your 1% gains for learning efficiency. Complete this task once you have compounded your 1% gains at least three times. This means you have:   Identified a marginal gain  Tried to reach it  Reflected on the experience  Identified the next marginal gain with our 5-step framework  Repeated this process twice   Remember that marginal gains in the early stages often involve  identifying gaps in knowledge,  learning more  about what action to take, and  building basic self-awareness.   Tip:   Check out what other learners think of as marginal gains in the  #wins-and-gains  channel on our Discord community. This is an excellent source of ideas. We encourage you to post your marginal gains on this channel, too!  Fundamentals:  Don't underestimate the basics! Most learners struggle to improve for years because they lack these fundamentals.  **Learning Plan**  **Key Dimensions:**  * Retrieval * Deep Processing * Mindset  **Recommended Pace:**  * 2-3 hours of theory per week for 1-2 weeks * Intersperse theory with practice  **Recommended Practice:**  * 2 weeks of practice before moving to the next stage * Continuous use of these methods throughout the program * Ample opportunities for practice in subsequent stages  **Progression Tips:**  * Complete each lesson in one sitting * Complete the focus tasks for each lesson before moving on * Prevent overwhelm by avoiding the pursuit of total mastery in the first lesson * Gain experience with the techniques as instructed

The Pomodoro technique is widely known and adopted but has disadvantages. After all, it did originate from a literal tomato cooking timer, not research!    The first disadvantage is that your brains ability to focus will  vary depending on the situation. If youre focusing well (often called being in  flow ), you dont want to cut off precious focus time prematurely, nor do you want to extend unfocused time if you cant stay focused for the full 25 minutes.   The second disadvantage is that it doesnt explain how to use the rest time. Having only five minutes to rest may be insufficient.   Many people do not find the traditional Pomodoro technique sustainable and effective in the long term. As a result, most experienced and productive people do not use it at all. Instead, it is better to personalise the Pomodoro technique and optimise it for yourself. One variant that is commonly recommended instead is the  Flowmodoro technique, which more closely resembles our guidelines and variation.  Ultimately, the important thing is that your method works for you. In our experience, OFF-rest timing is the most flexible and adaptable variant we have encountered, with compatibility in diverse settings.  OFF-rest timing is short for  Optimised-For-Focus rest timing. It aims to keep your efficiency and focus high for as many cycles   as possible throughout the day. Use OFF-rest timing for extended work periods where you will have multiple cycles of work and rest.   The type of rest you take depends on the circumstances, but you still want to be as productive as possible during your breaks. Analogy:  Imagine moving a heavy boulder. The hardest part is getting it rolling in the first place. It takes very little energy and effort to keep it moving if you keep pushing it a little bit whenever it slows down. But if you wait until the boulder has completely stopped before pushing again, you must spend much more energy to get it back in motion. Determining the type of break -  Step 1: If you are relatively fresh, take a topical or miscellaneous break. Step 2: If you are low on energy, take an active relaxation break (we suggest doing focus training or mindful meditation). Step 3: Regardless, use a timer to measure how long you stay focused, and ensure that your rest time is one-third of your focused time. You should rest    as soon as  your brain is starting to become dull. This way, you can refocus and feel restored much more quickly. If we wait until we are noticeably tired before resting, refocusing and restoring our energy will take much longer, making us less productive overall.   You may also use the Wim-Hof breathing method during breaks to rapidly re-energise if you have less than 10 minutes. A tutorial on this helpful technique is    here. Writing an essay (or any high-level writing, such as a literature review) requires you to construct a clear and logical flow of ideas. One of the most common problems with learners at all levels is that ideas are not communicated in an order and flow that is easy to understand. New perspectives and concepts are introduced randomly, conclusions are not well-justified, points lack examples and are unclear, or the overall progression of ideas is hard to follow without deep concentration. Fundamental principles of good writing: 1. Language is appropriate for the expected standard, but simple and concise.2. Arguments and perspectives are represented logically.3. Ideas develop in a way that is easy to follow. Tip:  Most people spend far too little time planning their structure and flow. This leads to wasted time trying to figure out the flow while writing. Plan the flow first, then elaborate on each point to develop your writing. It's the same as creating a clear logic layer before entering the more detailed layers.    Place more emphasis on crafting the logical flow so it is as simple and understandable as possible. This is one of the biggest separators of good writing vs. mediocre writing. Methods such as BHS can help you map your ideas and identify the most important concepts and chunks. This can help you to structure your writing and linearise it similarly to performing WPW.  The remaining work concerns gaining procedural skills and practising writing with good technique. Most people struggle with both the declarative flow and the procedural skills. This is why good writing skills are rare.   The ReCOVer system is one method for helping to structure your essay writing during assessments and examinations. Recall:  After applying the BHS, we should have a deep and heavily connected knowledge of the topic. In this step, we will recall all the relevant points concerning our essay question stem.  Craft a flow of main points and chunks, precisely as you would a logic and concepts layer, but use the question stem or essay prompt as your guideline for what kind of chunk structure is most relevant.   During exams, you should spend up to 10 minutes on this step.    Difficulties with this step likely indicate a lack of higher-order learning of the topic during preparation. Chunk:  Once we have our draft flow, remove any weaker or less relevant points for supporting the main argument. Consider whether the flow of ideas is indeed the most easily understandable. This step is akin to Skinning your map.  We want to end up with a clear logic layer with:   A one-directional flow and progression of ideas (this helps to linearise it in the essay)  Supporting concepts and arguments that clearly support each chunk of the logic layer  A total of three to four main chunks (to keep the argument concise for most essays - this may change depending on your level of writing)    In an exam, spend up to 5 minutes at this step. Order:  In this step, we will clearly linearise our structure.  Arrange your biggest chunks and major sub-chunks (if any) into a linear order. An example is given below:   Introduction  Main point 1  Main point 2  Main point 3    Main point 1  Supporting concept 1  Supporting concept 2  Bridging concept 3    Main point 2  Supporting concept 1  Supporting concept 2    Main point 3  etc.     This is the order in which you will write your essay, starting with an introduction that cleanly covers the main ideas and how you will flow between them. Each chunk is unpacked with supporting evidence and statements.   The table below gives an indication of how the number of chunks and complexity of topics translates into essay length.  **Essay Structure and Length by Academic Level**  **High School** * Chunks: 3-5 * Sub-Chunks: 0-1 * Points per Chunk: 2-4 * Total Discussion Points: 12-20 * Essay Length: Approximately 2000 words  **Early University** * Chunks: 5-7 * Sub-Chunks: 1-2 * Points per Chunk: 3-5 * Total Discussion Points: 20-40 * Essay Length: Approximately 3000 words  **Late University** * Chunks: 5-7 * Sub-Chunks: 1-3 * Points per Chunk: 4-7 * Total Discussion Points: 40-70 * Essay Length: Approximately 10,000 words  **Academic Publication or Thesis** * Chunks: 5-10 * Sub-Chunks: 2-4 * Points per Chunk: 5-10 * Total Discussion Points: 50-100 * Essay Length: Approximately 30,000-100,000+ words If you feel like your organisation of chunks is messy, it is unlikely that your essay will be good. Furthermore, you are likely to waste significant time during the writing process trying to figure out how to structure your points and references mid-sentence.   If even the plan looks bad, its unlikely that the execution will be better. If it is, then it would undoubtedly be slower and less accurate.    In an exam, spend up to 5 minutes at this step. Verbalise:  The final step is to expand your skeleton outline into sentences and paragraphs. Depending on your technical writing skill and mastery of the language, this should be the easiest step since you already know what you want to say and how all the supporting statements and evidence fit into the bigger picture.  If you have many points, you should create bullet points in the same order and then turn the bullet points into sentences. You will be unlikely to have time to do this in an exam, but exam essays tend to be much shorter with fewer points, so it is also less likely that you will need this extra step.   In an exam, your remaining time is spent at this step, excluding time for review.    If you struggle with this step, you may have a lower level of language mastery (which requires more practice and interleaving to reach the desired level of language fluency). **Problem Felt**  * Lack of evidence to support points or excessive use of vague statements * Difficulty connecting recalled points to major themes or conclusions * Confusion about the flow of ideas or lack of direction in the essay * Difficulty writing smoothly and naturally without the aforementioned issues  **Likely Cause(s)**  * Inadequate recall or superficial initial learning * Insufficient chunking or lack of relationship-based learning * Poor organization of chunks or sub-chunks * Limited language proficiency

Information is given value, meaning, and strength of memory based on it's relationship to other pieces of information. For example, if a fact is memorised by itself - it is not related to anything else. If a concept is understood, we might think we will remember it, but if the concept is not related to any other concepts, we will still forget it. Examples of methods that improve memory and depth:   All of the methods below are examples of techniques that help form networks of knowledge, which improve memory and depth:    Creating analogies that are accurate and comprehensive  Relating new information to knowledge you already have  Simplifying new terminology by giving them simpler names you find more intuitive  Applying information and solving a problem immediately  Comparing new information with other new information to see how they are related to each other  Grouping new information into chunks based on shared similarities  Drawing arrows between chunks based on how the chunks affect each other  Positioning chunks on your notes in different places depending on the other concepts they might need to be connected to Think about the  cognitive processes  involved in each of the methods above.  Notice how methods 1, 2, and 3 create connections between new information and existing knowledge (we call these  analogous relationships ), whereas methods 5 to 7 create connections between new information ( we call these in-context relationships ). Method 4 could be both analogous or in-context, depending on the type of problem we are trying to solve.    Therefore, we can say that there are two types of networks we can form with new information: analogous and in-context.  When we learn, we should always try to make both types of connections, if possible.  There are also ways to help prime our brains to create relationships (both analogous and in-context). For example, doing some problem-solving or practice questions  before  learning a topic can help prime us about potential relationships.  Correct prestudy techniques  that build big-picture understanding also give us more points of relevance to not only build analogous relationships but also make it easier to perceive and hypothesise in-context relationships. Examples of analogous relationships:   Jill learns about the physiology of muscle contraction. She tries to make sense of it by creating an analogy of a person climbing up a ladder.  Geoffrey learns about how a dam generates electricity using water currents. He makes sense of this by relating the mechanisms to his understanding of how wind turbines work that he learned in the previous semester.  Sarah is learning about the principles of economics. She forms an analogy by equating supply and demand to a seesaw, where the equilibrium point is the balance achieved when both sides are equally weighted.  Robert is exploring the principles of genetics in his biology class. He draws upon his prior knowledge of Mendelian inheritance from a previous semester to understand how genes are passed from one generation to the next in complex genetic traits.  Emily is learning about international trade and globalization in her economics course. She connects this to her previous studies in history, understanding that trade routes and the exchange of goods have shaped the course of civilizations throughout history.  Samuel is diving into the study of 20th-century literature. He relates the themes and social contexts of the literature he is currently reading to his earlier coursework in sociology, recognizing how societal changes influenced the literary works of that era.  Ethan is exploring the concept of chemical reactions and catalysts. He relates this to a cooking analogy, likening reactants to ingredients in a recipe and catalysts to a chef's secret ingredient that speeds up the cooking process.  Alex is studying the structure of an atom in chemistry. To simplify it, he creates an analogy where the nucleus is the"core" of an atom, and electrons are like planets orbiting around it, similar to our solar system. Examples of in-context relationships:   Sarah is studying environmental science and has just learned about the water cycle. She immediately applies this knowledge to analyze the recent weather patterns in her region, forming an in-context relationship between the theory of the water cycle and its real-world application.  In a history course, Michael is exploring the causes and consequences of World War I. He compares and contrasts these with the events and outcomes of World War II, identifying in-context relationships between the two major historical events.  In a biology lecture, Finn is studying cell division and the cell cycle. He makes theoretical connections to the concept of mitosis and meiosis, understanding how these processes govern cell replication and genetic diversity.  In an economics seminar, Joanna is delving into supply and demand dynamics. She observes the theoretical link between supply and demand curves and the concept of market equilibrium, where the quantity demanded equals the quantity supplied.  In a music theory class, Jihee is learning about chord progressions and harmonic structures. She discerns the theoretical connection between diatonic chords and the concept of tonal harmony, understanding how certain chords naturally complement each other within a musical key.  In a chemistry lab, Emily is experimenting with chemical reactions and observing how different factors affect reaction rates. She draws connections between her observations and the chemical principles she learned in lectures, creating in-context relationships between theory and practical experimentation.  John is taking a finance course and has just studied the concept of compound interest. He immediately applies this knowledge when evaluating different investment options, forming in-context relationships between the financial theory he's learned and its practical implications for personal finance decisions. Why does the difference in types of relationships matter?:  Although we separate them into analogous vs. in-context in this lesson, many methods can help with both (directly or indirectly). The goal is not to be absolutely certain about which relationships are in-context vs. analogous but rather to remind ourselves to always look for both types of connections when possible. We may struggle to relate information to prior knowledge but find it easier to make in-context relationships, or vice-versa.  If you cannot make  any  relationships, it likely means what you are learning is too detailed for your current level of knowledge. Instead, we need to build a stronger foundation of surface-level knowledge with prestudy. We will introduce more techniques related to this concept in the later stages. Our brain adapts to solve problems and learns things along the way. Good memory and depth of understanding are symptoms of your brain creating good knowledge structures. It does not come from simply  intending  to remember or understand something!  In the classical view of Bloom's taxonomy, memorising and understanding are considered the bottom levels. We commonly refer to this as lower-order learning. However, Bloom's has also been criticised for representing knowledge and learning in a hierarchy. We know now that this is not entirely accurate.   In reality, memory and understanding occur when we engage in effective learning strategies. It is not a linear order we must follow, starting from the bottom and working our way up to the top. In authentic learning, we constantly go back and forth between levels. Dr Justin Sung created his own framework to explain this.   In the lessons to come, you will learn to engage in this encoding cycle, operating exclusively in the higher orders of learning, to create strong memory and understanding. Memory and depth is a symptom:  Our brain adapts to solve problems and learns things along the way. Good memory and depth of understanding are symptoms of your brain creating good knowledge structures. It does not come from simply  intending  to remember or understand something!  System 1 vs. system 2 thinking:  The brain recognises if information is worth processing or not. It always looks for an energy-efficient shortcut. This split in the way our brain thinks has been noted in lots of research, including  Thinking Fast and Slow  by Daniel Kahneman, who won the Nobel Prize based on his work. He identified two systems of thinking, one fast and reactive (based on pattern recognition) and one slow and deliberate. The hot-cold empathy gap:  Differences in our brain's processing ability can also be seen in hot vs. cold decision-making. This phenomenon is where we make different decisions when under pressure or stimulated (hot) than when we are calm and calculating (cold).   For example,  while you are cold, you decide to study for 2 hours after school. However, after school, you are tempted to watch YouTube or go out with your friends, and you make a different decision in the heat of the moment.  This difference in how we think causes so many problems that there is even something called the  hot-cold empathy gap, which talks about the problems that arise from the bad decisions we make when we are hot.

The  Swiss cheese model  shows that its extremely difficult to miss information because you are revisiting and asking questions in several rounds. By comparison, if you use conventional linear learning, it is actually much easier to miss things as our brain is not a photocopy machine and will miss many points. Recommended task 1Complete TLS with deliberate order control  Combine what you have learned about order control and complete another two learning sessions using the Traffic Light System.   Complete this task once you have finished two sessions with a Kolb's after each session.   Pay special attention to:   Your note-taking  Your question-quality  Your grouping/chunking    You may also want to get feedback on your attempts from senior community members on Discord.   Technique Training:  In this stage, we introduce variations of inquiry-based learning, which help you to create more meaningful relationships and networks.  **Learning Plan**  **Key Dimensions:**  * Deep processing * Self-regulation * Self-management  **Recommended Pace:**  * Theory: 10 hours over 4 weeks  **Recommended Practice:**  * Practice while learning theory: 2-4 weeks  **Progression Tips:**  * Prioritize the"Inquiry-based learning" lesson within the first week. * This lesson introduces an advanced encoding and note-taking technique. * Most learners require more practice with this technique compared to previous ones.Recommended task 2Explore your interleaving  Order control can influence your retrieval strategies as well. Many retrieval strategies in the  interleaving table  rely on focused revision based on knowledge gaps you identify. The benefit of this focused revision is enhanced by applying a little bit of order control.    Instructions    Practise the  simple relational  teaching technique, explained in the interleaving table.  When you find a knowledge gap, think of at least one other question related to the same concept that may reveal further knowledge gaps.   Complete this task after completing your enhanced retrieval session.  Technique Training:  In this stage, we introduce variations of inquiry-based learning, which help you to create more meaningful relationships and networks.  **Learning Plan**  **Key Dimensions:**  * Deep processing * Self-regulation * Self-management  **Recommended Pace:**  * Theory: 10 hours over 4 weeks  **Recommended Practice:**  * Practice while learning theory: 2-4 weeks  **Progression Tips:**  * Prioritize the"Inquiry-based learning" lesson within the first week. * This lesson introduces an advanced encoding and note-taking technique. * Most learners require more practice with this technique compared to previous ones.

The  WPW technique  involves looking at the topic as a whole, breaking it down into parts, and then returning to it as a whole.  It challenges your higher-order structures and clarity of prioritised relationships (ability to explain the whole clearly), the strength of relationships between layers (ability to move into parts and back into whole smoothly), and recall of detailed information (ability to explain each part accurately).   This table outlines how using this technique can feel and what these feelings mean at a cognitive level. Refer to this to ensure you understand the techniques purpose and improve your metacognition. Connecting your emotional experience with the cognitive processes of learning can help you make fine-tuned adjustments and solve any issues. **Cognitive Outcomes of Slow Teaching**  * **Deep Evaluation of Knowledge Structure:**     * Forces a thorough examination of knowledge organization and connections.     * Tests mental schemas against higher-order criteria (e.g., logical coherence, importance).  * **Identification of Knowledge Gaps:**     * Anxiety may arise due to frequent discovery of gaps in understanding.     * This is beneficial as it exposes hidden gaps that may have been obscured by the illusion of explanatory depth.

About this strategy:  Collecting and processing is a  transitionary technique, meaning we use it to transition between different strategies. In this case, we use collecting and processing to transition from heavily linear notes to processed notes. It helps learners spend more time thinking and analysing the information to break the habit of passive, mindless note-taking.  If you already feel comfortable with non-linear note-taking (e.g. mindmaps), you do not need to use this transitionary technique. Many people dont learn new information properly. They write it down in their notes and assume they have learned it. But why dont you absorb some information even after writing pages of notes? And why can you learn other things (e.g. details about your favourite TV show) without writing any notes?   Clearly, our brain doesnt learn information just because we wrote it down. This is where processing notes comes in. Step 1: Collecting:  While  collecting, make the information as concise as possible. Only include keywords; remove words that are not needed, like the or and. You can use symbols like  or + to represent words or meanings. This step is very easy and does not activate any learning. It is simply a setting-up step for processing. Step 2: Processing:   Processing  involves thinking about the information to make sense of it and simplify it. Try to represent the information in the simplest way possible, bringing together as many concepts as possible.  Try moving from collecting to processing after every concept so that we are progressively building a more extensive  network. As you continue to process, your"web" or"network" of knowledge should grow as more relationships, arrows, and groups form.  Dont be afraid to oversimplify things! The details will always be on the left in the collection column. You want to focus on representing the main ideas as effectively as possible on the processing side. Important note:   Processing  your notes is a core learning skill. Therefore, the note-taking skills and methods you learn in this step should carry over to all other note-taking you do later. In the future, when we teach you to create mindmaps (or more accurately,  chunkmaps ), we expect that they will still be  processed. Grouping and creating relationships is one of the most important skills for improving your learning efficiency and deep processing.   The thought processes involved in forming groups and creating relationships are crucial for high-quality encoding. Ask yourself:   How can I group this information?  Does it still make sense?  What does it relate to?  Why is this important?  How does it fit into the big picture?    The power of grouping comes from the activation of higher-order learning.   Whenever we form groups and relationships, we want to ensure we  prioritise them.  This involves making a value judgment on how important one group or relationship is compared to others. Key principle:  Making a value judgment on the importance and relevance of information is ultimately subjective. Try your best to make an accurate judgment by reading and learning about the topic.   The time and effort involved in making this judgment directly contributes to effective learning. On Bloom's revised taxonomy, we are activating the  evaluate  level of learning. This creates significant improvements in depth and retention of knowledge and is one of the most  crucial  steps to efficient learning.  You often wont be sure which concepts are the most important or which way of grouping makes the most sense.  This is normal!  When this happens, revisit the material to gain more information and clarity. This  back-and-forth process  (where we think, question ourselves, seek answers, and consolidate) is precisely what higher-order learning feels like!   It may differ greatly from how you usually learn if you have mostly lower-order habits. Prioritising to a high level - To help you prioritise relationships or groups, ask yourself these questions. Step 1: What are other ways that these concepts are similar to each other? Step 2: What wider purpose do these concepts have? Step 3: Why do I need to know this? Why is this relationship important? Step 4: What is the simplest way I can group these ideas together while keeping the relationships accurate? There is no right or wrong here.  The key is switching to a  problem-solving mindset.  This makes it easier to retain information compared to memorisation through repetition.   This foundational skill of processing notes will help tremendously when transitioning to more advanced techniques later in the program.    You may notice that this technique shares similarities with the well-known Cornell Note-Taking Method. The Cornell method is also a beginner-level technique; however, it is more limited and does not transition to more efficient note-taking. The Cornell method reflects a more outdated approach to note-taking. It is superior to the typical linear methods that most learners still use but is less effective than the methods you will learn in this program.

By now, you should:    Be more aware of how tasks become easier and less risky by breaking them down into smaller steps.  Have experienced how the magnitude and impact of consequences can be reduced by acting earlier with small steps.   In this week's challenge, we will turn your fall into a safety net into a bounce-back from a trampoline. This is about turning mistakes into learning opportunities where you end up in a better position than before your mistake.   Mistakes are a valuable source of learning. However, the lesson isn't always clear! There are two main types of"lessons" mistakes can teach us. Mistakes can teach us about:   Barriers  Conditions   Expand the accordions below to understand each type. Barriers:  Barriers are anything that prevents us from being successful. This includes:   Not having enough energy  Inadequate planning  External distractions  Competing priorities  Inadequate resources  Insufficient knowledge and information  Existing habits and behaviours   As you can see, barriers can be (and often are) behavioural or internal rather than physical or external. People often try something, fail, and then say they can't do it because there isn'ta physical or tangible change they can make. However, this doesn't mean it is fixed.   For example,  if the barrier is insufficient knowledge, the way to overcome this is to learn more. If the barrier is an existing habit, the solution is to start challenging and changing this habit. If it is due to competing priorities, the solution may be to re-evaluate priorities, be stricter with time and task management, or schedule time away from competing priorities.  Having a solution-finding mindset is crucial. We don't just want to find problems. We want to find a solution.    Always believe a solution is possible. Remember, someone out there could find it. There's no reason that someone can't be you! Conditions:  Doing something correctly and successfully often requires certain conditions to be met. When we first attempt something, we rarely know of all these conditions.  When the task is more complicated, there are usually more conditions that need to be first discovered and then met. This process takes time, discovery, experimentation, and observation.  If there are no barriers to your success, the reason you made a mistake may be that you simply did not know all of the variables to consider.   For example,  many students believe hard work is all it takes to succeed academically. However, there are many other conditions to be met (such as deep processing and self-regulated learning).   Gaining awareness of these conditions as quickly and as early as possible is crucial for growth and development.  Week 2 instructions -  Step 1: Pick a task or skill that you are likely to make errors for. This should preferably be a task with a high skill requirement (e.g. sport, music, learning and studying, etc.) Step 2: Give your best attempt at performing the skill for an hour. If studying, study for one hour. Step 3: Evaluate how successful your attempt was. If studying, evaluate your retention and depth of understanding (this can intuitively assessed or physically tested if you have testing materials). Step 4: Hopefully, you've failed! Identify the lesson type from the experience. Were there barriers to success? If so, what? Or if not, could there be conditions for success that you are unaware of? What could they be (you don't have to be certain)? Step 5: Create a clear focus for experimentation based on what you have learned from the mistake and make another attempt. Step 6: Repeat this process for multiple sessions (different subjects, levels of depth, types of challenges, etc.) and after every 3 attempts, reflect on how many cycles it takes to start seeing improvements in your outcomes and success. Step 7: At the end of the week, reflect on how accurate your ability to predict barriers or conditions is. You will probably notice how difficult it is to anticipate barriers and conditions without experiencing it first! As before, you may wish to repeat week 2 to gain more insight and experience with identifying learnings from mistakes and to complete enough cycles to get a feel for how many mistakes are required to see improvements in results. It's never-ending!:  For complex skills, you may be surprised at how many attempts and cycles it requires to remove enough barriers and understand enough conditions to succeed even once. You may also notice that it is almost impossible to predict the less obvious issues you will face before attempting something.    This is why the faster you can cycle, the faster you can improve.  Imagine how long it would take to remove all the barriers and understand the conditions for success if we only attempt something once or twice after days or weeks of contemplating!

In-class guidelines - Maximise learning and reduce overwhelm from classes, lectures and workshops by following these guidelines. Step 1: Always prestudy. Prestudy improves your retention, engagement and enjoyment in class. Step 2: Focus your prestudy on the big-picture concepts, not details. Step 3: Always paraphrase and process information before writing notes. Step 4: Don't avoid feeling challenged. Good learning feels uncomfortable because your brain is actively working. Step 5: Write non-linear notes. Mindmaps and spatial representations are much more effective than linear notes. Step 6: Limit your word-count. The more you write, the less you're processing. Learning happens in the brain. Check out our article on  how to prestudy  for more guidance on this critical skill. Alert: This is a high-yield lesson:  In our experience, learners see dramatic improvements in learning quality and overall learning experience by applying the techniques from this lesson. Likewise, we commonly find significant issues for learners who do not practise or apply the methods from this lesson sufficiently.  I don't have time to do any prestudy. What should I do?:  Prestudy is more valuable than reviewing previous material. This is because effective prestudy reduces how much you forget and prevents you from becoming continually overwhelmed.   If the bucket leaks, it's more important to plug the holes than to keep filling it. How do I know if I am doing prestudy correctly?:  Prestudying should help you feel:   Less overwhelmed  More able to consolidate and make sense of information from class or lectures  Generally aware of the main ideas that will be covered  Able to ask deeper or more interconnected questions (if asking questions is possible)   If you do not feel this, ensure your technique is correct by reading  this guide. I've never tried prestudying before. How long will it take to do it correctly?:  With daily practice, learners can develop sufficient skill after around 1 to 2 weeks. However, this range is only approximate. Each person has their own pace, and you should focus on using your own perception of skill to guide your progress.  Read our article on  how to know when to move on  for more guidance. I struggle to think and process before writing notes. What should I do?:  This can indicate a weakness in deep processing. Don't worry. It will get easier with time! You can train this by delaying your note-taking for as long as possible (until you feel like you will forget). Over time, try to extend how long you can delay note-taking.   Tip:  You will find it easier to delay note-taking when you constantly relate information to the big picture and try to simplify and group new information together.

The purpose of revision is to  recall  the information in your study material.  However, there are different ways to revise. Some people only practise  recognising  the information. Other people rely on  cues  to help them recall it.  It is better to have  true recall, where you can remember and apply the information even without cues.   To help with your revision, we will go through techniques that build true recall.

By now, you should have:    gained some experience with hypothesizing how to improve a method and making adjustments to evaluate the hypothesis  deepened your metacognition to be much more mindful of the effectiveness and purpose of different learning methods   In this week's challenge, we'll improve our observation skills, helping us to make more meaningful adjustments. Week 3 instructions -  Step 1: Continue with week 2 instructions. It is unrealistic to gain enough experience on adjusting methods in just one week. You will need to continue this process for weeks or months. Step 2: Start to vary the amount of time and effort invested in different methods. Step 3: Observe the effect this has on your memory and depth of understanding by testing yourself every 3 to 7 days. You can use practice questions, practice projects/challenges, or brain dumps (writing everything you remember in-depth - like writing a book). The purpose of this week's challenge is to start isolating variables. When we use multiple learning methods, it becomes difficult to find cause-and-effect relationships. It's unclear which methods (or parts of methods) are helpful and which are not. By investing more or less time in some methods, we can see which gaps appear or are filled.   When we do this, we can often discover that:    We spend lots of time using some methods, but fading them out doesn't seem detrimental to our learning.  We spend very little time using some methods and investing more time and effort in them improves our learning more than we expected.  Where we spend our time and energy doesn't necessarily reflect the most effective methods. Instead, it may simply be a result of our existing habits and behaviours. Cause and effect:  By understanding which variables contribute to which effects, we gain the ability to finely control our learning system. This is a lifelong skill.

Self-management is complicated to train because so many variables influence each other. Gaining an awareness of some of these variables and how your tasks, activities, and self-management methods affect these variables is a necessary part of improving your self-management skills. Normally, it can take years to gain this awareness.  This first week's challenge will help you identify these variables significantly faster. Week 1 instructions -  Step 1: Use a time-tracking app on your phone to track your activities and tasks throughout the day. Step 2: At the end of the day, reflect on whether you felt that you were in"flow" and how intentional your time spent was. Did you procrastinate and get distracted? Did the day go according to plan? Step 3: Each day, seek to identify at least one or two factors that may have affected your energy, concentration, distractibility, and ability to execute on your plans as intended. Step 4: At the end of the week, summarise your observations by listing the factors that seem to affect your energy, concentration, distractibility, and ability to execute intentionally. Note the impact of these factors.

In this lesson we will...: 1. Stop asking"what" questions2. Develop deeper and more refined chunk structures3. Learn to actively manage cognitive load In the previous stages, we focused on asking a wide range of questions to learn how to invoke curiosity and see how the questions we ask influence the way we process information.  In Ascent I, we will start reducing the number of questions and focusing our efforts on just a few high-yield questions that produce consistent and predictable patterns of connections. By asking questions centred around evaluating and judging the  importance  of information, our chunks also reflect these judgments.  Therefore, we call this method   importance-based chunking, which you will see and hear in our community and Live Clinics often. Alert:  Importance-based chunking is considered a core pillar of our program. It is a method of thinking that will influence every form of information processing from here onwards. Instructions for importance-based chunking -  Step 1: Ask the question,"Why is this keyword or concept important?" Step 2: Look for the possible answers (there will usually be multiple reasons for something to be important). Step 3: Move on to the next keyword (in whatever order feels relevant to you) and repeat this process. Step 4: With each keyword, you will start seeing similarities in why some things are important. These become potential chunks. Step 5: Make a judgment on which relationships are important enough to keep and which similarities are important enough to choose to form chunks. Step 6: Continue to build your knowledge by constantly repeating this process for each keyword until you have covered all of the concepts. While you are performing this, aim to process and consolidate each new keyword as you go. You want to use chunking and mapping to reduce cognitive load before introducing new ideas. Keyword list:  We start with a keyword list like below. We should be familiar with this by now. Finding first relationships and possible chunks:  Next, we start with a keyword we intuitively feel might be relevant or important to start with. We ask  why this is important  and  how it might be related to the other keywords. We repeat this process for a few keywords to get a general sense of how the keywords are related to each other in order to form groups.   In the image below, each yellow link indicates a potential relationship.    For teaching purposes, we have also written the rationale to emphasise that we should try and be clear and explicit about the nature of the relationship instead of simply drawing a line. Representing our new flow non-linearly:  Based on this pattern, we can try to hypothesise a chunk structure that is represented as below. Note that this step is simply rearranging the keywords to make it flow more clearly.   Now that we have mapped the flow, it's easier to now think of additional relationships to see if it makes sense. Trying to think of additional relationships in the list form or simply mentally can create cognitive overload. In some cases, it's possible to map many relationships purely in list form.    Transitioning from list to map depends on how comfortable you are with managing the cognitive load. If in doubt, draft your hypothetical flow earlier rather than later. Evaluating further relationships:  At this point, we can start thinking about how all of these concepts are related to each other. This process of inquiry-based learning is almost identical to the TLS. We ask how it's related to each other, why it's important, and look for information to answer these questions.  As we progress through a few more keywords, we naturally start learning slightly deeper and in more detail than before. We may need to do some online searches or consult other resources to get the answer we want.   Below we've represented the new relationships we found from doing this in  yellow. During this process, some additional keywords were revealed that weren't part of our original list. These have been included in  pink  as they felt important and relevant to include at the big-picture level.    After doing this, we can see that there are many additional relationships between items that are difficult to arrange on our map. When this happens, we can look at the relationships we've found to hypothesise an  alternative overall chunk structure. Revising our chunk structure:  We can see that there are many connections around the"Types" chunk in the bottom left. There are also many chunks branching from the central"retinal detachment". By observing this, we can hypothesise a way to chunk the information based on using the"Types" chunk as a more central part of the flow.  After lots more thinking, we might arrive at a flow that looks like the below.    You can see how much simpler and more intuitive it looks!  Our chunks are starting to represent the  judgments  we have made on what is important and how they are related to each other.  As we continue to go deeper into each keyword and repeat this process for more detail, we may need to adjust our chunk structure again to fit more keywords and relationships.  Throughout the entire process, we use our chunking and non-linear note-taking to manage our cognitive load and keep it at optimum. We strategically utilise our mental effort (increase cognitive load) to engage higher-order learning, and offload with chunking and non-linear note-taking to reduce cognitive load and prevent overload.    Once we have importance-based chunks, we can then ask how these chunks (or sub-chunks) are related to and affect each other. This gives us  inter-chunk relationships. When our inter-chunk relationships are clean, this creates a simple, clean  backbone  to our learning, making the learning process much faster.  The ability to do importance-based chunking and create clean inter-chunk relationships is one of the main differences between beginner and intermediate learners!  At this stage, we also want to completely remove what questions (i.e. fact or recall-based questions). These questions only lead to lower-order learning, whereas questions that  force us to compare concepts against each other  (e.g. why X is important, how it relates to Y) also produce higher-order learning.  One amazing thing about our brain is that when we do higher-order learning, it will automatically complete lower-order learning, too. This means that why and how it relates questions can completely replace what questions. It might seem impossible or counterintuitive, but just give it a go!   Understanding why something is important gives us an idea of how the information is  structured. In other words, we know  how  to think about the subject, which is incredibly important and a defining feature of an intermediate learner.  When our brain doesnt know  how  to think about the subject, its trying to solve lots of problems at the same time:   What does this mean?  Why do I need to know it?  How do I use this?  Is this relevant?  Do I need to memorise all the details?  How do I solve problems with it?  How does it fit in with everything else?   Since a lot is happening in your brain, you become less focused. This can cause you to spend more time and energy without learning much.   When information is not well-organised,  retention is low.   The convenient thing about learning  how  to think about something is that these general logical frameworks and patterns repeat. A repeating pattern:  As a young child, you might have thrown something on the floor. In doing so, you learned about a framework called cause and effect. You naturally interleaved this by throwing lots of things and hearing many noises.  Thus, the framework of evaluating how important something is through the lens of"cause and effect" is something that most people can understand easily as an adult. Repeating frameworks and structures can be found in subject domains as well. You will discover many of them when asking why a concept is important. As you build experience, you will learn what questions and structures to ask to produce the most logical chunks.  Here are some common types of chunks you will find in different subjects. This list can help guide your thinking when considering why concepts from different subjects might be important.    Please note that this is not a comprehensive list. If you rely solely on this list to create importance-based chunks, you will encounter problems later. Instead, always start by asking yourself, Why is it important?. Biology:  In biology, importance could mean chunking based on:   Function  Structure vs. function  Scale (microscopic to macroscopic)  A group of causes leading to a group of effects    Example:  Instead of chunking organelles based on their location, try chunking them based on their function. Chemistry:  In chemistry, importance could mean chunking based on:   Mechanisms of reaction  Real-world applications  Electronegativity  Common reagents or products    Example:  Instead of writing an unordered list, try chunking reactions based on the mechanisms that cause them. Physics:  In physics, importance could mean chunking based on:   Real-world applications  Conceptually important precursors to other concepts    Example:  Instead of following the textbook, try chunking concepts and equations based on common applications. Maths:  In maths, importance could mean chunking based on:   Real-world applications  Conceptually important precursors to other concepts    Example:  Instead of following the textbook, try chunking groups of equations that produce a certain effect on a graph. English:  In English, importance could mean chunking based on:   The author/director/artists background, motivations or values  The storys significance    Example:  Instead of writing an unordered list, try chunking themes and techniques based on which of the authors values they derive from. History:  In history, importance could mean chunking based on:   Major cultural, political, religious, philosophical or social movements at the time  Cause and effect  Predominant emotions or motivations of key players   Subjects like history may have the most diverse range of possible importance-based chunking structures. Since history tracks a massive range of human events and activities, there is also a diverse range of reasons why a historical event may be important.   Example:  Instead of chunking events based on geographical location, try chunking them based on their motivating forces. Law/Legal Studies:  In law, importance could mean chunking based on:   Underlying social principles of justice  Pragmatic considerations and compensatory mechanisms  Landmark cases that exposed gaps in the system  Social, political or economic principles that must be protected    Example:  Instead of listing cases and bills in isolation, try chunking them based on the underlying legal principles they relate to. Languages:  In languages, importance could mean chunking based on:   Common usage  Situational usage  How they affect messaging or communication   Languages are the hardest to chunk by importance because languages are not inherently logical. In this case, importance would relate to how you intend to use the language.   Example:  Instead of writing an unordered list, try chunking words and phrases based on their emotions or use cases. Again, this list is just a reference to give you an idea of common structures you will encounter. Recommended task 1Practise importance-based chunking  Attempt to use importance-based chunking at least 3 times with a Kolb's after each session. Remember, success is to find your mistakes and knowledge gaps early!  Ascent I:  Upgrade your encoding with the Aim step. The first part of our 3-step hyper-efficient encoding system.  **Learning Stage: Deep Processing and Self-Regulation**  **Recommended Duration:**  * Theory: 8-10 hours over 4-6 weeks * Practice: 4-6 weeks  **Recommended Practice:**  * Focus on deep processing and self-regulation techniques.  **Progression Tips:**  * Practice more than theory compared to previous stages. * Limit focus tasks to 2 at a time. * Avoid progressing too quickly through lessons. * Each lesson introduces new conditions and nuances to consider. * Regular and diligent Kolb's learning cycle is essential for smooth progression.Recommended task 2Explore more interleaving methods  While your encoding abilities are growing in full stride, it's important to stay on top of our SIR. It's also important not to only stay in our SIR comfort zones. Some methods of retrieval may be new and unfamiliar, yet surprisingly helpful to add to your system.   Instructions:   Complete at least two more interleaved retrieval sessions, using at least one method from the  interleaving table  you have not attempted before.  Ascent I:  Upgrade your encoding with the Aim step. The first part of our 3-step hyper-efficient encoding system.  **Learning Stage: Deep Processing and Self-Regulation**  **Recommended Duration:**  * Theory: 8-10 hours over 4-6 weeks * Practice: 4-6 weeks  **Recommended Practice:**  * Focus on deep processing and self-regulation techniques.  **Progression Tips:**  * Practice more than theory compared to previous stages. * Limit focus tasks to 2 at a time. * Avoid progressing too quickly through lessons. * Each lesson introduces new conditions and nuances to consider. * Regular and diligent Kolb's learning cycle is essential for smooth progression.

Note:  This lesson is originally from Technique Training, later in the program. It has been brought forward due to popular demand. If procrastination is not a serious issue for you, you can skip this lesson and cover it during Technique Training instead. BEDS-M is a highly effective framework for improving focus. However, not every component is of equal importance. Below, we will recap on each item as well as indicate its relative impact. What is it?:  The burnt ships strategy has been referred to in several historical contexts by generals and conquistadors. In summary, the strategy is to burn your transport ships after using them so you have no path of retreat. How do we use it?:  For improving focus in our mundane (not going to war) lives, this strategy translates to setting high consequences of failure for tasks.   For example,  some learners have formed accountability pairs where the consequence of failure is to stand up and shout in the middle of a crowded hall during school or lectures.  Note: we are not recommending you start screaming in a crowded hall... How useful is it?:  This strategy is a useful adjunct to other methods when other methods alone are not enough. While it can seem like a fun and exciting strategy, it is more suited for short-term change. It is less effective in the long-term than the other methods.  This is because it operates at the"consequence" level of behavioural psychology. Changing behaviours through"consequence" is generally either not sustainable or simply inadequate if other elements are not addressed.   Use this strategy when other methods seem insufficient or when there are certain behaviours that are a high, urgent priority to change. Important considerations: 1. The consequence of failure must be high enough that you are genuinely fearful of it. Social challenges involving embarrassment have been reported to be effective by other members.2. The consequence must be enforced. Accountability buddies are helpful and recommended.  What is it?:  If there is a cookie jar in front of someone who loves cookies, it takes a lot of motivation and willpower to refrain from eating it. Even someone who feels so-so about cookies may be tempted to eat them. On the other hand, controlling your environment means removing the cookie jar, and therefore the need to use motivation and willpower in the first place. How do we use it?:  You must make your environment conducive to success, which requires you to remove anything that may cause procrastination.    Removing distractions is much easier and more productive than resisting distractions using your willpower. How useful is it?:  This strategy is potentially the most powerful of all. In many cases, this strategy alone is enough to change behaviours permanently.  The latest research on behavioural psychology indicates that modifying our environment is one of the biggest influencers to sustainable and successful behaviour change.  To make productive and meaningful changes to our environment, pair this strategy with the Distraction cheat sheet.  What is it?:  A simple sheet of paper where you can track distractors. Over time, this allows you to make adjustments to your environment to remove them. How do we use it?:  Have a piece of paper next to you while you are studying. Whenever you become distracted while studying, write that distraction onto your distraction cheat sheet. Then, when you are setting up the  environment, you refer to this cheat sheet to eliminate any new distractions. How useful is it?:  This strategy is primarily effective when paired with  environment  optimisation. However, there is an additional benefit of increasing awareness of what affects your focus, which can improve your general focus self-regulation.  What is it?:  It'sa schedule. How do we use it?:  Please refer to the scheduling lessons earlier to learn how to set up a basic,  sustainable  schedule. How useful is it?:  When used correctly and sustainably, schedules improve our ability to focus at a psychological level. This is likely due to:   Improved clarity over tasks and events  Reduced cognitive burden of decision-making  Reduced flow disruption  Reduced context switching, which has significant cognitive switching penalty effects   A basic schedule is a core tool for any learner, even if set loosely.  What is it?:  These are the easiest and most achievable goals needed to accomplish larger goals. The key is the word"minimum". MVGs must be the smallest possible action step where the motivation required is as close to zero as possible. How do we use it?:   Start with a larger desired behaviour  Identify the steps needed to begin engaging in the desired behaviour  Break down the steps until you get to a point where it feels so easy that being unable to achieve it feels ridiculous    For example,  a chain of MVGs for moving from the couch to the study desk may be:   Take a single deep breath and look out the window.  Take 3 more breaths  Stand up  Walk to your study room  Sit at your desk  Take out your studying material  Study for 2 minutes   Note that better  environment  set-up makes MVGs easier as there are fewer barriers. How useful is it?:  MVGs help train out existing bad habits and train new ones. For some firmly ingrained habits, you may only be able to chain 2 or 3 MVGs together before giving up and reverting. However, consistent exposure allows marginal improvement until it becomes easier to get closer to the desired behaviour.  MVGs alone are usually insufficient when the  environment  is not prepared.

Retrieval  means taking information from your long-term memory and bringing it into your working memory to recall, solve problems and answer questions. Key principle:  Retrieval practice is a well-evidenced and important part of learning. No learning system is complete without adequate retrieval practice. There are many different ways to do retrieval practice. We recommend utilising as many of these as possible. Time invested in these methods pays itself off in improvements to your retention. Dedicated retrieval sessions:  In these sessions, you are scheduling time for retrieval practice. Refer to the  interleaving table  for more advice on the types of methods you can use. Many students have a dedicated retrieval practice day every weekend.     Dont look at your notes within at least 12 hours of the session.  Checking notes too recently can simply cue the information back into your working memory without you actively needing to retrieve it. This is unrealistic practice for exam situations.   Always generate an answer, even if you are unsure.  Getting the wrong answer helps you remember the right answer when you correct yourself later. Commit to answering every question or test, even if it's just a guess.   Practice retrieving information fully as if you were being assessed.  For example, when teaching, teach out loud and articulate properly. When writing answers, write the best answer you can. When creating questions, write the most specific and high-quality questions you can. In an exam situation, our retrieval needs to be highly accurate and at a high standard. Practising this high standard of retrieval during revision helps identify subtle weaknesses we might not realise are there until we are put to the test. Retrieval while studying and during class (micro-retrieval):  Whenever you study, look for opportunities to do some micro-retrieval.
