For example, before writing notes in class, always give yourself time to think and process what was said. Its better to write about a group of concepts  after  youve thought about it rather than write it down first  and then  think about it. Not only does this increase intrinsic cognitive load, which improves retention, but it also makes you more strategic and deliberate with note-taking.    This is also called elaborative rehearsal or"maintenance rehearsal" in some learning models (depending on the level of processing involved).     Never write any notes before you have processed the information and made sense of it mentally first.  When you do write notes, always try to write from memory first before referring back to your notes.  Avoid writing in full sentences or paragraphs. Stick to keywords, brief statements, doodles, arrows, and lines. It is much more efficient and versatile to express information non-linearly than to describe every relationship and connection one at a time. Retrieval during execution:  Some learners will have the opportunity to retrieve their knowledge as a natural part of their work. For example, doctors or clinical medical students must retrieve their knowledge while diagnosing patients. Software developers must retrieve their programming knowledge as part of development work.  In these settings, try to identify which orders of learning and types of knowledge (declarative vs. procedural) are being tested. Use dedicated retrieval sessions to fill any deficits.  Refer to the  interleaving table  for more guidance around different interleaving methods and how they relate to orders of learning and types of knowledge. In modern research, we understand that over-reliance on retrieval practice without adequate encoding can be problematic. This usually occurs when encoding is done to a low quality, resulting in high rates of knowledge decay (forgetting) and an overwhelming amount of material to learn through tedious repetition. How to avoid over-relying on retrieval -  Step 1: Continuously work on improving your encoding skills (we will teach you these methods in the next stages). Step 2: Ensure you have multiple orders of interleaving (refer to the interleaving table in your learning resources). Step 3: Use lower-order rote-memorisation (direct recall with spaced repetition) as a last resort for information that does not integrate into your knowledge network. Step 4: Always try to connect new information to the big picture and think of why it is important. This helps to integrate it into your knowledge networks.

Note:  This lesson was originally in Base Camp, but has been duplicated and moved into the Supplementary Learning modules by popular demand. You may notice the video references some things that you have not covered yet. Don't worry! It won't affect your ability to learn from this lesson. Those with a fixed mindset see their ability level and intelligence as fixed and unchangeable. To overcome a fixed mindset, we must:    Take action  - one of the easiest ways to change our thoughts, feelings, and beliefs, is through action. it is a misconception that we have to  feel  like taking action first. We can take action despite not feeling motivated to.   Reflect effectively  - use Kolb's to turn mistakes into learning.   Focus on the chain performance  (see below)    One useful model to help with your marginal gains is to identify what determines a desirable outcome. Simply wanting an outcome does not create it. By analysing the chain of performance, we can understand which aspects hold us back the most. Our efforts can become more targeted and effective. What determines outcomes? -  Step 1: Outcomes and performance (which come from...) Step 2: Skills and processes (which come from...) Step 3: Attributes and behaviours (which come from...) Step 4: Mindsets, beliefs, and identities. Examples of skills and processes:   Encoding and retrieval skills  Time management and prioritisation skills  Self-reflection and problem-solving skills  Focus and attention management skills  Emotional self-regulation skills   You can see how deficiencies in any of these skills could cause significant problems with outcomes and performance. Examples of attributes and behaviours:   Open-mindedness  Willingness to experiment  Willingness to face discomfort  Diligence and commitment  Patience  Humility  Self-compassion   When these attributes (or the behaviours naturally associated with these attributes) are absent, it can cause significant challenges to developing skills. For example, imagine trying to develop the skills in this program without patience or open-mindedness! Examples of mindsets, beliefs, and identities:   Fear of failure  Beliefs about intelligence and abilities  Beliefs about talent and capabilities  Identity as a learner  Identity as someone who tries their best  Identity as someone who always gets back up   Mindsets, beliefs, and identities influence our attributes and behaviours. Further reading on fixed and growth mindsets:  Here is a  blog article  by Dr. Justin Sung on how fixed mindsets are instilled and create common problems. To dive straight into fixed and growth mindsets, read this great  summary article  by Maria Popova about Carol Dwecks famous research on children and praise.  You might also like to read  this article  by James Clear, the author of Atomic Habits. He discusses how you can use habits to modify your fixed mindsets.

Attention Management is a system for optimising flow. Rather than prioritising the time of day, it prioritises maintaining flow states for as long as possible, particularly by bridging flow states between tasks.  Attention management can allow for extremely high levels of productivity that can appear superhuman to others. When used alongside refined prioritisation systems, it may be possible to increase your meaningful goal-oriented output by 10x compared to the normal person. Key principles for attention management: 1. Train yourself to have a keen level of self-awareness at where your attention is and if it aligns with your intentions. You must self-regulate your actions to prevent yourself from straying into unintended tasks and activities.2. Maintain flow at all times. Do not worry about when you finish or start a task. Simply operate in deep flow, trusting that this produces the fastest possible output at high quality.3. Remove decisional delays throughout the day. Every decisional delay is a flow-breaker. You want to be as close as possible to a robot following a set of pre-programmed instructions.4. Optimise your environments ahead of time to remove the need for environmental optimisation during the day.5. Utilise work-rest timing with active relaxation and recovery via breathing techniques for as many rests as possible. Note:  You will be forced to break out of flow and attention management when there are scheduled events, such as meetings, appointments, classes, etc.  This is unavoidable, so you may wish to plan small reprioritisation sessions intermittently throughout the day, before and after these mandatory disruptions and events.  How do we measure the success of our attention management?:  In conventional time management, we measure productivity by our ability to achieve planned tasks within a set time period. However, this has its own limitations. What if you are at 60% efficiency and focus? What if you waste 20 minutes procrastinating? What if you get distracted so much that you only get 45 minutes of real work done?   With attention management, we measure success based on our ability to achieve a planned task (e.g."study the 3rd unit of biology to an 80% level of depth) and how long we were able to maintain flow. It prioritises the achievement of the task over the time it took, understanding that our performance during flow states is likely near our maximum pace anyway. When is attention management not appropriate?:  Attention management prioritises the achievement of the task over the time it took, understanding that our performance during flow states is likely near our maximum pace anyway.  This approach is not always the best. For example, when time-boxing tasks, what is more important is that we limit the time we spend on a task rather than the magnitude of output. This is one reason why attention management is an advanced skill that requires significant experience with conventional time and task management principles. How do I schedule for a day with attention management?:  With conventional time management, we may set a task like"study biology" for two hours. If the task takes longer, we may micromanage our time and reschedule other items or use pre-scheduled buffer time. If the task ends earlier, we risk introducing decisional delays in planning what to do with the unexpected extra time.   Of course, when there is not much to do with a relaxed workload, this is no problem. We can simply rest and take time off! However, these small delays can be stressful when under heavy workloads.   With attention management, we create more generic time blocks such as"study". Once we achieve the goal for quantity and depth of learning we wish to achieve, we simply move to the next priority item for studying. If there is no next priority item for studying, we can directly move on to the next task in our plan.  Often, we can use these small pockets to handle"non-urgent and important" tasks or"urgent but not important" tasks - both of which do not require task-level completion, but simply some level of time dedication. Microlearning might also be implemented. What about the power of deadlines and Parkinson's law?:  Parkinsons Law says that any activity or task fills the time given to it. If an essay is due in two weeks, it takes two weeks. If its due in two days, it takes two days. The difference in quality is insignificant. This is why so many of us leave assignments to the last minute  we know we can do it, and the result will be much the same!   Read about    Parkinsons Law on Wikipedia.    Even though we are not keeping to time-based deadlines with attention management, Parkinsons Law can still be applied to our advantage.   To create a Parkinson's Law effect, set an estimated time to complete your task and a timer. Your goal is to finish it in that time. If you finish early, move to the next task. If you finish late, then Parkinsons Law wasnt enough to boost your output anyway.  Note that timing every focus session can also create unnecessary stress and pressure which has a psychological toll. It can be useful when you want to push yourself, but it is not recommended as a regular tactic. Are there any long-term benefits of attention management compared to conventional time management?:  There are some significant benefits to using attention management on a regular basis:   You develop a keener self-awareness of your attention vs. your intentions. This helps to notice procrastination, distractions, and drifting focus earlier, which allows for faster self-correction.  It becomes easier to refocus and centre once distracted because you are constantly practising to bring your attention in alignment with intentions (almost like extended focus training).  It becomes easier to enter flow states of deep focus and concentration that use energy and cognitive resources very efficiently. Recommended task 1Experiment with attention management  Utilise attention management for at least three days and perform at least two Kolb's cycles on the experience.   Complete this task once you have completed your final Kolb's cycle after your third day.   Camp II:  Learn the most advanced interleaved retrieval methods in our program.  **Key Dimensions:**  * Retrieval * Self-Management  **Recommended Pace:**  * Theory: 3 to 4 hours across 2 weeks  **Recommended Practice:**  * Consistent practice for 3 to 4 weeks for optimal results  **Progression Tips:**  * Advanced retrieval techniques, including"WPW," are introduced in this stage. * Retrieval and self-management techniques require a spacing interval, making practice time-consuming. * Mastery of techniques can take months, but progress to the next stage is typically possible after 3 to 4 weeks of diligent practice.

Below are some high-priority recommended tasks that anyone can implement more of in their lives. Not every item in BEDS-M has been covered here. The tasks are focused on  Environment  and  Distractions sheet  because these are two of the most versatile and powerful adjustments.   If you want to add your own tasks to help turn theory into practice, go to your dashboard after finishing this lesson and add a custom task!    Use custom tasks to keep yourself on track with action items you want to put into practice (even if a recommended task isn't given). Recommended task 1Optimise your environment and refine it with a Distraction sheet  Turn your environment into a powerful focus zone.   Write down a list of things that distract you. They could be physical things, digital distractions, or even people.  Write down a list of changes you could make to your environment to remove the distractions or deter/inform people to help you focus.  Make the changes to your environment as best you can.  Next time you have a focused session, note any distractions on your distractions sheet.  Adjust your environment again based on your distractions sheet.   Repeat this process for at least  three sessions. By this point, you should have made some high-yield changes to take control of your focus.   Remember to share your experience and get ideas for how you can optimise your environment in our supportive and helpful community!    Tip:   To take this to another level, attempt to use MVGs next time you feel like procrastinating and write down any barriers you encounter on your distractions sheet. For example, maybe you didn't have all the right tabs open, which caused you to feel like procrastinating.  This way, you can continue refining your environment with positive influences to make your path to focus as frictionless as possible while removing the negative distractors!  Rapid Start:  The fastest way to upgrade your learning is to clear your path of any obstacles. The Rapid Start lessons are designed to do just that!  **Learning Plan**  **Key Dimensions:**  * Self-management * Mindset  **Recommended Pace:**  * Theory: 2-3 hours spread over 1-2 weeks * Practice: 1-2 weeks  **Recommended Practice:**  * Focus on 2-3 tasks at a time. * Complete practice exercises for each task before moving on.  **Progression Notes:**  * Complete lessons to accumulate 2-3 focus tasks. * Practice until tasks are completed. * Move through remaining lessons after completing tasks. * Estimated progression time: 1-2 weeks   This stage will focus on improving your enabler skills. Working on these skills improves your ability to work through the program and accelerates your development speed.

Open our guided planning form  here  and fill it in as you learn about each section in the upcoming lessons. You may return to this stage and use this form for future planning as many times as you like! Key principles: 1. Dont confuse intention setting with goal setting. Goals involve plans.2. SMART goal setting is a good starting point but isnt enough to optimise success.

By now, you realise that there are many ways to chunk. The critical part of learning that drives efficiency is to engage in  evaluate  where the value of those chunks is judged and critiqued. This is where chunking by importance comes into play.   Consider a topic around cellular biology. Read the below sample excerpt on how the topic is presented and think about how you might chunk this topic. Sample textbook excerpt:   Title: Types and Arrangements of Epithelial Cells    Cell Types     Squamous Cells   Description: Flat, scale-like cells.  Function: Efficient diffusion and protection in stratified squamous epithelia (e.g., skin, oral cavity).     Cuboidal Cells   Description: Cube-shaped cells.  Function: Secretion and absorption in simple cuboidal epithelia (e.g., kidney tubules, gland ducts).     Columnar Cells   Description: Tall and slender cells.  Function: Nutrient absorption and secretion in simple columnar epithelia (e.g., digestive tract).      Cell Arrangements     Stratified Epithelium   Description: Multiple layers of cells.  Function: Provides strength and protection, e.g., stratified squamous epithelium in the skin.     Simple Epithelium   Description: Single layer of cells.  Function: Facilitates diffusion, secretion, and absorption. Found in simple cuboidal and simple columnar epithelia.     Pseudostratified Epithelium   Description: Appears stratified but isn't.  Function: Moves mucus and particles in the respiratory tract for protection (e.g., pseudostratified columnar epithelium).      Important Epithelial Cell Linings     Stratified Squamous Epithelium   Function: Provides protection against abrasion and pathogens (e.g., skin, oral cavity).     Simple Cuboidal Epithelium   Function: Regulates urine composition through secretion and absorption (e.g., renal tubules).     Simple Columnar Epithelium   Function: Facilitates nutrient absorption and secretion of digestive enzymes (e.g., digestive tract).     Pseudostratified Columnar Epithelium   Function: Moves mucus and particles to protect the respiratory system (e.g., respiratory tract). You might decide that rather than trying to memorise the functions of each important cell lining, we can chunk them into types vs. arrangements, since the important linings are simply examples of different combinations of types and arrangements.   Expand the accordion below to see the mindmap that is created by following this process and noting the functions that extend from each combination. Initial mindmap attempt: If we instead chunk the information based on why   cell types and arrangement are  important, we can completely rearrange it to form the alternative mind map below. Alternative mindmap: By chunking it differently, we emphasise different relationships. This decision is made based on what makes the topic more memorable, more intuitive, and less overwhelming. For most learners, simpler means better and the first attempt at creating a chunk structure is almost  never  the simplest it can be. Key principle:  Often, the best chunk structure comes from seeing the topic from a different perspective to how it was originally taught. There must be an active effort and willingness to consider other ways of chunking. Almost always, finding a perspective of chunking that you have not found before will feel difficult or even impossible. Try anyway.   As a general rule,    always assume    that there is a missing perspective or simpler way. Make it your job to find it. Yes, it is difficult. Yes, you must still find it!   Those who push themselves to this standard will unlock a rare level of knowledge mastery and learning efficiency that is seen only in the top learners of the world. In this instance, the function came first; however, chunking by function is not always best. For example, in Chemistry, function is not the reason why some functional groups or reactions are important. It could instead be due to them having similar reaction mechanisms or reactivity. In English, importance could be assigned to the authors perspective or elements of their background and worldview.   This is why the question is"Why is it important?" and not What are their shared functions?    Many learners struggle to find the most meaningful and important chunks for a topic. In many cases, the question of"why is it important" was not explored fully and to a sufficient standard.   We can think about this as finding immediate reasons vs. root reasons.    A root cause is the fundamental reason something happens. Similarly, a  root reason  could be considered the fundamental reason for something to be important. This can be illustrated by a story from the famous marketer, Seth Godin, who explains why people are motivated to buy things.    Godin asked why people buy drill bits (the metal things on the end of drills to make holes).    The immediate answer could be that they want to make a hole in the wall. But this is not the  root reason.   People don't want a hole in the wall. They want the screw that will go inside the hole. But this is still not the root. They don't just want the screw; they want the picture that will hang on it. And they don't just want the picture; they want the feeling they will get from looking at the picture on the wall.  In this case, the  root reason  is that people buy drill bits to feel good when they see a picture on the wall.    Similarly, in learning, the    root reason    something is important is sometimes not immediately obvious.   For example, when studying about  genetic diversity  (the variety of genes and genetic characteristics within a population or a species), learners may conclude that genetic diversity is important because it leads to variation.   However, if we ask,"why is variation important?", we can arrive at a root reason that variation is essential for the adaptability, resilience, and long-term survival of species and ecosystems.  Therefore, instead of creating a chunk called"variation", we may create one called"survival" instead. Notice how the second chunk is much broader and can likely encompass many more sub-concepts or sub-chunks. It will probably also be easier to create relationships to and from. The "So what?" approach:  Some people use the"So what?" approach where every time we find a reason for importance, we then ask"So what?" until the question no longer makes sense to ask. At this point, we have presumably found the  root   reason. Recommended task 1Shoot, Shoot!  Complete two Aim and Shoot attempts for two  different  topics followed by a Kolb's after each session.   Remember, at this stage of our skill competence, we're looking to find mistakes that help us ascend into the middle and upper levels of conscious incompetence.   Complete this task once you have finished your second Kolb's.  Ascent II:  Learn the next step of the Bear Hunter System: Shoot, which refines your non-linear note-taking skills even further.  **Key Dimensions:**  * Deep processing * Self-regulation  **Recommended Pace:**  * Theory: 8 hours spread over 3-4 weeks * Practice: 8 weeks  **Progression Tips:**  * Ascent 2 has a high practice-to-theory ratio. * Errors from previous stages may become apparent. * Expect to spend more time in this stage than in previous ones. * Apply techniques to various learning needs. * If you have mastered previous stages, this stage should be an extension of your current methods. * If you do not notice improvements in learning efficiency, consider a skill audit to identify technique errors before progressing.

By now, you should:    Be more comfortable with trying and failing  Understand how you can turn mistakes into learning consistently  Recognise that mistakes are necessary for learning  Recognise how difficult it is to improve when attempts are delayed   In this week's challenge, we will increase the frequency of making mistakes to increase the rate of improvement and growth.   One way we can increase our frequency of mistake-finding is to shorten the cycle time and increase the intensity. By increasing the intensity and pressure while performing, we make mistakes more obvious. Week 3 instructions -  Step 1: Continue with the same challenge as week 2. Step 2: Gradually reduce the time for each attempt, while increasing the intensity of the time spent. For example, instead of one hour, try to spend 45 minutes but achieving the same result or covering the same volume. Step 3: Stop each session as soon as you feel like you have made a mistake. Step 4: Spend 10 to 15 minutes reflecting on the nature of the mistake, formulate a new experiment to overcome it, and repeat the cycle. In this challenge, we aim to improve the efficiency of our cycles by reducing the proportion of time that does not reveal mistakes. Behind the scenes:  When training iCanStudy coaches, we use intensive cycles of 15 minutes to find mistakes in giving feedback to learners. When training speaking skills, our cycles may be as short as 30 seconds. This intensive, frequent cycling has allowed us to reduce our training time by over five-fold.   Some skills naturally take longer to reveal mistakes, so what's important is not the objective number of minutes but the  feeling  of intensity that you subject yourself to.  Start slow and gradually build up over time.

With your high level of BHS mastery, we can start applying BHS with more complicated routines and schedules. Expand the accordions below to explore some examples of how BHS (and Hipshot) can be utilised to gain enormous time efficiency advantages.  At this stage of your technique, you should aim to adjust your schedules and routines to learn with some of the more advanced implementation strategies. Standard BHS implementation:  This standard implementation uses all the normal BHS techniques daily. It is a good starter schedule to get used to replacing normal study with BHS.    Characteristic features of the standard BHS implementation:    Aim always happens during prestudy and covers at least one week's worth of material.  Shoot happens before (if viable), during, and after main learning events  Skin always happens after the main learning event.     Time efficiency:  5/10    Difficulty:  Low   Instructions:    Perform Aim step for one weeks worth of material at the end of the previous week (e.g. on Sunday)  Do a shoot for only the most basic and important concepts/relationships if you have time after the Aim step. If you dont have time, do this in the morning of, or at any time before learning it in your  main learning event.  Continue the Aim-Shoot cycle during the  main learning event, finishing your Shoot step.  If you have time, do the Skin step at the end of the day or, alternatively, at the end of the week for the entire weeks worth.    You can also do a Skin for each days material and continually add to the GRINDEmap with each new days content, then re-Skin the entire week again. Both methods will be effective. Whats more important is that you do what you have time for and can do consistently. Some busier weeks may require you to alternate. Accelerated BHS implementation:  This accelerated implementation is an intermediate schedule to get used to working independently from the slower pace and restrictions of classes and lectures.   Characteristic features of the accelerated BHS implementation:    The Aim always covers an entire topic at a time.  You are working at a different pace and order than your peers.  Aim and Shoot always occur before main learning events.  Skin occurs at the end of the week for an entire topic at once.    Time efficiency:  7/10    Difficulty:  Moderate   Instructions:    Spend one weekend Aiming and Shooting for around 60-70% of the content for the entire next topic you will study for a single subject. This gives you a significant lead and reduces the pressure of trying to"keep up".  Every weekend, do the same process for a different subject, allowing you to stay weeks ahead of the formal curriculum pace for most subjects at all times.  During the week, complete the Shoot for the remaining 30-40% of content before, during and after main learning events, much like how you might in the standard BHS. The goal is to finish both Aim and Shoot for the entire topic for a subject within one week of the formal timing.  Perform Skin at the end of the week.   Attending classes or lectures after you have already self-studied the material is useful as a recheck, re-skin and easy multi-order retrieval session.    Tips:    Use materials or resources at your disposal for self-study, including Google.  Dont worry about covering 100% of the curriculum  you have plenty of chances to pick up things you missed as you will be so far ahead.  Continue a weekly + monthly interleaved retrieval (appropriate to knowledge type and order) to find gaps, maintain retention, and ensure comprehensive mastery  Continue lower-order retrieval methods for layer 4 details throughout. This should not take much time if you have effective encoding methods.  You should still have plenty of time available each week to focus on other subjects as needed for tests, assignments or unexpected difficulties. Ultra-accelerated BHS implementation:  This ultra-accelerated implementation is for students adept at using the standard and accelerated BHS with a high mastery over the core technique. It creates total academic independence from curriculum pacing, creating the most free time and efficiency.   Characteristic features of the ultra-accelerated BHS implementation:    Aim, Shoot and Skin all happen before main learning events using self-study material  The time during curriculum weeks is used for interleaved retrieval and getting further ahead or more skills development.  Entire topics are studied in a single session as the norm.    Time efficiency:  10/10    Difficulty:  High   Instructions:   The instructions are brief because it relies on simply cramming through as much as possible, as fast as possible, without sacrificing the quality of learning or retention. The objective is to be so far ahead of formal curriculum pacing that most pressures and challenges are eradicated.  This combination is essentially impossible for most learners who have not had substantial cognitive training.   On weekends, breaks or other times without classes or lectures, Aim, Shoot and Skin for the entire subject's topic.   Continue to do this for your subjects every weekend, aiming to be at first a few weeks ahead for one subject, then a few weeks ahead for every subject by the next week, then several weeks ahead for all subjects by the week after.   Continue with spaced interleaved retrieval and prioritise gap-finding and perfecting your knowledge mastery for the remaining time.    Tips:    Because your speed of self-study should be much higher than the curriculum pace, you should be able to complete the entire next topic for a subject in approximately one day or even less.  Dont worry about getting 100% coverage. Instead, aim for 90% coverage to 90% retention. You have plenty of time to cover the gaps later.   Attending classes or lectures after you have already self-studied the material is useful as a recheck and easy multi-order retrieval session. Recommended task 1Implement BHS using an accelerated implementation approach  Apply BHS in your learning routine using an accelerated or ultra-accelerated implementation approach. Continue Kolb's regularly as necessary for refinement.   Complete this task after at least one week of experience with this implementation.    Base Camp:  Resume upgrading your self-management skills and combine your advanced encoding methods to create nuanced systems of learning.  **Key Dimensions:**  * Self-management * Deep processing * Mindset  **Recommended Pace:**  * Theory: 5 hours over 4 weeks  **Recommended Practice:**  * 8 to 10 weeks of practice * Most theory covered within the first 4 weeks  **Progression Tips:**  * Base Camp covers a wide range of skills. * Practice time varies depending on individual challenges. * Most learners spend approximately 8 weeks in this stage. * Progress at a comfortable pace to consolidate the system. * The most technical lesson is"Hipshot".

By now, you should:    be more comfortable with exploring relationships  have more experience with tolerating the discomfort and mental effort involved in exploring relationships   In this third week, we will add some pressure to help train deep processing habits by applying a higher standard to our thought processes.    In this challenge, we will start to prioritise and evaluate the significance of the relationships we find. This is a higher level of thinking than simply  identifying  relationships. It is also one strategy that helps to make it less overwhelming, similar to how a maze is easier to navigate when we actively try to map the correct path. Week 3 instructions -  Step 1: Continue as you have for week 2. Step 2: Whenever you identify new relationships and connections, assign a value out of five for how significant this relationship is compared to other relationships. Significance should be determined by it's importance to the wider topic or big picture. Step 3: If you are uncertain about the significance, allow yourself three minutes to look it up, read further, or explore the topic in more depth to gain clarity about the significance. Try your best to assign the most accurate value you can within this time. Step 4: Continue to reflect at the end of every session. This time, additionally take note of how assigning explicit value on the importance of a relationship affects your retention and depth of understanding. Although the instructions are simple, this week may be the most difficult challenge of the month!  You should notice that gaps in your knowledge become clearer and more obvious. Relationships that previously felt straightforward become more uncertain and challenged. You will likely have to read and learn more than before to gain clarity and make a value judgment.   It is generally impossible to make a value judgment with 100% certainty. The time it takes to get this level of clarity may be unrealistic. The objective is not to create a completely certain judgment but to  try  to gain as much certainty before making a judgment as possible. The value of uncertainty:  This extra step will drive your deep processing to a higher level, but it can also increase the uncertainty you feel while studying. This is because you are becoming mindful of more variables you previously ignored (which strengthens your network and helps deepen your understanding and memory).

By now, you should have:   Realised that your quality of learning is affected by the type of thinking (cognitive processes) you engage in.  Experienced higher-order learning, which involves comparing, contrasting and prioritising information instead of just learning it in isolation.  Practised using non-linear notes to represent the maps and schemas formed through higher-order learning.  Experienced how your method of writing notes can help or hinder your ability to engage in higher-order thinking.  Used questioning and inquiry-based learning (via the TLS) to guide your thinking and the types of relationships you explore.  Experienced how asking different questions makes you explore different types of relationships, which changes the types of schemas and maps you form.  Practised forming backbones to your learning, which forces you to prioritise, re-arrange, and re-label groups of information based on what makes the most sense to you.  Experienced how forming backbones can help you see information in a different order and perspective from how its taught or presented in textbooks.  Practised spaced interleaved retrieval for different orders of learning.  Utilised Kolbs experiential cycle to drive improvements.  Experienced how the quality of your reflection and abstraction dramatically affects the usefulness of your experiments.  Started to intuitively see progress through the lens of marginal gains instead of as binary success or failure or big leaps.  Practised using various self-management techniques (time and task management) to maintain optimal and healthy productivity levels. Alert:  If you havent completed all these, we recommend practising your current skillset more before moving on. Taking time to reach  moderate to high conscious competency  in the previous techniques prevents technique errors and cognitive overload. The following are new techniques or systems you will learn in Ascent I. **New Techniques**  * **Aim:**     * Intermediate-level skill for inquiry-based learning.     * Builds on the Traffic Light System (red light) skill.  * **Maintaining Focus:**     * Extension of focus techniques from previous stages. The following are techniques from previous stages that you should continue using.     Technique  Notes      Traffic light system (green light)  While the red light will be replaced by Aim, the green light will not be replaced until later in Ascent II.    Scheduling basics  Continue to refine and habituate your scheduling so it is more personalised for you. Be critical about what is working well and what isnt.    BEDS-M  Lock in the habits of BEDS-M during this stage. We will build on this at Basecamp.    SIR  There is no change to the technique, but you will start noticing that you will need to revise less as your retention increases.    Non-linear note-taking  All notes should always be processed. In Ascent II, we will replace this, but for now, continue to make your chunks clearer and your relationships more obvious.     The following techniques will be replaced and upgraded.     Prior Technique  Replaced with      Traffic light  red light  Bear Hunter System  Aim    Prestudy basics  Bear Hunter System  Aim

Complete the recommended tasks below before moving on to the competence assessment and checklist below. Recommended task 1Complete a Base camp challenge fortnight  Complete a challenge fortnight (two weeks) applying all the methods and techniques you have learned. The methods should include:   Encoding and SIR methods  Time and task management  Focus and attention management  Regular critical reflection   The challenge can be considered successfully complete if you are able to achieve all methods to good effect and with relatively high consistency. Ensure at least 5 Kolb's cycles within this fortnight.   Complete this task once you have completed the challenge fortnight and your final Kolb's cycle.   Base Camp:  Resume upgrading your self-management skills and combine your advanced encoding methods to create nuanced systems of learning.  **Key Dimensions:**  * Self-management * Deep processing * Mindset  **Recommended Pace:**  * Theory: 5 hours over 4 weeks  **Recommended Practice:**  * 8 to 10 weeks of practice * Most theory covered within the first 4 weeks  **Progression Tips:**  * Base Camp covers a wide range of skills. * Practice time varies depending on individual challenges. * Most learners spend approximately 8 weeks in this stage. * Progress at a comfortable pace to consolidate the system. * The most technical lesson is"Hipshot".Recommended task 2Complete a skill audit  Using the checkpoints and meta-checklists so far, complete a skills audit on your current skill level and learning system. Implement any findings as experiments into your next Kolb's cycle.   Complete this task once you have completed your skill audit.   Ascent II:  Learn the next step of the Bear Hunter System: Shoot, which refines your non-linear note-taking skills even further.  **Key Dimensions:**  * Deep processing * Self-regulation  **Recommended Pace:**  * Theory: 8 hours spread over 3-4 weeks * Practice: 8 weeks  **Progression Tips:**  * Ascent 2 has a high practice-to-theory ratio. * Errors from previous stages may become apparent. * Expect to spend more time in this stage than in previous ones. * Apply techniques to various learning needs. * If you have mastered previous stages, this stage should be an extension of your current methods. * Noticeable improvements in learning efficiency should occur. * If not, technique errors are likely. * Consider a skill audit to identify errors before progressing. You should have an extremely high level of competency (at or approaching unconscious competence) for all prior techniques.  You should have significant efficiency gains and a high level of knowledge mastery and retrieval.   **Level of Competency**  **Hipshot Variation:** - Replaced Aim and Shoot with hipshot variation comfortably. - Use hipshot variation with moderate effort.  **Focus Training:** - Perform focus training consistently. - Tolerate feelings of boredom or restlessness during focus training. - Experience increased focus in general. - Use focus training intermittently throughout the day.  **PEER-Peer System:** - Confident in creating new habits. - Manage or eliminate procrastination. - Understand the effectiveness of different parts of the system for various habits and moods.  **Priority 0+1 System + Scheduling:** - Prioritize and schedule accurately and flexibly on a daily basis. - Process has become second nature.  **OFF-Rest Timing:** - Adjust rest timing and rest-activity dynamically to optimize productivity. - Process has become second nature.

By now, you should:    Feel more comfortable with learning from mistakes and even seeking mistakes  Clearly understand the relationship between growth and failure   For many, it will take months before this mindset becomes habitual. Success in this challenge is not a complete change in mindset but exposure to the growth mindset that begins the change journey.  In this week's challenge, we will push our fixed beliefs to the limit by proactively targeting attributes and skills we believe we are bad at. Week 4 instructions -  Step 1: Identify a skill or attribute that you believe you cannot change about yourself (or at least very easily). For example, critical thinking, problem solving, creativity, musicality, social skills, intelligence, laziness, etc. Step 2: Spend a maximum of one hour to learn more about the skill or attribute from any resource. Step 3: With what you have learned, create an experience that challenges you to perform or demonstrate this skill or attribute. Step 4: Using what you have already experienced from week 2 and 3, create an intensive reflective cycle. Identify experiments that can help you identify more barriers or conditions to improve with each cycle. Step 5: Repeat this process to accumulate mistakes and deepen your understanding of the skill/attribute. Step 6: At the end of each day of experimenting, reflect on what you have learned to consolidate your findings. Step 7: At the end of the week, reflect on what you understand now about the skill or attribute and how fixed (or not fixed) it is after undergoing numerous cycles of attempts. A lifelong skill:  The process described in this challenge is a method of upskilling and improving that you can continue for life. When there are no"courses" or"teachers" to tell us how to improve, this process becomes the central"curriculum" that guides our development. It is a method of what is called  self-directed learning.   Many of the methods you will learn in the core program were created by our team or our alumni with this process!

metadata.docName: How do I track marginal gains?
metadata.originalText: Progression note:   In the early phases of learning a new topic, our brain needs a big-picture frame of  relevance. It needs to know why it should care about the new information we're about to consume.  When we discussed  prestudying, we were able to split the cognitive load across multiple focused sessions to build the big picture, give ourselves more relevance, and increase our efficiency.    Learning in layers takes this to the next level by giving our    order control    and    inquiry    process a clearer focus.   By creating focused layers, we engage in what is called  self-regulated    scaffolding, which is when we teach ourselves by deconstructing a topic and reconstructing it in a way that is easier and more manageable for us to understand and develop expertise.   Representing this visually makes it easier to see what learning in layers helps us to achieve.    Knowledge and expertise are inherently structured in layers. For many, the formation and discovery of these layers come after years of learning. While it certainly takes time and dedication for expertise to build, this process can be massively expedited by deliberately targeting our learning on each layer.    Knowledge is often presented in a mix of layers. Details and concepts are interspersed with infrequent hints to logic. Learning in the same order, without  order control, creates cognitive overload as our brain struggles to make the information relevant.   As learners, we must strategically apply our cognitive load to extract and make judgments on the layers.  The contents of each layer are usually not explicitly given to you. We must construct these ourselves using the methods of chunking we have already practised.   Sometimes, the apparent"logic" in our resources is not the best chunk to use after independently evaluating using our importance-based chunking process.    As we have already learned, we should always utilise  order control  to find the most relevant information first. Once we have a foundation of relevant information, we can find the next set of more detailed relevant information. Continuing this process helps us build a comprehensive knowledge structure.  As shown in the graph below, conventional methods of learning take in information that is usually at a high level of detail very early. The relevance of information is fluctuating which makes it difficult for our brain to create a model of knowledge. Therefore, the difficulty of learning is high throughout. This is tiring and inefficient.    Instead, by learning in layers, we can invest our cognitive resources more strategically, as follows:    Spend more effort upfront to identify the logic. This establishes a foundation of relevance.  Work through increasing layers of detail, continuously moving to the next most relevant piece.  Over time, the difficulty of learning reduces as our knowledge network becomes more robust.  Step 1: Anchor the goal:  Identify a meaningful, challenging goal you want to achieve in the medium term (between nine months and three years from now). Step 2: Dissect the goal:  What  knowledge,  attributes,  skills,  processes, or  resources  do I need to achieve this goal?  Think about:   Mental health  Focus and concentration  Time management and prioritisation skills  Studying skills   Ask yourself,"How sure am I?"   This is an opportunity to identify if you truly know what is needed to achieve a goal, or if your ideas are based on unreliable sources or assumptions. Step 3: Evaluate your target level:  For each factor you identified in step 2,  rate  (out of 10) what level you need to reach to achieve your goal. Then, describe what this rating means to you so that it is clear and explicit.   For example:   You may need 7/10 time management and 8/10 studying skills to reach your goal of scoring in the top 5% of your cohort at the end of the year. Then, describe what 7/10 time management and 8/10 studying skills mean to you. Step 4: Evaluate your current level:  For each factor,  rate  (out of 10) your current level and justify your rating. We can often overestimate or underestimate our abilities, so justify your rating as objectively as possible. Step 5: Identify the 1%:  How could you go from where you are now to becoming 1% closer to your target level.   Tips:    Do not try to improve on more than 2 skills or attributes at the same time. This causes overload and will hinder your ability to improve.  Marginal gains usually progress from  knowledge  to  actions  to  behaviours, habits,  and  positions.   In the early stages, you first need to acquire  knowledge  on what action you need to take to improve your skills, processes, or attributes.   Next, you must take  action  to commence the process of improvement.   Over time, your actions change your  behaviours and habits  or your  position. Your position is how much control you have over achieving your goal. When you are in a better position, you can reduce risks more.      When you review your marginal gains again in the future, you often only need to repeat these final two steps (Step 4: Evaluate your current level and Step 5: Identify the 1%) as the first three steps will be similar. Key principle: Often, the early stages of progress (or"gain") are confusing because there are so many things that must be worked on. Over time, as we work on each part, one step at a time, it becomes easier and clearer to see what our next best step is. In the beginning, the best step is sometimes just to take a step at all!   Therefore, since the focus is on a    marginal    gain, you do not need a comprehensive list of every knowledge, attribute, skill, process or resource, but simply the ones that seem clearly necessary as the next step.    Below is an example of marginal gains for a learner. Anchoring the goal:  I want to achieve at least 90% in my end-of-year exam. Dissect the goal:  To achieve this, I need:   Adequate mental health to stay focused, energised, and cognitively effective. To achieve this, I need:  More knowledge about how to maintain mental health  More self-awareness about my own mental health and what causes it to fluctuate  Techniques to improve my mental health  Practice and experience using these techniques to manage my mental health    Good focus and concentration, so I can study effectively and not waste time. To achieve this, I need:  Fewer distractions  To achieve this, I need to understand what my distractions are and processes/skills to reduce distractions    Ability to enter into deep flow more easily  I need to know what deep flow is and how to trigger it  I need techniques and processes to help me do this  I need practice and experience using these processes      Excellent time management and prioritisation to stay on top of my workload and tasks to cover the high content volume before the exam. To achieve this, I need:  Better time management methods. This means:  More flexible and sustainable  More consistent  To achieve these, I need more knowledge on how to have better time management and effective methods I can use      Better task prioritisation so I spend my time wisely. To achieve this, I need:  Knowledge of how to prioritise  Self-awareness of mistakes I make with prioritising  Tools and processes to prioritise effectively  Practice and experience with these processes      Excellent learning skills. To achieve this, I need:  High-quality encoding skills to learn deeply and reduce how much I forget. To achieve this, I need:  Knowledge of what makes good encoding  Techniques to help me encode at the level I need to  Practice and experience to use these techniques proficiently    High-quality retrieval practice to find gaps and maintain my memory and performance. To achieve this, I need:  Knowledge of what makes good retrieval practice  Techniques to help me retrieve adequately  Practice and experience to use these techniques proficiently       To achieve any of the above, I also need:   Sufficient feedback on my techniques to know that I am doing it accurately  Frequent reflection and self-feedback to identify mistakes and correct them  A growth mindset to experiment and make mistakes without being paralysed by uncertainty and asking"what if" questions that stop me from just trying things. Evaluating target level:  Mental health: 7/10 - I need it to be good enough to keep me optimum and performing well but it does not have to be good enough that I am invulnerable to all stress.  Focus and concentration: 8/10 - I have lots to do and limited time. I must use my available time effectively and produce meaningful work in blocks I have set out. I do not need to be so perfect that I am able to focus and concentrate in any setting, since I will mostly be studying in controlled environments.  Time and task management: 7/10 - I do not have a very busy schedule and I don't have many other commitments. My time and task management needs to be good enough that I spend my time working on the things that will help me achieve my goal, but I have enough room for error that it does not have to be perfect.  Learning skills: 9/10 - This exam is very difficult and competitive. To achieve at least 90%, my learning skills will need to be very good, with deep mastery of all the core parts of my system.  Feedback: 8/10 - I will need lots of feedback to improve my techniques as I suspect I will have many difficulties improving, based on my past experience.  Self-reflection: 8/10 - I have never tried to work on these skills actively before and I know that I struggle with self-reflection, including tasks like identifying marginal gains. To improve quickly, I must be able to identify problems and self-correct very quickly to improve as much as I need to, even when external feedback is slower.  Growth mindset: 8/10 - I have some fixed mindset tendencies, and to improve, I must experiment actively and try new techniques even when I am unsure how to do it correctly. This makes me feel uncertain, but I must be able to do this more comfortably to improve as quickly as I need. Evaluating current level:  Mental health: 4/10 - I can handle some stress but I feel like I get overwhelmed easily. I have some knowledge and processes to help manage mental health, but not much.  Focus and concentration: 3/10 - I get distracted and procrastinate very easily. Sometimes I am able to have deep focused work, but it is inconsistent and unreliable. I do not have much knowledge and experience with managing my focus effectively.  Time and task management: 6/10 - I mostly do what I need to do, except I sometimes find it difficult to complete all my tasks and adapt my schedule when things don't go to plan. I have some knowledge on what I can do but have not practised using any techniques.  Learning skills: 5/10 - My academic performance is highly variable, and I average 70% on tests. I sometimes do very well (90%+) but have never felt confident to get a high mark. I spend lots of time studying, but this doesn't translate into predictable results very often. I have some knowledge of what I can do, and I have learned some techniques, but I have not practised them much, and I am unsure what to start with.   Feedback: 2/10 - I rarely ask for feedback and do not feel comfortable asking for feedback. I know where and how to ask for feedback but I have very little experience with engaging in it.  Self-reflection: 2/10 - I struggle with self-reflection and feel confused by the process. I do not know how to start.  Growth mindset: 3/10 - I feel uncertain about trying new things and tend to ask lots of questions before giving things a go to gain more certainty. As a result, I delay experimentation for days (or even weeks) until I feel certain enough. I know what it means to have a growth mindset, but I do not know what I can do to change this, and I have limited experience in trying to develop a growth mindset. Finding the 1%:   In this example below, the rationale for how the 1% was identified is explained in italics for your education. You are not expected to write down the parts in italics in your own marginal gains tracking.   Mental health: Learn about what affects mental health and some processes to improve it.    Rationale: In Step 4, we wrote,"I have some knowledge and processes to help manage mental health, but not much". Therefore, the next step should be to improve knowledge and gain more clarity.    Focus and concentration: Learn about what affects focus and some processes to improve it.    Rationale: In Step 4, we wrote,"I do not have much knowledge and experience with managing my focus effectively". Therefore, the next step should be to improve knowledge and gain more clarity.    Time and task management: Practise and apply some techniques I know for improving time management and see how it goes.    Rationale: In Step 4, we wrote,"I have some knowledge on what I can do but have not practised using any techniques". Therefore, the next step should be to apply what we have learned to get more information on barriers, mistakes, or successes.    Learning skills: Apply one or two of the learning techniques I know of and see how it goes.    Rationale: In Step 4, we wrote,"I have some knowledge of what I can do, and I have learned some techniques, but I have not practised them much, and I am unsure what to start with". Therefore, the next step should be to apply what we have learned to get more information on barriers, mistakes, or successes.    Feedback: Ask for feedback on my attempted work at least once.    Rationale: In Step 4, we wrote,"I know where and how to ask for feedback, but I have very little experience with engaging in it". Therefore, the next step should be to gain some experience to get more information on barriers, mistakes, or successes.    Self-reflection: Engage in 20 minutes of self-reflection every Wednesday and Saturday by reviewing step 4 and step 5 of my marginal gains tracking.    Rationale: In Step 4, we wrote,"I... feel confused by the process. I do not know how to start.". Therefore, the next step should be to improve self-awareness and familiarity with the general process of self-reflection. Once there is general familiarity, we can work on improving quality. Ironically,"I do not know how to start" is often a sign that we need to just start with our best attempt and improve after an initial attempt instead of simply thinking about how to start, which can be an endless and futile process.    Growth mindset: Try to use a technique I am not sure about and see how it goes. Find some mistakes and barriers that I did not know about now (as per conscious competence model).    Rationale: In Step 4, we identified that we delay experimentation and attempt to seek certainty instead. This is a sign of a fixed mindset because it is not possible to gain certainty on a new technique before making some mistakes and attempting it first. Therefore, the best next step is to just attempt something new and identify mistakes, helping us move along the journey of unconscious incompetence.     There are currently many marginal gains I can pursue at this point. I am likely to become overwhelmed if I attempt more than three simultaneously. Therefore, I will pursue the gains around focus and concentration, growth mindset, and learning skills first because these seem to be the most important or limiting for me currently. I can pursue other gains later if I evaluate them to become of a greater priority. General rule:   Often, the early stages of progress (or"gain") are confusing because there are so many things that must be worked on. Over time, as we work on each part, one step at a time, it becomes easier and clearer to see what our next best step is. In the beginning, the best step is sometimes just to take a step at all!   Therefore, since the focus is on a    marginal    gain, you do not need a comprehensive list of every knowledge, attribute, skill, process or resource, but simply the ones that seem clearly necessary as the next step.    Below is an example of marginal gains for a learner.

Instructions -  Step 1: Look at your goals and identify your top priority. You can use reverse goal setting principles to identify this. Step 2: Block out time to accomplish your top priority. Step 3: Fill the rest of your timetable according to your lower priorities. Step 4: In the case of unforeseen events, shuffle lower-priority items to protect the highest priority tasks. To determine your priorities, use the  Eisenhower Matrix, which categorises tasks by importance and urgency.   We recommend prioritising and scheduling your tasks  the day before. On the day, keep a list of tasks that pop up and throw them into the Eisenhower matrix to prioritise and schedule for the next day. Key principles for prioritisation: 1. Decide if tasks are important or not based on the consequence of failure; not an intuitive feeling of importance which is often based on urgency.2. Tasks with a low consequence of failure (that are acceptable and manageable) are not important, even if it"feels" important.3. Avoid scheduling urgent tasks first as this can lead to urgency trapping where time is constantly spent on urgent tasks and your future self is never more equipped to handle them or remove workload at the root cause. Priority 0 refers to your  values and goals. We recommend stepping back and reassessing these  once a month. Regularly reassessing your Priority 0 is important because it ensures that your Priority 1 tasks are aligned with your goals. It doesnt take long and will save you a lot of time by keeping you on the right track. What are some examples of urgency trapping?:  A common example of urgency trapping among students is filling all their time with urgent homework and never having time to work on study skills. If academic challenges increase faster than your skills, the gap between skills and challenges widens. This means students falling into this trap can eventually fail to reach academic goals as by the time it's felt as urgent, it is already too late.  This doesnt mean you must schedule urgent tasks later in the day. It simply means you should schedule important and non-urgent tasks into your day  first  and fit in the urgent tasks around them.   For example,  you could schedule 20 minutes every evening for meditation, which is important but non-urgent. You can then fill the afternoon with other tasks. This way, your urgent tasks will not preclude your important task (meditation). How do you identify Priority 0?:  Priority 0 comes to core values and long-term vision. Values are your fundamental beliefs that dictate how you behave and act. Long-term visions are open and abstract ideas of the type of person we want to be and the type of life we want to live. What is the difference between a long-term vision and a long-term goal?:  Your long-term vision is  not  a SMART goal. Setting goals that are too long-term can create tunnel vision. Over time, our personality and priorities are guaranteed to change. While some things remain with you for your entire life, its naïve to believe that we know enough about ourselves now to confidently think that it will not change for the next 10 years.   This is especially true before the age of 30 in modern society when you are transitioning into adulthood and experiencing many things for the first time. These new experiences are some of the most transformative moments that shape who you will be for the rest of your life. Instead of setting goals that are very specific, we can instead set visions.   Visions are a more open and abstract idea of the type of person we want to be and the type of life we want to live. This ensures that we have a broad checklist that we can use to calibrate our medium-term goals, making sure we are always heading in the right direction but not being so narrow that we close off other opportunities.   For example:   A long-term vision might be to have financial freedom, working at an intellectually stimulating job but with enough free time to explore multiple hobbies and interests. We want to be the type of person who is patient, calm, confident and capable of adapting and overcoming challenges enjoyably and easily.   This means that medium-term goals that do not take us closer to this long-term vision are unlikely to be good decisions. For example, getting tutoring to help us get better grades does not give us the skills to overcome challenges independently, nor does it increase our time management to give us the skills to have enough free time for hobbies and interests. How often does Priority 0 change?:   Always evaluate your long-term vision open-mindedly. It can be tempting and comfortable to believe that our long-term vision never changes, but this is unlikely.    We are a living, dynamic organism that constantly takes in information about the world and ourselves. We are always learning more about our thoughts, emotions, values, and experiences, shaping our personality and priorities. It is normal for your long-term vision to change, often in unexpected and sometimes radically different ways. Instead of going into this exercise assuming that your thoughts are unchanged, deliberately look for reasons, thoughts or emotions that indicate that what you want long-term  has  changed.   This helps us overcome the uncontrollable biases that can blind us from seeing our thoughts objectively. You can read more about cognitive biases  here. You can evaluate your own cognitive biases  here.   We recommend reviewing your Priority 0 every 6 months. How often does Priority 1 change?:  This priority 1 step should be repeated once every day or week, depending on how variable your schedule is. Busier people with more things going on in their lives will need to do this daily. How can I be confident to delete tasks?:  If the task is truly important, it will return to your list. Unless you have ample free time and a willingness to spend it on these tasks instead of relaxation, sleep, or more non-urgent, important tasks, it is not advisable to spend time on them. Recommended task 1Apply Priority 0+1 to your prioritisation routine  Apply the Priority 0+1 to your prioritisation routine for the next five days. You can continue working through the program during this time if you like. Perform at least two Kolb's cycles on your experience.   Complete this task after five days of utilising Priority 0+1 and finishing your second Kolb's.   Base Camp:  Resume upgrading your self-management skills and combine your advanced encoding methods to create nuanced systems of learning.  **Key Dimensions:**  * Self-management * Deep processing * Mindset  **Recommended Pace:**  * Theory: 5 hours over 4 weeks  **Recommended Practice:**  * 8 to 10 weeks of practice * Most theory covered within the first 4 weeks  **Progression Tips:**  * Base Camp covers a wide range of skills. * Practice time varies depending on individual challenges. * Most learners spend approximately 8 weeks in this stage. * Progress at a comfortable pace to consolidate the system. * The most technical lesson is"Hipshot."

metadata.docName: How do I increase dimensions or skills?
metadata.originalText: After your diagnostic assessment, you will have your dimensions initially scored. This is a snapshot of your current processes and abilities. This will change over time as you train.   Skills refer to your ability to use techniques and execute them masterfully to achieve a desired effect. Your ability to execute certain skills depends on your dimensions. On the other hand, dimensions are more like attributes that reflect your habits, processes, and tendencies. Dimensions are not fixed.  For example,  non-linear note-taking  is an important skill for efficient learning. The dimensions of  deep processing  and  self-regulation  are necessary to perform this skill accurately. At higher levels of these dimensions, your ability to execute these skills also increases. In addition,  mindset  must be sufficiently high enough to learn the skills in the first place.   We recommend repeating your diagnostic assessment every  two to three  months. You will have the option to redo your diagnostic assessment after one month. Completing your assessment will update your scores with the latest calculation.   Most lessons in the program are marked with the dimensions they improve. Learning new skills and creating new habits improves your processes, which directly enhances your dimensions. Some dimensions take longer to improve than others. For example,  deep processing  and  mindset  require substantial cognitive retraining, which naturally takes longer than a dimension like  retrieval,  which is much more technique-focused.   We recommend you focus your attention and efforts on each lesson to learn the respective skills. This should naturally improve your dimension scores over time.

**Level of Competency**  **Techniques**  **Unconscious Competence** - SIR (Spaced Retrieval, Interleaving Methods)  **Substantial Comfort** - Non-linear note-taking  **Consistent and Easy** - Focus  **Consistent and High-Quality** - Bear Hunter System  Aim (Concept Lists for Question Creation)  **Clear Representation of Chunks and Relationships** - Traffic Light System  Green light (Aim Questions in Processed Notes) Recommended task 1Complete a challenge week and skill audit.  Use all of the methods you have learned so far for at least one week, with at least three Kolb's sessions. Once you have done this, complete a skill audit using the checklist items and meta-checklists available.   Complete this task once you have done your skill audit.   Ascent I:  Upgrade your encoding with the Aim step. The first part of our 3-step hyper-efficient encoding system.  **Learning Stage: Deep Processing and Self-Regulation**  **Recommended Duration:**  * Theory: 8-10 hours over 4-6 weeks * Practice: 4-6 weeks  **Recommended Practice:**  * Focus on deep processing and self-regulation techniques.  **Progression Tips:**  * Practice more than theory compared to previous stages. * Limit focus tasks to 2 at a time. * Avoid progressing too quickly through lessons. * Each lesson introduces new conditions and nuances to consider. * Regular and diligent Kolb's learning cycle is essential for smooth progression.

By now, you should have:    started to understand how our existing systems of learning can be improved  identified methods that can be removed or adjusted to improve our overall effectiveness  started forming deeper metacognition about learning methods, in general.   In this week's challenge, we will take the insights we've gathered over the past few weeks and start forming a coherent, deliberate learning system. Week 4 instructions -  Step 1: Continue with the instructions from week 2 and week 3. Remember, this is a continuous cycle of refinement. Step 2: Design a learning system that incorporates the more effective methods (including any adjustments you've made) in a logical sequence. You may wish to create a daily schedule so it is easy to follow. Step 3: Aim to create a system that differs from your current methods by no more than 20%. Creating too many changes too abruptly is likely to result in overload and unnecessarily high error rates. Step 4: Utilise the new system for the next week (or more), making minor adjustments to help your new system work smoothly. Making more changes:  If your new system is not as effective or comprehensive as you hypothesised, start again from step 2 and redesign it. If your new system consistently fails to be more effective than your existing one, you may be attempting to make too many changes simultaneously. This introduces excessive variables we lack insight into, increasing the failure rate. The purpose of this final week is to consolidate our insights from experimentation over the past few weeks. If we do not consolidate our findings, we can end up with a long list of adjustments and changes that are so overwhelming to think about that our overall productivity and efficiency plummets!   After gaining new insights about our learning, we always want to:    Stabilise it in a new system  Gain familiarity with the new methods  Make minor adjustments to help the system    Once we have a couple of weeks of experience with this new system, we can repeat the cycle by mapping, adjusting, and experimenting like in week 2. Core program:  Throughout the core program, you will learn many new methods that you will need to integrate into your system. Some learners with low levels of self-regulation struggle to upgrade their learning systems, becoming overwhelmed more easily.    The experiences you have gained from completing this challenge will be invaluable in helping you overcome this difficulty to construct a personalised system efficiently.

When we learn by creating problems and then answering them, it changes what information we consume and how we think about things. This process of seeking an answer and being curious, followed by satiating that curiosity, is called   inquiry-based learning.   When we learn by solving problems, it also gives us more information to ask  even more  questions. This ongoing cycle results in a deeper, more applicable understanding and a higher retention rate.   People often struggle to apply the knowledge theyve learned. The real issue is that people usually do not try to apply information immediately after they've learned it.   Thats like running down a road because someone told you to, only to realise an hour later that you dont know where youre going or why youre running! Key principle:  Studying only produces learning when the information you consume has a purpose. Problems can help create purpose. Conventional learning is unfocused and causes you to learn too much information at once, making it harder to process. This causes you to forget the information quickly, so you will waste a lot of time going back and relearning it. In addition, your brain doesnt like the unstructured nature of conventional learning, so you will feel the information is tedious and unenjoyable.   Its like throwing a bunch of items into a room, then realising you dont know where anything is, so taking it all out and organising it again. The better approach would have been to organise it first!     To start implementing inquiry-based learning, you can train your brain using the traffic light system (TLS). TLS is divided into  red light  vs.  green light  phases of learning.   Red light  forces us to think of questions and directs our focus. It helps break bad habits of passive learning or just reading and consuming information without a clear big-picture purpose.   Green light  is when we satiate our curiosity by answering those questions. This is when we read, listen, and search through content to find relationships, write our notes, and think about how everything is connected.   When you first start studying, create a list of keywords and concepts. It doesn't have to be comprehensive but try to cover the main ideas of the topic. Questions to ask during red light - Once you have a list of keywords, ask yourself the following questions... Step 1: Which keywords feel easier or more relevant to me immediately? (Start with these keywords) Step 2: What am I curious about? Step 3: Why do I need to know this? Step 4:  What problems does it help me solve or how can I apply this? As you ask yourself these questions, you may think of other questions to ask, especially relating to what you are curious about. Make a note of these questions for the green light step coming next.   By the end of red light, you should have a list of questions to answer.    Go and answer those questions to learn the topic in an organic way. Dont   read everything and try to memorise all the information. Instead, focus on naturally answering the questions you have asked. Tips for green light - While answering your questions, follow these principles... Step 1: Think of multiple answers for each question. The point is not to find one perfect answer but to use the question as a springboard to find multiple relationships. Step 2: As you explore each keyword, you will find many relationships between keywords. Try to express these in your notes. It will be messy at first! Step 3: After every 2 or 3 keywords, look at your growing map and try to simplify it and make it cleaner. Make judgments on what is important and worth keeping. Step 4: Continually revise your overall structure. Your map won't be"concrete" until much later. Everything you map now is just to help you think and organise your thoughts. Green light finishes once you have answered all your questions from red light. Go back to  red light  and continue this cycle until you feel like you have learned most of the topic.    Some information may still be so detailed and specific that you struggle to answer the related questions. These can go on your flashcards or other lower-order rote-memorisation method for now. Keep your note-taking non-linear!:  During TLS, you'll find that you discover lots of new relationships! Make sure to use good non-linear note-taking to help your brain think about and explore all these different relationships. Trying to figure it all out in your head will be overload!   If you're still using the collecting and processing technique, now is a good time to start moving towards fully non-linear"mindmap" style note-taking.  Which keywords should I start with?:  You can start from any keyword that you feel is an important idea. However, with correct inquiry, you should be able to start from any keyword and identify which words are truly the most important.   The goal of inquiry is to compare and contrast ideas and see how concepts influence and are related. We then group words based on this information. Remember not to let your groups be dictated by how it was presented to you or your preconceived (potentially suboptimal) ideas about how the information should be organised.    To be an effective learner, we must arrive at our own critically examined conclusions about how to organise the information.

Ensure that the gaps between your revision sessions are getting longer. Do at least one or two retrieval practice sessions in the first 10 days and continuously expand the gaps for all subsequent sessions.   It is inefficient to always practice under the same conditions. The brain learns much better by practising with slight variations each time.   Actively look for gaps. To do this, apply the Aim step again to the study material and your GRINDE map. You will see things in your GRINDE map that you dont know well and can deliberately ask questions to explore your gaps in a more targeted way. Note:  This sublesson is a recap. You should already be applying all of this so far! If not, this is your cue to re-evaluate your system. If there are gaps in the basics of your revision strategy, the following lessons will become much more challenged than necessary.

This overview of our course curriculum is designed to imitate the  imperfect  style of teaching you may receive in university:   Lists of resources are provided for you  Resources are often lengthy, complex at times, and difficult to navigate  There is overlapping material between the resources  A single, long-form video lesson is the core of the lecture When to view this lesson:  While you may view this lesson whenever you wish, you have a natural opportunity to review this at the end of Fundamentals 2 (High yield phase) as this lesson is used for the checkpoint. Understand the relationship between practice methodology and skills development  Appreciate the complexity of learning  Draw the basic conceptualisation of learning from information to retrieval  Describe the considerations and principles that influence each stage of the learning process from information to retrieval    While the lecture objectives are related to the lesson's content, these  meta objectives  are for developing your self-regulated learning skills.   Navigate and consolidate multiple resources effectively, such as videos, articles, and text summaries, to synthesise knowledge and develop strong conceptual models  Use efficient note-taking practices to facilitate the development of conceptual models  Read and listen critically to identify pertinent relationships and grouping opportunities that assist in the development of conceptual models Imperfect teaching:  The information that is provided to you is deliberately fragmented. No single resource contains all of the necessary information, and while the way information has been grouped for you is logical, it is not likely to be cognitively ideal for your learning. This requires you to form groups and pull relevant information from multiple sources independently.   Throughout the program, we will refer to this content and demonstrate how the skills you have learned can be used to navigate these sorts of imperfect resources that are common in real educational environments. Expand the accordions below to view extra resources to assist you. Keywords and concepts list:  As you will learn throughout the program, creating a list of keywords and terminology is a good starting point for many techniques.
