The  action  of collecting keywords does not produce much cognitive benefit. The benefit comes from what we can do with the list of keywords and how starting with a list changes the frame in which we perceive the content.   For example,  seeing keywords in a table of contents or under chapter headings in a textbook influences us to group them similarly, even if there are simpler or more personally intuitive alternatives. This framing bias often prevents us from making the groups and relationships best suited for our brains.   As the literal action of scanning for and listing keywords generates minimal learning benefits, we have completed this step for you to save time. You can download the list of keywords from the video lecture below.   Download the concepts list PDF  here. Summary document:  Lectures often come with some brief text to guide students through the lesson. Sometimes, these are useful for getting some basic ideas and relationships, while other times they are too brief to be of much use. We have prepared a brief document that summarises some of the main points of the video. How will you choose to utilise this resource in your studying process?  Download the summary document PDF  here. Technical reading:  Lectures are typically accompanied by textbooks or articles that are full of dense information. Not all of the information is always relevant, so it is up to the learner to evaluate the material and extract value from it strategically. In some cases, information that initially may not seem relevant is actually useful for helping to understand the core parts of the lesson.   Use the following Report on Learning like you would use a textbook chapter.   Download the report on learning  here. Search engines or AI:  In most cases, you will also have to use search engines to independently look up information that is relevant to what you are learning. It can often be faster and more efficient to find the answers to your questions by searching for them directly than to find the answer in a dense textbook.   However, as you will learn in the program, the questions you ask are crucial for steering your learning and discovery in the right direction.

Before we submit our form, we should make some fine-tuned adjustments to the schedule and timing using the tools below.   Once you have finished making these adjustments and created a schedule in your calendar, submit your form to save it. You can access it from your Toolkit in the menu at any time.   Submitting the form will also unlock the next stage.    We previously introduced the idea of the  theory-practice cycle.   We'll now explore this in more depth so that you can adjust your plan to ensure the maximum chance of success in learning new methods. As you can see in the diagram below, most of your learning will happen offline, as you practise theory that you learn online via tasks and techniques.    Importantly, most of your"practice time" will be time you are  already spending  learning. By replacing our current methods with new ones, we can fit lots of practice without spending much"extra" time. **Correct Practice:**  * Maintain the same study hours as before. * Replace existing study techniques with new ones learned from the program.  **Incorrect Practice:**  * Continue using current study techniques for the same amount of time. * Dedicate additional time to practicing new techniques.  Depending on how much time you commit to learning and studying, you will need to adjust the amount of time you have for theory. Key principle:   To prevent overload, never exceed a5:1 ratio of practice to theory.   For example, if you spend 25 hours studying per week, then you should spend at most 5 hours on learning theory. Even this may be excessive. Learners have been able to achieve remarkable progress with as high as a 40:1 ratio of practice to theory time. You may wish to read our article on  how long to spend on each lesson or stage  for further guidance.   Once you enter the growth phase, your progress will slow down. This is because we are entering into a progressive sequence of cognitive retraining.   This process cannot be rushed and requires rock-solid foundations. Our method of retraining is a world-first to maximise your memory and fundamentally change how you process information! Recommended task 1Complete your first 30-day plan  The 30-day plan is a resource you can use as many times as you want. Make sure to hit the first 30 days of your learning journey the right way by completing your plan and saving it.   Complete this task once you have saved your plan and added it to your calendar (like we taught you in the Rapid Start modules).    Remember to keep your spaced interleaved retrieval sessions in your schedule as well!   30-Day Plan:  Hit the next 30 days running! In this stage, we'll walk you through a method for planning that optimises your chances of success.  **Self-Management**  **Recommended Pace:** * This stage is a guided planning framework. * You may complete this stage as quickly as you would like.  **Recommended Practice:** * Not applicable  **Progression Tips:** * This stage is designed for repeated use. * Learners who make deliberate plans using this framework:     * Face fewer obstacles     * Improve their skills more easily and smoothly As soon as you have completed this stage, you may move to the next one. There is no minimum recommended practice requirement other than following the instructions for planning.

One of the first things to learn in many sports where there is a risk of falling, and injury is to fall safely. Whether it's  Judo  or  snowboarding, learning to fall is considered one of the most crucial beginner skills.  Unfortunately, we rarely learn to"fall safely" in learning and life.  When we fall safely, mistakes and failures do not impact us or leave any lasting consequences.  This week's challenge will teach you how to reduce the consequences of making mistakes. Over time, this will help to reduce the fear and anxiety of experimentation and failure.   In this challenge, we will observe how the impact of errors can be reduced by:   Reducing the risk of errors by breaking down larger tasks into smaller steps  Reducing the magnitude of consequences by acting on these steps sooner   The risk of mistakes and failure is most significant when there is a high  chance  of failure and the consequence is  significant. Often, this only occurs when we try to tackle something in one big attempt, or we have delayed the attempt until there is no time for recovery. Week 1 instructions -  Step 1: Choose a skill or task that you know you are not very good at (high chance of making many errors). Step 2: Break down this task into smaller and smaller steps or components until you feel that the chance of error for each individual step is very low. If you can't break down the task in this way, choose a different task! Step 3: Experiment with doing more of these steps in a single go and observe how it affects your chance of making an error. At what point do errors occur? Is it earlier or later than you expected? Step 4: Reflect on the consequence of those errors. Are they smaller or greater than you expected? Step 5: If you felt this was easy, choose another skill where the risk of error feels more significant and repeat from step 2. Speed is your friend:  After we have broken down something into smaller steps, the consequences of failure can increase when we wait too long. For example, the consequences of how we learn tend to increase as we get closer to an exam. Or if we put off learning to learn for years, the stakes may be higher when we are eventually forced to deal with it!   Once you complete step 2, move swiftly into step 3 for experimentation to observe your error rates. You may wish to repeat week 1 more than once until you feel comfortable with these steps. How do I reflect? How do I know if I have made an error with a technique or task?:  An error can be defined in multiple ways:   1. When the activity has not produced the desired result.    For example, if you have attempted a mindmap, but it does not help improve your memory or depth of understanding, it has failed to achieve the primary outcome it was intended to achieve. Therefore we can say that some error was made.   This kind of error is most useful to detect when we are just starting a new method or technique. We often don't know enough about it to identify more specific errors. Instead, we can start by broadly identifying that  something  isn't working. In later attempts, we can gradually begin to unravel what this  something  is.   2. When we are unable to follow a set of procedures.    For example, a step in mindmapping may be to create arrows between concepts to show relationships. We may attempt this and find that our old habits prevented us from creating many arrows, and instead, we ended up writing relatively linear blocks of notes with only a few arrows. This indicates an error in following the procedure as instructed.  This kind of error is most useful when there are specific step-by-step instructions that may be difficult to follow. This often comes after the first type of error has been identified in an earlier attempt.   3.   When we get external feedback.   Getting feedback from someone more experienced and knowledgeable is a valuable source of error identification. This kind of error is most useful when we are unsure what kind of error is being made and our self-feedback does not provide enough insight for another attempt or experiment.   External feedback should not be relied on as a  replacement  for self-feedback, as the ability to give feedback on our own work is a crucial skill for self-regulated learning. Self-feedback should be the dominant form of spotting errors, with external feedback providing clarity and calibration. What skill should I use for this challenge?:  You can pick any skill that you feel a lack of confidence in. Since you are in the iCanStudy program, you may want to choose a skill related to learning. If you are completing any of the other 30-day challenges at the same time, you can use the challenge itself as a"skill" you are trying to develop.   The key is that you should feel:    Uncertain about how to do the skill correctly  Unconfident about how to improve   As long as the process meets these two conditions, it will be a good fit for this challenge.   Alternatively, you may also use a basic learning technique, such as non-linear note-taking, as taught in  this video. I feel uncertain about this challenge. I'm not sure how to do it correctly. What should I do?:  There is a high chance that this feeling is a symptom of a fixed mindset. One of the most common symptoms is uncertainty and even insecurity when faced with a task where success is not guaranteed. The feeling of"what if I do it wrong?" manifests as"I need more information and I need to ask more questions (so I don't do it wrong)".   Use this as an opportunity for self-reflection to start retraining your brain. It's easier than you think!     Ask  yourself why you want to ask questions and need more information.   Assess  if the reason is because you want to make sure you do it right.   Spot  unproductive thought patterns such as asking lots of"what if" questions   Challenge  your thoughts by asking,"So what if I don't do it right?"   Retrain  your brain by acknowledging that you're worried because you care, but the best course of action is to give it a go first and ask questions later   Reassure  your brain by reminding it that there is no consequence to making mistakes and that this is a safe environment to make them   Take action  by giving it a go!   The purpose of this 30-day challenge is to give you experiences with controlled mistakes in a safe, consequence-free setting. We then build your skills to take these mistakes and turn them into valuable, unique learning opportunities.   There is no consequence to"failing" this challenge.  The challenges themselves are also completely optional. As long as you try to give this challenge a go, you will receive some benefit!   Remember, your win condition is to try, fail, and try again. This behaviour    itself    is the target.
In this lesson, we use the GRINDE checklist to improve this example mind map.

This guide is a comprehensive resource for implementing interleaving in your learning system. At the top, you will find examples of interleaving techniques for you to use and adapt. At the bottom, there are more notes on general guidelines and frequently asked questions.   These techniques offer so little benefit that they should not be used in any circumstances. There are always better alternatives for the same amount of time. **Declarative-Focused Learning Techniques**  * Passive re-reading and re-writing * Passive re-listening * Practice questions (passive method) * Peer/group discussion (undirected)  **Procedural-Focused Learning Techniques**  * Re-reading and re-rewriting notes * REBIM (Re-reading, Elaborating, Background Information, Interleaving, Metacognition) Passive re-reading, re-writing, and re-listening:  Simply listening, reading, and re-writing notes without any particular cognitive focus. Usually boring, monotonous, disengaged, and repetitive. The focus may be on trying to memorise what is being written, repeatedly saying the sentences mentally, or just"trying to understand". This does not activate most effective learning pathways and is highly time-consuming and demotivating. REBIM (Repetitive Execution Beyond Initial Mastery):  A technique coined by Dr Justin Sung, REBIM refers to the repetitive completion of tasks or activities for skills and processes that can already be done to a high level of proficiency. For example, coding the same simple scripts repeatedly, solving the same difficulty of maths questions despite already being able to answer them easily, or engaging in the same kind of dialogue or conversation for languages when already fluent. The benefit of the technique is negligible considering the alternative techniques that could be used instead. Practice questions (passive method):  Solving problems or answering questions and immediately checking the answer. Often paired with answering the question mentally only or concluding,"I could solve that," without going through all the steps and checking the answers to see if it makes sense. Because the question seems familiar and the answer makes sense, learners can believe that they can solve it in a realistic retrieval setting. However, the human brain tends to overestimate our abilities and underestimate challenges. As a result, this method consistently fails to identify knowledge gaps while wasting valuable practice questions. This is a common way of using practice questions with several alternative methods that are much more effective. Peer/group discussion (undirected):  Group discussion can be useful. However, most group study wastes time  compared to  what could be achieved in the same amount of time through alternative techniques. Undirected group discussion has so many more effective alternatives that it has no place in a learning system. Note that some of the techniques are suitable for multiple orders of learning. These techniques are marked with an asterisk (*). About declarative knowledge types:  Declarative knowledge is information that is based on facts, concepts and logic. It is the type of knowledge that involves encoding content material. Declarative knowledge is sometimes also called conceptual knowledge or descriptive. Characteristics of declarative knowledge:  One of the characteristics of declarative knowledge is that the information itself remains static. For example, if you learn about how a kidney functions, the nature of this information does not change, since the facts are already set. Your understanding and nuanced appreciation of the information may change, the facts remain static.    This is unlike procedural knowledge whereby the knowledge on how to execute on certain processes innately changes as mastery grows. Examples of declarative-focused disciplines and subjects:   Examples of predominantly declarative knowledge:    Science subjects  History  Politics  Law  Economics theory  Accounting theory  Mathematics theory  Business and finance theory  Coding logic  Language vocabulary, syntax, grammar and structure **Knowledge Mastery (Order of Learning)**  **Lower-Order (Best for Direct Fact Recall and Detail Memorization)**  * Techniques:     * 3Cs     * Flashcards (simple)     * Brain dump (linear)     * Generated questions (isolated)     * Teaching (isolated)     * Feynman method     * Method of loci or Modified Method of Loci     * Story/link method     * Ben system  **Mid-Order (Best for Knowledge Application, Basic Problem Solving, and Obvious Relationships)**  * Techniques:     * Flashcards (simple relational)     * Brain dump (mindmap)     * Generated questions (simple relational or multi-relational)     * Teaching (simple relational)     * Practice questions (direct method and advanced group method)     * Feynman method  **Higher-Order (Best for Discussions and Complex Problem-Solving Where Multiple Concepts Affect Each Other)**  * Techniques:     * Chunkmaps     * Flashcards (evaluative)     * Brain dump (mindmap)     * Generated questions (evaluative)     * Teaching (Modified WPW)     * Peer/Group discussion (evaluative)     * Practice questions (extended method and advanced group method)     * Feynman method 3Cs (Cover, copy, check):  A low-level"fill-in-the-blanks" style retrieval technique whereby you cover part of your notes, then attempt to rewrite or redraw the covered part from memory. You then uncover the notes and check. This has been shown to be effective at improving short-term retention of basic information, especially among younger children. Flashcards that use image-occlusion methods are a more practical and efficient variation of this. Most effective for labelling diagrams and processes. Flashcards (all variations):   Flashcards, either physical or through a digital medium such as Anki (preferred). The characteristic feature of simple flashcards is that each card tests isolated facts, concepts or processes (see  generated questions - isolated ). Concepts do not relate to each other. Often useful for specific definitions, constants or other highly specific pieces of information that do not fit into relational notes. When higher-order encoding is inadequate, simple flashcards are often overloaded. This causes substantial wasted time through repetition and steep forgetting curves.   Each card tests the relationships and influences that two or three concepts have on each other. Useful for key relationships that are highly specific and difficult to remember. These are often processes that are conceptually separate from the rest of the topic and are, therefore, hard to represent through relational note-taking. These relationships may not easily fit into chunks.   Each card holds a generated question (evaluative). See  generated questions  below for more details on this method. Generated questions (all variations):   Create your own practice questions. Isolated questions focus on retrieving single facts, concepts and explanations of isolated processes. Many"what" based questions tend to be isolated. The types of questions at this level are ideally served via simple flashcards.   Simple relational questions challenge you to explain or discuss the relationship between  two  related ideas. Multi-relational questions challenge you to retrieve the relationships between  more than two  related ideas. These can be combined as part of  Practice questions - advanced group method.   Evaluative questions challenge you to discuss relationships between multiple related ideas and conclude on the relationships' strength and importance. A crucial part of evaluative questions is that the learner must make a value judgment on the importance of concepts compared to other concepts. This level of questioning has significantly beneficial learning effects for depth of knowledge and retention.  Because evaluative questions are time-consuming to create, it is recommended to create questions in one session and answer them in another. Creating questions produces retrieval benefits, as does answering them. By splitting the question creation and answering process, we are able to receive two rounds of positive learning effects. One retrieval session also prepares us for the next.  These can be combined as part of  Practice questions - advanced group method. Brain dumps (all variations):   This is an easy technique for testing mass retrieval at low to mid levels of knowledge mastery. Type out everything you know about a particular topic. Follow learning objectives as a guide if they are available. Due to the limitations of linear notes on representing relationships, it is inefficient for higher-order testing. It is also inefficient at finding gaps as it relies on"dumping" all our knowledge until we stumble upon retrieval difficulties. Linear brain dumps are helpful as an end-stage retrieval technique used just before assessments. Depending on available time, brain dumps can be on individual concepts or entire topics.   This variation is the same as a linear brain dump but in a mindmap form. Mindmap brain dumps are superior for higher-order knowledge testing and inferior for lower-order detail testing. As more difficult assessment and retrieval standards force you to think in relationships and chunks, this form of brain dumping is strategically more high-yield for most. Mindmap brain dumps are recommended in the early stages of retrieval practice to find big-picture gaps quickly, before transitioning to lower-order methods. Teaching (all variations):   Teach a topic to a friend or imaginary student. Teaching to an imaginary student is preferred because when teaching to a friend, they may fill in gaps for you with their own knowledge instead of forcing you to confront gaps or inadequate explanations. In the isolated variation, teaching is limited to directly explaining facts, concepts and processes. Isolated teaching is a relatively time-consuming retrieval method that is lower-order centred. As a result, other alternatives, such as flashcards, tend to be preferred.    We now explain and discuss how different concepts and processes relate to each other. Simple relational teaching is a relatively effective form of retrieval and can be incorporated in the early to mid stages of revision scheduling as it can find gaps in knowledge quickly.   Pro tip:  Isolated and simple relational teaching is most useful when applied  during  learning sessions whereby you teach something you have just processed immediately after learning it. We sometimes call this"micro-retrieval". Forcing yourself to teach what you learn straight away activates beneficial generative effects of learning and can help improve deep processing.   Taught in the late stages of the program, the modified WPW is potentially the most difficult learning technique in the program. It requires an expert level of knowledge mastery to execute correctly. It challenges all orders of knowledge mastery with rapid knowledge gap identification. Unfortunately, the benefit of this method is directly related to the learner's skill level at using the technique and their encoding proficiency. Modified WPW can be considered impossible unless the fundamental encoding techniques have reached total mastery, with at least near mastery of the Ascent-stage encoding methods. Practice questions (all variations excluding passive):   This is a conventional way of using practice questions. It involves solving problems and answering questions from memory. Answers are checked immediately after. Learning material is reviewed for deeper understanding if answers are incorrect. This method activates beneficial generative learning effects by answering the questions and helps us focus on knowledge gaps.  However, the direct method increases the risk of missing gaps or classifying incorrect answers as"silly mistakes". This is because the root cause of the mistake can remain hidden. This method is not recommended for most situations compared to alternatives, as practice questions are a limited and valuable resource.   After answering practice questions to your best attempt, mark the questions you felt unsure about. Then, review your learning material and create your own"perfect" answer sheet. If an official answer sheet is available, you can check your answer sheet against an official answer sheet. Any question you were unsure about indicates a knowledge gap for you to pay special attention to, regardless of whether it was"officially" correct or not. This is because getting an answer correct for one question does not guarantee you will get it correct in variations of the same knowledge. A lack of confidence in answering is a sufficient indication of a gap.   In the extended method, we test our knowledge by:   Answering the questions  Evaluating our confidence  Creating perfect answers  Checking our answers against official answers to find even more gaps   As the extended method of practice questions is time-consuming, we recommend dedicating a large block of time or splitting the method across multiple retrieval sessions. Examples are given below. Peer/group discussion (evaluative):  Unlike in undirected group discussion, this method sets an explicit goal to critique, judge and argue the importance of different relationships and concepts, especially in view of the bigger picture. Methods of facilitating group study to achieve this are taught in the program. Chunkmaps (or GRINDEmaps):   These are mindmap variations taught as a core part of the encoding process in our    Bear Hunter System    taught in the    Growth phase.   Although the foundational skills are trained since  Fundamentals 2,  this method is not relevant until Ascent 3.   It is listed as a retrieval method because if you have previous content that you studied before learning to create chunkmaps, it is worthwhile to re-encode it into chunkmaps to stimulate higher levels of mastery and improve retention.   In this situation, this technique should be the first step of the revision process and performed as early as possible, making time for other forms of interleaved retrieval. Most people struggle to create a chunkmap with previously poorly encoded information, largely because it was initially never processed at higher levels. Method of loci, link/story method, and Ben system:  These are all different memorisation techniques. Some of these are taught in the course. However, many are not. The ones that are taught in the course are taught in late stages.   Although these memorisation techniques are powerful for rote learning of isolated information, the use cases are very narrow. Learning these techniques early also tends to create over-reliance on these methods instead of developing proper encoding techniques, which are several-fold more efficient for most real-world learning needs. They are exclusively lower-order methods and are typically over-sold by"memory champions".  They are recommended in specific use cases with large volumes of specific (sometimes ordered) information to learn in a single discrete list. Even then, there are usually more effective ways to encode this. Feynman method/technique:  The Feynman method is not a single technique but a guided structure for learning. The classical instructions are:    Choose a Topic: Select the topic or concept you want to learn or understand.   Teach it to a Child: Explain the topic as if you were teaching it to a child or someone with no prior knowledge of the subject. Use simple language and avoid jargon.   Identify Gaps and Simplify: As you explain the topic, pay attention to areas where your explanation is unclear or where you struggle to simplify. This helps you identify gaps in your understanding.   Review and Refine: Go back to your primary sources and learning materials to fill in the gaps and improve your understanding. Simplify your explanation further if necessary.   The Feynman method is an early learning method that is popular for good reason. It is one of the first comprehensive methods for learning that forces multi-order knowledge mastery.  For most people, using the Feynman method would be a great addition to their learning system. However, as the principles that make this method effective have been researched much more since it was originally described, the methods we teach allow you to achieve the same effects but better and more consistently.   However, even though our methods directly supersede it, the Feynman method can still be helpful as a"micro-retrieval" technique.  To do this, try to teach everything you learn"to a child" immediately after learning it. Activating components of the Feynman technique during learning sessions can help keep us anchored to the big picture.  Note that our encoding techniques also directly encourage the same effects. This interleaving technique is a composite of generated questions, peer/group discussion, and practice questions. It is an involved and extensive retrieval technique but one of the most consistently effective and useful across all declarative-focused subjects. Instructions -  Step 1: Choose a topic. Step 2: Create a study group with at least 1 other person of a similar knowledge level to you (or higher). Step 3: Each person creates a practice exam. Ensure to cover some mid-order questions as well as more challenging and nuanced higher-order questions. Strictly lower-order questions can be addressed through other retrieval methods. Step 4: Create a perfect answer sheet for your exam by first brain dumping to the best of your ability, then reviewing your learning materials to refine and perfect the answers. Step 5: Swap the practice exams with each other. Hold onto your answer sheet. Step 6: Answer the exams from memory to the best of your ability. Step 7: Mark answers where you were uncertain for further review. Step 8: Create a perfect answer sheet for this practice exam by reviewing your learning materials. Step 9: Swap answer sheets with each other. Step 10: Compare your answers against your answer sheet and their answer sheet. Look for differences between your answer sheet and theirs. Step 11: If there are any points you disagree with wish to explore in more depth, discuss with the other person to arrive at a conclusion. This method tests multiple orders at multiple perspectives and forces positive generative effects of learning during steps 3, 4, 6, 8, 9, and 10. For each hour of time spent, the amount of learning consolidation and gap-testing is one of the highest out of all retrieval methods.  As this method is time-consuming, we recommend splitting this process over multiple days. Recommendation:  This method tests multiple orders at multiple perspectives and forces positive generative effects of learning during steps 3, 4, 6, 8, 9, and 10. For each hour of time spent, the amount of learning consolidation and gap-testing is one of the highest out of all retrieval methods.  As this method is time-consuming, we recommend splitting this process over multiple days. Note that some of the techniques are suitable for multiple orders of learning. These techniques are marked with an asterisk (*). About procedural knowledge types:  Procedural knowledge is stored in different parts of the brain than declarative knowledge and involves some different cognitive processes. Procedural knowledge is based on actions, behaviours and processes, rather than facts or concepts. It is the knowledge of how to perform skills or procedures. In most settings, both declarative and procedural knowledge are required, but different subjects and settings require different levels of each. Characteristics of procedural knowledge:  Procedural knowledge is dynamic and action-oriented, involving the ability to perform tasks and actions effectively. It is skill-based, context-dependent, and often implicit, requiring practice and hands-on experience for mastery. Unlike declarative knowledge, which consists of static facts, procedural knowledge is closely tied to performance and practical application. It can be refined and improved over time with experience, making it a key component in various fields where knowing how to do something is essential, such as sports, arts, and professions. Examples of procedural-focused disciplines and subjects:   Examples include:    Conducting a lab experiment with technical precision (the theoretical knowledge on how the experiment should be conducted is declarative, while the skill of conducting it accurately and precisely may be procedural)  Driving or riding a bike  Cooking or baking (the theoretical knowledge of how to cook or bake a meal is declarative, while the skill of executing on the theory is procedural)  Study skills (the theoretical knowledge of learning principles and how techniques should be performed is declarative, while the skill of actually performing them in practice is procedural)  Solving mathematics or physics problems (the theoretical understanding of how to approach a problem and how different concepts can be brought together to create a solution is declarative, while the ability to actually work out the answer through calculation is procedural)  Speaking a language fluently or writing fluently (the vocabulary, grammar and syntax knowledge is declarative, while the skill of speaking or writing is procedural)  Creating an accounting balance sheet accurately and efficiently  Building an app or developing something with coding languages (the theoretical understanding of the language logic and syntax is declarative, while the skill in executing on it accurately and efficiently is procedural) The relationship between declarative and procedural knowledge:  In most knowledge applications, a combination of solid declarative and procedural knowledge is essential. If someone possesses strong procedural math skills but lacks a deep understanding of mathematical concepts, they may excel at solving familiar problems but struggle with new challenges and the utilization of different math concepts.   Conversely, an individual with strong declarative knowledge but limited procedural skills may understand problem-solving approaches but struggle with accurate calculations. For instance, a proficient coder with procedural expertise may effortlessly script common functions but face difficulties with coding challenges, technical interviews, and new projects beyond their comfort zone.   In contrast, someone with high declarative knowledge and weak procedural skills might conceptualize innovative projects but encounter issues like coding errors, bugs, inefficient practices, and reduced coding productivity.    Truly excellent learners typically excel in both domains. **Knowledge Mastery**  **Lower-Order**  * Best for following rules and procedures * Examples:     * Basic vocabulary     * Applying formulas     * Coding syntax     * Basic coding functions     * Data transformation processes  **Mid-Order**  * Best for basic knowledge application * Examples:     * Basic phrases and conversations in a foreign language     * Simple problem-solving     * Creating simple applications and functions     * Straightforward analyses  **Higher-Order**  * Best for challenging applications * Examples:     * Unpredictable conversations in a foreign language     * Complex problem-solving in unfamiliar contexts     * Creating advanced functionality and applications     * Series of related analyses and data transformations  **Techniques**  **Lower-Order**  * Retrieved execution (simple) * Simple flashcards * Ben system * Simple challenges  **Mid-Order**  * Retrieved execution (integrative) * Integrative challenges * Variable modification  **Higher-Order**  * Retrieved execution (applied) * Edge-case challenges * Variable addition Ben system and simple flashcards:  See the explanation for these methods above. In most scenarios, these methods are less effective than more procedurally heavy alternatives. An exception is language learning, where it is a valuable adjunct for vocabulary acquisition. Retrieved execution (all variations):   Retrieved execution is practising a skill or process from memory without any cue or guidance. The simple version is limited to isolated applications of the process without variation. It is often the first way to practice a new skill. However, many will continue with this simple version even after mastery. At that point, this would become  REBIM  (see above) and ineffective.   The integrative variation involves taking multiple skills and processes and bringing them together to achieve a greater or more complex function. For example, coding more complicated functions using a series of more basic functions. For languages, it may be forming common phrases and dialogues by chaining together simpler phrases. The objective is to create a more diverse application of the same knowledge to improve something called  conditional knowledge. This is the knowledge of when and how to use your declarative and procedural knowledge.    Learners should aim to move to integrative retrieved execution at the soonest possible time, immediately after gaining basic competency with simple execution. This provides stronger generative effects, greater cognitive load and more interleaving.    While integrative practice combines individual components to form a more complex product, applied practice starts with a broader project that requires multiple integrated components to make it function.   Integrative starts from understanding the basic procedures and thinking about how they can be combined to form meaningful chains and sequences. Applied works  backwards  and starts from conceptualising a target output and thinking of how it can be achieved.   In coding, this could mean developing a web application to fulfil a real need. This requires you to strategise and execute the design, implementation and QA for the application.    For languages, you could think of a common scenario in which you would use your language (e.g. conversing with your friends at a mall, in German). Then, think of multiple variations of the conversations that could arise in that setting and practice each variation. Ideally, you can then test what you have practised by actually immersing in that scenario (e.g. going to a mall with your German friends).   Applied retrieved execution is the most challenging but most effective form of practice. Learners should aim to move towards applied execution once they are confident with integrative execution. Challenges (all variations):   Challenges are similar to retrieved execution. However, challenges involve a prompt or problem stem that must be solved using your skills. Challenges can be self-created or externally provided. Simple challenges help to build basic procedural competency with simple applications. Simple challenges require  simple retrieved execution.   Integrative challenges, like integrative retrieved execution, involve combining multiple component pieces to solve a more complex problem. These challenges sometimes involve using two or more component processes or skills in ways that were previously not directly taught. A common example is solving physics or maths problems where different formulas and concepts come together to calculate a multi-step problem.   In edge-case challenges, the problems are centred around atypical examples of how the skills and processes can be used. They are almost always different from how the skills were originally taught, and the learner needs to think about how certain processes and skills apply in this situation. The context in which the processes need to be applied is often unfamiliar.  Strong  declarative knowledge  of the principles is needed to navigate this. This is because declarative knowledge is a significant component of identifying valid approaches to solving the problem in the first place. Edge-case challenges may be present in hackathons or competitions. They are challenges that are so unfamiliar that most people struggle to know how to start.   Fortunately, creating edge-case challenges is much easier than solving them. This is because working on solving an edge-case challenge requires in-depth knowledge of the correct approach and the procedural skill to execute. On the other hand, creating an edge-case challenge merely requires knowledge that concepts are related to each other and that they may hypothetically influence each other. Whether or not it really works is the job of the solver.   This insight means we can use edge cases instead of evaluative generated questions in the advanced group method of practice tests described above in the declarative techniques section.    Protip:  Artificial intelligence can assist you in creating more complex variations of existing problems! Variable modification or addition:   Variable modification simply means changing the variables in a given problem, challenge or question so that you can solve the same problem again with different figures. The nature of the problem is the same, and the approach is the same. This is good for practising low-level procedural skills before moving into more integrative stages of retrieval.   Variable addition involves adding variables to an existing problem, challenge or question to increase its difficulty. The variable added needs to make logical sense (therefore requiring good declarative knowledge) while also being solvable.   Beneficial learning effects are generated by thinking of:   which variable to add to make the problem more complicated,  whether the variable makes sense,  what value to give the variable in order for it to be reasonable,  whether a solution actually exists when this variable is added, and  evaluating how the variable interacts with other existing variables.   More than one variable can be added if it is viable.   For maths or physics, we can add variables by giving added contexts and conditions. For example:   Adding air resistance for a mechanics question and changing the trajectory due to collision  Adding a second-order differential equation and adding added initial conditions for calculus  Adding limits of integration when evaluating the integral of a function  Calculating the maximum volume of a cylinder but adding a surface area constraint and limiting the ratio of radius and height to 1:2   For coding, we can add variables to functions and challenges, such as additional conditions. For example, time-out periods, additional user inputs, further data transformations, etc.  For languages, we can add variables such as the number of speakers in a conversation, the number of viewpoints of an opinion, or the level of detail and conditions within statements or questions during a conversation. For example:"Where do you want to eat"  vs. "If you're hungry, we can eat here. Otherwise, you can pick somewhere else"  vs. "I've already eaten here before, but I don't mind eating here again. If you're hungry, we can eat here. Otherwise, you can pick somewhere else, as long as it tastes good."    Protip:  Artificial intelligence can assist you in creating more complex variations of existing problems!  Combination for mostly procedural knowledge:   This is appropriate for people with mostly procedural requirements and minimal declarative knowledge needs.   Predominantly procedural fields have extremely simple (but effective) retrieval schedules. This is because:   The skills often continually build on themselves, which causes natural consolidation and repetition.  Retrieval competence is strongly related to the level of retrieval practice, error feedback, and difficulty. This means that while it can take time, the path is straightforward.  Lower-order competence is directly tested with higher-order methods. This is unlike declarative knowledge where lower-order knowledge may not necessarily be tested to the same level of detail.   Therefore the basic structure of procedural-focused retrieval systems should be to simply start with the lower-order methods and progressively aim to move towards higher methods. Combination for mostly declarative knowledge:   This is appropriate for people with primarily declarative knowledge requirements and minimal procedural needs.  This is given as a guide for a comprehensive system. You do not need to follow it exactly (though you may). Where parts are not working for you, please adapt the routine based on your observations and needs. **Pre-Learning Phase**  * **Prestudy:**     * Create a big-picture framework for the topic.  **Day 1: Major Learning Event**  * **Encoding:**     * Establish well-organized knowledge through proper encoding.  **Day 1: During Study or Review**  * **Micro-Retrieval:**     * Test understanding and prevent lower-order learning patterns.  **Day 2-5: First Interleaved Retrieval Session**  * **Brain Dump (Mindmap):**     * Identify knowledge gaps and establish a foundation. * **Simple and Simple Relational Flashcards:**     * Address lower-order gaps.  **Ongoing: Flashcard Review**  * **Simple and Simple Relational Flashcards:**     * Cover lower-order needs opportunistically.  **Day 7-14: Second Interleaved Retrieval Session**  * **Relational and Evaluative Generated Questions:**     * Fill in gaps and consolidate knowledge. * **Perfect Answer Sheets:**     * Create a resource for future retrieval sessions.  **One Month: Third Interleaved Retrieval Session**  * **Exam Creation and Exchange:**     * Challenge understanding with a multi-order exam. * **Perfect Answer Sheet Creation:**     * Identify remaining knowledge gaps.  **Two Months: Fourth Interleaved Retrieval Session**  * **Evaluative Peer/Group Discussion or WPW Teaching:**     * Focus on multi-order gap testing. * **Advanced Group Method of Practice Testing:**     * Continuously repeat for ongoing consolidation. Combination for mixed knowledge types:  When retrieval requirements are mixed, we recommend using a basis of the declarative combination (as declarative knowledge is often the most time-consuming and challenging to master).  For subjects/topics with a higher procedural component, swap declarative techniques for procedural techniques. Naturally, you should find that topics with a high procedural component tend to have a more minor declarative component.   The extra interleaved retrieval sessions are unnecessary in these cases, and so for those subjects, the techniques may start heavily declarative, but after gaining a high level of declarative knowledge from the initial encoding (and maybe one interleaving session), the techniques move to exclusively procedural.   In summary:    Use declarative methods until you have good declarative competence (this should be much faster for procedurally heavy topics). In some cases, your initial encoding alone will be sufficient for the declarative components (depending on your deep processing and encoding ability).  Transition to procedural methods, progressing from lower to higher order.  Should I type or write out answers to questions?:  In all cases, you should always generate answers fully. This can be written or typed but never just answered mentally. As the benefit of most of these retrieval methods is in the cognitive process involved, whether you type or freehand write answers does not matter. You can use whatever is faster. If the retrieval method requires drawing or mapping (e.g. chunkmaps), freehand is superior as most apps do not allow the level of freedom as freehand writing and drawing. Do I need to write out answers in full for practice questions?:  In all cases, you should always generate answers fully. This can be written or typed but never just answered mentally. This prevents gaps caused by the  illusion of explanatory depth. Do I need to teach out loud if using teaching methods?:  It is preferred to teach out loud. While inconvenient, speaking out loud forces us to slow down and be explicit about retrieving our knowledge. This prevents gaps caused by the  illusion of explanatory depth. Feel free to speak quietly to preserve your voice. What if I go out of scope of my curriculum?:  Use your learning objectives from your curriculum (if you have one) to gauge how out of scope to do your retrieval. Aim to go slightly out of scope for optimal results, as staying too rigidly in the scope of your curriculum can make it harder to re-encode complex concepts. How accurately do I need to space out sessions?:  Refer to our article on spacing  here.  Generally, good quality interleaving is more important than accurate spacing. Your schedule can be off by days or even weeks as long as the quality of each session is good. What if I don't have enough time for all these sessions?:  Do your best. As your encoding skill develops, you will retain more (making retrieval sessions faster and easier) and learn faster than the cohort (allowing you to get ahead).  As an efficient system puts you ahead of the cohort schedule (if there is one), you should be able to"cram" your encoding early on so you can focus mostly on interleaved retrieval for the rest of your semester, term, or course duration.  Until your skill develops to this point, continue to do your best and fit as much interleaved retrieval as you can manage. For foreign language learning, I heard that I shouldn't try to speak or write anything until I feel liked I have mastered it already.:  We are aware that there are some popular theories in the language learning space, especially around immersion learning and comprehensible input.  However, the popular concepts in these theories seem to be far removed from the actual research. We suspect this has occurred due to the influence of unqualified"language learning gurus" who misinterpreted the research.  To our best knowledge, there is no benefit from withholding retrieved execution practice for language learning. Furthermore, it would go against most research on effective learning, including research on immersion learning and comprehensible input hypothesis itself!

By now, we should have:    Identified clear trends and changes that improve and worsen our energy, concentration, distractibility, and execution ability.  At least two weeks of time tracking data.   In this week's challenge, we will attempt to craft a theoretically ideal day of perfect flow and observe where the cracks appear in our self-management system. Week 3 instructions -  Step 1: Review the time tracking data from the last two weeks and identify any patterns of behaviour or time allocation that were unintentional or undesirable. Was the real time you spent different to what was scheduled? Step 2: Plan one perfectly productive day. Include steps and considerations that you will include to ensure that you maintain peak flow and productivity, based on your general insights and observations so far, as well as any insights from your time tracking data. Step 3: Execute on this day. At the end of the day, reflect on how successful your day was compared to the plan. Identify any barriers, mistakes, or oversights. Additionally, reflect on any urges you felt (even if you overcame them). Step 4: Take one or two elements from your perfect day and add it to your regular day. Step 5: Execute on this"enhanced" regular day. At the end of the day, reflect on the impact these enhancements made. Was it helpful? Does it feel sustainable? In what situations might it be ineffective? Step 6: Repeat this process from step 2.

Microlearning is suited for individuals with minimal time availability and who are unable to study for single long sessions.    We recommend Base camp level skill proficiency but may be effective for some learners as low as Ascent 2 level of skill and habit formation. Microlearning system instructions -  Step 1: Prime Step 2: Map Step 3: Explore Step 4: Dive Step 5: Consolidate Each studying session can be as short as a few minutes and cover only one of the above five steps. Each step can be divided into multiple sessions or conversely, multiple steps can be performed in the same session, depending on availability. Important:  Ensure there is significant interleaved retrieval that is either scheduled or naturally integrated as part of your work. More minor details should be offloaded to a personal knowledge management or second brain app.

Tips to execute WPW correctly: 1. Use reverse explanations.2. Always look for big-picture relevance.3. Dont get locked into the way you taught it or learned it the first time. The ideal WPW flow may not be the same as your GRINDE map layers.4. Teach based on your memory/recall.5. Doodle a mind map as you are teaching. 6. Speak out loud to slow yourself down. Dont rush.7. Give yourself at least 3 days after finishing your GRINDE map.8. Use full recall to find gaps, then revise them to perfect your recall.9. If you struggle at first, keep trying. This means you are finding gaps. WPW teaching should follow two stages. Stage 1: Layman's terms:   Laymans terms  refer to common terminology that most people can understand, even without prior knowledge of the topic.   There are four parts to this stage.  You should cover them in order, using only laymans terms (no terminology):   Explain what the topic is generally about (big picture).  Explain why this is important to know.  Explain how the topic can be divided into major components.  Explain how the components relate to each other.   Once you have covered these four parts, move on to Stage 2.  This stage tests our understanding of  concepts and relationships  at the first and second layers of learning. This is good for protecting yourself against conceptual questions, difficult short-answer questions and curveball questions in exams.   By the end of this stage, the listener should have a solid conceptual understanding of the overall topic, why it is important, what its major components are, and how those components relate to each other. Stage 2: Technical explanation:  This stage involves introducing terminology.    There are two parts to this stage:    Add technical terminology to the components you have explained. (This is known as  labelling.)  Using the terminology, explain each component in full detail. (This is known as  technical elaboration.)   Ineffective teaching introduces the terminology first and teaches the definition and explanation later. By forcing yourself to explain the topic without relying on the terminology, you challenge your understanding of the concepts and relationships.  During the technical explanation, you must recall details and specific information. It protects us against detail-oriented questions, definitions and pure-recall questions.   By the end of this stage, they should know the technical names of concepts and components and be able to make sense of the details. Recommended task 1Attempt 3 sessions of WPW  Following the guidelines in the WPW lessons, complete three attempts of WPW for an entire topic. You will need to set aside at least 3 to 4 hours for each attempt.  Complete a Kolb's after each attempt. You can continue to review the example dissections as you practice.   Complete this task once you have completed the final Kolb's for your third attempt.   Camp II:  Learn the most advanced interleaved retrieval methods in our program.  **Key Dimensions:**  * Retrieval * Self-Management  **Recommended Pace:**  * Theory: 3 to 4 hours across 2 weeks  **Recommended Practice:**  * Consistent practice for 3 to 4 weeks for optimal results  **Progression Tips:**  * Advanced retrieval techniques, including"WPW," are introduced in this stage. * Retrieval and self-management techniques require a spacing interval, making practice time-consuming. * Mastery of techniques can take months, but progress to the next stage is typically possible after 3 to 4 weeks of diligent practice.

Although this is a specific example of learning medicine, we recommend this sublesson for  all learners. This sublesson discusses a common mistake called"framing bias", elaborated on below. Medical studies have been used to explain this concept because it is one of the strongest and most common ways that we see framing bias influence learning. Framing bias:  Framing bias is the cognitive bias that says that our conclusions and decisions are influenced by how the options are presented to us. In learning, framing bias commonly affects the chunks we form by making them similar to how information is chunked for us by teachers, resources, lectures, etc.  These chunks are often not the most cognitively optimal for our brain, which is why order control and independent chunking through the correct process of inquiry is crucial. Find the way:  Many learners conclude that there is no other way to chunk information because the way it is given to them seems logical and intuitive. However, there are often multiple ways to logically and intuitively chunk information  while  being cognitively more advantageous. Our job as learners is to  find  those ways, not accept that"there isn't any other way". Below is an example of how a medical topic of retinal detachment can be chunked differently when we challenge framing bias. Example:    In the first iteration above, you can see that, at first glance, the topic seems organised. However, notice how everything funnels through the topic"Retinal detachment". The lack of lateral relationships also creates  waterfalling.  Many other diseases will have a structure that is almost identical to this. As a result, the information in this map will be forgettable and the learner will have to memorise all the terminal points of information.   Below, we now try to add lateral relationships to express the relationship    between    ideas.     In the next iteration above, we have introduced more lateral connectivity. However, it is disorganised. If we leave it here, we would have the problem of  spiderwebbing.  By evaluating the connections, we can see that the chunk"Types" near the bottom has a high density of connections. If the concepts related to"Types" were chunked together, there would be no need for so many lines.   We must explore other chunk structures to try and resolve this.   This process creates significant cognitive load and can take time.     In this final iteration, we have re-chunked the entire topic. The problems of  waterfalling  and  spiderwebbing  have been resolved using a different chunk structure. It also represents this topic in a more unique way, which makes it more memorable.   While it looks much cleaner and simpler, it (amazingly) retains the relationships identified in the second iteration. Moreover, the relationships are much easier to remember because they are reflected in the core organisational structure of the topic now.

Aiming  is about pointing your brain in the right direction. By asking the right questions, we encourage our brain to think in specific patterns and form specific relationships.   Two questions that consistently create high-quality networks for any subject are:    Why is this important?    How is this related to ___?      To make new information relevant and immediately form networks  To activate higher-order learning as early as possible  To create groups (chunks) and relationships that are robust, versatile and suit our retrieval needs    The table below outlines how using this technique can feel and what these feelings mean at a cognitive level. Refer to this table to improve your metacognition and ensure you understand the purpose of the technique. Connecting your emotional experience with the cognitive processes of learning helps you make fine-tuned adjustments and solve any technique issues more easily. **How it can feel:**  * Overwhelming due to the numerous relationships discovered while exploring"Why is it important?" * Confusing due to the various non-linear structures for main concepts, requiring significant effort to determine the most logical structure * Time-consuming as each keyword or concept demands thorough examination  **Cognitive outcomes:**  * Rapidly creates a network of potential relationships for refinement * Adds a high level of prioritization and evaluation, fostering higher-order learning * Deep examination consolidates learning, with the feeling of overwhelm diminishing as speed and efficiency improve * Over time, the brain enhances its ability to create networks, prioritize them, and determine the most suitable hypothetical structure  How does Aim fit with prestudying?:   Prestudy and Aim often overlap. However, they are not the same thing.   The purpose of prestudying is to prime our brain to create relationships before an intensive main learning event. We explore the purpose of prestudying in this article  here.  The Bear Hunter System is a system of learning, whereas prestudy is a chronological step in the learning journey. The Aim step should be one of (if not the very first) step in the learning journey.  If this happens  before  an intensive main learning event, this can be considered prestudy. If there is no prestudy, Aim should still be the first step of the learning journey. As it is very difficult to manage this kind of thinking in an intensive lecture or class, Aim may have to occur after the main learning event during a self-study session instead. This is not optimal.  However, the amount you can cover in prestudy is a decision. You could do a very brief prestudy of just 5 or 10 minutes, in which time a full Aim may not be possible. In this case, you would do a very superficial Aim to develop only the largest chunks and major relationships.  Alternatively, it's possible to do a much more comprehensive prestudy. In this case, we could do a full Aim and even add more detail. Once you learn the Shoot step which comes after Aim, this could also be done during prestudy.   In summary, prestudy is about    when    you start learning. The Bear Hunter System is    how    you learn. Once I learn Aim, should I stop doing the TLS red light?:  Yes, the Aim completely replaces TLS red light. While red light creates some initial connections and relationships, Aim is a more focused and strategic use of questions to create only those relationships that are likely high-yield. What is the difference between "Why is it important" and "How does it relate to ____"?:  These two questions may seem conceptually identical. However, we have arrived at these two key questions after years of trial and observation.   Why is it important?   This question focuses our attention on the big picture. It creates a focus, purpose, and alignment between the ideas. It helps us push through to the  Evaluate  level of thinking where we make judgments and prioritisations of networks. Asking this question critically helps our chunks become more memorable and intuitive.   How is it related to [another concept]?   This question explores additional relationships that may not be obvious. When we explore why something is important, it is difficult to escape our existing biases and preconceptions about the information. Two concepts may initially seem unrelated, and only when forced to explore their relationship do we find new perspectives and potential chunks. Sometimes, this leads us to re-evaluate our entire chunk structure and reasons for importance.

Who is this for?:  This lesson can be helpful for anyone who struggles with focus and distractibility, even without any formal diagnoses. Recommended adjustments - Make the following adjustments and modifications to your techniques to reduce hyper-fixation or distraction. Step 1: Stabilise on relevant medications, as per recommendations of a medical specialist. Step 2: Practise mindfulness meditation (takes about two months before tangible benefits are felt)¹. It can also be easier to engage in walking or active meditation for those who struggle with seated/resting meditation. Take a small notebook with you to jot down. Step 3: Use physical cues for ultra-short-term intentions and goals (you can time yourself to check these cues with analogue timers). Step 4: Only practise one technique at a time². Step 5: Keep a daily progress book  strictly limit any experiments or adjustments to just two points per day. Step 6: Selectively mute Discord channels. Step 7: Schedule Kolbs (taught at the beginning of Briefing) daily  preferably immediately after medication if you have a focus period after medication. Step 8: Limit your Kolbs to 30 minutes at most  use an analogue timer and when time is up, stop no matter what. Step 9: Be patient with yourself - you're doing great! ¹ In some less common conditions, mindfulness meditation and cognitive behavioural therapy (CBT) may be less effective. For example, there is ongoing research as to the utility of CBT approaches in early childhood trauma. We recommend consulting with a trained psychologist or psychotherapist to find approaches suitable for you.  ² Some techniques take time to develop and do not require active focus during other activities. For example, scheduling effectively can take weeks because time must pass to gain feedback on the effectiveness of your strategies. In these situations, it is acceptable to work on multiple methods simultaneously. Always be guided by your sense of  system stability. If you feel there are too many techniques and therefore have to concentrate on applying each new technique, you are likely already overloaded. Turn new techniques into habit before adding more load to your system.   Check out  this  resource from  How to ADHD, which goes through more strategies on how to manage the difficulties of ADHD mentioned in this video. Disclaimer:  These recommendations are based on our experience and non-specialist expertise around ADHD and ASD. If the personalised recommendations of a specialist contradict our recommendations, we recommend following their advice over our own.

Prestudy helps ensure you are on the right path at the beginning, which makes a significant difference throughout the rest of your learning. Fixing your prestudy can automatically fix more complicated issues down the track, saving a lot of time and effort.   Random information is often harder to remember than relevant information that is meaningful to you. This is because your brain recognises the importance of the information and is willing  and able  to store it more readily. This increases your retention and speed of recall.     Prestudy creates an outline for your brain to fill in later. Principles of effective prestudy - Whenever you do any prestudy, follow these principles to ensure you're getting the most out of it: Step 1: Group information based on how the information is related Step 2: Focus on the big picture - details can come later Step 3: Spend more time thinking about why something is important or relevant, instead of just trying to remember or understand it Step 4: Try to create analogies and connect the information to what you already know Step 5: Study the entire topic rather than individual classes or lectures so that you can see the full scope of relationships Step 6: Aim to be shallow and broad, rather than deep and narrow New terminology: Chunking:  The process of taking lots of information and putting it in groups is called  chunking. We will refer to chunks (big groups), sub-chunks (groups within groups) and chunking (grouping) throughout the program. See our guide  here  on how to prestudy for a list of common questions and more explanations on some of the nuances of prestudying. We will revisit the concept of prestudying in future lessons as we teach you more techniques as well. Video references:  The study on photography by a university professor is in the book,  Art and Fear, Quantity Always Trumps Quality.    Here is a summary by James Clear, one of the worlds leading authorities on practical habit building.

GRINDE maps are the final and best version of your mind map. GRINDE is an acronym that we use to refine our mind map during the skin step.   You may notice similarities with VPReFRE; this is because GRINDE is a mindmap-specific extension of the same guidelines.    This is where we chunk the information on the mind map, as outlined in the previous lesson.    Here, you reflect on the order control, just like with VPReFRE. Pay attention to the order of the relationships, logic, concepts, and details to determine whether it reflects the way your brain understands the topic.  Your mind map should fulfil the layers of learning; if it doesnt, take this opportunity to sort the information into clear layers ( backbone/logic  concepts  important details ). Tip:  This additional effort to make the layers clear is a characteristic indication of an advanced level learner. Make sure your arrows are interconnected and not all pointing in a single direction or going purely outwards. There should be connections between chunks and concepts within the same layer as well as between layers (where relevant and important).  Good chunking creates nice, simple branching. Good interconnectedness extends this into a web of concepts that clearly influence each other. There is a clear sense of purpose to the information. Wheel and spokes:  The  wheel and spokes  problem is when there may be good chunking, but all of the relationships seem to radiate out from a central hub. This indicates a lack of  lateral  connectivity between chunks and concepts. Use doodles, visuals, and as few words as possible. An image will help you retain much more information than lines of words. You should also explore shorthand and contractions that you can use to form your own personal abbreviation system!   Have a flow of information and ideas in your mind map. This way, your mind map will be more logical because it shows the influence of concepts on other concepts. You always need to relate information to the big picture, as this will give you a sense of direction in your learning.  Whether or not your mind map is  directional  will heavily depend on how you stick to the Aim step, your problem-solving mentality, and how importance-based your first layer of logic is.   Make sure the layers are easy to see. You can do this by using different sizes, colours, or fonts.  Although this may seem insignificant, it helps you to pick out the main logic layer, concepts and sub-concepts much faster. This speeds up your revision time and facilitates your brains ability to create relationships. Cognitively, it improves learning because it requires you to be highly explicit about which concepts and relationships are most important.  Think of it like building mental highways and motorways.

metadata.docName: I have an exam soon. How should I prepare for it?
metadata.originalText: Before we discuss this, it's important to note that the"best possible result" is not the same as the"best result".   For example, we have many students entering our course just a few weeks before their exams, expecting that they can take a predicted mark of 60 or 70% and turn it into 95% in a matter of weeks. This is simply unrealistic.    Change takes time.    When coming up to an imminent test or exam, it's important that we gain mastery over our current techniques, filling in any immediate gaps without destabilising our existing system.  The worst thing we can do is learn many new techniques that take training to master, learn them incorrectly, and do even worse than if we had just slammed through a million flashcards instead.  This is why the course is divided into High-yield and Growth phases.   Focus on the High-yield techniques.   These are techniques that you can gain reasonable mastery of in under two weeks. They give immediate returns and are often enough to improve your results significantly. The table below gives you an indication of how much improvement you can expect based on our observations of thousands of learners. **Normal Result**  **After Mastering Fundamentals** ------ Below 50%  50 to 70% 50 to 70%  Around 80% 70 to 80%  Around 85% 80 to 90%  Around 90% Over 90%  Around 93% The table above is a general reference. There is significant variation among individuals.  For example, some students who typically achieve in the 80s can jump to the mid-90s after just Fundamentals if a particular fundamental aspect of studying held them back. Caution:   Before we discuss this, it's important to note that the"best possible result" is not the same as the"best result".   For example, we have many students entering our course just a few weeks before their exams, expecting that they can take a predicted mark of 60 or 70% and turn it into 95% in a matter of weeks. This is simply unrealistic.    Change takes time.    When coming up to an imminent test or exam, it's important that we gain mastery over our current techniques, filling in any immediate gaps without destabilising our existing system.  The worst thing we can do is learn many new techniques that take training to master, learn them incorrectly, and do even worse than if we had just slammed through a million flashcards instead.  This is why the course is divided into High-yield and Growth phases.   Focus on the High-yield techniques.   These are techniques that you can gain reasonable mastery of in under two weeks. They give immediate returns and are often enough to improve your results significantly. The table below gives you an indication of how much improvement you can expect based on our observations of thousands of learners. **Normal Result**  **After Mastering Fundamentals** ------ Below 50%  50 to 70% 50 to 70%  Around 80% 70 to 80%  Around 85% 80 to 90%  Around 90% Over 90%  Around 93% The table above is a general reference. There is significant variation among individuals.  For example, some students who typically achieve in the 80s can jump to the mid-90s after just Fundamentals if a particular fundamental aspect of studying held them back. Stop progressing through the course and consolidate the techniques you have already learned. Use your existing set of techniques wherever appropriate to prepare for your exam Steps to consolidate your current system -  Step 1: Complete a skills audit by checking against our milestone checklists at the end of High-yield phase, Technique Training, and Ascent 3. Step 2: Use Kolb's (taught in Briefing) to optimise weaknesses or deficiencies in your current system. Key principle:   Stop progressing through the course and consolidate the techniques you have already learned. Use your existing set of techniques wherever appropriate to prepare for your exam

metadata.docName: How do I avoid making silly mistakes in tests or exams?
metadata.originalText: Most students who make silly mistakes during tests and exams believe that it is a problem with exam technique.  However, this is    very    rarely the case.   Making silly mistakes is usually a combination of poor exam technique with inadequate preparation. In most cases, it is more due to the latter. Even if the mistakes seem incredibly obvious, we have found that they most often represent a deficiency of understanding and application.   A common example is when assigning units or converting between different units during maths or physics. Almost invariably, when the student develops a deeper understanding of what the units actually represent, the mistakes disappear, since erroneous ways of converting now not only go against what was memorised but also fundamentally do not make sense. There is a greater chance for"alarm bells" to ring.   The best course of action is to develop a preparation and study system that reduces the number of gaps in knowledge application so that errors are less likely to occur,  then  optimise exam technique.  These techniques are all taught in the program.   One of the biggest barriers to improvement is trying to overcome silly mistakes through intention or effort. Many students either ignore the mistake, assuming it will not happen again, or try to"be more careful" by vaguely trying harder.  Neither of these approaches is likely to be successful. Steps to solving silly mistakes - Follow these 3 steps to get to the root of your mistakes Step 1: Identify the biggest root cause Step 2: Experiment with a targeted solution Step 3: Reflect and identify other potential causes Step 4: Repeat cycle of experimentation and reflection If you're unsure where to start with identifying your potential causes, the double generation practice test strategy below is a useful first step.  **Cause: Test Anxiety and Nervousness**  * Symptoms:     * Mistakes have a random pattern.     * No clear association with question difficulty, type, subject, or error type.     * Mistakes increase with more high-stakes exams. * Solutions:     * Graduated exposure during self-study. Practice timed tests under test conditions. Reduce time allowed and do them in a group to add even more pressure.     * Deep breathing techniques before tests, such as the Wim Hof breathing cycle.     * Create pre-test rituals that invoke calmness and confidence.     * Avoid last-minute studying before entering the test room.  **Cause: Insufficient Lower-Order Knowledge**  * Symptoms:     * Mistakes tend to be around discrete fact recall of specific details. * Solutions:     * Incorporate a lower-order retrieval method into your learning system, such as flashcards.  **Cause: Insufficient Higher-Order Knowledge**  * Symptoms:     * Mistakes are around:         * Conceptual questions asking about two or more concepts         * Big picture ideas         * Approaches and strategies to solving problems * Solutions:     * Increase higher-order learning during your encoding and retrieval sessions.     * Most of the encoding techniques in our program are suitable for higher-order learning.  **Cause: Insufficient Procedural Practice**  * Symptoms:     * Mistakes are around the application of knowledge to highly procedural tasks. This can be common in software development, engineering, and maths. * Solutions:     * Incorporate more generative procedural/execution type interleaving in your retrieval sessions.  **Cause: General Knowledge Gaps That Are Not Identified During Retrieval**  * Symptoms:     * A combination of various mistakes across multiple orders of learning.     * Similar mistakes are made when doing practice tests. * Solutions:     * Double generation practice test strategy to more clearly identify knowledge gaps.  Often, we can pick up signs of errors when doing past papers or practice questions. Unfortunately, we can overlook these when checking answers too early or not even answering the question fully.  This strategy is a more optimal way to use practice tests to prevent errors from slipping undetected and finding knowledge gaps more clearly. How to perform the double generation practice test -  Step 1: Do a practice test Step 2: Answer each question and write it out as you would in a real exam Step 3: Mark areas you were not confident Step 4: Create your own answer sheet with perfect model answers using your notes and other resources Step 5: Compare your answers against your created answer sheet Step 6: Compare your answer sheet against the official answer sheet (if available) Once you've completed these steps, you will have a list of:   Questions you were not confident about  Questions you got wrong based on your answer sheet  Model answers that were lacking based on an official answer sheet   Each of these items are potential knowledge gaps. You should review and restudy them with an encoding technique of choice.
