Key principle:   Once you've completed these steps, you will have a list of:   Questions you were not confident about  Questions you got wrong based on your answer sheet  Model answers that were lacking based on an official answer sheet   Each of these items are potential knowledge gaps. You should review and restudy them with an encoding technique of choice. What if I don't have a practice test?:  You can create your own, find similar questions online or in textbooks, use online artificial intelligence to help you create some, or form a small study group and create practice tests for each other.  Forming a practice group is the best strategy if possible as you gain the benefit of being challenged from someone else's perspective. Can I answer the questions in my head instead of writing it down?:  No. Answering mentally is not enough to uncover  illusions of explanatory depth. This is a common reason why learners can use practice tests and still miss several knowledge gaps. Making my own answer sheet takes too long. Can I just use an official one?:  In the worst-case scenario, yes. However, it is recommended not to skip this step as it offers more generative opportunities and is one of the most useful aspects of this strategy. Although it takes more time, the benefit is much greater as it forces both lower-order and higher-order thinking and evaluation of material to craft what you judge to be the"perfect" answers.  Anecdotally, creating your own answer sheet provides the equivalent learning benefit of completing 5 or 6 practice tests while missing this step.  It also creates a" proxy learning" effect where you can address knowledge gaps that the question did not even directly test you on because you are having to learn all around the question to craft your answer. What if I don't have an official answer sheet?:  It is acceptable to skip this step if you don't have time. If possible, form a small study group and compare your model answers with a friend's model answer sheet. This reduces the chances that you may have missed something.

Those with a fixed mindset see their ability level and intelligence as fixed and unchangeable. To overcome a fixed mindset, we must:    Take action  - one of the easiest ways to change our thoughts, feelings, and beliefs, is through action. it is a misconception that we have to  feel  like taking action first. We can take action despite not feeling motivated to.   Reflect effectively  - use Kolb's to turn mistakes into learning.   Focus on the chain performance  (see below)    One useful model to help with your marginal gains is to identify what determines a desirable outcome. Simply wanting an outcome does not create it. By analysing the chain of performance, we can understand which aspects hold us back the most. Our efforts can become more targeted and effective. What determines outcomes? -  Step 1: Outcomes and performance (which come from...) Step 2: Skills and processes (which come from...) Step 3: Attributes and behaviours (which come from...) Step 4: Mindsets, beliefs, and identities. Examples of skills and processes:   Encoding and retrieval skills  Time management and prioritisation skills  Self-reflection and problem-solving skills  Focus and attention management skills  Emotional self-regulation skills   You can see how deficiencies in any of these skills could cause significant problems with outcomes and performance. Examples of attributes and behaviours:   Open-mindedness  Willingness to experiment  Willingness to face discomfort  Diligence and commitment  Patience  Humility  Self-compassion   When these attributes (or the behaviours naturally associated with these attributes) are absent, it can cause significant challenges to developing skills. For example, imagine trying to develop the skills in this program without patience or open-mindedness! Examples of mindsets, beliefs, and identities:   Fear of failure  Beliefs about intelligence and abilities  Beliefs about talent and capabilities  Identity as a learner  Identity as someone who tries their best  Identity as someone who always gets back up   Mindsets, beliefs, and identities influence our attributes and behaviours. Further reading on fixed and growth mindsets:  Here is a  blog article  by Dr. Justin Sung on how fixed mindsets are instilled and create common problems. To dive straight into fixed and growth mindsets, read this great  summary article  by Maria Popova about Carol Dwecks famous research on children and praise.  You might also like to read  this article  by James Clear, the author of Atomic Habits. He discusses how you can use habits to modify your fixed mindsets.

We've already looked at how you can enhance your learning system with spaced, interleaved retrieval. However, as you can see from how extensive the  interleaving table  is, it can take a long time to find the right combination of methods and master them.  Instead of halting your progress while you learn these, this lesson introduces some high-yield techniques that can work well for many learners across most disciplines and levels of learning. Test - When you test yourself, remember... Step 1: Apply your knowledge. Step 2: Create your own questions and use premade questions. Step 3: You can share these questions with your friends in a small study group. Creating your own challenge and exam questions can be more effective than using past papers and premade practice questions. Even if you dont fully know the correct answer, you learn more from creating the question and attempting to answer it than you would from answering an existing question.   Use premade questions to:    Save time  Familiarise with the style of questioning in your actual assessments (but remember there's no guarantee and it could change)  Find gaps in your knowledge by testing yourself in ways you wouldn't have thought of    Use self-made questions to:    Strengthen your understanding of material you've already learned from a different angle  Find knowledge gaps in content you've already learned  Practise retrieval in all the ways (orders and types of knowledge) you need (it's easier to target weaknesses when you can control the type of retrieval challenge Protect your performance:  An over-reliance on premade questions is one reason curveball questions throw students off so much. Creating your own questions is especially useful in subjects like maths and physics, which can often trip up students with real-world applications of formulae and equations that students dont usually think of.    Stay ahead by anticipating curveballs before they are thrown! Good learners always use a combination of self-made and premade questions. Note that there are significant overlaps between this method and the  advanced group study method  taught in the  interleaving table. If you feel confident with the  advanced group study method, you can use that instead. Target - When you target your retrieval practice, remember... Step 1: Look for areas you feel less confident in or have not used before. Step 2: Start with areas of weakness, even if it feels uncomfortable. Teach - When you teach content, remember... Step 1: Some methods of teaching are more effective than others. Step 2: Dont just explain  teach! Change the order and approach when presenting the information so it makes sense for someone who hasnt learned it before. Teaching challenge:  An effective way to find your weaknesses is to explain why a concept is important  before  introducing any terminology. This means you need to find a way to explain it in lay terms.  For example,  imagine you are teaching the concept to a 10-year-old. Using these methods, you can find knowledge gaps. Knowledge gaps are:   Any parts you struggle to recall  Any parts you get incorrect  Any parts you feel unconfident about    Knowledge gaps are not only when you get things incorrect!   Every time you find a knowledge gap, review your material and re-encode it using the other methods we have taught you. Whichever methods were used to learn it initially can be used again. However, try to connect the information more deeply or try to make sense of it from another perspective. This helps to improve your retention and depth. How much do I have to practise this before I know I have sufficient competence?:  Try to do all three (test, target, and teach) at least once before moving on to the next set of lessons. You can continue to refine this skill over time, but the gaps in knowledge you find from using this technique will change how you interpret future learning. It will be beneficial to have this experience at least once or twice before moving on.    There are two signs that you should spend longer to practise before moving on:    You struggle to apply these methods  You find it very easy but do not find any knowledge gaps    If you struggle with these methods, it is worthwhile to spend a couple more weeks practising them to reap the benefits. This prevents overload and maximises your learning efficiency.   If you do not struggle but also do not find any knowledge gaps, it most likely indicates that the technique was performed incorrectly. Unless you truly have pristine knowledge, you should be able to find gaps. Retrieval techniques are meant to find gaps. Not finding any means it was ineffective.  If the technique was easy  and  ineffective, it may indicate that they did not cover all the necessary orders of learning or types of knowledge testing. In this case, read our article on  measuring your learning efficiency  to see if you are testing the appropriate levels.

metadata.docName: Where can I get help or feedback?
metadata.originalText: We're here to help you. This article shows you how you can ask for help in the correct platform to ensure you get the help you need promptly.     If you have questions on theory, techniques, tasks, or principles in our program, rather than wanting feedback on your work, search for your query in the  Learning Support  hub. If it is not answered, you can submit your question to our forum  here. We're reading!:   We're here to help you. This article shows you how you can ask for help in the correct platform to ensure you get the help you need promptly.     If you have questions on theory, techniques, tasks, or principles in our program, rather than wanting feedback on your work, search for your query in the  Learning Support  hub. If it is not answered, you can submit your question to our forum  here. If your questions concern your account, billing, or technical support unrelated to learning techniques, please visit  help.icanstudy.com  or start a conversation with our chatbot.   Getting feedback on your work is a crucial part of skill improvement. There are multiple ways to receive high-quality feedback. **Feedback Methods**  **Self-Feedback**  * **When:** Always the first step * **How:** Use Kolb's, rapid skill screens, and skill audits  **Peer Feedback**  * **When:** Regular check-ups * **How:** Ask in the appropriate Discord forum, following guidelines  **Expert Feedback**  * **When:**     * Complicated issues that community members cannot resolve     * Total system check after self-audit     * Unique circumstances and challenges     * Stubborn mistakes despite self-feedback and peer feedback * **How:**     * Click the link to be evaluated     * If a ticket is necessary, submit work through the messenger window     * Disable adblockers to allow chatbot to load  **Note:** Expert feedback has a minimum wait time of 7 business days. No feedback without self-feedback:   If your questions concern your account, billing, or technical support unrelated to learning techniques, please visit  help.icanstudy.com  or start a conversation with our chatbot.   Getting feedback on your work is a crucial part of skill improvement. There are multiple ways to receive high-quality feedback. **Feedback Methods**  **Self-Feedback**  * **When:** Always the first step * **How:** Use Kolb's, rapid skill screens, and skill audits  **Peer Feedback**  * **When:** Regular check-ups * **How:** Ask in the appropriate Discord forum, following guidelines  **Expert Feedback**  * **When:**     * Complicated issues that community members cannot resolve     * Total system check after self-audit     * Unique circumstances and challenges     * Stubborn mistakes despite self-feedback and peer feedback * **How:**     * Click the link to be evaluated     * Submit work through the messenger window     * Disable adblockers to allow chatbot to load * **Note:** Minimum wait time of 7 business days

Before moving to the next lesson, we recommend taking this time to complete the recommended tasks below as well as any other custom tasks and overdue recommended tasks in your focus list. Recommended task 1Apply layers of learning into Shoot  Using what you've learned about layers of learning, attempt at least 2 study sessions using the Aim-Shoot cycle and layers of learning together. Complete a Kolb's for each session to secure your marginal gains.   These sessions will likely take more time and concentration than previous attempts until competence increases again.   Complete this task after your second Kolb's.  Ascent II:  Learn the next step of the Bear Hunter System: Shoot, which refines your non-linear note-taking skills even further.  **Key Dimensions:**  * Deep processing * Self-regulation  **Recommended Pace:**  * Theory: 8 hours spread over 3-4 weeks * Practice: 8 weeks  **Progression Tips:**  * Ascent 2 has a high practice-to-theory ratio. * Errors from previous stages may become apparent. * Expect to spend more time in this stage than in previous ones. * Apply techniques to various learning needs. * If you have mastered previous stages, this stage should be an extension of your current methods. * If you do not notice improvements in learning efficiency, consider a skill audit to identify technique errors before progressing.

To move on, you must have around 80% competence with the skills taught so far. Use the list below to check whether you're ready!    Note:  Lower levels of competence with even one of the items can be problematic during later stages where we build on these foundations. Fundamentals checkpoint -  Step 1: You have experimented with changing your methods and have learned from mistakes. Step 2: You are finding it easier to notice and track your skill progress in relation to marginal gains. Step 3: You have experience with managing discomfort and confusion while learning. Step 4: You are using multiple techniques simultaneously in a system. Step 5: You are finding it easier to prevent your notes from being too wordy by using keywords and avoiding full sentences, paragraphs, and lists. Step 6: You are finding it easier to use non-linear notes and represents relationships between ideas. Step 7: You are able to organise similar information into groups and categories based on how they are related to each other. Step 8: You have implemented a basic spacing schedule. Step 9: You have used interleaving methods that are appropriate for your knowledge type and required order of learning. In the sublesson, you will see a demonstration of the level of skill we expect by the end of the High-Yield Phase.  Please complete the recommended task below before viewing the sublesson. Recommended task 1Practise your core High-Yield encoding techniques  Practise your core fundamental encoding techniques, including:   Non-linear note-taking  Delayed note-taking  Grouping and categorising    If you do not have any material to practise on, please use our interactive curriculum as a resource. You can also use Wikipedia articles in combination with YouTube videos on the same topic to create your own custom"curriculum". Perhaps there's something you've always wanted to learn about but never had the opportunity. Now's your chance!  You can then compare your attempts with the demonstration in our Fundamentals checkpoint lesson to assess your skill level.  Fundamentals 2:  In this value-packed stage, you'll learn a whole suite of methods that take most people their entire lifetime to learn. Each technique is high-yield and forms a core part of any complete learning system.  **Stage: Deep Processing and Self-Regulation**  **Key Dimensions:**  * Deep processing * Self-regulation * Self-management * Retrieval  **Recommended Pace:**  * 5 hours of theory spread over 2-3 weeks * Practice interspersed throughout  **Recommended Practice:**  * 4 weeks of practice * New theory introduced intermittently  **Progression Notes:**  * Wide range of new techniques introduced * Focus on one lesson and its tasks at a time * Avoid accumulating more than 2 focus tasks *"In-class learning" and"Note-taking" lessons are time-consuming but crucial for developing deep processing and self-regulation habits       Fundamentals 2 is designed to help you create significant improvements in a very short period of time. If you are in a rush, have exams coming up, or want to improve your learning as quickly as possible, aim to master the techniques in Fundamentals 2 as the highest priority. Mastery of these techniques is usually sufficient for top-level achievement in most curriculums.    From the next stage,  skill development will slow as we gradually upgrade our mental operating system (i.e. undergo cognitive retraining), helping us reach even greater levels of performance and efficiency.

Reminder:  Are there any aspects youve forgotten or been lazy about? After being so caught up in learning efficient encoding skills, it's common for people to have fallen behind on their fundamentals, like good SIR.  Use this as a reminder to check your system. You might want to perform a rapid skill assessment or full skill audit!

The purpose of spacing is to practice your retrieval and reinforce information in your memory. In general, you should wait until you are  just starting to forget  the material.   If your spacing is  too frequent, this will create unnecessary repetition and work as you're testing yourself too early in the forgetting curve.  If your spacing is  too infrequent, this will cause you to waste time during each revision as you've forgotten too much. Note about language:  Our usage of the term revision may be confusing for members in North America. Revision refers to the act of looking over material that has already been studied to consolidate and review. Spacing interval guidelines: 1. Learn something for the first time2. Review: later the same day3. Next review: 2 or 3 days later4. Next review: 5 to 10 days later5. Next review: 10 to 20 days later6. Next review: 1 to 2 months later As the interval between retrieval sessions gradually increases, this is an example of  expanding-gap spaced retrieval. Research is inconclusive on the benefit of expanding-gap spaced retrieval compared to fixed interval spacing. However, we still recommend expanding-gap spaced retrieval because it makes your workload significantly more manageable. In practice, we can use this simple schedule:: 1. Revise on the same day you learned it.2. Do a mid-week revision of everything from the previous 2 days. (You can skip this if there isnt much to revise. For high school students, this mid-week revision is often unnecessary as the volume of information is substantially lower than at university.)3. Do an end-of-week revision for the entire weeks content.4. Do an end-of-month revision for the entire months content.5. Revise the entire test or exams content in the 1 or 2 weeks before the assessment. Read our learning resource article  here  for more information on how to time your spacing intervals. 🤯 Before your brain explodes...:  The tips in these sublessons are designed to be a guide to return to as you continue to practice. You do not need to remember all of the tips immediately!   When you create retrieval plans in your schedule and practise, refer to these lessons for guidance. It will be much easier than trying to remember everything before you've even started applying it!

Chain analysis  involves finding the causes of your responses and emotions. Clinical psychologists use this technique to help with neuroticism, and it is a versatile life-skill to gain mastery of. There are many ways to perform a chain analysis. One simple and effective version that psychologists may employ is outlined below. 1. Target behaviour:   What is the specific behaviour that you want to change?   It could be a bad habit like procrastinating, over-eating, spending too much time on social media, binge-watching Netflix, or even emotional behaviours you use to cope, such as getting angry at someone and yelling at them. 2. Prompting event:   What was the event that sparked the chain leading to the target behaviour?   Be as specific as possible. 3. Vulnerability factors:   What made you vulnerable to the prompting event?   This could be your state of mind, mood, preceding events, or even your habits that caused the event to affect you more than it might have for others or if it had happened on another day. 4. Links:   What happened between the prompting event and the target behaviour? These are the links in the chain.    What happened? How did you behave? How did you react?  How did you feel at each point? How did your feelings evolve throughout the links?  What thoughts did you have after the prompting event and before the target behaviour?   Be as specific as possible. 5. Consequences:   What were the short-term and long-term consequences of your behaviour? 6. Solutions:   What potential solutions can you use to change your behaviour in the future?     Exposure:  How can you limit your exposure to the prompting event or similar events? If this isnt feasible, how can you limit your exposure to vulnerability factors?   Skills:  What skills can you develop or use to mitigate your vulnerability factors, reduce your exposure, modify and influence the events, behaviours, emotions and thoughts, or manage the consequences?   Cognitive modifications*:  How can you change your mindset or perspective about an event so that it has less of an impact on you emotionally and mentally? How will you remind yourself about this next time a similar trigger arises?   Contingency management:  We cant always get things perfect. If all the previous steps fail, what can we do to reduce the short-term and long-term consequences? How can we help ourselves be more successful at reducing the previous steps in the future? Perhaps we need stronger reminders or a more specific or clearer structure and system.    Even if you dont have the skills to manage and deal with problems directly, you can drastically reduce the influence and impact it has on you by changing how you perceive it.    For example, anxiety often becomes worse when you are aware of and actively focusing on not being anxious. This is an anxiety spiral and can lead to panic attacks. A cognitive modification such as acknowledging your anxiety, appreciating your brains reaction to try and protect you from danger, but reassuring it that there is no real danger to be afraid of, is a successful technique for managing anxiety spirals.   Cognitive modifications can be simple changes in mindset or perspective that you try to remind yourself of in the future. Or they can be more specific frameworks and techniques you learn from trustworthy sources. Reminder: Custom tasks:  If you struggle with neuroticism, a chain analysis will help you. We recommend creating a custom task to add chain analysis to your focus list. We have not created a recommended task for you because not everyone struggles with neuroticism and needs to do a chain analysis.    We leave the responsibility and decision to perform a chain analysis to your judgment.

Key principles: 1. Make sure you are using reverse explanations instead of terminology crutching.2. Do as much as possible from memory. You want to test your retrieval, not your short-term memory or ability to read a mind map. Terminology crutching  is the most common mistake learners make. It makes the WPW process significantly easier, but by removing desirable difficulty. Tip:  WPW is a great technique to use for your end-of-week revision. Its both broad and detailed, covering a vast scope of information and multiple forms of interleaving. It helps you to target your weaknesses quickly and efficiently.

Prestudy is an important part of a studying system because it primes the brain for deeper learning. Much of the difficulty with processing information is that the rate and density of information we need to process during lectures, classes or even meetings can be"too much, too fast", and sometimes"too much, too fast and too complicated".  Prestudying can reduce this load when done correctly.    Instead of trying to figure out everything for the first time during an intensive main learning event such as a class, lecture, workshop, or long study session, prestudying addresses the big-picture questions in advance.  This frees up cognitive resources so that your brain can focus on making sense of the more detailed information later.   When we enter an intensive learning event without priming our brain, it easily enters cognitive overload. This makes information easier to forget, and knowledge is slower to build.  In effect, we are fighting an uphill battle.    When these events occur on a daily or weekly basis, it is challenging to recover from this consequence.   In many cases, it is so challenging that we compromise on the knowledge that we can build by taking shortcuts or sacrificing our performance.   For the learner, it feels more overwhelming with every class or lecture.     On the other hand, with effective prestudying, we spread the confusion and cognitive load across multiple sessions.    We focus on building a big-picture understanding of the relevance first, then learning about greater details. Over time, we become less confused and it is easier to understand new information as our foundation is stronger. Knowledge is faster and easier to build. You've prestudied correctly if...: 1. You have a clear big-picture understanding of the topic2. You have covered the entire topic or sub-topic, not just one class or lecture3. You do not have any details or facts memorised yet (i.e. you were exclusively focused on the big picture)4. You see how the major ideas of the topic relate to each other and could be grouped together5. You have some hypotheses around how the concepts might relate to each other6. You feel ready to learn more detail It's best to prestudy as much as you can before classes or lectures if you're in a formal curriculum. This is because assessments are timed to the end of these classes, so studying ahead gives you more time for revision.  High quality and comprehensive prestudy is one of the first steps that is necessary to truly break away from the study grind.  However, simply covering content is not sufficient because you are unlikely to have the retention, nuance and level of mastery required. Furthermore, most learners lack the efficiency of study skills to actually study comprehensively at a pace faster than lectures and classes. In fact, it's usually the opposite in that class or lecture pace is too fast and they gradually fall behind!   Correct prestudying technique involves framing the learning and creating organisational structure. This should always be one of the first steps involved in learning.  Even if prestudy is done so comprehensively that lectures or classes become unnecessary, the first stage of the study session should still be a framing and organising step.  If there isn't enough time to prestudy comprehensively, then doing only this step is still incredibly beneficial for overall efficiency. Correct framing involves...: 1. Avoiding smaller details2. Identifying the main ideas or groups of ideas3. Identifying the key relationships between these ideas and groups of ideas4. Gaining a big-picture understanding of the topic Most learners do not realise what the intention of prestudy is and have insecurity about not learning smaller details during prestudy. However, committing to details in the early stages of studying activates isolated, lower-order learning processes that waste time and make content much more difficult to understand. Tip:  Most learners do not realise what the intention of prestudy is and have insecurity about not learning smaller details during prestudy. However, committing to details in the early stages of studying activates isolated, lower-order learning processes that waste time and make content much more difficult to understand. This is the easiest to answer.   You can prestudy at any time before the main learning event. This entirely depends on your time availability. A common strategy is to prestudy for the upcoming week in the prior weekend, but many students will also prestudy for the day in the morning of.  For high school students with moderately efficient techniques, you should be able to prestudy at least 4 to 6 weeks of content in a single weekend, allowing you to completely move beyond the class pace and completely escape the exam study grind.  If you don't have any time, even a 10-minute skim before the class is beneficial, as long as the above principles are still followed.

Using the BHS takes too much time.:  This objection is understandable. The process of encoding the first time can take considerably longer when first learning the skill. Although this becomes faster and more efficient as your skill develops, the short-term consequence can still seem unacceptable.  However, when we start measuring the efficiency of our learning holistically, we find that although we can lose some time in the initial encoding, we more than make up for this even in the early stages of skill competence.  Read our article on how to accurately measure your learning efficiency  here. It's too hard! It takes too much effort and it's too tiring.:  Effective learning always takes effort. Unfortunately, many learners have been conditioned to believe otherwise. In reality, avoiding difficulty and discomfort during learning is only viable for easy content at moderately low volumes.  With practice, you will learn to associate higher cognitive loads with effective learning. Instead of desirable difficulty only feeling difficult, you will start to feel that it is also desirable!  In other words, as your skill improves, the cognitive load does not reduce, but you become better at managing it and will no longer be bothered by it. Rather, many learners start to become bothered with passive learning because they realise how ineffective it is! The questions I am thinking of aren't the same as how I am assessed.:  When encoding information, we should always prioritise what our brain finds relevant. This is the shortest path to expertise. Once we have developed expertise, we can practise retrieving it in the way we need to for assessments.   This is much more efficient than limiting our encoding ability (and therefore forgetting more) by trying to match it artificially to assessments in the initial encoding phases.    An analogy for this is in constructing a network of roads in a city. Once the roads are constructed, you can use them in whatever way you want to get to wherever you need to!   You may also be concerned that your questions and learning sit outside the scope of an assessment. However, it is more efficient to go slightly outside the scope of a curriculum to improve your depth and retention of information instead of staying within the scope but suffering from reduced retention or depth.  You can also use your own judgment alongside learning objectives (if available) to prevent you from wandering too far from the topics scope.

Make sure to complete our recommended tasks whenever you see them! Each task is designed to turn theory into practice. Remember, skills are only helpful if we can use them. Recommended task 1Schedule time for iCanStudy theory using good time management principles  Let's set up for success! Create a simple schedule for when you will cover theory through our iCanStudy program weekly or fortnightly.    We recommend between 1 to 3 hours per week of theory.  Remember that practice should happen by gradually replacing your existing methods with the new techniques you learn.    If you spend more than 20 hours per week learning, you may be able to manage up to 5 hours per week of theory before risking overload.    When scheduling, remember to keep in mind:    Urgency trapping and why you should time-box important tasks (like upgrading your learning with our program)!  The dangers of overscheduling. Keep your schedules easily manageable at first.   Occasionally, you may learn a technique that requires extra time investment per week, such as a daily reflection. Ensure you time-box these to an appropriate amount of time to stay consistent and achieve your schedule.  Rapid Start:  The fastest way to upgrade your learning is to clear your path of any obstacles. The Rapid Start lessons are designed to do just that!  **Learning Plan**  **Key Dimensions:**  * Self-management * Mindset  **Recommended Pace:**  * Theory: 2-3 hours spread over 1-2 weeks * Practice: 1-2 weeks  **Recommended Practice:**  * Focus on 2-3 tasks at a time. * Complete practice exercises for each task before moving on.  **Progression Notes:**  * Complete lessons to accumulate 2-3 focus tasks. * Practice until tasks are completed. * Move through remaining lessons after completing tasks. * Estimated progression time: 1-2 weeks   This stage will focus on improving your enabler skills. Working on these skills improves your ability to work through the program and accelerates your development speed.

We have covered the four layers of learning and the tools to learn each layer properly (including GRINDE maps, flashcards, and the modified method of loci).  The only thing left is a  strong revision strategy  to fill any gaps.  Before revising, you should quickly sweep through your course material to see if youve missed anything you need to learn. You can also use the modified method of loci to improve your mind map by replacing lists of information with drawings.

Group study can be a valuable tool to motivate one another, revise, and prepare for assessments. However, it may be easier to procrastinate and distract each other if it is unstructured. There are three major roles that you should appoint to people in your group. Three major roles: 1. Leader: Directs the flow of discussion and probes for deeper exploration2. Timekeeper: Ensures discussions are focused and relevant, without wasted time3. Fact-checker: Asks challenging questions and forces people to justify or prove their answers or statements. Expand the accordions below for a detailed breakdown of each role. Leader:  Leaders steer and manage the groups attention. Their role is to ensure the discussions are aligned with the agenda and learning objectives. Leaders encourage active participation and look for more perspectives and deeper discussions. They push people to the limits of their knowledge comfort zone and help the group face cognitive difficulty.   Leaders are kept in balance by the time-keeper who helps prevent the discussion from taking too long and the fact-checker who ensures the accuracy of the discussion is high. Timekeeper:  Timekeepers keep up the pace and pressure. They monitor whether the group is running over time for a particular topic, which may risk being unable to cover other points of equal or greater importance.   Timekeepers also keep track of the main points during discussion and summarise what has been discussed so far if the group gets stuck and needs to consolidate thoughts. They should also pay attention to the energy levels and focus of the group and suggest timed breaks or increase the time pressure during teaching or discussion to bring the groups focus up to optimum. Fact-checker:  Fact-checkers bring challenge, inquiry and accuracy to the group. They are responsible for paying close attention to what other members are saying and finding loopholes, inaccuracies or probing and challenging questions that they may not have thought of before.   They should ask questions such as Why is that important? or Why does that happen? or"So what?". Fact-checkers must actively look for perspectives that have been missed and connections that have not been made. They should have Google, resources, and an AI at the ready to constantly look up the validity of statements and see if challenging questions make sense to ask the group.   The fact-checker role is one of the most difficult but also one of the most important. This role is normally given to the person with the greatest expertise and higher-order thinking skills. However, in all situations, roles should be swapped around between members so each person can have the opportunity to fill each role. If there are more than three people in the group, increase the number of fact-checkers. If only two people are in the group, remove the leader role.  Switch roles regularly. You may wish to switch roles after two or three hours, or after each session. Tip:  If someone is disengaged from learning and prevents efficient progress, remove them from the group.

In this example, there is one main area of improvement and uncertainty on whether the depth of detail and breadth of the topic covered is sufficient. Could you hear it while listening to this students attempt yourself?   What mistakes in your own WPW attempts do you now recognise at this stage?

metadata.docName: How do I improve my question quality?
metadata.originalText: Not all questions are created equal. Asking high-quality questions and invoking curiosity are fundamental skills for high-level inquiry-based learning (such as our Bear Hunter System).   The most important questions to ask for any concept are:    Why is it important?  How is it related to [another concept]?   These force you to connect the information at a higher level of knowledge mastery, reinforcing relationships and preventing information from being learned in isolated silos.   The natural consequence is an increase (often significant) in retention. Inquiry-based learning is controversial in the learning science space. This is because there are few guidelines for best-practice and it depends on the quality of the facilitator. There is very little research on learners using inquiry-based learning for their own learning.  The most effective way to use inquiry-based learning (which we teach), is to remember the cognitive principles it must abide by. Key principles: 1. Asking questions must help you find relationships2. Answering questions must force you to evaluate the importance of information and make value judgments3. You must exert mental effort to make sense of the information4. Information must always be connected to a big picture5. Information must always form a network6. The network should be logical and make as much sense to you as possible (i.e. just because the information is connected does not mean it was connected in a meaningful way) You can use the above points as a checklist to see if your inquiry process adheres to all the points.  Note that anything that prevents you from finding multiple relationships or avoids the need for making a value judgment on the importance of relationships will reduce the quality of your learning. This is one of the most common reasons for ineffective chunking or reduced-quality encoding.    Questions are answered with a simple, direct answer. The purpose of asking questions is to identify multiple relationships which you can then evaluate and make judgments on.  Questions are too"what" based. These questions are isolating and do not require students to look at relationships between other pieces of information.  Questions neglect to ask"why is it important?" - This is one of the most critical questions to ask in order to create chunks rapidly, as well as identify key relationships.  Questions are only asked for individual concepts, but not on chunks which have been identified.     Information is retained less and recall is slower.  Curveball questions in exams are more difficult to answer as ideas have not been related to other ideas during the study process. Variations are therefore harder to synthesise and conceptualise, which curveballs test you on.  Mindmaps seem disorganised. This is because the backbone of concepts and logic are not formed strongly as either questions were not asked of the largest chunks. The importance of inquiry:  You can use the above points as a checklist to see if your inquiry process adheres to all the points.  Note that anything that prevents you from finding multiple relationships or avoids the need for making a value judgment on the importance of relationships will reduce the quality of your learning. This is one of the most common reasons for ineffective chunking or reduced-quality encoding.    Questions are answered with a simple, direct answer. The purpose of asking questions is to identify multiple relationships which you can then evaluate and make judgments on.  Questions are too"what" based. These questions are isolating and do not require students to look at relationships between other pieces of information.  Questions neglect to ask"why is it important?" - This is one of the most critical questions to ask in order to create chunks rapidly, as well as identify key relationships.  Questions are only asked for individual concepts, but not on chunks which have been identified.     Information is retained less and recall is slower.  Curveball questions in exams are more difficult to answer as ideas have not been related to other ideas during the study process. Variations are therefore harder to synthesise and conceptualise, which curveballs test you on.  Mindmaps seem disorganised. This is because the backbone of concepts and logic are not formed strongly as either questions were not asked of the largest chunks. Identifying exactly where your inquiry-based learning is going wrong can be very nuanced and subtle. There are many variations and therefore many possible solutions. Feedback and frequent attendance to Live Clinics will be most effective at identifying your issue.   Unfortunately, it is not easy to outline all of these variations through a single article.

The next letter in the acronym, P, stands for  Processed.  You must process the information somehow  we want to process it as a  problem  by  asking questions. Again, visuals will also help you to process information.   Re stands for  Relational. Do not focus on remembering individual pieces of information; always relate it to other information. If you read linearly, your brain does not process the information as effectively, and any information that goes in will quickly go out. That is why retention dramatically increases when you focus on strengthening the  relationships  between pieces of information. We do this by using mind maps.   F stands for  Freehand. Typing is too single-minded and doesnt encourage much thinking. Freehand writing forces us to process the information more. It also allows us to represent things more visually, whereas typed documents dont represent relationships well. App and device recommendations for digital note-taking:  A good note-taking app has the following features:   Facilitates the correct cognitive processes for learning based on modern research (most important)  Has good organizational features for multiple notes (less important)   To facilitate the correct cognitive processes, a note-taking app should have an infinite canvas and allow for convenient, fast freehand drawing with a digital stylus. Since most note-taking apps were designed based on  what would sell best  rather than  actual learning research, many of them are restrictive to efficient learning.   We have independently reviewed and tested all of these apps under a variety of circumstances.    The following apps are well-known but inefficient:    Evernote: Has leading levels of note organization but a horrible and linear note-taking experience, which is extremely restrictive.   Notion: Has high levels of note organization and lots of note-taking options, but all of the options are linear. For this reason, Notion is extremely limited and not recommended for efficient or effective note-taking.   The following apps are well-known and reasonable:    Notability: Has decent note organization and a good writing experience, but is severely limited by its lack of infinite canvas.   OneNote: Has good note organization and a somewhat infinite canvas, but the canvas is limited by its low level of zoom-out, which eventually causes notes to become very difficult to read. The writing experience is also average, with no palm detection or pressure sensitivity (two small but meaningful features that make your note-taking more immersive).   The following apps are recommended:    Freeform for iOS:  Comes bundled as part of iOS and is therefore"free" (if you purchase an iPad). Works well but lacks some of the aesthetic functionalities that Concepts has. Recommended app for those on iOS and on a budget.   Concepts: Was originally an app for design and architecture. Concepts is overloaded with features you will never use, but it has an excellent writing experience with full infinite canvas and acceptable note-organization features.   Squid: One of the best (and most underrated) note-taking apps on the market. Excellent, intuitive, simple writing experience and good note organization alongside infinite canvas. Not overloaded with features like Concepts.   Leonardo x OneNote or Evernote for Windows: Windows does not have  any  good note-taking apps, but Leonardo is the best alternative. It is a fully-featured drawing app with infinite canvas, but it has no inbuilt organization. You simply save project files and can export drawings as images or PDFs. Its lack of organization can be overcome by exporting these files and importing them into something like Evernote or Onenote.   Explain Everything: An app created for online teachers and instructors. We use this app to record all of our tablet videos. It is fully featured with great organization features but is more expensive. Basic references for the advantages of freehand:  At a technical level, there are several different studies and findings that support freehand expression. As a summary:  Freehand note-taking increases deep processing by facilitating higher cognitive load, improving focus (potentially due to increased anchoring), and providing more opportunities for visual or abstract expression.  One of the most famous studies on this is  The Pen is Mightier Than the Keyboard  by Oppenheimer. However, his study does not conclusively explain  why  freehand offers these advantages.  Cognitive load theory  and  multimodal learning theory  provide a more robust explanation. References for the power of freehand expression combined with visualisation:  Fuson KC. Relating Math Words, Visual Images, and Math Symbols for Understanding and Competence.
