Before you watch:  The following demonstration uses our  interactive curriculum module  as practice content.  We strongly recommend that you attempt this yourself before reviewing the demonstration to make an accurate judgment of your true skill level. You can access the interactive curriculum module from the  Supplementary Learning  page in your  Toolkit.   This video has been intentionally edited to show the thinking time and thought processes involved in studying the topic.    You've reached the end of the High-Yield Phase. The Growth Phase is unlocked 14 days after you completed Rapid Start. Based on our recommended rate of progression, it should already be unlocked for you now! If Growth Phase is still locked for you...:  If you were moving through the lessons faster than this and the Growth Phase is still locked, please use this time to practise and deepen your mastery of the High-Yield phase. Over 90% of the problems our learners face in the Growth Phase come from inadequate practice during the High-Yield phase!

Complete the recommended tasks below before moving on to the competence assessment and checklist below. Recommended task 1Complete an Ascent II level challenge week  Complete a challenge week by applying all of the methods you have learned so far. Make sure to incorporate:   Aim-Shoot cycling on different topics  Appropriate layers of learning identified via order control during the Aim-Shoot cycle  Multi-order interleaved retrieval practice  Appropriate time management and prioritisation  Appropriate focus and attention management  Kolb's at least 3 times during the week with cycling of experiments into subsequent Kolb's cycles    Complete this task once you have completed your challenge week.   Ascent II:  Learn the next step of the Bear Hunter System: Shoot, which refines your non-linear note-taking skills even further.  **Key Dimensions:**  * Deep processing * Self-regulation  **Recommended Pace:**  * Theory: 8 hours spread over 3-4 weeks * Practice: 8 weeks  **Progression Tips:**  * Ascent 2 has a high practice-to-theory ratio. * Errors from previous stages may become apparent. * Expect to spend more time in this stage than in previous ones. * Apply techniques to various learning needs. * If you have mastered previous stages, this stage should be an extension of your current methods. * If you do not notice improvements in learning efficiency, consider a skill audit to identify technique errors before progressing.Recommended task 2Complete a skill audit  Using the checkpoints and meta-checklists so far, complete a skills audit on your current skill level and learning system. Implement any findings as experiments into your next Kolb's cycle.   Complete this task once you have completed your skill audit.   Ascent II:  Learn the next step of the Bear Hunter System: Shoot, which refines your non-linear note-taking skills even further.  **Key Dimensions:**  * Deep processing * Self-regulation  **Recommended Pace:**  * Theory: 8 hours spread over 3-4 weeks * Practice: 8 weeks  **Progression Tips:**  * Ascent 2 has a high practice-to-theory ratio. * Errors from previous stages may become apparent. * Expect to spend more time in this stage than in previous ones. * Apply techniques to various learning needs. * If you have mastered previous stages, this stage should be an extension of your current methods. * If you do not notice improvements in learning efficiency, consider a skill audit to identify technique errors before progressing. **Technique(s)**  * Bear Hunter System  Shoot * Focus, Scheduling, BEDS-M, Revision  **Level of Competency**  **Bear Hunter System  Shoot**  * **Conscious Competence:**     * Can connect aim and shoot steps accurately and seamlessly.     * High levels of chunking and accurate relationships between chunks.     * Moderate effort required.  **Focus, Scheduling, BEDS-M, Revision**  * **Unconscious Competence (Mastery):**     * Habits have been formed.     * Skills are performed automatically and effortlessly. Ascent II checkpoint items - To move on, you must have around 80% competence with the skills taught so far. Use the list below to check whether you're ready! Step 1: You have replaced TLS with Aim-Shoot cycling and can do this with a reasonable accuracy, consistency and some reduction in effort. Step 2: Your inquiry process consistently leads to meaningful and high-quality relationships. Step 3: You are able to form importance-based chunks at the root reason level at least 70% of the time. Step 4: You are able to represent the layers of learning clearly in your non-linear note-taking. Step 5: You are able to express the main relationships and chunk structures in non-linear note-taking easily. Step 6: Writing non-linear notes is comfortable and at the level of unconscious competence. Step 7: You are able to do consistent Kolb's to a high quality and regularly. Step 8: You are able to perform multi-order interleaved retrieval with high levels of competence and confidence. Step 9: You are able to manage your time, priorities, focus, and attention effectively.

Note-taking guidelines - Follow these guidelines to make sure your note-taking helps your brain's ability to process information. Step 1: Always manipulate knowledge in a different way to how you consumed it. Step 2: Use non-verbal and non-linear note-taking to manipulate knowledge through visual and spatial parts of your brain. Step 3: Improve your note-taking with baby steps. Step 4: Limit your word-count. Look for big chunks of text in your notes that can be expressed through mindmaps, symbols, arrows, and doodles. While using imagery can be a powerful memory cue, there are some important considerations to ensure you receive the maximum benefit. Avoid over-visualising:  Over-visualisation refers to when learners turn too many words into visuals. Using the same"landmark" analogy, having too many images and visualisations reduces the benefit on our memory because there are too many landmarks. It's like having a different landmark at the end of every street! This can get overwhelming.  Images are best used to reduce big chunks of text or create memory anchors for core principles that are either conceptually important or at higher risk of forgetting. Keyword anchoring your images:  Images can help create memory anchors for knowledge. These act as mini cues within your notes to help trigger your ability to recall information. Think of them like"landmarks" in your notes.  To make it easier to review your notes in the future, add a keyword under each drawing to make sure you remember what the drawing stands for. This way, we get the benefits of creating"landmarks" while also ensuring we don't forget what the drawings represented in the first place! Avoid hieroglyphics:  Hieroglyphics refers to a method of visualising notes where the same images are used repeatedly instead of words. When this occurs, the imagery loses its memory benefit because it is too repetitive and common. Instead of being a"landmark", they become like streetlamps. Imagine trying to remember every streetlamp in a city!  In the following example, the following equations have been converted into images:   Metal + acid = salt + hydrogen gas  Metal + water = metal hydroxide + hydrogen gas    As you can see, images are used to replace a word directly, and they are used repeatedly. Therefore, this would be considered"hieroglyphics" and is not any more memorable than using words.   Consider the next example:    Here, we've used common elements of imagery to create a more memorable image for the reaction of"metal + acid = salt + hydrogen". The additional processing and thought required to think of a more"creative" image also improves memory and retention. Draw it yourself:  While taking screenshots and diagrams others have made can be tempting, do so sparingly. A significant portion of the advantage of creating visualisations comes from the processing and thought required to think of imagery that is appropriate and memorable. By avoiding this work, we also avoid the beneficial effect it has on encoding.  Furthermore, diagrams and images from more"technical" sources are often too dense and detailed to be memorable. Images are just memory cues:  In the next sublesson about  Notes and processing, we will introduce other methods of thinking and note-taking involving creating groups and relationships. Creating groups and relationships is superior to simply creating an image.   In the above example, with a chemical reaction, this information is better learned through good logic and a deeply processed understanding of the concepts. Relying on a superficial memory cue like this image is less efficient.  The example with chemistry was used in this lesson as it is a common example where learners often use hieroglyphics.    Visualisations and memory cues can  help to reinforce  the information if it has been processed and grouped already.  I can't draw very well. What should I do?:  Check out our article on  how to draw to learn! It's much easier than you think. After all, we're drawing to learn, not sell our doodles to an art gallery. I'm not used to writing notes this way. What should I do?:  If this is mostly new to you, we recommend you take it slow. Correct note-taking is an important part of studying. It's also a bottleneck for many students because linear note-taking is extremely limited.   You may want to gradually change how you write your notes over the next 2 to 3 weeks. You can also practise other techniques in the meantime  especially the in-class techniques, which overlap heavily.  Over time, it will become easier and more natural for you to write non-linear notes!

Key principles: 1. Marginal gains is a way of thinking about progress that helps us create the optimal path for success.2. Kolbs helps us do the practice in a way that helps marginal gains compound positively so that we are always moving in the right direction.3. Marginal gains depend on the stage of competence. Most gains involve exploring the types of mistakes before even the first success! As our skills develop, we follow the  Hierarchy of Competence. **Skill Development Stages:**  **1. Unconscious Incompetence:** - No awareness of a new skill - Unaware of own lack of proficiency  **2. Conscious Incompetence:** - Attempting to perform the skill incorrectly - Aware of own inadequacy  **3. Conscious Competence:** - Correctly analyzing and applying the skill - Requires conscious effort and attention  **4. Unconscious Competence:** - Performing the skill intuitively and effortlessly - Minimal conscious thought or effort required   Kolb's  is the engine of improvement. Using Kolb's helps us learn from each experience so that we improve and learn from each mistake. Being good at Kolb's gives you  confidence  to experiment because you know you can"succeed" no matter what, either by doing the skill correctly or by gaining valuable learning from the attempt.   Marginal gains  is the road we travel. It'sa perspective on development and progress. It helps us to reframe progress in a way that is safer, more effective, and more motivating. However, it does not tell us  how  to gain the 1% improvement. Kolb's fills this role.   You can read more about this in our article about the difference between marginal gains and Kolb's    here.

Tracking marginal gains is about  seeing progress.     Failure to track marginal gains means a failure to see progress. This is one of the most common causes of demotivation in the early stages of skill development. You can't trust your brain:  The human brain is terrible at tracking small changes that occur gradually. Therefore, most people will have absolutely no intuitive sense of whether they are making marginal gains during the early stages. Tracking it actively is one of the only ways to visualise and"feel" the progress. Viewing progress through marginal gains is what creates a  positive motivation cycle.   Without marginal gains, the outcomes become invisible, and we enter a  demotivation spiral. Over time, this can lead to burn out as our efforts seem to be in vain. Read our guidelines  here  on how you can track marginal gains. You will need this to complete the recommended tasks at the end of this lesson. You can access these guidelines again at any point from your  Learning Support  page. Moving forward:  In the later stages of the program, we will teach you additional methods that you can add to these steps to make them more targeted. For now, gain familiarity and experience with thinking about progress through the framework introduced in the article above.

This lesson relates to the Goal Analysis section of the 30-day plan form. Key principles: 1. Dont sacrifice medium to long-term success for short-term outcomes. You may sometimes need to sacrifice short-term outcomes to set yourself up for more meaningful success later. Avoid always playing catch-up for short-term, urgent tasks.2. Hard work alone is not enough to achieve uncommon results. The method and direction of your hard work are critical.3. Long-term directions are more important than specific goals. This is because many things can change as you gain more information and experience.4. Make your goals specific and measurable. Using SMART goals here is appropriate.

A flashcard is a card that has a question or prompt on one side and an answer on the other. If youre using an app, your flashcards may have more than two"sides". The prompt on a flashcard can commonly be:   A question  Fill in the blanks  Image occlusion (i.e. fill in the blanks of an image)   Using flashcards as a learning strategy can be effective when executed correctly. Crucially, we want to avoid:    Having too many flashcards:  This creates overwhelm and requires a high regular and repeating time investment. Only layer 3 and 4 should be on flashcards.   Testing on too many things in a single prompt:  When we do this, it becomes difficult to decide whether we get a card"correct" or"incorrect" if there is a combination of correct and incorrect answers. Flashcards vs. summary cards:  Flashcards are different from summary cards. Flashcards test you on information, while summary cards are discrete cards that provide a summary of information. Unlike flashcards, summary cards do not produce a generative effect on learning. Some people have hundreds or thousands of flashcards. This is inefficient and unsustainable. This can be reduced with proper layers of learning as more information becomes naturally relevant and deeply encoded. Key benefits of flashcards: 1. It allows spaced retrieval practice for lower-order information2. It functions as a"second brain" reference source of information3. It is easy to use4. It enables microlearning Flashcards are powerful because they allow you to engage in  microlearning, or short bursts of studying (e.g., while taking the bus). Conceptual learning cant usually be done in short bursts, making flashcards a great tool to fill in small gaps in your schedule.  Dont use flashcards in long study sessions, as this doesnt activate microlearning conditions and is thus less effective. Keep it to short bursts of 3 to 10 minutes per session.   Dont use physical flashcards, which are harder to access for microlearning. Apps, like Anki, are much more accessible and effective, as they calculate the best time for you to review each flashcard based on spaced-repetition algorithms.  This level of flexibility, control and convenience is impossible with physical flashcards. The (extremely minor) benefit of writing flashcards by hand is insignificant compared to the benefits of going digital.   You might also be interested in this YouTube video on maximising your flashcards  here.

By now, you should:    Have a better understanding of the key factors that seem to obviously affect your energy, concentration, distractibility, and ability to execute intentionally.  Be more used to paying attention to the variables that influence your flow state throughout the day.  Have some data on time tracking for at least one week.   In this week's challenge, we will adjust these variables to observe the impact they have on our overall self-management ability. Some factors may be rate-limiters, whereby addressing them disproportionately impacts our overall self-management. Week 2 instructions -  Step 1: Continue tracking your time as per week 1. We will use these insights next week. Step 2: Identify one key factor that seems to most significantly or repeatedly impact your energy, concentration, distractibility, or ability to execute intentionally. Step 3: Create a simple experiment to address this factor. The experiment should be very realistic and simple to achieve. The purpose is to explore the impact of modifying this factor, so intensive and drastic changes are not recommended. Step 4: If you are struggling to create an experiment, you may wish to explore some strategies in our Discord community to get ideas. Step 5: Every two days, reflect on how your experiment has impacted your energy, concentration, distractibility, and execution ability. You may identify additional factors that were missing before. Step 6: Your aim is to clearly identify at least one clear trend that improves your flow throughout the day and one clear trend that worsens it.

After seeing all of the skills and strategies in this course, you may feel its too  difficult  or  time-consuming  to implement and practise them.  Often, learners have expressed that their current methods are good enough. However, many people have commented to us in our community, through emails, reviews, and in-person, that they regretted this months or years later.   People often compare themselves with those immediately around them. They feel satisfied as long as they outperform the people they can see. However, this is misleading if you are having to compete against others outside of your immediate visibility.   Ask yourself:  Am I comparing myself to  others, or am I comparing myself to my  standards? Key principle:  Always seek to set realistic and appropriate standards and use them for comparison. Comparing yourself to others to understand your performance is risky, unreliable, and unhealthy. What is good enough now may not be good enough for the future. People have a misconception that if things become more difficult, they can solve it by just trying harder. However, this often fails because everyone else also tries harder!   Ask yourself:  Am I preparing myself for future challenges? Key principle:  Always seek to use and familiarise yourself with methods and approaches that will help you both in the short  and  long term. If your current method is heavily effort and motivation-dependent, this leaves very little room for error or improvement. Loss aversion is a phenomenon whereby people always prefer  not to lose  something they already have, even if it means forgoing an opportunity to  gain  something else. Because of loss aversion, you may tend to think your current process is good enough and hesitate to make changes.   Ask yourself:  Am I investing in gaining skills and assets that will benefit me for life, or am I avoiding the opportunity to gain tomorrow out of fear of losing today? Key principle:  Become aware of when you make decisions out of fear and avoidance. This can indicate that your decisions are being made with too much emotional mind and not enough rational mind! Further reading on loss aversion:  Loss aversion is a phenomenon that has been shown repeatedly in multiple replicated  studies. There is also some newer  evidence  that suggests that the effects of loss aversion can be subtle and unpredictable. Part of overcoming the good enough mentality is systematically sorting through our risks. Sometimes, the risks we  perceive  in a decision or action may not be the full scope of the risks. **Perceived Risks and Hidden Risks**  **Perceived Risk: Risk of time wasted compared to alternatives.** * Example:"I don't have the time to practice these techniques when there is so much already I'm behind on!"  **Hidden Risk:** Risk of continuing to fall behind as current methods caused us to fall behind in the first place.  **Perceived Risk: Risk of investing time and effort into making unnecessary changes.** * Example:"I'll be ok with what I'm doing now. It's worked fine so far!"  **Hidden Risks:** * Risk of losing out on long-term opportunities due to inadequate skills. * Risk of being unable to overcome future challenges if the difficulty increases.  **Perceived Risk: Risk of learning new skills incorrectly or getting things wrong.**  **Hidden Risk:** Risk of never learning new skills due to avoiding experimentation.

Optional:  This sublesson is for those in school. If the advice on this page is not relevant to you, feel free to move onto the next sublesson. Note that some of the techniques in this sublesson may still be useful and transferrable for many other contexts. Many students have expressed that it is difficult to use some of our techniques at school. Often, this is because the teacher enforces or advises students to use particular techniques.  Although their intentions are generally good, mainstream teacher training does not have much focus on learning science. As such, the techniques enforced by teachers can be outdated or anecdotal. Sometimes, their recommendations can be detrimental to students short-term and long-term learning. General rule:  Generally, if our advice on learning conflicts with what you hear from school, we recommend following our advice instead.    Most teachers do not have the time and freedom to become specialists in learning skills. As leaders in this industry, we continue to work with schools and institutions globally to help train teachers with the latest research and practice observations. If your teacher does not allow you to use our techniques in class or overwhelms you with homework, you can try these approaches:    Discreetly use the technique anyway.  This is the easiest solution. Your teacher may force you to write linear or passive notes, but if they arent checking, you could get away with using our techniques anyway. If you can do your own work or study your own way in class without being detected by the teacher, do so.   Do the minimum possible to satisfy your teacher.  If the above isnt an option, spend as little time as possible on unweighted assignments or homework so you have more time for proper self-study. The time-boxing approach below may be useful here.   Use class as a way to revise the material and find gaps.  When sitting in class, instead of just trying to absorb information, you can use class as a dynamic quizzing environment. Actively look for things you arent clear about, or ask deeper and more detailed questions you usually wouldnt think about. This is a common strategy for university students who are doing effective prestudy.   Do your homework during class.  Most of the time, homework is just about finishing questions. If you have already learned the material or find it easy, you can do the homework during class.     Unlike time- blocking, which is a time management technique that involves scheduling blocks of time in your schedule to complete work, time- boxing  is when you do not exceed the amount of time blocked out.  For example, if you schedule one hour to complete something, time-blocking allows room for flexibility. Time-boxing dictates that at the end of one hour, your time is up.  Time-boxing is a useful method for completing work that is either  very important  or  very unimportant (but compulsory).   Many important tasks are not urgent. For example, improving your learning skills may be important but not urgent. These tasks can not usually be completed in a single'session' anyway. Therefore, time-boxing is useful because it allows you to commit time to making progress on life-changing, important activities.    For tasks without any real consequence but are mandatory to complete, time-boxing gives you enough time to complete them to a sufficient standard without wasting time beyond its value.  The best example is homework that does not contribute to your final grade. You need to do these tasks for school, but they arent as important as doing well in actual exams or tests, so you shouldnt spend too much time on them. How to time-box your homework -  Step 1: Assign the amount of time you need for important tasks such as self-study or working through this course. These are truly important tasks and should not be compromised. Step 2: Schedule this time into your day or week. Step 3: Allocate some of your remaining time for unimportant tasks like unweighted homework. This may be less time than you are used to spending on those tasks. Step 4: Complete your unimportant tasks as quickly as possible within the allocated time. The idea is not to get a perfect outcome but the best outcome in the time available (sometimes, we refer to this as the  best-for-time  outcome). This approach can also help to combat perfectionist tendencies. Parkinson's law:  This suggests that an activity will contract or expand to fill the time available. Give yourself lots of time, and it will take lots of time. Give yourself very little time, and you will still get it done (within reasonable limits). Pareto principle:  Otherwise known as the 80:20 rule, this states that 80% of an effect resides in 20% of the work or input. For example, 80% of your learning occurs due to 20% of the information (this is a powerful phenomenon that we will heavily exploit later), 80% of your time is wasted on 20% of distractions, and 80% of your essay is written in 20% of the total time. Eisenhower matrix:  This prioritisation framework indicates how unimportant but urgent tasks might be handled. If you feel your teacher is open to the idea, you may wish to openly discuss the techniques and methods you are trying to use and ask for permission.   In cases where students have shown they have an excellent level of knowledge and are ahead of the class, some teachers have even issued a hall pass to students, allowing them to not come to class and instead self-study in the library or do their own revision during class time.   If all else fails and you have to sit in class, learning nothing new, and are unable to self-study discreetly we can try to reduce our quantity of prestudy so class is more engaging.  In essence, we are deliberately increasing the level of confusion we would feel in class by having a less comprehensive and even shallower level of prestudy. The idea is that if you  must  attend class anyway, this is time that we would otherwise waste. By loading class time with more cognitive load instead of during prestudy, we make the class more challenging and, therefore, more engaging.   We can then devote the self-study time we would have spent on deeper prestudy on interleaved retrieval instead.   Alternatively, the other option is to stay ahead of the class and accept that class is boring. Perhaps you can practise some mindfulness meditation?   Either way, remember that feeling bored in class because you're so ahead of the material and you already know so much is a great problem to have!

Key principles: 1. Perfectionism is usually a symptom of anxiety and insecurity.2. The solution to overthinking is not underthinking, but rather more productive and targeted thinking.3. We can use certain thinking systems and steps to force more perspective on our perfectionist thoughts. About mental health and anxiety:   Anxiety is a real and potentially serious condition. If you suffer from anxiety to the point where it impacts your life in a significant way, we recommend you see a medical professional such as your family doctor.  Types of interventions   Intervention through medication  can help to kickstart a transformation, especially when certain emotions become ingrained, as your brain starts to adapt and mould to being better at feeling that emotion. Medication helps balance the playing field to give yourself a fighting chance without your neuroadaptive hormonal changes fighting against you.   Psychologist interventions  act like brain coaches, much like how this course coaches you through learning. Trying to realise and train yourself to think in specific ways that may cure your mental health condition can take years or decades if done alone.  Furthermore, seeking help from untrained individuals can lead you in the wrong direction or not give you the support you need.   Mental health diagnoses are confidential and do not impact almost any university program acceptance or career path outside of being a commercial pilot or some military positions. Even then, there is considerable leniency and humanism in the selection process. Curbing overthinking and anxious perfectionism  The following framework can help to reduce overthinking and anxiety-driven perfectionism. Instructions -  Step 1: Identify what emotions you are feeling. The most important part is to be specific. Step 2: Create a problem list of all the root causes for each feeling, whereby if these problems did not exist, you would not feel this way. The most important part here is to be both specific and comprehensive. Step 3: Breakdown the problems into a series of actionable tasks that can help you to overcome these problems*. Problems may be overcome by literally removing them, or changing our perspective or response to them. Step 4: Identify one task in your controllable list that you can do something about right now and act on it. *You may conclude that some problems are outside of your control. It can be helpful to mark which problems are inside and outside your control to help direct focus and energy.    The more specific you can be and the smaller you can break down your problems, the more likely you will have something you can act on. The more you can act on them, the less overwhelmed you will tend to feel. Even the act of breaking down the problems in the first place can significantly reduce how overwhelming it feels. Important:  We do not address the  negative  aspects of a perfectionist identity in this lesson. Those with a perfectionist identity may overly push themselves towards high achievement and put themselves under unhealthy amounts of pressure. The ability to gain perfect outcomes can be tied to a value judgement on themselves and their worth.    If this resonates with you, we recommend discussing with a trained therapist who can help guide and coach you to dissociate self-worth from achievement.

metadata.docName: What if I make a mistake? I feel uncertain. What should I do?
metadata.originalText: This is when a person asks many questions to gain certainty before trying something new. These questions often concern"what if" situations when something goes wrong. Information over experimentation is a fixed mindset symptom where the person tries to reduce the risk of failure. Key principle:   This is when a person asks many questions to gain certainty before trying something new. These questions often concern"what if" situations when something goes wrong. Information over experimentation is a fixed mindset symptom where the person tries to reduce the risk of failure. One of the most critical concepts for learning new skills is engaging in the theory-practice cycle as early and frequently as possible.  This can be different to what we think learning a new skill involves. Many people place high importance on understanding the theory and technique, while practice is to gain fluency and mastery (i.e."to get used to it")   However, in reality, practice is necessary to understand the theory more deeply and correct misconceptions. Because complex skills can have many different aspects to learn, practice gives us relevance and a clear idea of which parts of the theory to focus on. Asking too many questions before attempting something is detrimental. The graph below shows that:   When you have too little information about something, the chance of success is low. This is because there are too many unknowns around how to do the process correctly.  When you have the appropriate amount of information, the chance of success is high. This is because there is relative clarity on the process.  When you have too much information, the chances of success are low again. This is because there are now too many things you have to consider while attempting the process. This creates  overload. The value of questions:   Asking too many questions before attempting something is detrimental. The graph below shows that:   When you have too little information about something, the chance of success is low. This is because there are too many unknowns around how to do the process correctly.  When you have the appropriate amount of information, the chance of success is high. This is because there is relative clarity on the process.  When you have too much information, the chances of success are low again. This is because there are now too many things you have to consider while attempting the process. This creates  overload. The best time to make your first attempt and experiment is when you have:   Enough information to attempt the method  Enough information to know what doing it correctly looks and feels like  Not enough information to do it correctly   The objective of the early attempts is to understand which mistakes you are most likely to make. This dramatically improves your learning and skill acquisition because questions are transformed into more effective forms, as described below.  The amount of information needed to have clarity and start experimenting is relatively low. The amount of information needed for certainty is usually very high. Often, the amount of information we need for total certainty exceeds what our brain can handle  and  what is even  possible  to know before experience and practice. Key principle:   The best time to make your first attempt and experiment is when you have:   Enough information to attempt the method  Enough information to know what doing it correctly looks and feels like  Not enough information to do it correctly   The objective of the early attempts is to understand which mistakes you are most likely to make. This dramatically improves your learning and skill acquisition because questions are transformed into more effective forms, as described below.  The amount of information needed to have clarity and start experimenting is relatively low. The amount of information needed for certainty is usually very high. Often, the amount of information we need for total certainty exceeds what our brain can handle  and  what is even  possible  to know before experience and practice. "What if I make this mistake?"  In response, you could ask," What if you don't?"  These"what if..." questions have a low chance that this mistake will even be made. Time could be wasted on gaining clarity on a situation that is not relevant."I tried this and found I made this mistake. How could I overcome it?"  This question is directly relevant as it is rooted in real experience.  **Questions to Ask**  **Before and During Attempts**  * **Calibration Questions:**     * How do I know if I have done this correctly?     * What does it feel like to do this correctly? * **Risk Avoidance Questions:**     * Are there any common mistakes I should be aware of?     * Are there any situations where it is not appropriate to use this method?  **After Attempts**  * **Experimentation Questions:**     * I tried the method and found these difficulties.     * I think the problem is [ABC].     * Therefore, I think I should try [XYZ] next.     * What do you think?  **Notes**  * **Calibration Questions:**     * These questions may not always be easy to answer. Some things can only be felt and known through experience. * **Risk Avoidance Questions:**     * Not every mistake needs to be made personally. Some mistakes are unnecessary and can be avoided.     * These questions are open-ended and do not ask about specific hypotheticals. * **Experimentation Questions:**     * Follow the structure:         * What I did         * What I found         * What I think is the issue         * What I propose as an experiment

metadata.docName: How do I use the 30-day challenges most effectively? How do they fit in the core program? Can I do more than one?
metadata.originalText: The 30-day challenges are created to give you the experiences and insights you need to learn new skills optimally. They aim to give you a concentrated exposure to the types of organic experiences you may normally encounter in months or years of unguided experimentation and learning. After the 30-day challenges, you should expect to have deeper awareness and metacognition of each dimension, as well as a range of relevant experiences that make the core program easier to work through. Do I have to complete them?:  Every challenge is completely optional. Progression through the core program without the 30-day challenges is absolutely viable and reasonable if you do not have time. What if I do not have 30 days before my cohort starts?:  The diagnostic report recommends prioritising certain dimensions. If you feel like those dimensions are very limiting, we recommend completing the 30-day challenge before the core program or progressing through the core program more slowly than you normally would.  If you feel that you are confident to improve your dimensions straight away, you can ignore the 30-day challenges and start with the core program only.  The challenges exist so people have an option to get an early start on skill development in a way that does not require much guidance and support, before their cohort commences. Can I do multiple challenges at the same time?:  Yes, you can complete multiple challenges simultaneously. However, we recommend starting on the dimension that is most limiting for you, based on the archetype calculated from your diagnostic assessment. Can I do challenges later, while going through the core program?:  Yes, you can complete the challenges anytime you feel like you are plateauing through the core program or that your ability to consolidate new techniques from the core program is slow. These situations may indicate that you lack the relevant experiences and insights to learn the core program skills, and you may benefit from exposure to these experiences through the challenges. How should I progress through the high-yield phase if I am also doing the challenges?:  If you feel overwhelmed going through the core program and challenges simultaneously, we recommend focusing on only one. If you find the core program techniques relatively straightforward to implement and practice, prioritise moving through the core program until you face difficulties.  If you find the core programs overwhelming and difficult to integrate into your system, prioritise the 30-day challenges and move back to the core program when you have completed the relevant challenge.  If you feel that you can do both simultaneously, we recommend progressing through the core program at half the pace recommended in the stage overview descriptions.

Hearing other peoples situations, whether similar or different, often helps in our own personal situations. Although you may wish to transform your skills immediately, remember that some things cannot be rushed (otherwise, everyone would do it)!   People often underestimate how deep and complex learning skills can be because they are rarely exposed to real learning science. The process of trying to improve is also confusing and sometimes frustrating! Over the years, we've refined our program to make it easier and more achievable than it has ever been. But it still doesn't make the journey easy.   We've realised that difficulty is an unavoidable part of the growth.  Rather than try to remove discomfort, which may be impossible, we focus on teaching you the skills and mindsets to embrace discomfort and thrive. True empowerment is more than feeling comfortable:  We believe this is  true  empowerment: the ability to not only succeed when it is easy but to face challenges and overcome uncertainties when most others cannot.

The multipass system uses the Swiss-cheese model that was introduced to you earlier. The different passes are like the different layers in the Swiss-cheese model and enable you to cover almost all the material in just a few days. What is the relationship between the BHS and multipass?:  BHS is a foundational system of learning that gives you the core skills and steps for learning any new information. It gives you the methods of thinking and processing information to encode knowledge efficiently.  The multipass system is a strategy for implementing your BHS in different ways. It gives additional guidance on  when  to learn and a high-level direction for your  order control.  Each learning session still uses the steps from BHS. Multipass dictates what you will learn in each session and how to divide the topic into manageable and efficient learning blocks.  This is especially useful for large, dense topics that will inevitably take multiple'passes' to learn. Multipass gives a clear framework for how this topic can be divided into individual passes. Each pass involves the utilisation of the BHS methods.   There are other systems for utilising the core skills you learned from the BHS in the supplementary learning modules available from your toolkit. There are two ways we can perform the multipass system. Multipass methods -  Step 1: Inquiry-led: Order control is dictated by what feels intuitively relevant based on our inquiry process and aim-shoot cycling. Step 2: Objectives-led: Order control is dictated by learning objectives. **Inquiry-led Learning**  **Benefits:**  * Feels natural and engaging * Extension of conventional BHS * Multiple sessions covering varying layers of learning  **Disadvantages:**  * Less strategic under time pressure  **Use Cases:**  * Rapid learning for high volumes with flexible time restrictions * Material requiring strong foundation for future learning  **Objectives-led Learning**  **Benefits:**  * Learning directly relevant to assessable material * High likelihood of performance results  **Disadvantages:**  * Sacrifices optimal encoding for performance strategy * Reduced retention and response to higher-order questions  **Use Cases:**  * Imminent time pressures requiring high learning volumes with minimal study time * Topics with limited long-term relevance Note that both approaches focus on the logic and concepts layers of learning first. This is a key difference compared to conventional cramming which starts with detail layers with fluctuating relevance. As a result, there is much more time required to be performance-ready.   Regardless of the approach, using the multipass system helps prepare you for assessments better as it emphasises developing the logic and concepts layers first. As most questions are derived from logic and concepts, it is much easier to logically extrapolate and answer a wide range of questions. Interleaving and lower-order performance:  If there is time, adding some interleaving strategy (preferably a HVRP method such as WPW) is also recommended. You may also need to simultaneously supplement with a lower-order memorisation strategy like flashcards or Mmol.

Our brain is notoriously bad at understanding how skilled we are at things. Research on the  illusion of competence  has shown that we tend to overestimate our ability.   Later, we'll teach you a precise way to track and evaluate your true competence with any skill. For now, we can use this simple table to estimate your skill level. **Level of Competence**  **Confidently Unknown**  * Description: High confidence due to confusion between theoretical understanding and actual skill. * Level of Practice: Minimal or no practice in various contexts. * Evaluation: Practice required for accurate assessment of skill level. Further practice is necessary before proceeding.  **Developing Skill**  * Description: Low accuracy or consistency in using skills in different situations. * Level of Practice: Some practice has identified weaknesses and gaps. * Evaluation: Additional practice recommended to strengthen skills. Learning new skills may lead to overload.  **Stabilising Skill**  * Description: Skills are becoming comfortable and consistent, requiring minimal effort for accurate use. * Level of Practice: Significant practice under various challenges and situations. * Evaluation: Ready to learn new skills. Rapid Start skills list - Before completing Rapid Start, check that you are at or near the"stabilising skill" level with the following skills: Step 1: Evaluating tasks based on importance vs urgency Step 2: Scheduling important, non-urgent tasks to prevent urgency trapping Step 3: Using a calendar to create manageable and achievable schedules Step 4: Managing focus and procrastination to a high level of consistency The recommended skill tasks on the next page are designed to help you improve your competence in some of these core skills. References:   About the illusion of competence (for knowledge and skills):   Brown, J. D. (2012). Understanding the better than average effect: Motives (still) matter.  Personality and Social Psychology Bulletin,  38 (2), 209219.  Dunlosky, J., & Rawson, K. A. (2012). Overconfidence produces underachievement: Inaccurate self evaluations undermine students learning and retention.  Learning and Instruction,  22 (4), 271280.  https://doi.org/10.1016/j.learninstruc.2011.08.003   Dunning, D. (2011). The DunningKruger effect: On being ignorant of ones own ignorance. In  Advances in experimental social psychology  (Vol. 44, pp. 247296). Elsevier.  Ehrlinger, J., Johnson, K., Banner, M., Dunning, D., & Kruger, J. (2008). Why the unskilled are unaware: Further explorations of (absent) self-insight among the incompetent.  Organizational Behavior and Human Decision Processes,  105 (1), 98121.  Händel, M., Harder, B., & Dresel, M. (2020). Enhanced monitoring accuracy and test performance: Incremental effects of judgment training over and above repeated testing.  Learning and Instruction,  65, 101245.  https://doi.org/10.1016/j.learninstruc.2019.101245   Kahneman, D., & Tversky, A. (1977). Intuitive prediction: Biases and corrective procedures.  Decision Making: Descriptive, Normative, and Prescriptive Interactions, 313-327.  Koriat, A., & Bjork, R. A. (2005). Illusions of competence in monitoring ones knowledge during study.  J Exp Psychol Learn Mem Cogn,  31 (2), 187194.  https://doi.org/10.1037/0278-7393.31.2.187   Svenson, O. (1981). Are we all less risky and more skillful than our fellow drivers?  Acta Psychologica,  47 (2), 143148.

Key points: 1. Study systems built around spaced retrieval (i.e. repetition) are no longer unique or sufficient.2. Using techniques that change the slope of the forgetting curve is more effective than doing more repetition.3. We tend to believe harmful myths about studying and learning due to a lack of accurate mainstream information.4. We are more confident about things we know very little about, which can cause us to reject the advice we need most. Internationally best-selling author Adam Grant recommends asking yourself:   What conditions are necessary for my belief to be true?    By asking yourself this, you can objectively examine the likelihood that your belief is true.   For example,  if you believe your method of studying is the best, the conditions for it to be true are that:   You know all of the alternatives,  You know these alternatives deeply enough to assess their effectiveness accurately,  You are aware of what the best means,  You have started using the best method potentially without any training (and potentially from a young age), and  The best method must be very common (if your approach is similar to how others study).    Another approach is to assess your confidence. This is a method Dr Justin Sung mentions often. Consider the graph below of the Dunning-Kruger effect curve.   Since there are only two points in the graph where a persons confidence is high, you can ask yourself:   Do you have a high level of confidence?  Have you spent enough time learning about this to be considered a professional?   If your first answer is  yes  but your second answer is  no, you are probably near Mt. Stupid! Self-awareness is the key:  Were all on Mt. Stupid for something. If we can recognise that our sense of confidence may not match our actual expertise, we can be more open-minded and accept advice. This is a hallmark of a successful learner. You can learn more about the Dunning-Kruger effect  here.

This program teaches you learning  skills.  Unlike knowledge (which can be acquired very quickly), skills often take much more time and experience. This is especially true of learning skills which have often become habits. The time to untrain old habits and train new habits can be significant. About the Growth phase:  Compared to the High-Yield phase, a characteristic feature of the Growth phase is that it involves cognitive habit retraining. This is the primary reason progress is much more  variable  and generally  slower  than the progress you may have felt during the High-Yield phase. There are no instant fixes, but with the proper work and patience, the results will come. Learning is not something you just do  it is a skill that must be learned and practised over time to be perfected.   However, time is limited. Therefore, we have to invest our time in things that:    Are effectiveness (helps us achieve our goals)  Are efficient (low time, energy, resource cost)   Many learners have methods that are only efficient   or   effective.    To make change, we need to change things!  You will find throughout this program that your methods become different from many people around you. However, we encourage you to think with a critical lens. Ask yourself whether the methods we teach you seem valid and logical. Experience the effects yourself and come to your own judgment.   Common does not mean correct.

Complete the recommended tasks below before moving on to the competence assessment and checklist below. Recommended task 1Complete an Ascent III level challenge week  Complete a challenge week by applying all of the methods you have learned so far. Make sure to incorporate:   The full BHS (Aim-Shoot-Skin) on different topics  Clear and consistent identification and expression of layers of learning  Optimal chunking using the 2-4 Rule and intuitive chunking  Multi-order interleaved retrieval practice  Appropriate time management and prioritisation  Appropriate focus and attention management  Kolb's at least 3 times during the week with cycling of experiments into subsequent Kolb's cycles    Complete this task once you have completed your challenge week.   Ascent III:  Complete the final step in the Bear Hunter System with Skin. In this stage, you'll learn how to hyper-optimise your chunk structure to address any lingering limitations to your retention or depth.  **Key Dimensions:**  * Deep processing * Self-regulation  **Recommended Pace:**  * Theory: 6 hours over 3-4 weeks * Practice: 3-6 weeks, with most theory covered in the first 3-4 weeks  **Progression Tips:**  * Technique accuracy difficulty depends on competence in Ascent 2. * Pay attention to checkpoints and practice until criteria are met before advancing.Recommended task 2Complete a skill audit  Using the checkpoints and meta-checklists so far, complete a skills audit on your current skill level and learning system. Implement any findings as experiments into your next Kolb's cycle.   Complete this task once you have completed your skill audit.   Ascent II:  Learn the next step of the Bear Hunter System: Shoot, which refines your non-linear note-taking skills even further.  **Key Dimensions:**  * Deep processing * Self-regulation  **Recommended Pace:**  * Theory: 8 hours spread over 3-4 weeks * Practice: 8 weeks  **Progression Tips:**  * Ascent 2 has a high practice-to-theory ratio. * Errors from previous stages may become apparent. * Expect to spend more time in this stage than in previous ones. * Apply techniques to various learning needs. * If you have mastered previous stages, this stage should be an extension of your current methods. * Noticeable improvements in learning efficiency should occur. * If not, technique errors are likely. * Consider a skill audit to identify errors before progressing. Growth phase progress:   Ascent 3 marks the end of the intensive encoding strategies segment of the Growth phase.    In the later stages, we start to develop the usage of these skills more time-efficiently and in more complex combinations and systems. Issues with encoding  quality,  effectiveness, or  consistency  are unlikely to be improved by the lessons in future stages.   If you are doubtful of your competence at this point, the greatest improvement will come from practice and feedback rather than later theory. If you do not notice the following general improvements from a rapid skill assessment, there is a high chance that your techniques or system requires optimisation. Expected findings at this stage... -  Step 1: Significant improvements with retention after first encoding session. Step 2: Significant improvements with retrieval performance across a diverse range of challenges and contexts. Step 3: Reduction in total study time and increase in efficiency due to reduced time spent on relearning. Step 4: Significant improvements in confidence. Step 5: Significant improvements in growth mindset and ability to learn from mistakes and experiments. Step 6: A clear appreciation for the type of thinking that higher-order learning triggers and a high tolerance for cognitive load and discomfort. If your experience does not align with the above expectations, we  strongly  recommend seeking feedback on your practice. **Technique(s):** Bear Hunter System  **Level of Competency:**  * Moderate comfort with aim-shoot cycling and skin steps * High levels of chunking and clear, prioritized relationships * Memorable and well-flowing GRINDE maps Unlocking the next stage:  Base Camp is unlocked 21 days after Ascent 2 is completed. If you've been practising the new theory and progressing at the recommended pace, it should be unlocked already by now!   If the next stage is still not unlocked, please use this time to practise your current method and gain deeper mastery.

Skin is a step for exclusively refining your higher-order learning at the evaluate level.  As you have probably noticed by now, your chunk structure changes to reflect your most up-to-date understanding of how concepts are related to each other. It can take multiple Aim-Shoot cycles to get to a point where we now have a comprehensive view of all the relationships.  With the Skin step, we consolidate, integrate, refine, and make judgments on how we want our final chunk structure to look.  After all this time, it's finally time to create some more certainty! Further reading:  Ultralearning   book  and  YouTube video  (note that the concepts discussed in Ultralearning are incorporated and further refined in the BHS) The following techniques are new. **Technique:** Bear Hunter System  Skin (GRINDEmaps)  **Rationale:**  This advanced mind mapping technique enhances high-level chunking and relationship-priority learning. It enables the organization of information into a hierarchical structure, where key concepts are represented as nodes and their relationships are depicted as branches. This visual representation facilitates the identification of patterns, connections, and the overall structure of the knowledge being learned. The following are techniques from prior stages that you should continue using.     Technique  Notes      Bear Hunter System  Aim  Start reducing total question quantity in favour of higher quality.    Bear Hunter System  Shoot  This should be automatic. The aim-shoot cycle should be second nature.    Maintaining Focus + BEDS-M       Scheduling and Revision        The following techniques have been stacked and consolidated.     Previously separated as  Stacked and consolidated into      Bear Hunter System  Aim + Shoot + Skin  The Bear Hunter System

Now that we have close to unconscious competence of the traditional Bear Hunter System with Aim-Shoot cycling, we will build upon it with an advanced variation we call  Hipshot.   The Hipshot variation will switch quickly between Aim and Shoot. It is essentially a faster method of Aim-Shoot cycling that exploits the high cognitive load tolerance you have trained and your ability to form relational networks rapidly. Instructions -  Step 1: Collect keywords as normal Step 2: Perform the Aim step mentally to identify a hypothetical logic layer. Step 3: Immediately Shoot on your mental model of the logic layer to validate or correct it. Step 4: As you gain clarity about the validity and intuitiveness of the logic layer, commit it to a GRINDE map. Step 5: Repeat this process from step 2 until you work through each of your layers. Step 6: Perform the Skin step once you are satisfied with your initial map and feel ready for refinement. Correct usage of Hipshot allows you to save time by:    Drastically reducing the time for Aim  Reducing the number of iterations before arriving at a map to Skin   While Hipshot increases speed and efficiency, it is vital that quality and accuracy are not impacted. Hipshot is only possible with close to unconscious competence with the traditional Aim-Shoot cycle. If you attempt Hipshot and struggle, please return to the traditional BHS. Note:  Like Aim-Shoot cycling itself, Hipshot often occurs as a natural byproduct of unconscious competence developing with Aim and Shoot individually.

Feel free to proceed to Summit once you feel comfortable with the techniques from Camp II. Similarly to Camp I, as these lessons do not involve much cognitive retraining, you should be able to progress relatively smoothly. **Revision Strategy**  * Level of Competency: Able to apply effective revision principles with ease and comfort.  **WPW Reteaching**  * Level of Competency: Proficient in using the WPW (What, Paraphrase, Write) technique accurately. * Importance: The WPW technique is highly effective in identifying knowledge gaps. Incorrect execution can significantly reduce its effectiveness.  **Attention Management - Advanced Time Management Skills**  * Level of Competency: Consciously competent in using attention management techniques. * Skills: Ability to recognize and address attention lapses throughout the day, identifying hidden time-wasting patterns.

As spaced, interleaved retrieval requires a spacing interval between retrieval sessions, it can take months to master interleaving techniques.  To complete these tasks, we recommend incorporating SIR into your schedule and having at least one week's worth of practice before moving on. You can continue to practice this over time instead of waiting months to master it before moving to your next lesson.   Less than one week of practice is not advised. Recommended task 1Create a one-month SIR schedule  Using the techniques and guidelines from the SIR lesson in fundamentals and the  interleaving table, schedule your retrieval sessions for the next month.   Complete at least the first week before marking this task as complete. You do not need to complete the entire month before moving on.   You may create your own SIR combination or use the example combinations provided in the interleaving table.  Fundamentals:  Don't underestimate the basics! Most learners struggle to improve for years because they lack these fundamentals.  **Learning Plan**  **Key Dimensions:**  * Retrieval * Deep Processing * Mindset  **Recommended Pace:**  * 2-3 hours of theory per week for 1-2 weeks * Intersperse theory with practice  **Recommended Practice:**  * 2 weeks of practice before moving to the next stage * Continuous use of these methods throughout the program * Ample opportunities for practice in subsequent stages  **Progression Tips:**  * Complete each lesson in one sitting * Complete focus tasks before moving to the next lesson * Avoid overwhelming yourself * Aim for experience with techniques rather than total mastery in the first lesson (Spacing, Interleaving, and Retrieval)

Key principles: 1. If the decision is not heck yes or heck no, it probably isnt too important what you decide.2. This is only for simple decisions with low consequences and low complexity.3. Using this framework helps train basic decisiveness on a daily basis. Some aspects of this framework are referred to in much more complex decision-making frameworks (which we will cover later).

Key principles: 1. Stay concise. Lack of conciseness can be due to a lack of clarity on how to communicate the importance of something. This leads to over-explanation and difficulties in smoothly transitioning to more detailed parts.2. Do not teach to the pen. The pace of teaching should not be limited by the pace of the mindmap/drawing. This is a common mistake when first using this method. Note that most of these mistakes are common in those first learning the WPW method, even with high levels of knowledge expertise.

metadata.docName: What is the optimal function of motivation?
metadata.originalText: In the early stages of the program, we learn about marginal gains and how it helps improve motivation. Later, we learn about motivation dependency.  How do these two concepts relate to each other? Why do we need to improve motivation when we should not be motivation-dependent? To answer these questions, we can explore the process of learning new skills and engaging in self-development. The process of self-development -  Step 1: Identify desired outcomes Step 2: Identify necessary skills and processes to achieve the outcome Step 3: Unlearn old habits, remove barriers, and start training new habits Step 4: Learn the necessary skills Step 5: Continue forward with your set of new skills and habits As you can see from the steps above, habit formation is not an immediate outcome. Forming habits takes time because:   We need habits of practice and self-reflection to develop other habits more intentionally and quickly  We have to spend time to unlearn old habits  We usually have to remove the barriers that prevent us from achieving 1. and 2.   After we have learned new habits, we no longer require motivation on a daily basis to perform the function that our habit fulfils. However, the early stages of learning a new skill require an upfront investment of time, diligence, and consistency. This is the period where demotivation can set in if we are unable to see the progress we are making. Key principles: 1. Motivation is useful as a resource when we need it to break through challenges and develop new habits2. Motivation-dependency is undesirable when we use it to perform tasks and actions that could otherwise be driven by habit3. Motivation should be used strategically to develop key habits4. Marginal gains improves motivation in the skill development pathway until new habits have formed (this can take weeks or months)

Make sure to complete our recommended tasks whenever you see them! Each task is designed to turn theory into practice. Remember, skills are only helpful if we can use them. Recommended task 1Fix urgency trapping   Identify any tasks that take up a significant amount of your time because they are urgent but not necessarily important. Remember, a task is important if the consequence of failure is  unacceptable  and  unmanageable.  Next, identify tasks that you know are important but not urgent. These tasks could make a big difference in the medium to long term.  Remember to include"learning to learn"!   Set time in your schedule to work on the important, non-urgent tasks regularly. Your urgent tasks should fit around this.   Rapid Start:  The fastest way to upgrade your learning is to clear your path of any obstacles. The Rapid Start lessons are designed to do just that!  **Learning Plan**  **Key Dimensions:**  * Self-management * Mindset  **Recommended Pace:**  * Theory: 2-3 hours spread over 1-2 weeks * Practice: 1-2 weeks  **Recommended Practice:**  * Focus on 2-3 tasks at a time. * Complete practice exercises for each task before moving on.  **Progression Notes:**  * Complete lessons to accumulate 2-3 focus tasks. * Practice until tasks are completed. * Move through remaining lessons after completing tasks. * Estimated progression time: 1-2 weeks   This stage will focus on improving your enabler skills. Working on these skills improves your ability to work through the program and accelerates your development speed.

The Shoot step is the second part of the Bear Hunter System. It is an upgraded version of the green light step of TLS that you are used to.  At this point, you can completely replace your TLS with using the Aim and Shoot steps. Aim vs. Shoot: 1. During Aim, we prime our brain to find meaningful relationships by asking importance-based questions.2. During Shoot, we satiate that curiosity to clarify relationships, add detail, explore further relationships, make judgments on chunks and relationships, and focus on constructing our map. Note that not every question has to be answered perfectly. Sometimes, you will ask questions that are so complex or advanced that it is not realistic to be able to answer them. The learning occurs when we attempt to answer it, which helps us to reinforce our relationships. Finding Shoot easy?:   Good! If youve aimed properly, shooting is easy.    Shooting becomes difficult when old bad habits or lower-order, linear learning interfere. Lower-order habits can cause us to have impaired order control. Using the non-linear note-taking system of Shoot will help to anchor your thoughts and keep them focused. The following techniques are new. **New Technique: Bear Hunter System - Shoot**  **Rationale:**  This technique enhances note-taking by combining multiple previously learned skills into a single, more efficient process. The following are techniques from prior stages that you should continue using.     Techniques  Notes      Bear Hunter System  Aim       Maintaining Focus and BEDS-M  Start turning this into a habit    Scheduling Basics and SIR  Should be second-nature     The following techniques have been replaced and upgraded.     Prior Technique  Replaced by      Traffic Light System  Green light  Bear Hunter System  Shoot    Notes and Processing*  Bear Hunter System  Shoot

Most people are confident in their studying skills, but studies consistently show that they are usually  overconfident, especially in areas where they have a small amount of knowledge. The  Dunning-Kruger  graph shows a persons confidence relative to their knowledge. Mount Stupid:  The first peak is  Mount Stupid. This is where people gain a lot of confidence because their knowledge of a new topic has increased quickly. However, in absolute terms, they still know very little because their starting point was nothing! As a result, people at this stage are often confident despite having low levels of knowledge.   Unfortunately, many learners are at this stage, as mainstream education teaches little about learning science. Valley of Despair:  The first dip is known as the  Valley of Despair. This is where people realise, after delving deeper into the topic, that there is actually a lot they dont know. This is typically the stage that PhD candidates are at after studying the topic for 5+ years. Slope of Enlightenment:  The  slope of enlightenment  is the period where people gain knowledge. This period can last decades and eventually culminates at the  peak  (when they have mastered the topic). One of the biggest challenges around overconfidence is that we are oblivious to our own ignorance.  If we are unaware of what we don't know, how can we ever know if we are overconfident... or just confident?   One way is to objectively assess our confidence level and time invested into building expertise. Because there are only two points where we have high confidence, we can ask ourselves:    Do I feel very confident?  Have I dedicated several years to intensively learning about this to become an expert?   If the answer to the first question is'yes' and the second is'no', then we can say we are probably overconfident. What's important to remember is that we still won't  feel  any less confident. But this doesn't have to dictate how we behave and think.   In psychology, the Wise Mind model is a popular framework for understanding the"types" of minds humans engage in.    Operating with different minds:    When we operate with the  emotional mind, we are controlled by feelings. Too much emotional mind can cause us to ruminate and spiral. We can panic, become anxious, impulsive, or allow our feelings (such as confidence or motivation) to dictate what we do.  When we operate with the  rational mind, we are controlled by logic and thinking. Too much rational mind can cause us to overanalyse and feel paralysed. It can also cause us to become unhealthily detached from our emotional needs or the needs of others.    Often, the best balance is in between.  We can both be in tune with our feelings but let our behaviours and thoughts be guided by what we rationally tell ourselves through our logic and knowledge. This is the wise mind. Relating the wise mind to overconfidence:  We can use these self-assessment questions to identify if we are overconfident. By understanding that we are overconfident, even though we feel justified in our confidence, we can prevent ourselves from engaging in detrimental behaviours.   The ability to use our rational mind to balance our emotional mind and prevent our behaviours and actions from being dictated by only how we feel is a critical skill for emotional and behavioural self-regulation. Since around 2020, there has been increasing debate around the validity of the Dunning-Kruger effect. The following is some  optional  reading for those interested in diving deeper into the state of the research. Against Dunning-Kruger:  The arguments against Dunning-Kruger are a valid scientific inquiry, with the most robust positing that all humans are generally susceptible to bias and that bias does not disproportionately affect those with lower knowledge.  This is a contentious point. While we know that bias affects all humans, some research suggests that systems of proactively moderating biases are effective. There is also research around the illusion of competency and the illusion of fluency, which closely parallel the findings from the Dunning-Kruger effect.  In reality, we are likely oversimplifying the relationship between knowledge and confidence. There are plenty of overconfident people who are highly skilled, and there are plenty of unskilled and unknowledgeable beginners who are rightly humble.   On the other hand, many non-researchers are quick to criticise the Dunning-Kruger effect based on their limited knowledge. In reality, only one or two articles are inadequate to form an opinion about the scientific validity of something. Ironically, non-researchers are often on Mount Stupid regarding their view on Dunning-Kruger... Against the graph:  Some people contest the validity of the Dunning-Kruger effect because the graph is not from the original paper. However, proponents of this tend to ignore the empirical support for this graph that has subsequently been observed in a range of studies. We provide some key examples in the references below. The state of the science:  The current state of the science is not conclusive. Based on the current literature, we can say with reasonable confidence that:   Knowledge and skill level  do  seem to have an influence on confidence.  Unlike many other psychological processes, the Dunning-Kruger effect has some neurophysiological evidence that supports the use of different cognitive processes in over or under-estimators.  The extent and universality of this is  not  clearly defined, and it seems to vary depending on context.  Overconfidence seems to be more consistently found for skills than domain knowledge, which varies more significantly between studies.  The level of over or under-estimation seems to depend on the difficulty and  complexity  of the task (more complex tasks are more likely to have overconfidence), the  actual knowledge  and expertise of the person, and the persons level of personal self-esteem and general self-confidence.  Peripheral, semi-consilient research on cognitive biases and perceptions of skill and expertise is broadly  supportive  of a Dunning Kruger pattern.  Knowing about the Dunning-Kruger effect can be  protective  for it.   The final point is incredibly important and is one of the most recent findings  i.e. that teaching someone about Dunning-Kruger can be enough to statistically eliminate it from some empirical studies. Our experience:  In our own experience, we have  strongly  found that new members who are overconfident have a massively higher proportion of technique error compared to students who are:   Intuitively overconfident, but moderate themselves and force humility and open-mindedness; or  Intuitively underconfident and doubt their abilities.    By far, the worst results received by our students are from overconfident beginners, who are usually overconfident because they have achieved prior academic success using their old techniques.  While the old techniques that worked well for them were usually quite valid, weve found that overconfidence can prevent them from changing the parts that didnt work or are holding them back. In many cases, this unwillingness to change or modify techniques causes barriers to further improving their techniques in later stages, where many component skills must be mastered before we combine them into an advanced version. References:  Acosta, G., Smith, E., & Kreinovich, V. (2020). Analytical Techniques for Gauging Accuracy of Expert Knowledge: A Simple System-Based Explanation of the DunningKruger Effect. In  Towards Analytical Techniques for Systems Engineering Applications  (pp. 27-32). Springer, Cham.  Burson, K. A., Larrick, R. P., & Klayman, J. (2006). Skilled or unskilled, but still unaware of it: how perceptions of difficulty drive miscalibration in relative comparisons.  Journal of personality and social psychology, 90 (1), 60.  Coutinho, M. V., Thomas, J., Alsuwaidi, A. S., & Couchman, J. J. (2021). Dunning-Kruger Effect: Intuitive Errors Predict Overconfidence on the Cognitive Reflection Test.  Frontiers in psychology, 12, 1040.  Dunning, D., Heath, C., & Suls, J. M. (2004). Flawed self-assessment: Implications for health, education, and the workplace.  Psychological science in the public interest, 5 (3), 69-106.
