metadata.docName: How do I avoid feeling overwhelmed during classes and lectures?
metadata.originalText: Although the techniques we teach you will help you to improve your overall learning ability massively, it doesn't happen overnight.  If you are struggling to process fast enough in class or lectures, there are a few key considerations and tips.   Your brain struggles to try and understand  what  to know if it doesn't know  why  it's relevant.  Prestudy primes your brain to know how to think about new information and how to assign it relevance. The most common reason for struggling to process during class is a lack of relevance. The brain is trying to learn the information and place it somewhere, but because there is no organisational structure, it doesn't know where to place it.  This creates  cognitive overload, and unless your processing ability is at a higher-intermediate to advanced level, this will be impossible to manage.  Prestudying separates these processes by helping you to build a big-picture scaffold of the information first, so that during class you can simply focus on connecting the information and integrating it into your existing network. Key principle:   Although the techniques we teach you will help you to improve your overall learning ability massively, it doesn't happen overnight.  If you are struggling to process fast enough in class or lectures, there are a few key considerations and tips.   Your brain struggles to try and understand  what  to know if it doesn't know  why  it's relevant.  Prestudy primes your brain to know how to think about new information and how to assign it relevance. The most common reason for struggling to process during class is a lack of relevance. The brain is trying to learn the information and place it somewhere, but because there is no organisational structure, it doesn't know where to place it.  This creates  cognitive overload, and unless your processing ability is at a higher-intermediate to advanced level, this will be impossible to manage.  Prestudying separates these processes by helping you to build a big-picture scaffold of the information first, so that during class you can simply focus on connecting the information and integrating it into your existing network. As the images below illustrate, prestudy gives new information a place to fit. Potential connections have already been scoped and hypothesised. The brain is able to use its limited resources to understand the new information, connect it to our network of knowledge, and evaluate whether it makes sense.   In most instances, effective prestudy means you should be able to think faster than a person can teach. This translates to asking deeper, more meaningful questions and being able to extract more value from your lesson and teaching session.   Another reason for being overwhelmed may be that you are currently unable to process auditory information efficiently because the necessary cognitive skills are underdeveloped. There is no way around this other than diligent training over time.   The techniques we teach will allow you to train and improve this as long as you approach your practice as instructed, following our practice guidelines. Training your ability to process auditory information -  Step 1: Listen and try to connect the new information to your prior knowledge as much as possible. Simplify the information, group/chunk it, and find similarities/differences while they are speaking. Step 2: When you feel like you are unable to hold onto any more information mentally, quickly map out all of your current thoughts on your notes. Step 3: Repeat from step 1, but now referencing your mapped notes occasionally to look for more ways to connect, simplify and group information. Step 4: Continue this process and aim to process 1% more information mentally by per attempt, before needing to write it down. At the university level, 10 to 15 minutes during a dense lecture before needing to offload signifies a high level of processing ability.

metadata.docName: What is the best way to space my retrieval sessions?
metadata.originalText: Encoding and retrieval are two sides of the learning coin.  You cannot be efficient and accurate without both encoding and retrieval. Outside of inadequate encoding,"retrieval failure" is a common cause of knowledge decay of previously encoded information.   When information is encoded to a higher quality, we need less retrieval. Key principles: 1. As encoding gets better, we forget less and need to do less retrieval2. Encoding skill takes time to develop, during this time, retrieval is crucial to address knowledge decay3. We always need some level of retrieval because our brain is never leak-proof (i.e. there is always some knowledge decay) Technically, encoding and retrieval are not completely separate processes. The majority opinion in learning science is that encoding and retrieval influence each other as fundamentally intertwined processes, rather than two distinct processes.  In our program, we discuss them separately because the physical actions and techniques used for optimising retrieval tend to be distinct from the methods used for first-pass encoding. From a practical perspective, keeping the two separate is more helpful.   As explained in the spacing lesson, it is not necessary to follow an ultra-strict spacing schedule.  We recommend using some form of semi-regular spacing that allows you to keep on top of your material. General rule:  Technically, encoding and retrieval are not completely separate processes. The majority opinion in learning science is that encoding and retrieval influence each other as fundamentally intertwined processes, rather than two distinct processes.  In our program, we discuss them separately because the physical actions and techniques used for optimising retrieval tend to be distinct from the methods used for first-pass encoding. From a practical perspective, keeping the two separate is more helpful.   As explained in the spacing lesson, it is not necessary to follow an ultra-strict spacing schedule.  We recommend using some form of semi-regular spacing that allows you to keep on top of your material. Here is an example of this rule which would be perfectly acceptable if applied in practice:  **Spaced Retrieval Schedule**  **Day 1 and 2 (Monday and Tuesday):** * Same-day retrieval: Learn and revise new material.  **Day 3 (Wednesday):** * One-day retrieval: Interleaved retrieval session for Monday and Tuesday content (and Wednesday content if new material was learned).  **Day 4 (Thursday):** * Same-day retrieval for new content: Learn and revise new material.  **Day 5 or 6 (Friday or Saturday):** * One-week retrieval: Interleaved retrieval session for all content over the last 2 weeks.  **End of Month:** * One-month retrieval: Interleaved retrieval session for the last 4 weeks.  **3 to 4 Weeks Prior to Assessment:** * One-month retrieval+: Retrieve everything relevant for the assessments. With the above schedule, we have a same-day revision, a mid-week revision, an end-of-week revision and an end-of-month revision. The end-of-week revision encompasses the last 2 weeks, and the end-of-month encompasses the last 4 weeks.  All material will be revised within 1 to 3 days of first learning it, then 1 to 2 weeks later, then 2 to 4 weeks later. When combined with prestudy and on-the-day studying, this means that any material is encoded and re-encoded (via interleaved retrieval practice) around 6 times in a given month. A track record of success:   Here is an example of this rule which would be perfectly acceptable if applied in practice:  **Spaced Retrieval Schedule**  **Day 1 and 2 (Monday and Tuesday):** * Same-day retrieval: Learn and revise new material.  **Day 3 (Wednesday):** * One-day retrieval: Interleaved retrieval session for Monday and Tuesday content (and Wednesday content if new material was learned).  **Day 4 (Thursday):** * Same-day retrieval for new content: Learn and revise new material.  **Day 5 or 6 (Friday or Saturday):** * One-week retrieval: Interleaved retrieval session for all content over the last 2 weeks.  **End of Month:** * One-month retrieval: Interleaved retrieval session for the last 4 weeks.  **3 to 4 Weeks Prior to Assessment:** * One-month retrieval+: Retrieve everything relevant for the assessments. With the above schedule, we have a same-day revision, a mid-week revision, an end-of-week revision and an end-of-month revision. The end-of-week revision encompasses the last 2 weeks, and the end-of-month encompasses the last 4 weeks.  All material will be revised within 1 to 3 days of first learning it, then 1 to 2 weeks later, then 2 to 4 weeks later. When combined with prestudy and on-the-day studying, this means that any material is encoded and re-encoded (via interleaved retrieval practice) around 6 times in a given month. Isn't this excessive and time-consuming?:  A spacing schedule like the above can seem excessive for those with lower levels of encoding. This is because revising an entire month's worth of content might seem like a titanic task.  In reality, effective encoding techniques will mean that approximately 80 to 90% of the material is retained by the end-of-week revision, meaning that one week's material can be revised in just a few hours.  Because each subsequent interleaved retrieval session reduces the amount of knowledge decay and re-encodes information, the end-of-month revision for 4 weeks' worth of material might be a half-day activity.  Based on the systems we teach in our program, we are also likely to be prestudying and learning material in batches, ahead of formal class/lecture/course schedules. This means that we have concentrated bursts of studying, getting a month or more ahead of'class', with spaced retrieval of just a few hours every week.   As such, the total time spent and ease of maintaining such a schedule is extremely easy. What if my encoding skills are not good enough yet?:  This kind of spacing schedule can be difficult for those with low encoding ability. However, because encoding and retrieval practice are both necessary for performance, deficiencies in encoding must be addressed through more retrieval. Over time, as encoding improves, you will naturally see the time taken for retrieval reduce.    In the short term, we recommend having a strict spaced interleaved retrieval schedule as a core foundation of your learning system.

Now, we will introduce a system to ensure we curb our neuroticism. Remember, we want motivation to enhance our performance, but we dont want to be dependent on motivation.  We will use the PEER-Peer system to execute actions. It has a low learning curve, is easy to set up, and you will see benefits immediately. Alert:  The PEER-Peer system is considered a core pillar technique of the program.

This learning system is specifically for healthcare and clinical practitioners who have a unique balance of:   High learning volumes  Daily intensive and diverse experience (natural interleaving)  Conflicts between what is required to be learned in work vs. in external curriculums and exams  Very limited learning time  Diverse and multi-order retrieval needs Progression note:  We recommend this system for those at least at Base camp level or beyond. The technical proficiency and cognitive habits to perform this system efficiently (without causing unnecessary time wastage and confusion) are very high. If you are below this level but have clinical learning needs, you may be able to extract from this system and apply aspects and principles to your learning system, even without following it strictly. Clinical learning system instructions - these steps may seem complicated at first, but they will become intuitive as you see how the system works and builds on itself over time. Step 1: Identify learning points from a key patient that was seen that day. Step 2: Map out the most important learning point with a brain dump (using a clinical reasoning, diagnosis and management map for the patient journey is usually the most appropriate structure). Step 3: Include the most obvious branching points on your map where the reasoning pathway or patient journey may significantly change (the depth and number of these branching points depends on your level). Step 4: Map out how these branching points will change the journey (this can be a general overview rather than detailed step-by-step maps). Step 5: At this point, we should have one fairly detailed patient flow with a number of basic branches, depending on different possible findings, results or complications. If you have pertinent and important gaps in your base flow, study to fill these in. Step 6: Start going through the most pertinent branching points based on which variations seem the most common or important (you may only be able to address a single branching point per day). Step 7: While studying these branching points and variations, study the relevant auxiliary topics that contribute to the variation (e.g. inflammatory processes and cytokines or antibiotic mechanisms and side effects). Step 8: Continue the above two steps for approximately two to three days. Step 9: Pick a new patient that has a pathway or variation that overlaps in some way with your previous maps. Step 10: Nearer to your exam or assessment, review through your learning material or curriculum for smaller details and learn through memorisation techniques. Initially, this process is very overwhelming due to the total amount of unknowns and gaps in knowledge. Over time, due to the extremely high interleaving that is created by this system and the naturally high interleaved nature of clinical practice, your knowledge will build across the board in a fluid and immediately practical relevant way. Eventually, each new patient introduces fewer unknown variables or new branching points. Key benefits of this system: 1. Primes for future relevant clinical learning experiences.2. Reviews common and important cases and conditions.3. Creates opportunities to learn topics that are relevant to the cases, although not related to the condition directly (e.g. studying pain management and infection as a branch from an initial orthopedic surgery case).4. Engages in extremely high interleaving.5. Promotes a high level of clinical fluidity and reasoning skills.6. Promotes a high level of retention for new material studied (due to high relevance, retrieval and priming).7. Promotes a high level of nuanced depth of knowledge. Example:  Check out the next sublesson,  Clinical system: Deep dive  to see an example of a student who has tried this.

Key principles: 1. Beware of the reversion response to fear2. Challenging goals require additional skills3. Developing skills require navigation through the fear zone4. Beware of the misinterpreted-effort hypothesis5. Aim to develop a growth mindset with the strategies at the end of this lesson Many people spend too long on becoming 100% certain about the theory before attempting to use skills and gain real experience. This is detrimental to learning and can cause a common issue of  information over experimentation.   In reality, practice is essential to give relevance to theory. Our questions become more targeted, and we can enter the  theory-practice cycle, which is the engine for skill development. When the theory-practice cycle is delayed or infrequent, skill development is slow and ineffective.    If accessing the growth zone is a significant barrier, complete our  30-day mindset challenge! Find it in the supplementary modules on your dashboard.

Key principles: 1. Rushing is often due to insufficient time invested into practice2. Rushing almost always leads to failure (usually while being overconfident)3. One of the most common features of a rusher is thinking Im different and justifying ignoring advice or skipping steps

metadata.docName: I'm afraid of missing details while studying. What should I do?
metadata.originalText: The fear of forgetting or missing out on details is valid and understandable. Fortunately, it is also unnecessary. This article will explain why.   A learning system involves multiple stages of studying. Missing a detail now simply means we will pick it up in a later stage, potentially using a technique that is much more suited for that detail.   In this diagram, you can see that multiple layers reduce the risk of errors. For learning systems, each layer represents an encoding or retrieval session. The holes in each session reflect the methods we use. A complete learning system should use methods of various orders of learning, with enough retrieval practice to catch knowledge gaps. Not all information is appropriate and relevant to learn immediately. You wouldn't think to learn high school biology by dissecting a biological science Ph.D. thesis and working your way backwards. We understand that it would be far too overwhelming with information we are not ready to process and make sense of.   The same is true for even a single textbook chapter. Some sentences, facts, and pieces of information are slightly too detailed for us to process them easily. Returning to this once we have more background knowledge often makes this information easier to process and understand. When information is learned in isolation, such as with most memorisation techniques, without connecting it clearly to the big picture or other related concepts, our retention suffers.  Instead, if we were to come back to it when we have a stronger organisational structure. Even tiny details can make more sense and seem obvious when we know more about the other related ideas.   Ironically, focusing on details because we are afraid of missing them makes us much more likely to forget them! Beware:   Isolation leads to information death. Our learning systems are built to be highly practical under realistic academic and learning pressures. This is what makes our methods so unique compared to other overly theoretical or idealistic methods. Common reasons for low retention: 1. Insufficient or inappropriate spaced interleaved retrieval2. Poor quality encoding3. Incomplete learning system with gaps (sometimes due to rushing or selective learning)4. Limitations due to poor self-management, such as excessive procrastination or urgency trapping

To focus is to concentrate deeply on a task and shut out distractions. It enables you to achieve more within a given time than someone who lacks focus. Here are some points to help you create and maintain focus:    Sleep:  You must protect your sleep. In general, aim for at least 8 to 9 hours of sleep. Sleep has dramatic carry-over effects on focus, energy, mood, and  sleep-dependent memory consolidation  (a critical learning phenomenon).   Remove distractions:  Before you start a focused work session, spend time removing all possible distractions. Remember to use your distraction cheat sheet to note down everything that distracts you, and make sure to remove all of those things at the beginning of your next study session.   Breaks:  Have breaks more often, but keep them short (i.e. high frequency, low duration). This is because our body recovers faster when it is less exhausted. Frequent, shorter breaks allow for longer overall focus throughout the day  so its better to have a 20 to 30-minute study session and a5-minute break than to study for 4 hours and have a1-hour break. To keep the length of your break under control, use a timer set to 5, 10 or 15-minute intervals.   We will add more advanced variations of these techniques later in the course.

Retention  is the ability to remember something without forgetting it and retrieve it whenever necessary. If our retention is poor, most of our time is spent relearning information weve forgotten. If we improve our retention, the time required to learn a topic drastically decreases. Measuring learning efficiency:  A common trap people fall into is prematurely thinking that learning techniques dont work for them because they are  measuring retention in inaccurate ways See our article  here  for how you can measure your real learning efficiency.   You may be interested in this video which mentions the idea of measuring learning using useless metrics:  https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=P4_j2VggToM

The following techniques have been replaced or upgraded. metadata.docName: My progress is too slow if I only work on 2 or 3 processes at a time. What should I do?
metadata.originalText: In our program, we teach that you should only focus on two or three processes to improve at any given time. Many learners feel their progression speed will be too slow if they restrict themselves to this pace. However, the reality is that this pace (which may appear slow) is likely the fastest way for someone to improve. The conflict lies in the learner's expectations vs. reality.   Many learners view skill development like adding tools to a toolkit. The more tools we have, the more equipped we will be to meet our challenges. While this is true to an extent and can be a useful analogy, the process of developing these skills (acquiring each tool) is more complicated.    Unfortunately, the process of acquiring skills is more like a computer solving a complex algorithm. Each skill has a set of variables that must be understood to acquire the skill correctly. Only after deciphering these variables can we use the skill. This takes time, effort, and processing power through mental energy and cognitive load.  When we try to develop too many skills simultaneously, we sacrifice our ability to acquire the skill, which increases confusion and frustration. By limiting the number of skills and processes we try to master at the same time, we improve our ability to acquire the skills quickly.   In the diagram above, you can see that the process of acquiring skills involves:   Identifying barriers  Analysing root causes that create those barriers  Proposing solutions  Repeating this process until there are no more barriers   This takes time, experience, and self-reflection, which helps us analyse and process experiences to turn them into learning opportunities.   By focusing on too many skills at the same time, we ultimately reduce our skill development speed because:    Our limited time, effort, and energy is spread across too many experiences  Our brain enters into overload as there are too many variables to keep track of simultaneously, creating confusion and increasing errors    Although it would be ideal to learn as many skills as we need all at the same time, this is unfortunately not realistic. Sometimes, the skills we need to acquire are greater than our ability to acquire them within a certain time period. Only after deciphering these variables can we use the skill. This takes time, effort, and processing power through mental energy and cognitive load.  When we try to develop too many skills simultaneously, we sacrifice our ability to acquire the skill, which increases confusion and frustration. By limiting the number of skills and processes we try to master at the same time, we improve our ability to acquire the skills quickly.   In the diagram above, you can see that the process of acquiring skills involves:   Identifying barriers  Analysing root causes that create those barriers  Proposing solutions  Repeating this process until there are no more barriers   This takes time, experience, and self-reflection, which helps us analyse and process experiences to turn them into learning opportunities.   By focusing on too many skills at the same time, we ultimately reduce our skill development speed because:    Our limited time, effort, and energy is spread across too many experiences  Our brain enters into overload as there are too many variables to keep track of simultaneously, creating confusion and increasing errors    Although it would be ideal to learn as many skills as we need all at the same time, this is unfortunately not realistic. Sometimes, the skills we need to acquire are greater than our ability to acquire them within a certain time period. As such, even if we  need  many skills, the best course of action is still to develop each skill as quickly as possible. To do this, we must counter-intuitively reduce the number of skills we try to acquire simultaneously.   By doing so, we improve our concentration and depth.

metadata.docName: How do you measure knowledge mastery?
metadata.originalText: Learning is inherently complex and individually variable. Measuring how good of a"learner" you are can be challenging and intangible. The following metrics are useful guidelines so that you can create meaningful goals and track your progress.   We find that a composite between Bloom's revised taxonomy and SOLO taxonomy (structure of observed learning outcomes) are useful measures for mastery of knowledge. We've outlined our framework below, inspired by Bloom's and SOLO taxonomies.  Higher levels of mastery mean your learning is deeper, which tends to correlate with being able to solve more complex problems and having a higher retention of knowledge. Level 1 (lowest):  You can recall facts and isolated details only. Your learning tends to be heavily repetition and memorisation-based. You can recognise terminology and understand what it means.   Common methods:    Flashcards on facts and definitions.  Cover-copy-check.  Questions on isolated details    Commonly tested by:    Fact recall questions Level 2:  You can explain isolated concepts and have discussions about individual concepts. You can solve simple problems using this concept. You can engage in simple discussions about the concept but will struggle to discuss extended ideas or atypical applications of the concept.   Common methods:    Flashcards on explaining concepts or processes.  Questions on explaining how a process or concept works.  Teaching someone about a single concept.  Process maps and flow diagrams showing how concepts work.    Commonly tested by:    Questions to explain and describe processes and concepts Level 3:  You can see how concepts are related to each other. You analyse and compare concepts against each other. Relationships are between individual concepts but are not strongly connected to the big picture of the entire topic.   You can have in-depth discussions about the topic. You may not be able to converse at an expert level, but you might know what questions to ask an expert.   Common methods:    Teaching about how two or more concepts are related to each other.  Comparing and contrasting multiple concepts.  Questions that test the relationship between two or more concepts.  Mindmaps that show how concepts are related to each other.    Commonly tested by:    Questions that ask you to compare and contrast multiple concepts against each other  Problems that require you to draw on knowledge from multiple concepts Level 4 (Significant learning benefits):  You have a heavily connected appreciation of the relationships between multiple concepts. You can see how individual facts and details fit into the big picture. Often, your understanding feels"simpler" and more"intuitive", with very little information purely memorised through repetition.   You should be able to have contextually nuanced discussions with an expert about the topic, navigating between concepts fluidly and quickly appreciating how changes to one concept can impact not only other concepts but the impact it has on the overall topic, process or function.   The difference in impact between level 3 and level 4 is significant.  **Level 3: Identifying Relationships, Similarities, and Differences**  * Impact on Learning: Moderately helpful * Description: Students begin to develop connections and networks by identifying relationships, similarities, and differences between concepts and ideas.  **Level 4: Making Value Judgments and Evaluating Importance and Relevance**  * Impact on Learning: Extremely helpful * Description: Students engage in deeper encoding and processing of information by making value judgments and evaluating the overall importance and relevance of the relationships, similarities, and differences they have identified. This forces them to critically analyze and synthesize information, leading to a more comprehensive understanding.  Common methods:    Chunkmaps that show how concepts are categorised into groups, based on similarities, as well as the relationships between these chunks, with emphasis on the more important relationships compared to weaker and less important relationships.  Questions that require justification and discussion around the relative importance of concepts with respect to other concepts and relationships.  Whole-Part-Whole reteaching technique (as is taught in the course).  Discussion on how different contexts affect the importance of concepts and their relationships.    Commonly tested by:    Questions that require nuanced discussion of the influence of concepts on each other  Complex problems that draw on knowledge of multiple concepts and their influence on each other  Contextually abstract problems and questions that require the application of knowledge in diverse, multi-factorial scenarios Level 5:  You are creating new and novel information using your knowledge. You create hypotheses and conjectures.   Common methods:    Novel research and exploration of a thesis.  Discussion of ideas that are extensions of known principles and prior knowledge.  Hypothesising structures and connections using prior knowledge.  Exploring new information like an experiment to confirm or reject these hypotheses.    Commonly tested by:    High-level narrative, commentary, or research  Typically, there are no assessments at this level in formal education prior to Ph.D. Key principle:   Learning is inherently complex and individually variable. Measuring how good of a"learner" you are can be challenging and intangible. The following metrics are useful guidelines so that you can create meaningful goals and track your progress.   We find that a composite between Bloom's revised taxonomy and SOLO taxonomy (structure of observed learning outcomes) are useful measures for mastery of knowledge. We've outlined our framework below, inspired by Bloom's and SOLO taxonomies.  Higher levels of mastery mean your learning is deeper, which tends to correlate with being able to solve more complex problems and having a higher retention of knowledge. Important considerations: 1. Both lower-order and higher-order methods are needed in a comprehensive learning system.2. Lower-order learning approaches only lead to lower-order outcomes.3. Higher-order approaches lead to both lower-order and higher-order outcomes.4. It is always better to start your learning with higher-order approaches, and use lower-order methods as a supplement or closer to assessments.5. Higher-order learning methods make learning new content easier over time as you build expertise. Lower-order methods tends to make you more overwhelmed as new information stays isolated rather than consolidating into a network. References  Adams N. E. (2015). Bloom's taxonomy of cognitive learning objectives.  Journal of the Medical Library Association: JMLA,  103 (3), 152153.  https://doi.org/10.3163/1536-5050.103.3.010   Chan, Charles & Tsui, M. & Chan, Mandy & Hong, Joe. (2002). Applying the Structure of the Observed Learning Outcomes (SOLO) Taxonomy on Student's Learning Outcomes: An empirical study. Assessment & Evaluation in Higher Education - ASSESS EVAL HIGH EDUC. 27. 511-527. 10.1080/0260293022000020282.

Study scheduling guidelines - Follow these scheduling guidelines to sustainably cover your core encoding and retrieval sessions needs. Step 1: A simple guideline for revision spacing is 1 day, 1 week and 1 month. If you have a similar spacing schedule that you have learned from our previous lessons, feel free to continue using that. Step 2: Schedule retrieval sessions in your calendar, and stick to that schedule. Step 3: Prestudy material before class. This will save you time later by reducing the amount of revision you need to do. Step 4: Schedule time for prestudy. Prioritise and protect this time, especially when youre busy. Step 5: Snowball your learning of the topic. Learn superficially but broadly during prestudy, and eventually add more in-depth knowledge every time you study it. The following is a basic schedule that you can follow to start with. This schedule should be sufficient for most learners to get used to following the essential guidelines above.   For more advanced schedules for spaced interleaved retrieval, read our article on spacing and scheduling    here.  **Pre-Study and Revision Schedule**  **Sunday (Anytime)**  * Prestudy the week's material:     * Focus on concepts and their relationships     * Create a basic mind map  **End of Every Weekday (After Class)**  * Revise the day's material:     * Build a detailed understanding of the topic     * Create flashcards and elaborate on the mind map     * Focus on concepts and processes, not memorization  **End of Every Week (Friday Evening or Saturday)**  * Revise the entire week's material:     * Use interleaved retrieval methods     * Identify and address weaknesses     * Begin memorizing details     * Use teaching and challenging questions  **During the Week (Any Available Time)**  * Go through flashcards:     * Focus on available time, not completion  **End of the Month (Weekend)**  * Revise the entire month's material:     * Use interleaved retrieval methods     * Most details should be memorized from flashcards  **Two to Three Weeks Before Test or Exam**  * Do more retrieval practice:     * Use interleaved revision techniques (practice papers, challenge questions, peer quizzing)     * Brush up on forgotten details If you have a lot of content to cover, you can add another revision session in the middle of the month (as a fortnightly review) or the middle of the week (as a2-day review).  You can adjust the exact days in the example schedule to suit your needs. For example, instead of Sunday anytime, you can do your prestudy on Saturday or Monday morning. You can do two weeks worth of prestudy at a time instead of one weeks worth. In later lessons, we will develop your ability to prestudy an entire topic (46 weeks worth) in one go. Preview is better than review:  Prestudying saves time on revision as you forget less. Therefore, as your prestudy technique improves, your revision time will decrease. Given the choice between doing good prestudy vs. reviewing previous material, always choose prestudy.   You can always catch up on reviewing material later, but prestudying helps you have less to catch up on in the first place.  I'm struggling to follow these schedules. What should I do?:  When we start to put more of these tasks in your schedule, it's common to identify weaknesses in your time and task management skills. In most cases, if you struggle to follow these schedules, it is because of:   issues with basic time management skills, or  issues with task prioritisation (such as prioritising retrieval sessions instead of good quality prestudy sessions)   Depending on where your issue is, this could be a cue for you to work on reviewing your self-management skills from the earlier modules. Just because this program starts with one lesson and moves in a linear order, don't let that stop you from going back and reviewing older lessons!   Great learners know when to go back and forward based on closely monitoring their knowledge gaps. How long do I need to practise using these schedules to know I have competence?:  Give yourself a couple of weeks to use this new kind of schedule. You can also work on other techniques simultaneously. Scheduling does not have a long learning curve, so most students can set a schedule and start using it relatively quickly.   However, it can take a little experimenting to make it suit your needs. Make sure you get real-life experience and reflect weekly on what isnt working and how you can improve it.

Our brain is the same as it has been since caveman times, but modern learning is very different. The way we tend to learn now is deeply influenced by the formal education system and the industrial revolution.  As a result, the way many people learn is disconnected from how our brains want to and prefer learning. Our  cave theory  tells us we need to learn how the brain likes to learn, which makes learning more effective, engaging, and even enjoyable!   The following are new techniques or systems we will introduce in Technique Training. **New Techniques**  * **Traffic Light System:**     * Rationale: Introduces self-facilitated inquiry-based learning for beginners.     * Skills learned in this technique serve as a foundation for more advanced learning in the Ascent stages.  * **Scheduling Basics:**     * Rationale: Provides additional scheduling tips to enhance time management and stay on track. Don't stop!:   Remember to continue the other parts of your system we've already learned.    Just because we don't teach you any new techniques about time management or retrieval (for example) in one stage doesn't mean you should stop using what you already learned.

Key points: 1. Consistency is better than sudden bursts of effort2. Marginal gains are not only easier and more sustainable, they also produce a greater total effect3. Marginal gains stack and compound, which is why starting imperfectly now is better than starting perfectly later and missing out on compound gains In our experience, it is extremely rare for learners to think about progress in marginal gains (especially among our younger high school learners). Adopting this method of thinking is a sign of intelligence and maturity, as it indicates the person:   Appreciates that skills take time to develop  Understands that time spent on trying to make"large strides" instead of marginal gains is often wasted and ineffective  Recognises that the worst case scenario is not that they develop the skill slowly, but they may fail to develop it at all. Key principle:  Marginal gains create safety of success. Therefore, progressing through"large strides" and"big jumps" is unwise because it sacrifices safety for a temporary (and unlikely) boost.

You've reached Summit! During this stage, you'll learn some advanced variations of the encoding techniques you already know. Using these variations, we'll optimise for situations such as cramming, dealing with curveball questions, and exam execution to make sure you can confidently perform at your peak in any situation.  **Cramming and Multipass**  * Advanced variation of the BHS (Brain-Based Learning System) * Suitable for rapid learning sprints * Enhances learning efficiency and speed  **Exam Execution**  * Techniques to optimize exam performance * Focuses on marginal gains in preparation * Complements strong learning techniques  All other techniques from previous stages continue as prior.   Watch the sublesson below to see a recap of what our system might look like at this stage.

As the techniques in this stage do not require much (if any) cognitive retraining, you should be able to progress through this stage relatively quickly. Use the table below to check your general competence level, and feel free to move onto Camp II as soon as you feel ready. **Techniques for Enhancing Learning**  **Flashcard Usage**  * **Level of Competency:** Advanced * **Benefits:**     * Active, accurate, and sustainable management of flashcards     * Fills rote-learning gaps in layers 3 and 4 of learning  **Modified Method of Loci**  * **Level of Competency:** Intermediate * **Benefits:**     * Seamless integration into GRINDE maps and mind maps     * Enhances learning in layers 3 and 4  **Focus Training and PEER-Peer System**  * **Level of Competency:** Beginner * **Benefits:**     * Fixed part of daily routine     * Potential improvements in natural ability to focus

General flashcard guidelines -  Step 1: Use digital flashcards (e.g., Anki) Step 2: Only use flashcards for layers three and four (the important and arbitrary details). Step 3: Reduce your number of flashcards as much as possible. You shouldnt need more than 50 flashcards for an entire topic. If there are more than 50, there may be a mistake in your conceptual learning. Step 4: Each flashcard should have a targeted question and a discrete fact. Do not include more than two or three facts on a single flashcard. Step 5: Even if you dont know the answer to a flashcard, give it your best guess before checking the answer. Its not about getting it right; its about generating an answer and correcting yourself to activate generation and hypercorrection effects. Key principle:  The  generation  effect is critical for effective learning. Generating an answer produces more learning than simply checking an answer. For example, reading the answers for a practice exam is less effective than taking the time to write real answers.  The  hypercorrection  effect is a perfect synergy. Making a mistake and being corrected produces a higher level of learning than getting it right the first time. The more confidence you had in your incorrect answer, the higher your level of learning will be. The Leitner system is an implementation strategy of spaced repetition commonly adapted and utilised for flashcards and microlearning.   As you can see from the diagram above,  incorrect flashcards move backwards  while  correct cards move forwards.  If we track the movement of a single card, it might look like this:   Answered correctly  Moves to box 2, due for a repeat in 1 hour  Answered correctly again  Moves to box 3, due for a repeat in 4 hours  Answered correctly again  Moves to box 4, due for a repeat in 16 hours  Answered incorrectly  Moves back to box 3, due for a repeat in 4 hours  Answered incorrectly  Moves back to box 2, due for a repeat in 1 hour    One benefit of the Leitner system is that it prioritises weak areas.    However, a disadvantage is that cards can accumulate quickly if you get many incorrect. If you have lots of flashcards (e.g., 200+), it is difficult to get them all correct. As a result, earlier stage boxes start overflowing, making it hard to stay on top of your flashcards.   This is where our flashcard management guidelines come into play. Instructions -  Step 1: Combine Step 2: Cut Step 3: Critique Expand the accordions below to learn more about each rule. Combine:   If you get a flashcard correct three times in a row:   Merge them with another conceptually related card that you also got correct three times to create a higher-order question.   For example, if one card asks about the definition and purpose of judicial law and another asks about the definition and purpose of parliamentary law, you could combine them to create a question like,"What are the key differences between the definitions and purposes of judicial law compared with parliamentary law?". A further combination could be even more higher-order, such as,"How important is judicial law compared to parliamentary law in heavily litigious industries?"   This way, we reduce the number of flashcards in the deck while testing ourselves at higher orders. When combining cards, ensure that you include the lower-order information from each card as a prerequisite knowledge to answer the new higher-order prompt. You wouldn't want to create a card on two conceptually related pieces, but ask a completely different angle that does not build on your existing knowledge. In the examples above, the knowledge of definitions and purpose is still tested, just at a higher order. Cut:   If you get a flashcard correct three times in a row (and there are no conceptually related cards to combine with):   Remove this card from your deck. This rule is flexible: if you are worried, leave the flashcard in the deck and let the spacing algorithm naturally extend the delay between attempts. However, if you feel cluttered with cards, these cards should be the first to be removed.  At the very least, change the wording on the card to prevent yourself from falling into a  recognition trap  where you are unable to recall the information without this highly specific cue. Critique:   If you get a flashcard incorrect three times (out of 5 attempts):   Go back to the material and learn more deeply about it. Go outside the scope of the curriculum, consider its applications, and try to create a more robust conceptual understanding. You must give the information more relevance to prevent yourself from repeatedly forgetting it.   These cards you get wrong consistently will take up more time because you never clear them from your deck. It is worthwhile to invest five to 10 minutes to consolidate more deeply instead of sticking with a lower-order approach, which has proved ineffective.

Key principles: 1. Our ability to decide is diminished by too many choices and variables2. Choice throttling involves restricting our decisions and thinking to individual variables This concept is also related to a common scientific tool known as the    dichotomous key. Instructions -  Step 1: Identify the important, specific variables that create uncertainty in your decision. Step 2: Ask a closed (often binary yes/no) question for each of these variables. Step 3: Make your decision based on the answers to each of your binary questions. Even complex decisions can be made much easier with this method.  Note that your ability to choice throttle depends on your self-awareness, insight, reflective ability and (depending on the context) your level of information about the decision and the consequences. The core program helps to give you tools to develop these (e.g. Kolb's, marginal gains, reverse goal setting, etc.).   Furthermore,  simpler  decisions do not necessarily mean  faster. For example, it may take months to properly answer yes or no to one question. This is often the case when determining which values matter most to us.

To use interleaving, review information from  multiple angles and perspectives. Research strongly shows that using more interleaving produces better results. It also protects you from  curveball  exam questions   because you are used to seeing the information in multiple ways. SIR is here to stay:  Remember, you will rely less on retrieval as you get better at encoding.  However, every learning system should always have at least some level of interleaved retrieval practice, even if your encoding is perfect. This is because the human brain always prunes information that isn't being used. The goal of every learner is to develop  expertise. Expertise means you can answer any question and solve any problem. However, an expert's"knowledge structure" or"knowledge network" (i.e. how they have organised information in their brain) is different to that of a novice.  If we don't know how to create the knowledge structure of an expert, it's very hard to develop expertise. Whenever we do any learning (encoding or retrieval), we should always try to build true expertise. This improves memory and fluency.   To understand what"expertise" looks and feels like, we recommend reading our article    here    on higher-order vs. lower-order learning to understand how we can think about mastery and expertise. Terminology:  From here on, we will continue to use terminology related to higher-order learning and developing expertise, as explained in the article.

metadata.docName: What should I do if my retrieval sessions are not effective?
metadata.originalText: As you learn from Fundamentals, retrieval practice is crucial for any learning system. Retrieval and encoding are two sides of the same coin. In the early stages of learning skill, when encoding is still not highly efficient, the knowledge gaps and retention issues must be addressed with sufficient retrieval practice.    Because encoding skill takes time to develop, the early phase of learning to learn often involves:    Lots of retrieval sessions  Many knowledge gaps  High time investment in retrieval sessions   This is the situation for most learners. Their"studying time" is almost completely occupied by:   Ineffective encoding practices such as re-reading, re-writing, re-listening and mindless note-taking  Repetitive retrieval sessions   When we learn about effective encoding vs. retrieval, we step into the world of"conscious incompetence", where we suddenly understand the problems with learning this way. As a result, our confidence in learning may rapidly reduce. This is a natural part of skill development where we now know enough to realise our mistakes.   However, unlike most learners, once we are aware of the limitations, we can begin to grow and overcome them eventually.   The purpose of retrieval practice is to find knowledge gaps and help to fill them. Retrieval can be considered ineffective if there are few knowledge gaps found (when knowledge is not at a high mastery). Many learners consider retrieval  ineffective  when many knowledge gaps are found, but this is the opposite. The only way to have fewer gaps in early retrieval sessions is for the initial encoding to be of higher quality. The best way to overcome the problems we encounter with retrieval is: -  Step 1: Make sure we are performing our retrieval to a high quality, as described in the lessons and interleaving table. Step 2: Make sure your retrieval sessions are targeting the right levels and with appropriate spacing Step 3: Gradually develop better encoding skills You can learn more about different ways of retrieval and interleaving    here, and you can learn about spacing    here.   Over time, as our encoding improves, we will find that our retention is higher, our retrieval sessions find fewer knowledge gaps, and learning new material becomes much easier. Interleaving past our comfort zone:   You can learn more about different ways of retrieval and interleaving    here, and you can learn about spacing    here.   Over time, as our encoding improves, we will find that our retention is higher, our retrieval sessions find fewer knowledge gaps, and learning new material becomes much easier.

metadata.docName: What is reverse causality and how do I prevent it?
metadata.originalText: Reverse causality flaw is when information is justified as being important because of another piece of information that requires memorisation.  This can create a closed loop between the two pieces of information whereby remembering  why  something is important depends on rote-learning the reason.  Ultimately, it defeats the purpose of the relationship or chunk, because instead of helping your memory by making things more logical, it adds another item for you to memorise.   For example,  consider a topic with 5 concepts: A, B, C, D, and E.  If we say that A is important because it is needed to create B, this relies on memorising the fact that A creates B in the first place. It does not connect A with any prior knowledge or any concepts outside of just A and B.  This is the closed loop.   In the illustration below, notice how the only connection that we can create is an arrow from A to B, and this arrow would have to be memorised because there is nothing else that we can use to logically infer the importance of A, except that it creates B.   The alternative would be,  for example, to justify the importance of A because of its influence on C and D, and that it is created by E. This connects A with C, D and E. When we then learn that A creates B, B now exists in the context of a wider network.   Note that  intuitive chunking  can help to prevent reverse causality.  Importance checklisting  or  Simple question and answer  can increase it. Checking for reverse chunking:  Reverse causality flaw is when information is justified as being important because of another piece of information that requires memorisation.  This can create a closed loop between the two pieces of information whereby remembering  why  something is important depends on rote-learning the reason.  Ultimately, it defeats the purpose of the relationship or chunk, because instead of helping your memory by making things more logical, it adds another item for you to memorise.   For example,  consider a topic with 5 concepts: A, B, C, D, and E.  If we say that A is important because it is needed to create B, this relies on memorising the fact that A creates B in the first place. It does not connect A with any prior knowledge or any concepts outside of just A and B.  This is the closed loop.   In the illustration below, notice how the only connection that we can create is an arrow from A to B, and this arrow would have to be memorised because there is nothing else that we can use to logically infer the importance of A, except that it creates B.   The alternative would be,  for example, to justify the importance of A because of its influence on C and D, and that it is created by E. This connects A with C, D and E. When we then learn that A creates B, B now exists in the context of a wider network.   Note that  intuitive chunking  can help to prevent reverse causality.  Importance checklisting  or  Simple question and answer  can increase it.

Key principles: 1. Performance goals are process and attribute-based.2. Performance goals give you crucial early feedback, which provides opportunities for self-correction and adjustment.3. Consider these attributes: time management, focus and procrastination, learning efficiency, learning consistency, and stress management.4. Pick just a few of the most important attributes to develop in the short term. Avoid overloading yourself and thus failing at all of them. Check out  this article  from the Learning Support page for more guidance on how to think about setting goals for learning efficiency.   As usual, it appears our brain is trying to sabotage us again! The human brain is notoriously bad at assessing our own abilities and skills.   Beware of thinking,  This doesnt apply to me  and  Im different. Being open-minded carries no risk while helping to prevent us from making unnecessary mistakes and wasting time. Note:  This is especially common among high achievers, who  often can safely  ignore advice aimed at the general public without consequence.   However, this also means that those who ignore advice have the highest chance of running into issues. As a result, we have found that most learners who struggle with learning the methods we teach in this program are actually high achievers who rushed the fundamentals!

Simply completing a mindmap does not make your learning effective. If misused, mind maps can be just as bad as other types of notes. What's important is to use the right cognitive process so that mindmapping helps your brain think. You know you are mindmapping correctly when you...: 1. Find it easier to think of new relationships and chunks by seeing what you have already written down2. Are able to reflect on how each individual piece connects to the big picture3. Find that it feels messy and confusing at first, but over time as the mindmap becomes more organised and accurate, your knowledge also feels more organised and accurate4. Continuously refine and update your mindmap (which indicates that you are constantly thinking of the big picture, holding yourself to a high standard, and keeping structures hypothetical)5. Constantly engage in higher-order thinking processes (analysing by finding similarities and differences and evaluating by prioritising and making value judgments) Having trouble?:  Remember to ask for feedback in our community. Our coaching staff are also always around to assist you. References on the efficacy of mindmaps:  Adam A, Mowers H. Get Inside Their Heads with Mind Mapping. Exam execution guidelines - Follow these guidelines to ensure consistent performance during assessments. Step 1: Get good sleep (especially before exams). Sleep is essential for exam execution due to sleep-dependent memory consolidation. The recommended amount is at least 9 hours in bed, per night! Step 2: Use priority-checking to double-check answers you are unsure about while maintaining an optimum pace for other questions. Step 3: Think like an examiner to evaluate your knowledge and consider how you can use it to solve challenging problems, especially in ways you are not confident about. View the interleaving table for methods. Step 4: Use slow, deep breathing to activate the parasympathetic response, mitigate anxiety spirals and eventually train yourself out from anxiety pathways that may have become a habit. Not every lesson has recommended tasks. This is because everyone has different things to work on. If this lesson is relevant for you, make some tasks of your own. You can do this by clicking  Add a task  and selecting  custom task  from the focus list on your dashboard! I only get nervous in high-pressure exams. What should I do?:  If youre already doing most of these things, you can use  graduated exposure therapy. This is when you put yourself in increasingly similar scenarios to what triggers your nervousness. For exams, that means practising answering questions in more pressured settings before your actual exam. This can be done by adding strict time pressures, using exam conditions, and involving friends to increase competition. I'm too busy and stressed before exams to remember these tips. What should I do?:  Make a pre-exam checklist and mark on your schedule when to start going through the checklist. This could be weeks before your exam. We all forget things when we're under pressure. The trick is to use the methods of self-management we have learned so you create safety nets for your brain.

The concept of marginal gains is widespread since James Clear published his international bestseller, Atomic Habits.  Utilising marginal gains is a powerful tool to have in your toolkit. It is a secret weapon of some of the worlds most successful and high-performing individuals. Mastering marginal gains at a young age is a competitive advantage that few people can beat.    However, it's essential to recognise the different types of gains. This is a practical nuance to the concept that we rarely see discussed.   In the graph below, you can see that there are three different coloured lines. The green top line represents positive gains. This is what we want! The grey line in the middle represents fluctuating gains. The red line at the bottom represents negative gains.   The table below outlines the nature of these three types of gains. **Types of Gains**  **Positive Gains**  * **How they occur:** Gains compound positively, with each 1% gain building on the previous one. This can involve building on knowledge, skills, and processes. * **Root problem:** No problems.  **Fluctuating Gains**  * **How they occur:** There is 1% change, but each change does not build on the previous one. As a result, gains do not compound. Skills and processes fluctuate between improvement and decline. * **Root problem:** Inconsistent approach to marginal gains.  **Negative Gains**  * **How they occur:** Gains compound negatively, with each 1% gain building on the previous one. However, they worsen skills, knowledge, and processes. * **Root problem:** Lack of reliable information, calibration, or feedback on progress. How to ensure you get positive marginal gains - Follow the guidelines below to make sure your gains take you closer to your goal. Step 1: Think about marginal gains consistently. Train yourself to view progress through this lens by default. Step 2: Whenever you make mistakes, feed them back in as reflections for your next cycle of experiments. Step 3: Avoid randomly experimenting on different changes every time. Step 4: Get feedback on your work and progress regularly. Step 5: Ensure you are getting advice from reliable, qualified sources of expertise.

By now, you've probably realised that creating mind maps is a surprisingly technical skill.  Below, we'll summarise some of the biggest steps as a general refresher of the process. Then we will explore some additional examples and dissections. Creating a good mindmap: An overview -  Step 1: Identify the biggest chunks. These will be hypothetical at first. There are usually 3 to 7 for a single topic. Step 2: Arrange the chunks and most important contained concepts so that the relationship and flow between them is expressed. Step 3: Identify sub-concepts and sub-chunks and add them to the map. Always think about how each new chunk or concept fits into the big picture. Step 4: Add more details until the topic is complete. Information that is highly specific and not related to other parts on the map can be added to memorisation techniques such as flashcards. General tips: 1. When arranging your first chunks, leave big spaces between them so there is room for sub-chunks and other concepts.2. Make the main flow of relationships between the largest chunks visually very clear. This is the"backbone" of the topic.3. Remember to relate each new item to the bigger picture as you go.4. Constantly refine and revise your structure. Do not overly commit to your first chunk structure. Everything is just a hypothesis! Missing something?:  If you spot a part of the process that you were neglected or skipping, attempt a mindmap following the guidelines above before moving on.

In this video, deepen your understanding of marginal gains and see how we can pick compounding instead of additive gains to give us the greatest effect, using school or university subjects/papers as examples.   This sublesson is aimed at students. Although it may not be as directly relevant for professional learners you can still get some helpful ideas from this example!    Interested in seeing what the full system you will develop throughout this program looks like? We've prepared a special sneak peek lesson for you  here. This is entirely optional for now, and you will be asked to review this lesson at the end of Fundamentals 2 anyway.

The first letter, V, stands for  Visual.  Here, you reduce the amount of words and use more visuals  pictures, symbols, doodles, shorthand and diagrams.  Using visuals greatly helps with  retention  as it incorporates multiple modes of learning. Visuals act as landmarks in your memory. It also makes your notes fun and personal! Key principle:   As with all note-taking, what you produce is less important than the processes you used to produce it.   Even though other people probably wont understand your doodles, you will have a deep and meaningful connection to them because you went through the cognitive process of imagining and creating them. About learning styles:   Belief in Learning Styles May Be Detrimental.  The Learning Styles Myth More references on learning styles and multimodal learning:  Conyers M, Wilson D. Believing in the Brain. Educational Leadership [Internet]. 2020 May [cited 2020 Jun 8];77(8):16.   Deale CS. Learning Preferences Instead of Learning Styles: A Case Study of Hospitality Management Students Perceptions of How They Learn Best and Implications for Teaching and Learning. The various forms of neuroplasticity: Biological bases of learning and teaching. Prospects (00331538) [Internet]. 2016 Jun [cited 2020 Jun 8];46(2):199213. doi: 10.1007/s11125-017-9388-7 Lesson correction: The hemispheric myth:  The video above may contain some premature assumptions that do not reflect the most recent research. Please read below.  The idea that the brain has different hemispheres with fixed specific functions is now under contention.    Different parts of the brain have different functions, as demonstrated through a large volume of brain imaging studies.  Hemispheres seem to have no relationship to personality.  People do not seem to have one hemisphere that is dominant at a cognitive level for information processing, though dominance for sensory and motor function is well established.  Different areas of the brain seem to be discretely activated for different functions, and a general preference for one hemisphere over the other has been demonstrated. However, recent findings also show other parts of the brain being activated for certain forms of information that are not consistent with the existing theory.  Teaching to include multimodal learning is supported by evidence. However, there is insufficient evidence to suggest that this benefit is due to the differential usage of hemispheres rather than cortices.   Based on the current level of research, the argument made in this video that the dominant hemisphere is responsible for more rational thinking while the non-dominant hemisphere is responsible for abstract thinking may be contended as there is insufficient evidence to make this conclusion. While motor and sensory dominance has been consistently demonstrated, this is not necessarily the same as information-processing dominance or limited to strict hemispheres.  While this is theoretically valid to explore, it may not practically matter if hemispheric dominance is true or not since incorporating multimodal learning has been shown to have a benefit, regardless of why.

metadata.docName: How should I write notes? (An overview)
metadata.originalText: Non-linear note-taking places a higher priority on relationships, which creates a higher level of retention and facilitates more active cognitive processes. The limitations of linear notes:   Non-linear note-taking places a higher priority on relationships, which creates a higher level of retention and facilitates more active cognitive processes. Unfortunately, most learners have never learned correct non-linear techniques. This can cause people to experiment with non-linear methods like mindmaps and find them ineffective. However, because they have not received training on the correct method, they do not realise that their technique is flawed. Non-linear note-taking is a surprisingly technical and advanced skill to do correctly!   Many students in modern education systems have been trained to read and write continuously. Even most teachers will encourage heavy note-taking in linear forms. This is a self-sabotaging habit and produces very little real learning. Limitations of continuous note-taking:  Unfortunately, most learners have never learned correct non-linear techniques. This can cause people to experiment with non-linear methods like mindmaps and find them ineffective. However, because they have not received training on the correct method, they do not realise that their technique is flawed. Non-linear note-taking is a surprisingly technical and advanced skill to do correctly!   Many students in modern education systems have been trained to read and write continuously. Even most teachers will encourage heavy note-taking in linear forms. This is a self-sabotaging habit and produces very little real learning. This can be challenging, off-putting and even scary as trying to figure things out and make sense of information is naturally a process that feels confusing and mentally uncomfortable.   To avoid this feeling, most students will write lots of notes, giving them a sense of security and comfort. However, by doing this, they also remove the brains incentive to process the information, resulting in much more passive and ineffective learning. They will, therefore, need to spend much more time overall revising.   On the other hand, students who can embrace this discomfort and use their confusion as fuel to enhance their brains ability to make sense of information can tap into a much faster and more efficient state of learning.

Spending time to unblock our future barriers is crucial to learning new skills quickly. Unfortunately, it'sa step that is often overlooked. As a result, learners can encounter more obstacles than needed. This can be frustrating and eventually demotivating as overcoming each obstacle takes time, energy, and attention away from making further strides. Key principle:  It's always worth investing time now to make things easier for yourself later. Your future self will have their hands full. Finding ways to make it easier for ourselves later means we give ourselves the space to adapt and overcome new and harder challenges.

Spaced, interleaved retrieval is one of the core foundational techniques of any learning system. Even as we teach you more learning techniques later in the program, SIR will remain. Important notes about training SIR: 1. SIR is not one technique - it is a method. The difficulty of using SIR depends on the difficulty of the interleaved retrieval techniques you use.2. Retrieval techniques are generally easier to master than encoding techniques. You can expect to master each technique with only 3 to 5 hours of experience.3. The consequence of not using SIR is very high. This is explained further below. Spaced retrieval is an easy strategy to implement straightaway, so if you arent doing it already, you should start now. If you already use spaced retrieval, improve your interleaving with our guidelines.   Systems that rely on   spaced retrieval lead to repetition and time wasting. Spaced retrieval alone is insufficient to give you a competitive advantage or to produce your desired results reliably.   However, retrieval practice is still necessary for a complete study system.    Notice that as the encoding skill increases, retrieval requirements never become zero. We always need some level of retrieval because our brain is never leak-proof (i.e. there is always some knowledge decay).   Read our article on encoding vs. retrieval  here  for a more detailed explanation. Two sides of the learning coin:  You cannot be efficient and accurate without both encoding and retrieval. Outside of inadequate encoding,"retrieval failure" is a common cause of knowledge decay of previously encoded information.   When information is encoded to a higher quality, we need less retrieval.

Before you watch:  The following demonstration uses our  interactive curriculum module  as practice content.  We strongly recommend that you attempt this yourself before reviewing the demonstration to make an accurate judgment of your true skill level. You can access the interactive curriculum module from the  Supplementary Learning  page in your  Toolkit.   This video has been intentionally edited to show the thinking time and thought processes involved in studying the topic. Prechunking is when we create some chunks  before  engaging in inquiry.  In many cases, this is not advisable as it can bias the chunks we form. However, this is not problematic if we make sure our chunk structures are only hypotheses during the entire chunking process.   Problems arise when the prechunks do not serve as a springboard for thinking, but rather are assumed to be correct and learners are resistant or hesitant to modify it. General advice about prechunking: 1. Prechunking is sometimes acceptable and saves time, but only if the chunks remain hypotheses and continue to be modified.2. If there is any difficulty with avoiding chunking in simply the same ways information is presented (i.e. engaging order control), avoid prechunking.3. If there are any issues with spiderwebbing or the Aim step feels ineffective, avoid prechunking. In the demonstrations (both in the sublessons below as well as in Live Clinics) you will notice that sometimes prechunking is used and sometimes it is not.   What matters is that chunks are formed independently and challenged with good inquiry.

This lesson will show you how to schedule according to your priorities. You can go through this like a checklist. Set your priorities first, then set your schedule.:  You should transition from setting  weekly  priorities to setting  daily  priorities. Every night, make sure to set your schedule for the next day. Guidelines -  Step 1: Schedule your top two priority items first. Step 2: Use either a diary or digital schedule. For most people, digital apps are more efficient and manageable, but if you have a low volume of tasks, pen-and-paper may be sufficient. Step 3: Be very specific in your scheduling entries. To avoid procrastination, break down your study time into 30-minute blocks, and make sure to leave breaks and gaps. Step 4: Overestimate the time required to complete each task by 25 to 50%. Step 5: Ensure 15 minutes of buffer between each task. Step 6: Have at least one hour of unscheduled time per day as emergency. Step 7: Schedule time for reflecting and setting priorities at the end of every day. Recommended task 1Review and adjust your schedule according to your priorities  Following the principles of effective scheduling and prioritising that you've learned so far, review your schedule for the next week and make adjustments if needed.   Complete this task once you are satisfied with your schedule.   Technique Training:  In this stage, we introduce variations of inquiry-based learning, which help you to create more meaningful relationships and networks.  **Learning Plan**  **Key Dimensions:**  * Deep processing * Self-regulation * Self-management  **Recommended Pace:**  * Theory: 10 hours over 4 weeks  **Recommended Practice:**  * Practice while learning theory: 2-4 weeks  **Progression Tips:**  * Prioritize the"Inquiry-based learning" lesson within the first week. * This lesson introduces an advanced encoding and note-taking technique. * Most learners require more practice with this technique compared to previous ones.

Cramming is taking a massive block of knowledge and processing it in a short span of time (usually before an exam).  Cramming is very useful if done correctly.     People usually cram by just grabbing a heap of content and learning how they normally would. They spend long hours compressed into just a few days or weeks. Unfortunately, cramming can be risky and stressful if using techniques with low retention or are inefficient at building higher levels of knowledge mastery.   This might have been you in the past.     An efficient cramming system should abide by the following principles. Principles of efficient cramming: 1. It must allow you to skip content strategically to make the most of your available learning time.2. It must allow a retention rate and mastery depth that is sufficient for the best possible outcome.3. It must be efficient enough to not sacrifice sleep (and therefore sleep-dependent memory consolidation or cognitive performance declines due to sleep deprivation) The system we will use for cramming is called the  Multipass System.  This is an advanced system because it requires you to already have excellent  order control, be an inquiry-based learner, and be able to write effective notes (using VPReFRE). Cram early:  One of the easiest and most helpful changes you can make to cramming is to cram  early. Instead of leaving it at the last minute, complete the course/curriculum as early as possible. This means you can spend the remaining time with whatever else you'd like to do, stress-free and guilt-free. All you have to do is keep yourself topped up and find gaps using occasional SIR to sail through to your assessments.   Many of our Summit-level learners can complete a typical one-year curriculum in just a couple of months of focused work.

Key principles: 1. Focus on continually building relevance and relating information to its importance in the big picture.2. Do a reverse explanation for why the entire topic is important. This can help you to bridge into your components and major chunks more naturally. Alert:  While doing WPW, you may find that your most fundamental logical flow differs from your original logic layer. This isn't always a problem, but you should evaluate whether your initial chunking was as intuitive as possible. WPW may be the first time chunks are actively challenged  especially in topics with more"obvious" chunk structures.   Difficulty in fluently explaining the importance of topics or concepts  may  be a sign that your chunks were information-based rather than importance or logic-based.

Core concepts: 1. Urgency trapping2. Task management vs. time management3. Human biological tendencies for prioritisation and how it harms us4. Urgency vs importance5. The life-changing value of prioritising importance instead of urgent6. Theory time vs. practice time We'll explore these concepts in more depth later in the program. You can read our article  here  on recommended apps for time management, task management and note-taking. You can also find this article again anytime from the  Learning Support  page on the left-side menu. Key principle:  Always practice  often,  early, and with increasing  challenges. Learning and time management are skills. And skills are about what you can  do,  not what you  know. One of the most common reasons people struggle to acquire new skills is when there is too much theory and insufficient practice.

metadata.docName: What is higher-order learning and thinking?
metadata.originalText: The goal of every learner is to develop  expertise. Expertise means you can answer any question and solve any problem. However, an expert's"knowledge structure" or"knowledge network" (i.e. how they have organised information in their brain) is different to that of a novice.  To understand what knowledge structures look like for experts, we can consider Blooms revised taxonomy below. Key principles of higher-order vs. lower-order learning: 1. Higher-order knowledge is integrated in a network. Lower-order knowledge is isolated.2. Integration occurs by analysis and evaluation (comparing information and making judgments on their relative importance).3. Higher-order learning is beneficial for retention and depth of understanding. Lower-order learning is forgotten quickly and easily. Orders of learning can be considered both processes and outcomes. This is important because higher-order learning processes lead to higher-order learning outcomes, but  not  vice versa. For example:   Orders of learning can be considered both processes and outcomes. This is important because higher-order learning processes lead to higher-order learning outcomes, but  not  vice versa. On the other hand, lower-order learning processes lead to lower-order learning outcomes.   For example,  if someone uses flashcards to memorise lists of facts, they will be able to  retrieve  their knowledge at this level with sufficient repetition.   There are some crucial differences between using higher-order learning methods vs. using lower-order methods.     Higher-order learning methods produce both lower-order and higher-order knowledge.  This is because it is impossible to compare ideas without understanding them first. Lower-order only produces lower-order knowledge.   Starting with a higher-order method is more efficient than starting with lower-order methods.  This is because higher-order methods build networks of knowledge, which makes information more memorable to begin with. This reduces how much you need to memorise with lower-order methods. We call this reducing your  lower-order burden.   You always need multiple orders of learning in your methods.  Even though higher-order learning creates more robust knowledge, the human brain always forgets. Retrieval practice is needed to ensure that we can perform at all necessary orders of learning.    As higher-order methods of thinking help form better memory and expertise across all levels, another way of thinking about learning is that memory and understanding are outcomes. This contrasts with Bloom's taxonomy, which represents these two as the bottom levels of learning in what seems like a hierarchy.  However, this is inaccurate based on the latest research, and scholars criticise Bloom's for this reason. Instead, the diagram below shows how engaging in cognitively beneficial thinking processes  produces  better memory and understanding.
