This is also an oversimplification of learning (which is extremely complicated, by nature). However, it is a more accurate depiction of learning and developing expertise.    Higher-order thinking involves comparing, finding trends and similarities, grouping, simplifying, making judgments on importance and relevance of information, and relating information to the big picture.  Lower-order thinking is isolated and involves repetition.  These processes were famously described by Benjamin Bloom but have been challenged by researchers since then.  Expertise requires higher-order mastery, which is created with higher-order thinking. Lower-order thinking is very bad at creating expertise.  Higher-order thinking creates improved memory and depth of understanding, therefore, we should endeavour to engage in higher-order thinking and learning often.    Learning happens in the brain. We cant see it with our eyes. Therefore, its important that you mentally know what higher-order learning feels like so that you can start monitoring if youre using the right processes. **Higher-Order Learning**  * Involves complex cognitive processes such as:     * Comparing and contrasting     * Evaluating     * Forming networks and relationships  * Requires significant mental effort and engagement * Learners actively engage with the material, asking questions and making connections * Can be challenging and potentially confusing, but also rewarding  **Lower-Order Learning**  * Involves basic cognitive processes such as:     * Rote memorization     * Repetition     * Understanding information in isolation  * Requires less mental effort and engagement * Learners passively receive information without making connections or asking questions * Can be tedious and potentially boring or sleep-inducing

We will now learn how to memorise the outer layer of information (i.e., arbitrary information) using high-efficiency memorisation techniques.  Only use rote-learning techniques and memorisation as a  last resort. These techniques only apply to the outer layer of information  focus on the three inner layers to maximise your conceptual understanding first!  **Technique: Correct Flashcard Usage**  * Rationale: Flashcards are effective when used strategically. After establishing a solid foundation through logical and relationship-based learning, rote-learning and memorization techniques can be employed to supplement knowledge gaps.  **Technique: Modified Method of Loci**  * Rationale: This advanced technique enables the memorization of numerous items in a single session.      Technique    Notes       Bear Hunter System with hipshot variation  Continue to improve your competency and optimise the Skin step. In this stage, we will see improvements in speed.    Scheduling basics + Priority 0+1 system  Continue to do this so it becomes habitual.    Revision  Continue with good revision practice. We will build on this in the next stage.    Focus training  It is critical that you continue to do this, as it will help you overcome the activation energy of change and create significant cognitive restructuring. In other words, it will turn you into a machine of pure, unwavering focus.    PEER-Peer System  Continue to develop new habits, lock in existing good habits, and remove distractions. This process should continue forever.    OFF-Rest timing  Continue to use this whenever you need to focus. This should continue forever.

Key principles: 1. Chunks are intuitive when they leverage prior knowledge. The more you rely on prior knowledge, the more logical it feels, and the less memorisation is required.2. When you compare chunk structures and decide which one makes the most sense to you, you are engaging in higher-order learning.3. Rather than looking for a single right answer, you want to find the answer that best fits your knowledge schema.4. Instead of just deciding on a chunk structure and not evaluating alternatives, look for opportunities to reverse-chunk so your chunking is truly importance-based.5. When performed correctly, importance-based chunking creates intuitive chunks. By checking whether our chunks are intuitive, we can see whether or not weve performed importance-based chunking. This table outlines how using this technique can feel and what these feelings mean at a cognitive level. Refer to this to improve your metacognition and ensure you understand the techniques purpose. Connecting your emotional experience with the cognitive processes of learning helps you make fine-tuned adjustments and solve any technique issues more easily. **Cognitive Outcomes of Chunking**  **Emotional Impact:**  * Feeling overwhelmed, similar to the initial stages of learning the Aim step * This occurs due to the need to determine the optimal representation and labeling of chunks  **Cognitive Benefits:**  * Enforces prioritization and evaluation at a higher level * Facilitates the creation of deeper, more memorable structures  **Temporary Challenges:**  * Sudden perception of slower and less fluent performance * This is a result of the increased standards for chunking and encoding * With practice, this will become more manageable

The following apps are recommended by the iCanStudy team after extensive personal usage and integration. Disclaimer:  The following apps are recommended by the iCanStudy team after extensive personal usage and integration. Book guides and book summaries:  We recommend  Shortform  as it has some of the industry's most useful, practical, accurate, and comprehensive book guides. It is useful as a book adjunct and to scope potential sources to explore more deeply.   We have negotiated a free trial and a 30% discount on annual plans for our members using our affiliate link    here. This is the highest level of discount offered by Shortform anywhere.   Shortform takes our recommended spot over the popular Blinkist as we have noticed the summaries of Blinkist can be inaccurate, miss important principles, and misrepresent ideas. Although this does not happen often, we have observed this enough times to be wary of the summaries. In some cases, books that were phenomenal when read in full seem mediocre in the summary due to misrepresentation.  However, Blinkist has a much larger library than Shortform. Therefore, if your budget allows, you may also wish to utilise Blinkist if Shortform does not have your target book. Note that Shortform continues to add new books every week, and members can vote for new books to be added. Task management and prioritisation:   Asana,  ClickUp, or  Amazing Marvin     Amazing Marvin is extremely powerful and probably the most customisable and fully featured task management app on the market. However, it can be overwhelming for beginners.  For most people who want something simpler, Asana has a free plan that is excellent for task management and will meet most people's needs. ClickUp is an alternative to Asana and has a different workflow and user interface. Many people prefer ClickUp to Asana due to its more intuitive workflows, especially when working in teams. Scheduling and time management:  Google calendars is our recommended option for most people as it is compatible with virtually every app and service, while featuring a robust set of time management features.  For Android,  Business Calendar 2  is a versatile and customisable app that utilises the widget space of Android phones effectively.  On Windows devices,  OneCalendar  can be a simple calendar integration app.  For iOS devices, the Google calendar app is usually sufficient. "Second brain", idea collection, knowledge bank, reference database:   Obsidian  is arguably the best app on the market for second-brain relational databases and a"source of truth". It can be used for a wide variety of uses. However, it has a steeper learning curve than apps such as  Notion, which are also perfectly suitable for most people.  For those willing to learn and invest time in setting up their knowledge base, we recommend Obsidian. For those wanting to get into it and do not have complex relational knowledge needs, Notion will be more than enough. Creating focus and removing distractions:  There are many apps and websites for removing distractions and improving focus. There are also many new apps being released all the time. This is a list of apps and sites we recommend in our experience. There may be newer and better solutions that we have not used yet.   Website blocking:   BlockSite  has the useful option to block its own settings page through password protection via someone else's email address.   Work-rest timing:   Tide  (on both Android and iOS). Many other apps do the same thing. Tide is not necessarily the best, but one that we have found adequate for most needs.   White noise generation:   Relaxio  and White Noise Lite (on  Android  or  iOS ). These apps are useful because you can modulate the type of white noise to fit the type of distracting external noise you are trying to block out.   Mindfulness meditation and sleep:   Calm  consistently ranks highly on our recommendations due to their high level of instructional guidance and support for beginners and the diversity of guided and unguided meditation modes for more experienced people. Habit tracking:   Remente  is our recommended app due to its wide variety of features and flexibility, though  Fabulous  is also a good option for beginners. We feel that Fabulous may be too rigid for more experienced habit-setters.

Be careful of the following barriers to shooting. These are mistakes that students have made in the past, which prevented them from successfully taking on these techniques.   Which barriers resonate with you? Aim-Shoot takes too much time!:  The time spent building your mind map will create more connections in your brain and ultimately save you more time. The real waste of time is returning to traditional forms of learning, where you memorise pages of text with no escape  (deathly spiral warning!). Also, building mind maps becomes much faster as you become more proficient at the technique, which makes learning even quicker over time.  We recommend reviewing our article  here  on how to accurately measure your learning efficiency to see if it really does take too long or if it just  seems  that way. Aim-Shoot is too difficult!:  Learners can become apprehensive of this technique because they cannot gain certainty about their knowledge for a long time.  However, the certainty that comes from traditional methods is actually an illusion of learning that does not reflect the knowledge you build mentally. This results in inconsistent performance and results, which lead to frustration, demotivation, and performance anxiety.  Remember that making and finding mistakes is a critical part of learning and improving. Students who do not find it difficult cause alarm bells to ring because it usually means they are not applying the technique to the correct standard! What if I miss something?:  You will miss more things by  not  using a mind map and following the layers. Mind maps structure your learning to match how your brain learns, which is ultimately the most optimal way to learn. You also dont have to worry about missing details as you jump around and use order control because using the full Bear Hunter System with SIR will prevent any gaps.

Reaching base camp is a fantastic achievement. Well done on getting here!  You have flipped your old learning on its head, so we will take time to adjust and consolidate to ensure your new system is stabilised at  unconscious competence.   You're now eligible to become an iCanStudy core member! Core members are learners in our community who give back by helping others. Core member benefits: 1. Priority support via Core member-only Discord channels with iCanStudy coaching staff2. Inside circle - gain early access and beta for new features and have your say in how our program and community develops3. Monthly mentorship and personal development meetings with coaches4. Fortnightly international core member meetings where you can keep each other accountable and connect with the most driven learners in our community5. Exclusive discount on membership renewals6. Access to iCanStudy's direct international network of professionals, employers, and institutions To learn more about how you can apply, read our article  here.   **Time Management and Prioritization Techniques**  * **Priority 0+1 System:** An intermediate technique used by Dr. Justin for several years, designed to enhance time management and prioritization.  * **OFF-Rest Timing:** A personalized system for optimizing work-rest intervals, reducing fatigue, improving focus, and maintaining high performance levels throughout the day.  **Focus Enhancement Techniques**  * **Focus Training:** An advanced technique that significantly improves focus over time, considered one of the most powerful methods in the course.  **Procrastination Elimination Techniques**  * **PEER-Peer System:** An extension of the BEDS-M system, this comprehensive approach aims to eliminate procrastination entirely.  **Advanced Techniques**  * **Hipshot Variation:** A time-saving variation of the Bear Hunter System, requiring mastery of the traditional system.      Technique    Notes       Bear Hunter System  You should eventually use the hipshot variation.    Scheduling Basics  You should incorporate this with the Priority 0+1 system.    Spaced interleaved retrieval  Your mastery over this should be very high by now.         Prior Technique  Replaced by      Aim + Shoot  Hipshot    Maintaining focus  Focus training + OFF-Rest timing    BEDS-M  Focus training + PEER-Peer system    Scheduling basics (prioritising guidelines)  Priority 0+1 system

The science around note-taking is complicated. Here are some of the key principles of note-taking that we know from reviewing the latest research. Key principles of note-taking: 1. The method we use to take notes matters less than how we are thinking as we take notes.2. Good note-taking can help our brain think by reducing unhelpful and extraneous cognitive load.3. Bad note-taking can make us avoid thinking, which reduces the effectiveness of learning.4. Wordier notes are usually less processed and therefore less effective. Typing can exacerbate this as it is usually easier to create high volumes of notes compared to freehand writing. In the sublessons below, we will explore some of these principles in much greater depth to see how you can refine your note-taking technique.

Main points: 1. Identify limiting habits that will sabotage your progress.2. Consider how you typically respond to stress, tiredness, lack of time, bad moods, and discomfort.3. Think about what factors made you fail to develop a previous habit.4. Limiting habits are automatic, and we are usually unaware of them.5. It can take you days or weeks to identify your limiting habits. If unsure, the first step is to start paying attention to your habitual response tendencies.6. Scripted actions are pre-planned responses that require as close to zero willpower as possible.

Key principles: 1. Changing decisions is affected by several cognitive biases such as status quo bias, sunk cost bias and loss aversion bias.2. Probability concentration shows that changing decisions can be advantageous.3. Decisions made with greater information, at a later point in time, can be more appropriate than not changing. Society typically values those who"stick with their decisions". However, this confuses resilience and persistence with narrow-mindedness. Resilience and grit are essential to overcome difficulties and challenges when the decision aligns with our goals, values, and priorities. On the other hand, changing decisions quickly is a sign of an adaptive and critical thinker.    Bestselling author and decision-making consultant Annie Duke says,"Quitting isnt always a sign of defeat. Highly successful people often know exactly when to give up."     Read more about Annie Duke's perspective on quitting and decision-making  here.   You may also be interested in  this article, which shows some statistics about how people who changed their decisions felt in retrospect.  Or learn more about the fascinating world of cognitive biases  here.

metadata.docName: How do you measure learning efficiency?
metadata.originalText: Most people think about learning efficiency as how much content you can cover in a learning session. For example, an efficient learner can cover 5 lectures in 2 hours.   However, this is very problematic for multiple reasons:    It does not consider the retention of knowledge  Extra time is spent relearning information that is forgotten  It does not consider the quality of learning that occurred or the person's ability to perform and use their knowledge in different ways    Ultimately, thinking about efficiency as the"amount covered per session" is a useless metric.     What we want to know is the amount of time it takes for a learner to gain high-quality, usable knowledge and retain this information for their purposes. Efficient learning can be summarised as:   Achieving the necessary mastery of knowledge  Having sufficient retention of this knowledge at each level of required mastery  Being able to achieve the above in a short amount of time The learning efficiency equation:   Most people think about learning efficiency as how much content you can cover in a learning session. For example, an efficient learner can cover 5 lectures in 2 hours.   However, this is very problematic for multiple reasons:    It does not consider the retention of knowledge  Extra time is spent relearning information that is forgotten  It does not consider the quality of learning that occurred or the person's ability to perform and use their knowledge in different ways    Ultimately, thinking about efficiency as the"amount covered per session" is a useless metric.     What we want to know is the amount of time it takes for a learner to gain high-quality, usable knowledge and retain this information for their purposes. Efficient learning can be summarised as:   Achieving the necessary mastery of knowledge  Having sufficient retention of this knowledge at each level of required mastery  Being able to achieve the above in a short amount of time How to calculate the values -  Step 1: Identify the highest and lowest required knowledge mastery levels. Refer to our article on"How to measure knowledge mastery". Step 2: Assess your retention after one week (as a percentage) for each level. We use one week because our spacing intervals should allow for a one-week retrieval session. It can be extrapolated to the level of retention you may have for an assessment. Step 3: Identify the retention you require for each level for your assessment. Step 4: Calculate partial mastery by dividing your real retention after one week by the required retention. Step 5: Calculate total mastery by taking the average of your partial masteries. Step 6: Calculate the total time you spent on all learning and retrieval sessions for this subject (across however many weeks). Jeremy is studying for a second-year university economics exam.    Knowledge mastery (highest): For his level, he is assessed at a maximum of level 4.  Knowledge mastery (lowest): He is sometimes asked about specific fact recall, so he is assessed at a minimum of level 1.    After a couple of weeks of studying, Jeremy has found that his one-week retention is as follows:  **Retention and Assessment Requirements by Level**   Level  Retention  Required for Assessment  ---------  1  60%  80%   2  60%  90%   3  50%  70%   4  30%  60%   By dividing retention by the percentage required for assessment, we can calculate the partial masteries:      Level  Retention  Required for assessment  Partial masteries      1  60%  80%  60%/80% = 75%    2  60%  90%  60%/90% = 67%    3  50%  70%  50%/70% = 71%    4  30%  60%  30%/60% = 50% Simple summary:     Jeremy is studying for a second-year university economics exam.    Knowledge mastery (highest): For his level, he is assessed at a maximum of level 4.  Knowledge mastery (lowest): He is sometimes asked about specific fact recall, so he is assessed at a minimum of level 1.    After a couple of weeks of studying, Jeremy has found that his one-week retention is as follows:  **Retention and Assessment Requirements by Level**   Level  Retention  Required for Assessment  ---------  1  60%  80%   2  60%  90%   3  50%  70%   4  30%  60%   By dividing retention by the percentage required for assessment, we can calculate the partial masteries:      Level  Retention  Required for assessment  Partial masteries      1  60%  80%  60%/80% = 75%    2  60%  90%  60%/90% = 67%    3  50%  70%  50%/70% = 71%    4  30%  60%  30%/60% = 50% To calculate his Total mastery, we simply average the value of all the partial masteries.   The average of 75%, 67%, 71%, and 50% is 66%. Therefore, Jeremy's  Total mastery  is 66%.  He has spent a total of 30 hours studying so far.   Therefore, we can say that Jeremy's learning efficiency is 66% over 30 hours. When we complete this calculation for inefficient learners, we often find that as hours increase, the total mastery percentage does not increase much. For example, if Jeremy is not a very efficient learner, his 30 hours may increase to 60 while his mastery only improves to 76%.    By doubling his hours of studying, his results have only improved by 10%.   This is because certain methods are inherently limited. Analogy:   When we complete this calculation for inefficient learners, we often find that as hours increase, the total mastery percentage does not increase much. For example, if Jeremy is not a very efficient learner, his 30 hours may increase to 60 while his mastery only improves to 76%.    By doubling his hours of studying, his results have only improved by 10%.   This is because certain methods are inherently limited. Key considerations: 1. Efficient learners can achieve a higher percentage in less time.2. More time does not necessary increase the percentage of achieved mastery and retention. In most cases, the rate of forgetting becomes so high with large volumes of learning that learners never reach 100% retention, especially at higher levels of mastery.3. The method of learning strongly dictates how easily higher levels of mastery can be attained. Some methods are frankly incompatible with achieving higher mastery, unless you have very high deep processing.4. Learning efficiency depends on the required level of mastery and retention. Lower requirements are easier to meet in less time. Example values of efficiency for different learner scenarios: **Learner Type**  **Inefficient Methods**  * Low deep processing:     * Lower mastery levels     * Struggles with higher levels     * Low retention     * High time investment (excessive relearning)     * Example: 50% mastery over 100 hours  * High deep processing:     * Achieves most mastery levels     * Low to moderate retention     * Moderate time investment     * Example: 70% mastery over 50 hours  **Efficient Methods**  * Low deep processing:     * Achieves most levels but struggles with higher levels     * Moderate retention     * Moderate to high time investment     * Example: 70% mastery over 50 hours  * High deep processing:     * High achievement across all levels     * High retention     * Low time investment     * Example: 90% mastery over 30 hours

Layers of learning focuses on implementing a structure when learning information. Key principles: 1. Always start from the logic layer and work out towards the later layers.2. The logic and concepts layers remain hypothetical and can be refined and adjusted at any point of the learning process.3. Most information resources are not divided into optimal layers. Usually, detail comes far too early.4. Order control allows us to rearrange the order in which our brain consumes and organizes information; layers of learning tell us how to rearrange it. Alert:  Layers of learning is one of our program's core pillar concepts. Expand the accordions below to read an overview and description for each layer. Logic:  The first and innermost layer is  logic. You start with the backbone of information, finding the big-picture purpose of what you are learning. This backbone is made up of the largest concepts, chunks, and most important relationships.  We build these relationships by asking, Why is it important? and How does it relate? and seeing how everything logically connects together.    People often skip this step entirely  this is like rushing to build a house and neglecting the foundation. Concepts:  The second layer is the  concepts. These are the main details and ideas that make up the bulk of subject-specific understanding.  The concept layer builds upon the first layer by taking all of those big ideas and asking, How does it happen? and What is it?.   Students who ignore the first layer will often struggle with learning the concepts. Details (important):  The third layer is the  important details.   Details are specific information that often needs to be rote-learned and memorized. However, some details will be  important  because they help you understand the concepts, while others may just be  arbitrary. Important details allow abstract concepts to be understood more concretely.   You can identify the important details by asking, Does knowing this detail help me understand the concept it is attached to? If the answer is yes, its important. If the answer is no, its arbitrary! Details (arbitrary):  The fourth and outermost layer is the  arbitrary details. These are disconnected pieces of information that may seem random or unnecessary but are still in the curriculum or exam.   We only learn these details because we need them for assessments. They must be rote-learned, at least until one day when we have learned far more, and they naturally make sense.  When we look at the total volume of information we need to study, only about 10% is conceptual, and about 20% is important details. This means the remaining 70% is arbitrary details. So, if you just study from beginning to end, you will not be able to retain much of the information because most of it is arbitrary and unconnected! You will find yourself trying to remember a lot of information that doesnt have any significance or meaning.  Conversely, if you start with deep, foundational information, this will  dramatically  increase your overall studying speed because by increasing the relevance of information, it turns arbitrary details into relevant details! Key principle:   Layers are not static.  How relevant a detail is depends on whether we have the structure to give it relevance. This means that errors in building an importance-based logic and concepts layer often mean we have more seemingly irrelevant details. The trick to reducing layer 4 (arbitrary details) is to improve layers 1 and 2.   Even if a tree has 10,000 leaves, it may only have 5 or 6 main branches. Spending time on the branches allows the leaves to arise more naturally.    As a general guide, it is usually best to overinvest in developing clear layers 1 and 2, as this makes a dramatic difference to the time it takes to cover layers 3 and 4, where the majority of time can sometimes be wasted. Learning in layers is one of the most powerful principles you can integrate into your learning system. It makes large volumes of content less overwhelming and more relevant, improves retention, and creates deeper mastery. One of the reasons for this is that learning in layers optimises your cognitive load.   Read our in-depth article    here    on how layering and"scaffolding" improve learning. The value of theory:  Understanding how layering improves learning at a theoretical level helps you self-diagnose and adapt your learning if it isn't as effective as you want it to be. This helps you to coach yourself when you face new challenges, which is an essential part of self-regulated learning!

Optimising cue and response -  Step 1: Identify the cues that trigger the response (i.e., the good or bad habit). Step 2: Get rid of bad cues that cause you to respond undesirably. Step 3: Adopt and integrate good cues that facilitate you to respond desirably. For example, a bad cue might be  seeing a social media icon  when you open your browser or turn on your phone. Remove this cue by deleting the icon and browsing in incognito mode when you need to focus. Another bad cue might be  seeing your phone, which you can remove by putting it at the bottom of your drawer or giving it to someone else.  A good cue for studying might be  seeing your books open  on the right page, which you can arrange the night before. A good cue for exercise might be  seeing your running shoes, which you can arrange by hanging them off your doorknob. Further learning:  For a deeper and more focused set of lessons on how to create desirable behaviours and habits, check out the behaviour change modules in the supplementary learning section. Another thing that affects our behaviour is  conditioning. This occurs when an unrelated external stimulus can evoke the intended response.   For example, you can condition yourself to study (the response) by doing something that makes you happy immediately afterwards (the stimulus). Once your brain has conditioned itself, it will start to enjoy studying even when you dont do the reward activity afterwards. Read about classical (Pavlovian) conditioning:  Here is a  summary article  on classical conditioning.   If you want to learn more, here is an extensive article on  Wikipedia.

One of the key differences between inquiry-based learning and conventional learning is the order in which information is gathered.  **Conventional Learning**  * Learning begins with the initial information provided. * Information sequence is dictated by external sources (e.g., resources, classes, lectures). * Irrelevant or overwhelming information is processed immediately. * Can lead to feelings of overwhelm or irrelevance.  **Inquiry-Based Learning**  * Learning starts with the most relevant or curious point. * Information sequence is determined by the learner's thought process and knowledge network. * Irrelevant information is delayed until the learner is ready to make sense of it. * Information is always manageable and relevant, as the learner chooses their own path. Because the types of relationships we build depend heavily on our prior knowledge and how we can make sense of information, the"best" order to learn a topic will differ for each person.   Methods such as the  Traffic Light System  help us to be more deliberate about the order in which we learn new information. Many learners initially feel uncertain about learning in a different order from how the content is presented to them. Remember, resources are not created directly for your brain and set of knowledge. Experts try to take their knowledge network and present it in a way that they assume will make sense.    It is always the job of the learner to take this information and construct their own knowledge network.

There are a near-endless number of ways to challenge your knowledge. The below is just a small list of examples:   Teach an imaginary student  Draw a mindmap  Draw an image instead of using words (to find a visual way of expressing information)  Answer practice questions  Create your own challenging test questions  Create a test question that puts what youve learned into a real-world context  Take a difficult question you found in a practice test and modify it so that it involves different variables or adds an extra step  Form a study group and quiz each other (for some subjects, you can even debate the topic, with one side trying to prove that the other person is missing a point or understanding it incorrectly)    There is no single interleaving technique that is best for every subject and context. Finding the right combination of interleaving techniques takes some time and experience. Key principles for interleaving: 1. Use different interleaving techniques for different retrieval sessions. Using the same method to repeatedly challenge your knowledge in the same way has limited benefits.2. Cover your full spectrum of orders of learning and procedural needs across your combination of interleaving techniques.3. Start with higher-order interleaving techniques in the earlier retrieval sessions to find big-picture gaps and weaknesses. These are more harmful when missed than gaps in small details.4. Lower-order interleaving techniques can either be used in parallel whenever you have gaps in time, or crammed in the later retrieval sessions. Deciding on the best set of interleaving techniques -  Step 1: Identify what order of knowledge retrieval you need. How much lower-order vs. higher-order retrieval do you need? Step 2: Identify how procedural your retrieval needs are. For example, maths and coding are very procedural, while biology is mostly conceptual. Step 3: Experiment with using an interleaving technique that covers your order of learning and procedural needs. Key resource:  Weve compiled a comprehensive resource to help you use different interleaving techniques, including examples for different disciplines and subjects. We strongly recommend checking out the table and integrating some of them into your schedule.   This resource will be especially useful if you are interested in learning languages, maths, and coding more efficiently. Check out the interleaving table    here. You can also find this unlocked in your    Toolkit    on the left side menu or in the    Learning Support    page.    We also recommend checking out Live Clinic 39, which goes deeper into interleaving for procedural skills like maths and coding.

metadata.docName: PEER-Peer system: Easy, exit, reward and peer
metadata.originalText: We make things easy by establishing  minimum viable goals (MVG), which weve discussed before. Tip:  Students often make their MVGs too difficult! If procrastination is a problem, even standing up or sitting at your desk can be considered a good MVG. Exit means that at each MVG, you tell your brain you can quit anytime. This keeps your brain focused on the MVGs so that the final goal wont scare you away.   Set a reward for whenever you attain a goal. Make sure your reward requires a minimum amount of work; try to make this time as long as possible. The closer you can get to your final goal before rewarding yourself, the better. Its up to you to decide what is good enough for a reward. Tip:  Do not let the reward tempt you prematurely. Consider physically locking away the reward, perhaps with the help of others. Often, people rely on willpower alone to stay away from their reward. We can see how this is prone to failure. Peer  is the last step. It creates another level of accountability using someone else (e.g., family, friends, or new friends from the iCanStudy community).  We covered this when discussing the burnt ships strategy.   Once the behaviours have become habits, this step will no longer be necessary. The best thing about this system is that you will need to use it less and less as the behaviours all become fully automatic.

Most of the decisions we make on a daily basis are low consequence. Which food to order at a restaurant or which outfit to wear does not have any life-changing impacts. However, we still want to make good decisions. In the sublessons below, we'll introduce two common frameworks for decision-making that help you become more decisive and reduce decisional delays.

View this sped-up version of a1.5-hour hipshot demonstration performed on VCE Unit 1 and 2 Accounting (Victorian government curriculum in Australia).   This was initially live-streamed on the iCanStudy Students YouTube channel    here. Watch for...:  Watch out for differences in Justin's technique compared to yours. How is his approach to this topic different from what you are used to? What about how he chunks and creates relationships? Or how he uses the resources and Google-driven inquiry? If the pace or level of the techniques in this video is beyond your current level, dont be discouraged. You will get there with persistence and marginal gains (and in a fraction of the time it took Justin to!)

By now, we have a clear understanding of chunking. In this stage, its time to deepen this knowledge and make sure we arent missing anything.  As a recap, chunking actually increases   the amount of detail you can remember by making it easier for your brain to organise everything. This makes it easier to retain information, which is why we want to chunk as much as possible!   The 2-4 Rule is an important intermediate-level process that promotes higher-order ( evaluate  level) learning while chunking. Instructions - Follow these instructions to abide by the 2-4 Rule. Step 1: Always aim to have two branches coming off each node, and never have more than four. Step 2: When there are four or more branches coming out of an information node, you can form a new chunk. Step 3: When there are less than two concepts from a node, you can merge or re-chunk. This can be re-chunked into sub-chunks as follows:   Notice how, by re-chunking, we have also added a new lateral relationship between the sub-chunks that would have previously created spiderwebbing. Key principles: 1. When there are more than four points from a single chunk, it should be sub-chunked.2. Sub-chunking can reduce spiderwebbing and allow lateral relationships to be expressed more cleanly. The following example shows a situation where less than two concepts come from a single chunk. In this situation, it clutters the space without adding organisational value. In this situation, we can try to  concatenate  (i.e. merge).   When a chunk or concept links only from one chunk or concept to one other chunk or concept, we call this a  single-node chain. Key principles: 1. Nodes with one in-bound and one out-bound connection are called single-node chains.2. Single-node chains indicate that the chunk structure can usually be optimised as these nodes will have fewer relationships and connections.3. Single-node chains may become weak points that are at risk of lower retention of mastery. In this situation, we must move British colonisation somewhere.   After considering what makes the most sense, we might altogether remove British colonisation from the flow to create a flow of"Drivers" to"Consequences". This is logical, and since the entire topic is about British colonisation, we are unlikely to forget what the drivers or consequences are referring to. It is obvious enough that the drivers are"drivers for British colonisation", and consequences are"consequences of British colonisation".   Notice we have re-chunked"economic gain" to become"drivers" so that the relationship to"consequences" is much more straightforward and intuitive. The chunk"economic gain" might be reintroduced later if we find multiple other drivers that could break the 2-4 Rule again (e.g. political or religious drivers).    An example of this scenario is given below to show the reintroduction of"economic gain" as a sub-chunk:     By continually chunking, sub-chunking, and re-chunking to find the simplest structure possible, we free up cognitive and physical space in our notes to explore additional relationships.   In this way, our understanding can become deeper, more nuanced, and intricately organised. You can see that some important and complex relationships between concepts have been represented above, despite looking organised and simple.    Each decision to chunk, re-chunk, re-arrange on the map, and sub-chunk is a higher-order decision involving the    evaluate    level of thinking. Persistence is crucial:  Rechunking to abide by the 2-4 Rule can involve substantial cognitive effort and thinking. Remember, your role as a learner is to find the structure that works. Look for the solution and persist until you find it! If we give up because it is difficult, we will sacrifice the quality of our learning. As you continue this, it will become easier and faster. Sometimes, we can end up with less than two concepts from a single chunk and no further connections beyond this. As there are no further connections, we could say that the single-node chain leads to a  terminal node. Terminal nodes are like the leaves of a tree.    An example of this is given here and boxed in red:     This is also a single-node chain.    Concatenating this can be easily achieved by adding"malnourishment" to"Famine" directly. This creates a clearer and less cluttered map.   When you chunk in this way, you are building highly organised groups of concepts.  These concepts will also be represented more simply, as good chunking improves the spatial arrangement of your mind map. With more chunks, you will have fewer isolated points, which means fewer arrows and a less complicated mind map. The beauty of this is that you arent sacrificing your connections and relationships because the chunks are still connected!

As with our learning skills, our approach to helping you with decision-making is to give you transferrable skills and principles to apply in your own life. We want to give you practical tools that help you improve your life holistically from the ground up.   Decision-making is a crucial part of crafting this life. Often, we are pressured to make a  perfect  choice. Rather, it is healthier and more realistic to learn how to make the  better  choice, understanding that the outcome of every decision we make is only partially within our control.

We have helped many people learn to learn more efficiently and gain more time in their lives. However, there are also many who struggle to improve due to issues with   unlearning. The ego of unlearning:   Unlearning  is the ability to replace old habits, skills, ideas and practices with alternatives that are more effective. This requires us to detach from our ego and acknowledge that our practices are sometimes wrong. Studies have shown  that successful people have a strong ability to unlearn.  To adopt a new system or habit, you must take your old habits and unlearn them. If you retain the bad habits from your old learning system, this jeopardises the effectiveness of your new system. Be open to adopting new practices and exercise your unlearning skills.   Open-mindedness is a door that allows us to grow and transform, even if the change in methods and habits we need is  not  what we expect we need. Tips for ensuring effective unlearning -  Step 1: Observe how you react when new methods seem to contradict your expectations. Do you remain open or close off to the idea? Step 2: Spot your hesitancies. Are there methods you feel reluctant to try because it goes against your existing system or habits? This could become problematic. Step 3: Avoid'tug of war' practice. This is when you revert to old habits or alter methods to be'easier' when new methods become difficult or unfamiliar. This prevents our brain from properly retraining itself. Tug of war practice can be subtle! Step 4: Avoid'batman' style practice. This is when new methods are practised separately without replacing existing methods. Old habits by day, new methods by night! Like'tug of war', this prevents retraining of habits.

In the sublessons below, we demonstrate the usage of the Aim step across a variety of subjects. Even if your particular discipline is not included as an example, the  methods of thinking  are completely transferrable.  You can skip sub-lessons you are not interested in by marking them as complete. However, we recommend watching several different examples to get a clear understanding of the technique.   We recommend watching the following sublessons for all learners:       Demonstration of core techniques      Chunking for medicine     Both of these sublessons contain highly transferrable learning points for all subjects and disciplines. Observe the process:  Focus on the processes and approach to thinking about information rather than the actual example contents themselves.

Rote learning guidelines - To do rote-learning and memorisation more efficiently, follow these guidelines. Step 1: Basic association allows you to make items more memorable by assigning highly memorable imagery to them. We can draw this imagery in flashcards or our notes. Step 2: Our brain is not good at memorising using words alone. Use visualisations and imagery too. Step 3: Make sure you use active recall instead of recognition. Step 4: Recall is more effective when combined with good spacing and interleaving. Step 5: To memorise details you might forget, use digital flashcards for spaced repetition via the Leitner system. Step 6: Try not to do flashcards in batches. Instead, do them throughout the day via microlearning. Remember:  Rote-learning is only necessary for around 30% of university material or around 10 to 20% of high school material.  How long do I need to practise these techniques before moving on?:  Rote-memorisation techniques are often easy to master with very little practice. Try to use some of these methods in your learning for one or two study sessions. There is no need to delay moving on. It is more likely that you will need time to coordinate how these rote-memorisation techniques balance with your encoding and interleaved retrieval methods. This coordination can be worked on over time.

We use the anatomy of a habit to create a robust system for conquering neuroticism: the  PEER-Peer system. This involves planting  good  cues that help you study and removing  bad  cues that make you procrastinate. Make sure the bad cues are removed to the point that you would be too lazy to procrastinate! If at first you don't succeed...:  According to  nudge theory  and the broader field of behavioural science, changing your environmental cues is one of the most powerful influencers of behaviour. If your first cue optimisation is unsuccessful at creating the desired behaviour, optimise and optimise again!   It is rare for cue optimisation to be ineffective when repeatedly optimised. Nudge theory: Environment is more important than willpower:  Lots of research shows that the environment influences our behaviour more than willpower. This is called  nudge theory   a branch of behavioural analysis science that explores how modifying our environment nudges us to engage in good or bad default behaviours.   Successful, productive people use  nudge theory, while people who constantly burn out or rely on motivation only use willpower.  Read  this article  by James Clear, the author of Atomic Habits, on how we can optimise our environment for decision-making.

Spiderwebbing is a common feature of beginner mindmaps when chunking is done incorrectly. We call it spiderwebbing because the many arrows and relationships look like spiderwebs between the leaves of a tree.  Spiderwebbing occurs when chunking is not based on the relationships. This is usually because the chunks were formed without order control or importance/retrieval-matching.  As a result, the relationships are scattered and chaotic, reducing retention, increasing the time taken to do the mindmap, overloading intrinsic load and creating lots of unnecessary frustration. In this example, we have directly chunked in the same way as a textbook. Afterwards, we have identified relationships and tried to represent them.   Note that in step 1, this is a pattern of  waterfalling  as all the connections spread outwards or"down" the hierarchy. There are no lateral relationships.  Attempting to add lateral relationships retroactively causes  spiderwebbing  in step 2. Mistake:   In this example, we have directly chunked in the same way as a textbook. Afterwards, we have identified relationships and tried to represent them.   Note that in step 1, this is a pattern of  waterfalling  as all the connections spread outwards or"down" the hierarchy. There are no lateral relationships.  Attempting to add lateral relationships retroactively causes  spiderwebbing  in step 2. In the below image, you can see a better way in which the textbook hierarchy is converted to a list, preventing bias in chunking.  Relationships are identified and converted into meaningful chunks.  The resulting map represents all of the relationships from the list. Chunks circled in yellow indicate a unique/new chunk that was formed logically as a result of trying to create the simplest flow. In this way, our network of knowledge represents how we have processed and understood the knowledge, not just what someone else has given to us.  Notice the improvements in flow, directionality, lateral connectivity, and the lack of unnecessary arrows. To avoid spiderwebbing...: 1. Regularly check that the overall chunk structure you are forming is the simplest and most intuitive.2. Whenever there are relationships between items that cause arrows and lines to become chaotic, think about if you could bring them closer or even into the same chunk.3. Avoid committing to a fixed chunk structure too early on. Stay open to changing your chunking structure if you need to.4. Avoid chunking in the exact same way as a textbook - this is often not the best way.5. Evaluate whether an arrow or line is necessary. Is the relationship important enough to express it? What is the nature of the relationship I'm trying to express?

Even with a full learning and self-management system, there are some marginal adjustments that can be made to help ensure optimal performance in times of pressure and stress. Complete the sublessons below to learn some of the most impactful factors that are worth optimising for.

metadata.docName: How do I encode if I haven't learned any encoding techniques yet?
metadata.originalText: The first set of encoding techniques is taught in Fundamentals 2. Before this point, there are two methods of improving your encoding ability:   Try your best with what you know  Attempt the 30-day self-regulation or deep processing challenges    While this may not seem like a reliable solution, simply trying your best with the methods you already are familiar with is one of the best things you can do in the early parts of the program.  The reason encoding techniques are not taught directly until Fundamentals 2 is because correct encoding can be challenging and is generally harder to learn to perform accurately than interleaved retrieval. After just a few days of practice with interleaved retrieval, most learners can see immediate improvements in memory and depth of understanding. These improvements continue for months as learners deepen their interleaving skills.  However, with only a few days of practice with  encoding, some learners will still be confused and find it difficult to assess their level of improvement.   Gaining competence with interleaved retrieval first is beneficial because:    It creates a stronger learning system with regular spacing.  It generates familiarity with higher-order learning as many interleaving strategies force higher-order learning even without deliberate encoding techniques. This makes it easier to learn advanced encoding techniques later.  It produces a greater benefit in a shorter period of time. Even with terrible encoding skills, good interleaving offers an immediate improvement. Dangers of technique overload:   The first set of encoding techniques is taught in Fundamentals 2. Before this point, there are two methods of improving your encoding ability:   Try your best with what you know  Attempt the 30-day self-regulation or deep processing challenges    While this may not seem like a reliable solution, simply trying your best with the methods you already are familiar with is one of the best things you can do in the early parts of the program.  The reason encoding techniques are not taught directly until Fundamentals 2 is because correct encoding can be challenging and is generally harder to learn to perform accurately than interleaved retrieval. After just a few days of practice with interleaved retrieval, most learners can see immediate improvements in memory and depth of understanding. These improvements continue for months as learners deepen their interleaving skills.  However, with only a few days of practice with  encoding, some learners will still be confused and find it difficult to assess their level of improvement.   Gaining competence with interleaved retrieval first is beneficial because:    It creates a stronger learning system with regular spacing.  It generates familiarity with higher-order learning as many interleaving strategies force higher-order learning even without deliberate encoding techniques. This makes it easier to learn advanced encoding techniques later.  It produces a greater benefit in a shorter period of time. Even with terrible encoding skills, good interleaving offers an immediate improvement. For these reasons, we recommend that learners simply use their existing methods of encoding and focus on learning through interleave retrieval first.   For those who have the time and feel extremely limited by their current encoding methods  despite attempting interleaved retrieval already, the 30-day self-regulation and deep processing challenges are found on the  Supplementary learning  page.  Both challenges introduce basic principles of encoding that can help create a stronger foundation to build on in later lessons. While these challenges do not teach a step-by-step encoding system, following the instructions should substantially improve overall confidence with information processing so that it is no longer a rate limiter to continuing your interleaved retrieval.  You can learn more about rate limiters in skill acquisition  here.

Feel free to skip this lesson if you are not learning anatomy.    Many chunk structures can be used for learning anatomy. The goal is to reduce memorisation by creating chunk structures that offload the  most difficult aspects. Avoid creating chunk structures that are so obvious they contribute nothing to your learning.  The following are potential chunk structures you can explore. In most cases, you will need a combination of these approaches, even within the same region of anatomy.   Anatomical division (e.g. anterior vs. posterior)  Functional division (e.g. flexion vs. contraction)  Embryological rationale (can be highly effective if you keep it simple)  Clinical application   A chunk such as clinical application   can also be divided into multiple types of applications. Consider the differences between these clinical applications:   Surgical approaches and techniques  Forming differential diagnoses  Urgency of management  Mechanism of injury/disease  Management complications   If you have never considered clinical applications this way, this is a high-yield area for you to look into. Not only will this help with your immediate retention, but it is also a method of thinking you are expected to be competent in during your clinical years.    Unfortunately, many medical schools do not prioritise this.    When chunking for anatomy,  fluency  is about identifying multiple possible chunk structures, then evaluating which approach feels most intuitive and reduces rote memorisation as much as possible. Important:  It is important to have these chunk structures in mind  as soon as you begin studying. It would be inefficient to learn the information in isolation first and arrive at the structures later, since you are not giving the information relevance while you study it. Framing bias can also make it difficult to consider alternative chunk structures. Even though information is usually retrieved in  multiple ways  during clinical practice, many students can only think of  one way  to chunk the information. This is a red flag indicating that the learner is heavily influenced by framing bias and how they believe the information needs to be structured.  Significant interleaving is essential in all learning, but especially anatomy. Make sure to test on alternative chunk structures and lower-order fact recall through spaced interleaved retrieval.

How's your time management going so far?  The basics you learned in the High-Yield phase are often enough for most people. But if you're struggling with consistency, you might be running into these mistakes:    Not scheduling: If youre the type of person who wings it and has no structure in their studying, it is time to make some plans.     Vague scheduling: This happens when you are not specific enough with your scheduling (e.g. allocating time for Maths or English but not specifying what youre going to do).    Overambitious scheduling: If you cant follow your schedule because its overpacked and unrealistic, you may become demoralised and stressed and give up.

Key principles: 1. Due to the nature of wicked learning environments, its impractical to actually calculate EVs precisely.2. We can simplify the calculation with some generalisations which still allows a positive EV decision-making process. To help simplify the variables of real-life decision-making we can apply the following steps. Instructions for simplifying decisions -  Step 1: Remove only the smaller and insignificant variables. Step 2: Broadly categorise significance into four tiers. Step 3: Broadly categorise chance into four tiers. Step 4: Evaluate the EV of your decision based on this simplification.

Progression note:  The concepts and methods discussed in these systems assume a certain level of proficiency with the methods taught in the core program. While you are free to explore these at any time, we recommend at least a late Ascent 2 level of skill competency. Key principles: 1. Systems are a composite of techniques.2. The optimal system depends on your needs (and can be adapted following the core principles of learning). Systems can be incredibly powerful and liberating, but they aren't magic. Just because these systems exist does not mean you will necessarily be able to use them effectively.  If you find these systems difficult, there is a high chance that the base cognitive processes may be underdeveloped. In this case, please work through the core stages until you have sufficient cognitive mastery to apply these systems. None of these systems should be technically or cognitive difficult as they should be applications of existing habits you have trained.

Key principles: 1. The future is unknown and all decisions are uncertain; expecting certainty in complex decisions is unrealistic.2. Good decisions are about managing probability and information (this will be explored further in later lessons).3. Indecision is a decision and deciding based on comfort may be due to status quo bias, which may cause us harm.4. Opportunity, uncertainty and information all change with time and are related to each other.5. Holding off on a decision to gain more information is acceptable, but it is advisable to be aware of the consequences of the decision to hold off.6. Information and risk is modifiable with our actions.7. Gain information earlier in the process. Complete the sublessons below to see how these principles modify our decision-making process in increasingly uncertain situations. Further reading:  Learn more about status quo bias  here.  Read the paper by Kahneman et. al (1991) on how status quo bias and loss aversion can affect decision-making  here.

In the sublessons below, we demonstrate the usage of the Shoot step across a variety of subjects. Even if your particular discipline is not included as an example, the  methods of thinking  are completely transferrable.  You can skip sub-lessons you are not interested in by marking them as complete. However, we recommend watching several different examples to get a clear understanding of the technique.   Once you have proficiency with Aim and Shoot as individual steps, you can (and maybe already have without any instruction) start transitioning between Aim and Shoot seamlessly in a continuous cycle. Watch the video below to see this in action. You will see the results of this process in each of the subject-specific examples. Struggling with Aim-Shoot cycling?:  Don't worry. Keep practising, and you should naturally feel like you can do it. Aim-Shoot cycling is often something that develops as a result of upper conscious competence in each individual step.

"Traps" refer to behaviours and tendencies that cause  massive damage and harm. While there are not many single techniques that are so powerful that they completely transform your learning by themselves, traps can be so harmful that even  one  of them can make all of your hard work and"right methods" useless! Alert:   This lesson is considered high-yield.  Many learners struggle with improving due to these three issues. We recommend that you pay careful attention to catch yourself. These common traps are behaviours and patterns that cause many learners to waste energy with minimal benefit. The traps are...: 1. Deathly spirals2. Bandwagon fallacy3. Misfitting techniques  Deathly spirals:  This happens when you use a technique that helps in the short term but harms you long-term. The most dangerous part is the deeper you fall, the less time and opportunity you have to escape! The best approach is to  avoid  these entirely. Ill warn you about possible spirals throughout this program.  These can create something we call  learning debt. This is where your future self must do extra work to pay for inefficiencies in your current methods. Because your future self will probably be even busier than you are now, this creates a high chance of being increasingly overwhelmed as time passes.   A common deathly spiral is not having enough time to work on learning skills because there is too much time spent on learning using ineffective methods. Using ineffective methods helps short-term because (although ineffective) it addresses your immediate need to"cover content". However, long-term, having inadequate skills to tackle the volume or complexity of learning will force a no-win scenario where the only options are to fail or give up. Bandwagon fallacy:  This happens when you use a technique just because other people do. If a technique is  common, that doesnt mean its  good.   This can be insidious because common methods are often the ones many successful people use.  However, amazing athletes don't necessarily make amazing coaches.  Successful people often do not know what contributed to their success. In addition, people who  failed  with the same methods are often much less vocal and public about their failures.    To understand if a method is effective or not, we must know:    Who used the method and was  successful?  Who used the method and was  unsuccessful? Misfitting techniques:  This happens when you use techniques incorrectly or in the wrong situation. The right technique often makes short work out of the problem. If you need substantial effort with a method and it isnt giving you results, it might not be the right solution. We will also make these situations clear throughout the program for the techniques we teach.   In the diagram above, you can see that method 1 requires much less effort than method 2 to achieve the same result.  This means that method 1 is a superior approach to achieving the desired result.    When we increase our efforts using method 1, we can also achieve even greater results. On the other hand, when we increase our efforts using method 2, we may be unable to improve by much as the method itself limits us. Flashcards (especially using apps) have taken the world by storm. They have a cult-like following in some circles. Flashcards are useful for creating easier spaced retrieval schedules and utilising micro-learning (a form of learning where sessions are only a few minutes long).   However, overreliance on flashcards (due to bandwagon fallacy) can directly create a misfitting technique and a deathly spiral! How flashcards can become deadly -  Step 1: A system is created that increases dependence on more flashcards Step 2: It creates learning debt by relying on your future self to do more repetition to cover the material Step 3: It struggles to create knowledge networks, which results in poor memory and superficial understanding Step 4: This increases the need for more repetition and other methods to fill gaps in depth or complexity of knowledge Step 5: There is less time available to use other methods of learning (or even learn about other methods) Step 6: Every day, more cards accumulate, which increases the level of sacrifice needed to escape the spiral Fun fact: Software developers are usually not educators:  As internationally recognised learning experts, we are invited to advise some of the world's most popular learning software companies on their products.   Unfortunately, what we see behind the curtain is shocking.  In our experience, most learning software developers have minimal learning expertise in their team and there is a high resistance to engaging with our advice as it impacts the profitability of their business model.

Key principles: 1. Many skills are components of more advanced composite skills.2. It is not always clear how component skills contribute to overall learning efficiency until much later. Therefore, it is important to train each component skill thoroughly to prevent future issues.3. Stay open-minded and avoid selective learning. You will surprise yourself with how some techniques can impact your overall learning and self-management abilities! We're committed to your success:  Your success is our success. Nothing is more important for us than for you to become a successful, confident, and efficient learner. Consistently training efficient and confident learners is crucial for us as educators, and strategic for us as a company.   So we promise you this: if there is a faster and more efficient way to learn the skills we teach, we will tell you.    Our recommendations are based on the fastest, safest, most error-free pathway to cognitive retraining we have found. As a result, the consistency and magnitude of results from learners in our program far exceed the research and industry norms.

Over the past decade, we've identified 3 behaviours and principles that dramatically increased our learners' chances of successfully learning new skills. Explore the sublessons below to dive into each of them.
In the sublessons below, we'll refresh on some of the basics of scheduling and time management.

Chunking is a technique that increases studying efficiency by organising information into meaningful groups. It improves memory and reduces unnecessary cognitive load. Groups are created based on sharing the same underlying purpose, concept, rationale and/or other important and logical features. Key principle:   Chunking is a technique that increases studying efficiency by organising information into meaningful groups. It improves memory and reduces unnecessary cognitive load. Groups are created based on sharing the same underlying purpose, concept, rationale and/or other important and logical features. The same piece of information can be part of more than one chunk, depending on how it is"filtered". Just as individual pieces of information relate to each other, chunks themselves also relate to each other. A topic can be divided into a few big chunks, allowing learners to rapidly gain a big-picture understanding of topics.  These big chunks can be related to each other and mapped to form a"backbone" or overall structure and scaffold for the topic. These chunks can then be divided into smaller chunks to represent the major concepts, which can be divided into sub-concepts and, eventually, details.   Our brain finds it easier to understand information, especially complex information, when it is well organised. Chunking allows our brain to handle more information simultaneously by creating discrete groups.   If 100 items are grouped into 20 categories, which are grouped into a further 5 categories, we have two levels of chunking. We organise the information using just the largest 5 chunks, then"unpack" each chunk into the 20"sub-chunks". We can then organise the information further, allowing us to unpack it again into the full 100 items.   Amazingly, our brain is able to access these 100 items via the chunks, letting us have a high level of recall and retention for each item, while simultaneously reducing the"cognitive load" on our brain since it only has to keep track of 5 big categories, instead of 100 individual facts.  You can think of it like putting lots of files into folders. It lets us find the files we need faster, when we organise the information into more folders. If a folder has too many files, we can make sub-folders to organise them even more. This is exactly the same approach with learning.     Information is easier to understand, and retention increases.  Complicated concepts or groups of concepts are more logical and make more intuitive sense.  Learning lots of information is less overwhelming and takes less time.  Chunks are formed through evaluation and value judgments, not just because there are similarities. It is not enough to find similarities. The similarities must be important and meaningful enough to chunk them together. This extra step is the difference between'analyse' and'evaluate' in higher-order learning.     Mindmaps lack a clear backbone or structure. Points can look like they are all over the place or seem chaotic.  Points on a mindmap have many branches coming off from them (more than 4). These branches can usually be chunked and grouped further.  It is hard to keep track of relationships between ideas as there is an overwhelming volume of relationships. The information hasn't been prioritised, and the brain is trying to keep track of too many things at once.  Overall retention suffers significantly.  The time taken to study increases significantly.  The total amount of information that can be studied in a single sitting reduces massively.   Do it better   Identify areas where there are too many branches coming from a single point. Group these further.  Ask yourself why facts or concepts are important to help discover different ways you can chunk them. A lack of"why is it important" questions during the pre-study stages often leads to inadequate chunking or poor backbones in mindmaps later on.

Here are four easy techniques to increase your focus. Regardless of your experience level, we recommend ticking these off as a first step. Techniques -  Step 1: Intention Setting: Set specific, achievable goals you want to accomplish by the end of your study block. Make sure you physically write them down. Step 2: Ritualisation: Create a set routine to prepare yourself to focus. This routine will help your brain to transition into high-focus mode automatically. Step 3: Zone: Set up a study area that has zero distractions. Making sure you have no distractions while studying is extremely important for building focus. Step 4: Cheat Sheet: If anything distracts you while studying, write it down on a cheat sheet and remove that distraction from your study zone. References:  The famous  Marshmallow study  showed that willpower is less important than the environment.   Functional magnetic resonance imaging (fMRI) studies  showed some distraction pathways in our brain and the reward responses they trigger.  This  study  showed that the ability to resist temptation and distraction is independent of the ability to create and maintain focus.   Psyched Up: How the Science of Mental Preparation Can Help You Succeed  is a book covering pre-performance ritualisation.   Pre-performance ritualisation  helps with self-regulating pre-performance states and creating automaticity in high-skill task execution.

What questions are usually too simple and unconnected to produce high retention.  Why  and  How  questions connect the information to other pieces of information and therefore are much more meaningful.  Questions that are more  emotional  or  personal  and that  spark curiosity  also connect much more. Since the objective is to enter a problem-solving mindset, do not ask questions about problems to which you already know the answer. If you are genuinely curious about the topic, you will be able to generate more knowledge. Note:  Asking the right questions may be challenging in the beginning. However, by generating more curiosity, you will also find that you start to enjoy studying more, making it much easier to learn and retain information.

Key questions -  Step 1: What are the biggest downsides? Step 2: Are these significant? Step 3: Can I protect these? If so, how? Significance can be absolute or relative. Absolute significance may be that it is not an acceptable loss at any level. Relative significance may be unacceptable only if the opportunity is not high enough.   Its important to be comfortable with answering I dont know and not to assume the answers to these questions, as they have a direct impact on your decision-making process. If you answer"I don't know", the next action should be to seek information to gain more certainty. The faster we can do this, the faster we can make a decision. Marginal gains:  In decision-making, gaining more information about the variables we are uncertain about is often the earliest and most productive marginal gains of making a good decision.

Think of a hobby, series, sport or instrument. How would you teach a complete beginner about this?    You are probably imagining methods of teaching and learning that are drastically different from how you might study and learn more"formal" or"intensive" topics. Some of the topics we know best have been learned without ever using traditional"studying methods". Likely, your brain learned in a way that felt natural and efficient (and probably enjoyable).  Many believe that studying within curriculums and courses cannot be like this. However, information is just information. They would be surprised to find out that the learning methods we will teach you allow you to use your brain in that authentic, efficient way that it's capable of.

Social media algorithms promote tips and tricks that can be understood in 30 seconds. Unfortunately, social media algorithms do not care about helping you become a better learner. Falling into a trap of tips and tricks can create the perfect recipe for long-term failure. Thinking critically about "tips":  Tips and tricks are easy and popular. So, if they worked well, shouldn't everyone be a great learner?

metadata.docName: How does layering improve learning and cognitive load?
metadata.originalText: Learning in layers is one of the most powerful principles you can integrate into your learning system. It makes large volumes of content less overwhelming and more relevant, improves retention, and creates deeper mastery.  One of the reasons for this is that learning in layers optimises your cognitive load. Understanding how layering improves learning at a theoretical level helps you self-diagnose and adapt your learning if it isn't as effective as you want it to be. Progression note:   Learning in layers is one of the most powerful principles you can integrate into your learning system. It makes large volumes of content less overwhelming and more relevant, improves retention, and creates deeper mastery.  One of the reasons for this is that learning in layers optimises your cognitive load. Understanding how layering improves learning at a theoretical level helps you self-diagnose and adapt your learning if it isn't as effective as you want it to be. In the early phases of learning a new topic, our brain needs a big-picture frame of  relevance. It needs to know why it should care about the new information we're about to consume.  When we discussed  prestudying, we were able to split the cognitive load across multiple focused sessions to build the big picture, give ourselves more relevance, and increase our efficiency.    Learning in layers takes this to the next level by giving our    order control    and    inquiry    process a clearer focus.   By creating focused layers, we engage in what is called  self-regulated    scaffolding, which is when we teach ourselves by deconstructing a topic and reconstructing it in a way that is easier and more manageable for us to understand and develop expertise.   Representing this visually makes it easier to see what learning in layers helps us to achieve.    Knowledge and expertise are inherently structured in layers. For many, the formation and discovery of these layers come after years of learning. While it certainly takes time and dedication for expertise to build, this process can be massively expedited by deliberately targeting our learning on each layer.    Knowledge is often presented in a mix of layers. Details and concepts are interspersed with infrequent hints to logic. Learning in the same order, without  order control, creates cognitive overload as our brain struggles to make the information relevant.   As learners, we must strategically apply our cognitive load to extract and make judgments on the layers.  The contents of each layer are usually not explicitly given to you. We must construct these ourselves using the methods of chunking we have already practised.   Sometimes, the apparent"logic" in our resources is not the best chunk to use after independently evaluating using our importance-based chunking process.    As we have already learned, we should always utilise  order control  to find the most relevant information first. Once we have a foundation of relevant information, we can find the next set of more detailed relevant information. Continuing this process helps us build a comprehensive knowledge structure.  As shown in the graph below, conventional methods of learning take in information that is usually at a high level of detail very early. The relevance of information is fluctuating which makes it difficult for our brain to create a model of knowledge. Therefore, the difficulty of learning is high throughout. This is tiring and inefficient.    Instead, by learning in layers, we can invest our cognitive resources more strategically, as follows:    Spend more effort upfront to identify the logic. This establishes a foundation of relevance.  Work through increasing layers of detail, continuously moving to the next most relevant piece.  Over time, the difficulty of learning reduces as our knowledge network becomes more robust.

Anti-procrastination tips: 1. Make a list and break it down. Breaking down tasks reduces the risk of being overwhelmed. Making tasks more specific also helps us focus.2. Set yourself up for success. Our behaviours are shaped by our environment so start shaping your environment.3. Get started beforehand. Lean into your perfectionist tendencies and get the ball rolling.4. Group up. Leverage social pressure to keep ourselves accountable.5. Aim for a personal best. Meet yourself where you are and aim to push one step higher. No one is perfect, but everyone can do their best. Remember, these tips are only a quick introduction to get you started. We will cover many more techniques later throughout the course.

Information (or memory) is stored in your brain as a network. Your brain determines whether information is worth retaining ( remember Cave theory ) based on how connected this information is to other information.    If it's part of a bigger network, it judges it to be probably more important and, therefore, worth keeping.    If information is isolated and not very connected, it is probably less relevant. Your efficient brain will cleanly prune it! Reference regarding hypercorrection:   The hypercorrection effect in younger and older adults
