Main points: 1. Environmental optimisation is one of the most important things you can do to create focus.2. Dont try to change your behaviour through sheer willpower and effort. People with high levels of consistent productivity invest time and effort in optimising their environment.3. Remove bad triggers and make distractions harder to access.4. Add good triggers and make good behaviours more convenient to engage in.

Your brain doesnt work this way. You are not a factory  working faster will not necessarily produce more output. Instead, when you try to perform  faster, the quality of your work decreases, and errors increase.  Of course, making errors due to poor understanding or retention is an unavoidable part of the learning process. However, we should still aim to reduce  unnecessary or unproductive  errors to avoid becoming inefficient.   Often, people try so hard that they dont have the mental space, time or perspective to see if their actions are correct. They become so consumed with trying hard that they dont realise their efforts are not taking them towards their goals.  A great example is studying. Most students are so consumed with studying harder that they dont even realise how bad their technique is. Even if they realise, they do not have time to learn to work smarter! Furthermore, activities that seem unproductive may improve productivity, like relaxing to maintain mental health. Key principle:  Hard work can prevent smart work. Working smarter requires time and mental freedom. If a standard of performance is only possible with high levels of effort, the only option is to take a short-term loss.
Common mistakes: 1. No backbone2. Segmental mapping3. Framing bias4. Lower-order inquiry5. Importance checklist6. Insufficient chunking
In the sublessons below, we'll go even deeper with note-taking with more examples and guidelines.

Sneak peak (pun intended):  The video above is taken from Summit, one of the final points of the course. If you are interested, you can download and read our more technical curriculum overview document  here.

Many people pack their schedules too ambitiously because they have lots to do. Unfortunately, a full schedule means nothing if you cannot follow it.   Overscheduling is problematic for multiple reasons:    It creates an illusion of productivity. What matters is what you completed, not what you scheduled.  It makes it harder to assess your true workload capacity. This makes it harder to gradually improve as you don't know where your limits truly are.  It makes planning more difficult because your ability to estimate how long it will take to complete tasks is inaccurate.  It creates the undesirable psychological impact of getting used to falling behind and struggling to catch up. This is not a positive mindset that fosters high-quality work and well-being. Key principle:  Schedules become useless when you cannot follow them. Always start by scheduling with a relaxed and manageable timing. Then, fit more tasks and events as your time management skills improve. Overscheduling reduces productivity while making improvement more difficult. It's also essential to schedule relaxation and fun activities, especially when busy. The human brain is not a tireless machine. When we're busy, it's easy not to schedule relaxation time in our day because we want to get more things done.  Unfortunately, this can have a detrimental effect. It can lead to more unintentional breaks in the form of procrastination, which ultimately causes us to fall behind on our schedule and makes us feel guilty. Over time, trying to adhere to an over-full schedule can contribute to burnout. Instead, scheduling a dedicated time daily to unwind and relax can help us retain focus and energy during the day when we need to be productive. Key principle:  Even the most productive people aren't machines. Schedule time for breaks and relaxation during busy days. This ensures we are rested, energised, and focused.

Modern research tells us that when we multitask, the quality of our focus is reduced, and we can only switch between two or three things rapidly. This is known as  cognitive switching penalty.  This means that if we multitask while learning, the quality of our learning will decrease, and we will take longer to learn things because we are wasting time needing to refocus. Key principle:  Highly efficient people do not multitask. Instead, they  concentrate on one task at a time  to avoid the cognitive switching penalty. This is fundamental to developing deep focus. References on cognitive switching penalty:  Summary article  here.

Diet and (especially) sleep are two of the most critical factors for your cognitive performance (and health).   You must protect your sleep! We have covered how important this is in the past. Sleep deprivation is always a losing battle when we need peak cognitive performance.   Diet is about what to eat and when to eat. The food you eat is not going to turn you into a genius, but  research  shows that it helps your brain operate with better mental fitness. Interestingly,  when  you eat can have a bigger impact on assessment performance.   This is because immediately following a large meal, we typically become drowsy (especially if you are also sleep deprived)*. This is called  postprandial somnolence  and is the rest and digest response, which is opposite to the fight and flight response. You will feel less energetic and more drowsy after big meals. Therefore, avoid eating large meals within two hours before an assessment. *Research note:  More recent research (Bazar et al., 2004) into post-prandial somnolence indicates that it is not a redistribution of blood flow, but rather a change in neurohormonal concentrations that create the effect. The blood-flow hypothesis is still taught in many medical schools, as is the neurohormonal hypothesis. This latter hypothesis was  not  mentioned in the video.  Bazar, Kimberly A., A. Joon Yun, and Patrick Y. Lee. Debunking a myth: neurohormonal and vagal modulation of sleep centers, not redistribution of blood flow, may account for postprandial somnolence. Medical hypotheses 63.5 (2004): 778-782.

Many people deal with procrastination with unsustainable solutions. People attribute their procrastination to a lack of motivation. However, this leads them to become trapped in a cycle of fluctuating motivation, which leads to burnout over time.   When we give procrastination a name, it can prevent us from tackling the issue effectively. Procrastination can occur due to many different reasons. Each trigger and response can have different solutions. To end procrastination, we must be specific in what behaviour we are trying to change and what causes this behaviour.    Motivation always fluctuates because many variables influence it. Your plans must allow you to achieve your goals even on a bad day with low motivation. What influences motivation?: 1. Mood2. Sleep quality and energy3. Interest or curiosity in the activity4. Sense of purpose5. Punishments and rewards6. Sense of voluntariness and autonomy7. Alignment with identity and values8. Competence at activity9. Psychological reserve10. Physical well-being You can see how the solution to address one factor can be dramatically different to another. It is also unrealistic to assume all of these factors can be perfectly controlled on a daily basis. Instead, we must rely on habits and environments to move us into auto-pilot. By doing so, we can become willpower enhanced, rather than willpower dependent. **Willpower-Dependent Approach**  * Relies on motivation and willpower to initiate actions. * Productivity fluctuates based on motivation levels. * Inconsistent in execution. * Can lead to burnout due to reliance on willpower. * Limited reserve for unexpected challenges.  **Willpower-Enhanced Approach**  * Leverages habits and environmental cues to make actions effortless. * Productivity is driven by established habits. * Highly consistent in execution. * Prevents burnout by reducing reliance on willpower. * Allows for the utilization of willpower when challenges arise, enabling individuals to overcome obstacles.

Sleep is an essential part of our natural human physiology. Failure to get adequate, deep, high-quality sleep can affect our:   Imagination  Creativity  Brain development  Concentration  Emotional regulation  Hormonal balance  Memory consolidation* Double impact:  Those who get inadequate sleep may both be unable to sleep at night as well as unable to stay awake during the day - a combination of insomnia  and  hypersomnolence. This is a  well-studied  phenomenon where our brain processes and organises information so that we can use it more effectively. In simple terms, this means that not getting enough sleep would cause part of what you studied to go to waste!   There are four stages of sleep, corresponding to the brain activity, that can be seen at each respective stage. The first three are NREM (non-REM), while the final is REM, standing for rapid eye movement.   NREM Stage 1  Drowsy, low brain activity  NREM Stage 2  Light sleep, lower brain activity  NREM Stage 3  Deep sleep, lowest brain activity  REM Stage 4  Deep sleep, higher brain activity    Some consider a 5th stage of further REM sleep, which is argued to be an extension of stage 4. Not all stages are equal:  Most of the beneficial aspects of sleep are only activated in stage 3 and stage 4. Therefore, light and frequently interrupted sleep is much less restorative and beneficial. For years, people have thought about how to get less sleep and unlock more hours per day. Unfortunately, studies have shown that this is not possible. Although it is possible to schedule your sleep so that you slip into deeper stages almost immediately, some of the benefits of sleep are removed when the sleep session is too short.   The conclusion is that we need long, undisturbed, prolonged sleep of a high quality. How much sleep do we need?:  To accumulate enough prolonged deep sleep, most people require 8 to 9 hours per day. This is much more than most people assume they need. Further reading:  Learn about sleep-dependent memory consolidation  https://www.frontiersin.org/articles/10.3389/fnhum.2018.00018/full  or read this summary article  here.  American Sleep Association.  What Is Sleep and Why Is It Important?  National Institute of Neurological Disorders and Stroke.  Brain Basics: Understanding Sleep. Sleep Foundation.  What Happens When You Sleep?

Key principles: 1. Protect your time by avoiding high-risk blocks and preventing anticipated risks from hijacking your time.2. If you can't protect a block of time, do not schedule important things there. Instructions -  Step 1: Schedule the important tasks first.  Step 2: Identify factors that put your scheduled tasks at risk. Step 3: Take steps to protect those tasks. If you can't protect a task, move it to a safer time. Step 4: Repeat this process for lower-priority tasks.

Good time management means we can make things work on the  worst  days. In the sublessons below, we will build on your unconscious competence with all the earlier time and task management systems to implement more advanced self-management methods.

Prioritising maximises the value of your time. Just 15 minutes spent prioritising can save you days. This is why task prioritisation is more important than time management. Not all time is spent equal.:  Time spent on something that gets you to your goals is more important than time spent on something less meaningful. No matter how great your schedule is, it wont help you reach your goals if it is not  aligned  with those goals. To achieve your goals as efficiently as possible, you must prioritise the  content  of your schedule.   Don't confuse being busy with being productive. Productive usage of time means that your time is spent on things that get you closer to your goals (whatever those goals are).    At the end of every day, identify the one or two highest priority items that put the future you in the best position. Remember that urgent doesn't always mean important!   Once you have identified these, block them into your schedule. All of your other tasks should be centred around these blocks. Helpful questions for prioritising well - Ask yourself these questions to identify high priority items to block out in your schedule Step 1: What are the one or two most important things for me to complete? Step 2: What (if done) would provide me the best value or put me in the best position in the future? Step 3: What skill, attribute, or resource do I need that would be life-changing (even if it isn't urgent right now)? You may be interested in    this    YouTube video for a brief demonstration using the same principles.

Neuroticism is often associated with a fixed mindset and fear of failure. Neuroticism is considered one of the five core personality traits according to the evidence-based  Big Five personality framework.   Take an evidence-based personality quiz    here.    Some neuroticism is beneficial as it keeps you safe, creates high standards, and provides motivation. As a result, neuroticism is common among high achievers. However, excessive neuroticism causes significant mental health problems such as anxiety and depression. This may also explain why anxiety and depression are especially prominent in high-performing individuals.  In the sublessons below, we will introduce some frameworks for managing neuroticism through five stages: prevention, planning, execution, measurement, and management.

In addition to the Priority 0+1 system... - Use the following methods to enhance your task management ability. Step 1: 2-minute rule: Straight-away complete tasks that you can finish within 2 minutes. Step 2: Eat the Frog: First do the tasks that you dont want to do.  Step 3: Manage dependencies: Clear bottlenecks by identifying tasks that future tasks depend on. This often involves developing skills or working with others. The task may not appear to be a high priority at first but is important in enabling future tasks. We have compiled a list of recommended apps that our team has extensively experimented with and integrated into our personal productivity systems. View the list  here.

In the sublessons below, we will further develop your time management and prioritisation skills, building on your current level of unconscious competence with the earlier skills and strategies you have practised.

In the sublessons below, we demonstrate the usage of the Skin step across a variety of subjects. Even if your particular discipline is not included as an example, the  methods of thinking  are completely transferrable.  You can skip sub-lessons you are not interested in by marking them as complete. However, we recommend watching several different examples to get a clear understanding of the technique.

The  modified method of loci  builds upon the original method of loci (more commonly known as the Memory Palace) to help you remember large strings of arbitrary information. Instructions -  Step 1: Create a story or"scene" with different elements for each piece of information. Step 2: Make elements absurd. Mundane and boring elements are not memorable. Step 3: Keep elements spatially distinct. Don't have too many things happening in the same"location". Step 4: Make elements interdependent. Create relationships between elements to help with retention. Step 5: Draw out your"scene" in your mindmap canvas to serve as a memory anchor. This replaces the normal"visualisations" you have been using. Your drawing doesnt have to look nice; it just needs to consolidate the information in your brain. Even students with zero artistic ability can successfully use this technique. Usage notes for this technique: 1. As a lower-order memorisation method, it is not effective or efficient for information that has causal/functional/conceptual relationships with other pieces of information.2. This method excels with lists of information (ordered or unordered).3. It can be used for small lists of just a few items, or longer lists of 30+.4. This method should not replace higher-order encoding approaches if a higher-order relational network is viable.

English and languages have a few aspects we need to recognise before we can apply the Bear Hunter System. Once we understand the nature of these subjects, we can correctly apply the BHS to achieve top results much faster.

Some learners find it difficult to schedule all their studying sessions throughout the week.  Therefore, we've created some example schedules that you can use for typical school or lecture timetables. These schedules have been tried and tested across thousands of students and are safe templates for you to base your own schedules around.   Each schedule includes a  prestudy  and  revision  routine and is rated on:    Development speed:  How quickly you would be able to learn the skills in this program   Flexibility:  How easily you can adapt to changes in your schedule   Time efficiency:  How effectively the schedule maximises available time to produce meaningful work   Difficulty:  The complexity of trying to follow the schedule    Download the schedule examples PDF    here.

metadata.docName: How can I draw or doodle for learning and studying? I'm not a good drawer.
metadata.originalText: Many students are put off drawing because they feel that they just aren't artistic. However, drawing skills can be trained, just like almost every other skill. Luckily, you don't need to be an artist, you just need to be able to draw enough to activate the visual processing, artistic expression and abstraction part of your brain.

metadata.docName: How do I make processed notes for equations like in chemistry?
metadata.originalText: This video outlines a method of simplifying note-writing in chemistry using a form of chunking by commonality. Notice the use of spatial arrangement to help remember common features of the reaction.

Key principles: 1. Since outcomes are not certain, decisions can be made to further our position, which gives us more leverage and influence over future decisions.2. Decisions with a positive expected value are more likely to produce the expected outcome over multiple repetitions.3. Emotions are an important consideration of decision-making, but making only emotional decisions has a negative expected value. Making position-based decisions is the norm in most industries and professions where decisions are uncertain and high-risk.  Note that expected value is also called +/- EV or just expectancy (e.g.  plus EV  vs.  minus EV, or  positive expectancy  vs.  negative expectancy). Different fields use different terms, but they refer to the same concept.

metadata.docName: Arrows vs. lines when making maps
metadata.originalText: Sometimes, we see that lines are used to represent relationships instead of arrows. Arrows require a deeper level of processing to be more explicit about the nature of a relationship, whereas lines can simply indicate the general awareness of a connection that has not been prioritised at higher levels.  This simple decision to use arrows forces us to consider the nature of a relationship more deeply, activating higher-order learning. Consider that an arrow going one way is different to an arrow going another way, which is different to a double-sided arrow, a curly-bracket arrow, or a deliberately non-arrow line. Take note:   Sometimes, we see that lines are used to represent relationships instead of arrows. Arrows require a deeper level of processing to be more explicit about the nature of a relationship, whereas lines can simply indicate the general awareness of a connection that has not been prioritised at higher levels.  This simple decision to use arrows forces us to consider the nature of a relationship more deeply, activating higher-order learning. Consider that an arrow going one way is different to an arrow going another way, which is different to a double-sided arrow, a curly-bracket arrow, or a deliberately non-arrow line. Notice how each combination of arrows indicates a different type of relationship. All of the arrow combinations are variations on the same relationships identified by the lines.   Lines are still acceptable to indicate that the information is connected but without any directionality or cause/effect relationship. This is more common in the lower levels of detail, where information in a specific detailed chunk may be split into multiple component facts. The facts may simply exist within this lower-level chunk without having any directionality.

Key principles: 1. Due to the inherent uncertainty in decisions, judging the quality of a decision by the outcome is not likely to be accurate.2. Decision-making processes should be the primary metric for the quality of a decision.3. Outcomes can be used as information to be weighed against other information in the judgement of the decision-making process. This concept of outcome uncertainty forms a fundamental principle of the next set of strategies.

Follow these guidelines to optimise your sleep. If you have chronically struggled with sleep, we recommend applying most or all of these guidelines first. You can always pull back with a less rigid routine later once your sleep is improved and your sleep debt has recovered. Guidelines -  Step 1: Sound: Remove disruptive sounds, wear earplugs and/or play white noise. Step 2: Caffeine: Avoid caffeine (including caffeine in foods or tea) within six hours of sleep. For some, any coffee after noon can still be detrimental! Step 3: Routine: Do a wind-down routine 30 minutes before sleeping. Recommended to do meditation or focus training as instructed. Step 4: Incentive: Schedule events or activities the next morning to incentivise you to wake up early. Try to make them activities you look forward to or utilise an accountability buddy. Step 5: Bed space: Only sleep or light bed-time reading on or near your bed. If possible, do not study in your bedroom. If this is not viable, create a clear division between workspace and sleeping space. Step 6: Light: Use black-out curtains or remove any light sources as much as possible (including from your phone). Strong light in the morning can also improve circadian rhythms and give you a more natural sleep cycle. Step 7: Exercise: Engage in aerobic exercise during the day, preferably in the morning. Step 8: Devices: Turn off devices 30 minutes before sleep. Lead into your routine. Step 9: Napping: Avoid day-time napping if possible. Perform energising activities if you feel drowsy during the day (e.g. going for a walk or som breathing techniques like Wim Hof) Step 10: Consistency: Minimise social jet lag and sleep at similar hours on all days.
In the sublessons below, we'll explore the  anatomy  of sleep and how we can optimise it to get the deep rest we need and deserve.
